title: Who Predicts Whom? The Regression Setup
text:
Which one is y?

Theory

Does practice score predict final exam score — or the reverse?


The numbers are the same either way. Your research question picks which is y.


[DECK ch01-01 · 6 min · ~790 words · clicks 7 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us begin with the question that opens this entire course. Does a student’s practice score predict their final exam score, or does the prediction run in the other direction? If you open the spreadsheet, you will find the same two columns and the same numbers no matter which direction you read them. This means that the data, on its own, cannot tell you which variable is the outcome and which is the predictor. [CLICK 1] That decision belongs to you. Whatever you want to predict or explain becomes your y, and whatever you predict it from becomes your x. The same two columns can therefore support two entirely different studies. You draw the arrow between the variables; the data does not draw it for you. [NEXT]

title: Who Predicts Whom? The Regression Setup
text:
Same pair, two questions
The research question assigns the roles


Does early reading forecast later exams? y = GCSE · x = LRT




Do later exams trace back to age-11 reading? y = LRT · x = GCSE



[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 2 · ~72s] Let us make that idea concrete with a real pair of variables. The first is a reading test taken at age eleven, called the LRT, and the second is an exam taken at age sixteen, called the GCSE. Same students, same two columns, but the roles change when the question changes. [CLICK 1] If the question is whether early reading forecasts later exam performance, then GCSE is the thing we are predicting. GCSE becomes y, and LRT becomes x. [CLICK 2] Now turn the question around. We might ask whether later GCSE scores help us describe who had stronger or weaker reading scores at age eleven. That is a different statistical question. Now LRT is the thing being explained, so LRT becomes y and GCSE becomes x. The data did not change. The arrow did. [NEXT]

title: Who Predicts Whom? The Regression Setup
text:
Plot it, then summarize it









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Let us now fix one question, predicting GCSE from LRT, and look at real data from a single London school, which we will call Battersea. Each navy point represents one student, with the reading score shown along the bottom and the exam score shown up the side. Both scores are standardized, which means that a value of zero marks the national average. [CLICK 1] A regression now draws one rust line straight through that cloud of points. Notice that the line is not chasing any single point; instead, it gives the model’s predicted average exam score for each reading score. That idea, the average of y at a given x, is the whole engine behind regression, so let us write it down. [NEXT]

title: Who Predicts Whom? The Regression Setup
section: The line is a conditional mean
text:
The line is a conditional mean
The regression line models the predicted average of y at each x:

\[ \text{E}(y \mid x) = \alpha + \beta x \]


α intercept · β slope · roles fixed by your question


[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 2 · ~62s] Let us say what the rust line means in plain language. Pick a reading score along the horizontal axis. Individual students near that score still land above and below the line, but the line gives the exam score we would expect on average for that reading score. That is the conditional mean: the mean of y after we condition on a particular value of x. [CLICK 1] In symbols, the average of y given x equals alpha plus beta times x. [PAUSE] [CLICK 2] Alpha is the intercept, and beta is the slope. We will read those two numbers out loud in the next deck. The important detail is on the left side. It is y that gets averaged. The x value is the condition we hold fixed. So once you write the average of y given x, you have already chosen y as the outcome, and that choice came from your research question. [NEXT]

title: Who Predicts Whom? The Regression Setup
text:
What the line is NOT
Pitfall

Not “whichever column came first”
Not “whichever has more spread”









y is the thing your question predicts


Column order and variable spread never assign the roles. Nor is the line a cause — it is an association across students. And swap which variable is y, and you fit a different line.






[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~65s] Let us guard against two traps, because this is exactly where the setup is most often misread. The variable y is not simply whichever column came first, and it is not whichever variable happens to have the larger spread. Column order and spread are merely facts about how the data are recorded; the role of y is a choice that you make through your question. [CLICK 1] Two further cautions are worth stating. Even once the roles are fixed, this line is a comparison between students, an association rather than a cause. Higher reading scores go with higher exam scores in this sample, yet the line cannot promise that raising one variable would move the other. The relationship also does not reverse for free: if you swap which variable is y, you fit a different line, precisely because you have changed the question. [NEXT]

title: Who Predicts Whom? The Regression Setup
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The research question sets which variable is y — never the data’s column order or spread. The regression line then models the predicted average of y at each x, written E(y | x) = α + βx.




For predicted final exam score = 20 + 0.7 × (practice score), final exam score is y because that is what the question predicts.
Next: Slope and intercept, said right. Notes, Ch 1 §The Model · Practice Quiz 1

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~58s] Let us close with one decision and one equation worth carrying with you. Your research question sets which variable is y, never the order of the columns and never the spread of the values. The line then models the predicted average of y at a given x, written as alpha plus beta times x. Consider a fresh example: suppose the predicted final exam score equals twenty plus zero point seven times the practice score. Final exam score is y here, because that is the variable we chose to predict. The practice score is x, because that is what we predict from. In the next lecture, we will read the slope and the intercept out loud. The notes and Practice Quiz One are linked just below. [END]

title: Slope and Intercept, Said Right
text:
One school, one line










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch01-02 · 7 min · ~929 words · clicks 8 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~58s] This is data from a single London school, which we will call Battersea. Each navy point represents one student. The horizontal axis shows the LRT, a reading test the students took at age eleven, and the vertical axis shows their GCSE score at age sixteen. Both scores are standardized, which means that a value of zero marks the national average. [CLICK 1] A single rust line now summarizes the entire cloud of points. That line carries just two numbers, a slope and an intercept. Today we are not computing those numbers; our job is to say what they mean, state them in the units of the problem, place them in context, and treat them as a comparison across students. An intercept and a slope describe an association, never a cause. [NEXT]

title: Slope and Intercept, Said Right
text:
Sign points, size steepens









Figure 3














Figure 4















Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 3 · ~95s] Before we use Battersea’s fitted line, let us separate the two jobs done by the slope. In this sketch, all four lines begin at the same vertical-axis value, so the intercept is being held fixed. Start with the navy line, four plus zero point three x. Its slope is positive, so as x increases, the predicted value of y increases as well. [CLICK 1] The rust line is four plus zero point eight x. It has the same positive sign, so it rises in the same direction, but it rises more quickly because zero point eight is larger in magnitude than zero point three. This is the distinction to keep: the sign tells you whether the line rises or falls, and the magnitude tells you how sharply it moves. [CLICK 2] Now compare the downward line, four minus zero point six x. The negative sign reverses the direction, so larger x values go with smaller predicted y values. The size of the slope still describes steepness, but the sign tells you which way the change goes. [CLICK 3] The final line has slope zero. It stays flat at four, meaning that the fitted straight-line prediction does not change as x changes. In this model, that is a zero linear association, not proof that the variables can never be related in another way. The picture gives the whole reading rule: hold the intercept in mind as the anchor, then read the slope’s sign for direction and its size for steepness. [NEXT]

title: Slope and Intercept, Said Right
text:
Say it with the template
How to read this


For each additional [unit of x], predicted [y] differs by [slope] [units], on average.




For each additional 1 LRT point, predicted GCSE differs by 0.73 points, on average.



[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 2 · ~72s] Here is one sentence worth carrying with you, a template that fits every slope you will ever need to read. [CLICK 1] For each additional unit of x, the predicted value of y differs by the slope, measured in units of y, on average. Notice the verbs carefully. The phrase is differs by, not causes, which keeps the statement about association rather than cause. The phrase on average tells you this describes a comparison across many students, not a promise about any single one. That one sentence defuses two common traps at once. [CLICK 2] Now put Battersea’s slope into the template. For each additional one LRT point, the predicted GCSE score differs by zero point seven three points, on average. State it in the units of the problem itself, namely GCSE points read from the vertical axis, rather than in standard deviations. Even stated this way, it remains a comparison between students, an association and not a cause. [NEXT]

title: Slope and Intercept, Said Right
text:
The intercept is x = 0
Battersea’s intercept is −2.39: predicted GCSE when LRT = 0.








Pitfall: is x = 0 meaningful and in range?


LRT = 0 is the national average — but Battersea’s own mean LRT ≈ 2.7. The intercept describes a supported x value below this school’s intake, not the typical Battersea student. Read an intercept only when x = 0 is meaningful and within range.






[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~55s] Let us turn to the intercept, which here is negative two point three nine. This is simply the predicted GCSE score when LRT equals zero. Because these scores are standardized, zero is meaningful on the measurement scale: it marks the national reading average, and it lies inside this school’s observed LRT range. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] This is also where many people stumble. Zero is the national average, not this particular school’s average, since Battersea’s own mean LRT is about two point seven. As a result, the intercept describes a supported x value below this school’s typical intake, not the typical Battersea student. For many data sets, x equals zero falls outside the relevant range, where the intercept means very little. For that reason, you should read an intercept only when zero is meaningful on the scale and inside the range you are willing to use. [NEXT]

title: Slope and Intercept, Said Right
text:
When zero anchors the line









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Now let us use the intercept rule in a setting where zero has support. Suppose x equals zero is a real value on the horizontal scale, and the fitted line has an intercept of zero. That means the predicted value of y also starts at zero when x is zero. It does not mean the prediction simply copies x; the slope still tells us how the line moves away from that anchor. [CLICK 1] With a slope of zero point six, each additional unit of x is associated with a zero point six unit increase in predicted y, on average. At x equals five, the predicted value is three, because the line begins at zero and then adds zero point six for each of the five units. The key habit is to ask two questions before reading an intercept of zero: is zero meaningful on this scale, and do the data include or closely support values near zero? [NEXT]

title: Slope and Intercept, Said Right
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The slope compares — “for each additional unit of x, predicted y differs by the slope, on average.” Sign gives direction, size gives steepness. The intercept anchors — predicted y at x = 0; read it only when zero is meaningful and within range.




Next: Least squares — why this line and no other. Notes, Ch 1 §The Model · Practice Quiz 1

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~45s] Let us close with two sentences worth remembering. The slope compares: for each additional unit of x, the predicted value of y differs by the slope, on average, with the sign giving the direction and the size giving the steepness. The intercept anchors: it is the predicted value at x equals zero, and you should read it only when zero is meaningful and inside the range you are willing to use. State both of them in units every time. In the next lecture, we will see where this line actually comes from, through least squares. The notes and Practice Quiz One are linked just below. [END]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
Prove your line is best

Theory

You draw a line through the cloud. Why should anyone trust it over mine?


Least squares gives the one answer no different line can beat.


[DECK ch01-03 · 7 min · ~952 words · clicks 10 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~55s] Consider a familiar problem. You sketch a line through the cloud of points, and it looks reasonable, but the line I draw looks reasonable too, and the two do not agree. The natural question is which line we should trust. One tempting answer is that the best line connects the most points, yet a line through just two points can miss everything else. Another tempting answer is that the best line minimizes the perpendicular distance, but that describes a different method entirely. What we need is a single rule that selects one line and shows that it is best. [CLICK 1] That rule is least squares. It names exactly one line, and you will see why no different line can beat it. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
A miss is vertical









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 2 · ~65s] Let us look at some real students. This is data from Battersea, a single London school. The reading test at age eleven runs along the horizontal axis, the exam at age sixteen runs up the vertical axis, and each navy dot represents one student. [CLICK 1] Now draw any line through the cloud. For every student, that line hands you a prediction, namely the height of the line directly above that student’s reading score. [CLICK 2] The question is how badly the line misses. From each point, you measure straight to the line, moving up or down, never along a slanted or perpendicular path. That vertical gap is the residual, the actual score minus the predicted score. The gap is measured vertically because the vertical axis holds the quantity we are trying to predict, so a vertical gap is exactly the error in that prediction. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
Square them, then sum
The line’s total error is the sum of squared residuals:

\[ \text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\, y_i - (a + b x_i) \,\right]^2 \]


Square so misses cannot cancel · sum so every student counts


[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 2 · ~54s] We now have a residual for every student, and we would like to combine them into a single number. Take each residual, which is the actual y minus the prediction, and square it. [CLICK 1] Then add those squares over all n students. The result is the sum of squared errors, written S-S-E. A smaller value indicates a tighter fit. [CLICK 2] Notice why we square each residual before adding. Squaring makes every miss positive, so a point above the line cannot cancel a point below it; otherwise a wildly wrong line could fake a perfect score. Squaring also punishes large misses far more than small ones. As a result, every student counts, and the worst-fit students count the most. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
Your 1.40× versus OLS 1.00×









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] Let us compare two lines on the very same students. Start with the gold dashed line. I set this one by eye, and the long residuals show the cost of that choice. Its squared-error total is one point four zero times the least-squares total. In plain language, its S-S-E is about forty percent higher, mostly because the line is too flat. [CLICK 1] Now consider the least-squares line, drawn in rust. The points are identical, but the residuals visibly shrink, and the ratio falls to one point zero zero. That value is the floor. Every non-O-L-S line sits above it. A hand-set line can tie the floor only if it lands exactly on the least-squares solution. Least squares is the line that reaches it. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
Try to beat the floor

Fit-Your-Own-Regression-Line app on the real Battersea data — drag the intercept and slope sliders and watch the Your-over-OLS ratio. It bottoms out at 1.00× and never drops below.Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 0 · ~51s] This screenshot comes from the interactive app in the chapter, using these exact same real students rather than made-up data. In the live version, dragging the intercept and slope sliders changes three readouts: your own sum of squared errors, the least-squares sum of squared errors, and the ratio between them. The pattern to notice is that the your-over-O-L-S ratio can approach one point zero zero, but it does not go below it. That stubborn floor is least squares telling you there is nothing left to gain. The live version is linked in the notes, and it is well worth trying. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
One bowl, one bottom









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~45s] We should now ask why that floor can never be beaten. For this simple regression, each location on this plot is one possible line. Moving left or right changes the intercept. Moving up or down changes the slope. The color shows how large the squared-error total would be for that line. [CLICK 1] The surface forms a bowl, and the rust dot marks its one bottom. Move away from that point in any direction and S-S-E gets larger, with no tie and no flat valley. That one unique pair is why the least-squares answer is unique: your hand can land on that same point, but no different line can go below it. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
The formulas, shown not derived
The bowl’s one bottom has a closed form — no searching:

\[ b = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} \qquad a = \bar{y} - b\,\bar{x} \]


Rearranged, the line always passes through the means \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\).


[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 2 · ~49s] The good news is that you never have to search across that bowl by hand, because the lowest point has a closed-form solution. [CLICK 1] Read the slope as a ratio. The top, S-x-y, asks how x and y move together around their averages. The bottom, S-x-x, asks how much x moves on its own. So the slope is together-movement divided by x-movement. The intercept is y-bar minus the slope times x-bar. We show these formulas rather than derive them, since the calculus behind them lives in the notes. [CLICK 2] Reading the intercept formula gives a neat fact: in simple linear regression with an intercept, the fitted least-squares line passes through the point of means, x-bar and y-bar. [NEXT]

title: Least Squares: Why This Line and No Other
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Least squares picks the one line that minimizes SSE — the sum of squared vertical residuals, not the most points hit, not the perpendicular distance. The closed-form slope and intercept, \(b = S_{xy}/S_{xx}\) and \(a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}\), sit at the unique bottom of a bowl, so no different line can beat 1.00×.

You will never compute these by hand — software does. In the linear-regression chapters, this minimized SSE returns in R-squared, residual standard errors, and F-tests. Later GLMs use related, likelihood-based tools instead.





Next: Plug in, then sanity-check. Notes, Ch 1 §OLS Estimation · Practice Quiz 1

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Let us close with the idea to carry forward. Least squares picks the one line that makes the sum of squared vertical residuals as small as it can be, not the line that hits the most points, and not the perpendicular fit. [CLICK 1] You will never compute these formulas by hand, since software handles that for you. For this first unit, keep one boundary in mind. In ordinary linear regression, R squared, residual standard errors, and F tests all keep coming back to the minimized S-S-E. When we move to logistic and ordinal models later, the same modeling habits help, but the engine changes to likelihood-based tools. In the next lecture, we will put a fitted line to work. The notes and Practice Quiz One are linked just below. [END]

title: Plug In, Then Sanity-Check
text:
Your GPA will be 7.5

Theory

An admissions model reports ŷ = 0.5 + 7·x, with x a high-school GPA.

For a student with high-school GPA x = 1.0, it predicts a college GPA of 7.5.

[DECK ch01-04 · 6 min · ~674 words · clicks 6 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 0 · ~58s] An admissions office hands you this prediction equation. Y-hat, the predicted value, equals zero point five plus seven times x, where x is high-school GPA on the usual zero-to-four scale. Try a student with high-school GPA one point zero. The equation returns a predicted college GPA of seven point five. That is the problem: college GPA also stops at four point zero, so seven point five is impossible. The algebra worked, but the prediction failed the reality check. This lecture is about that habit: plug in, then sanity-check. [NEXT]

title: Plug In, Then Sanity-Check
text:
Substitute in order
How to read this

\[ \hat{y} = 0.5 + 7x \]



Put x = 1.0 in the slot: \(\hat{y} = 0.5 + 7 \times 1\)




Multiply, then add: \(\hat{y} = 0.5 + 7 = 7.5\)



[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 2 · ~82s] Start with the mechanics, because a prediction is substitution carried out in order. Here is the equation as it was given to us, y-hat equals zero point five plus seven times x. The intercept stands on its own, and the slope is the part attached to x. [CLICK 1] Now place one point zero into the x slot. We write zero point five plus seven times one, and we keep the substitution visible before simplifying. [CLICK 2] Then we multiply before we add. Seven times one is seven, and zero point five plus seven is seven point five. The mechanics have done their job: they produced a number. Now we ask whether that number is usable. [NEXT]

title: Plug In, Then Sanity-Check
text:
Does it clear the ceiling?
Pitfall
The arithmetic is correct. The prediction still is not.








A prediction must land in the outcome’s possible range


College GPA cannot exceed 4.0 — yet the equation returns 7.5. Correct substitution into a wrong model gives a confident, unusable number. Always test the prediction against what the outcome can actually be.






[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~54s] We now have seven point five, computed perfectly. Let us judge it. A college GPA lives on a fixed scale that runs from zero to four point zero, with nothing allowed above the top. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] Notice that seven point five is almost twice the maximum college GPA. This means that correct arithmetic inside the wrong model still hands you a confident, precise, and unusable number. That is the trap to defuse: right arithmetic does not amount to a trustworthy prediction. For that reason, make the range check a reflex. Before you report a y-hat, ask whether the outcome could ever actually take that value. If it cannot, then it is the equation that failed, not your algebra. [NEXT]

title: Plug In, Then Sanity-Check
section: The line forgets where data ended
text:
The line forgets where data ended









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~53s] The ceiling check catches predictions that are impossible. A second failure mode is quieter: the number may be possible, but the x value may sit outside the data that built the line. This navy line is drawn only across the high-school GPAs this study observed, from one to three. Inside that band, the prediction is at least supported by observed x-values; it still needs model-fit and uncertainty checks. [CLICK 1] Notice what happens when we extend that same line into the grey zone, past three, where no student in the data sat. The line simply keeps rising, because the equation has no idea that its data ran out back there. A prediction out in that zone is a guess dressed up as arithmetic. That is extrapolation: the equation does not know where your data ended. [NEXT]

title: Plug In, Then Sanity-Check
section: Which equation passes the sanity check?
text:
Which equation passes the sanity check?
How to read this

A: predicted college GPA = 0.5 + 0.8·x · B: predicted college GPA = 0.5 + 7·x


Direction — both rise (positive slope). Steepness — 0.8 is gentle; 7 is wild.


Realism — A stays inside 0 to 4 across the scale; B blows past 4.0. A passes this screen.


[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 2 · ~66s] Here are two candidate equations on the same zero-to-four GPA scale. Equation A is predicted college GPA equals zero point five plus zero point eight times x. Equation B is zero point five plus seven times x, the culprit from our hook. [CLICK 1] Direction alone cannot decide this. Both slopes are positive, so both predictions rise as high-school GPA rises. Steepness helps more. A one-point increase in high-school GPA adds zero point eight college-GPA points in Equation A, but seven college-GPA points in Equation B. [CLICK 2] Realism is a sanity screen, not proof that a model is good. Across the zero-to-four scale, A keeps its predictions inside what is possible, while B shoots past four point zero almost at once. So A passes this screen, while B fails it. Later, we would still check fit and uncertainty. [NEXT]

title: Plug In, Then Sanity-Check
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Computing ŷ is just substitution — put x in the slot, multiply, then add. Judging ŷ is the real work: is it inside the outcome’s possible range, and inside the range of x your data covered? Correct arithmetic on the wrong model, or past the data, is still an unreliable prediction.




Next: R² — slicing the variance pie. Notes, Ch 1 §The Model · Practice Quiz 1

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~55s] Take one habit with you: separate computing from judging. First, compute y-hat by substituting x, multiplying, and adding. Then judge it before you report it. Ask two questions. Could y ever be this value? And is this x inside the data range the model learned from? If either answer is no, the algebra may be right, but the prediction is not ready to use. We plug in, and then we sanity-check. Next, R-squared asks how much spread the line explains. The notes and Practice Quiz One are linked just below. [END]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
section: Explained two percent — fire the model?
text:
Explained two percent — fire the model?

Theory

A one-predictor fitted model accounts for 2% of the variation in an outcome.

Fire the model? Hold on — 2% of what, and good for what?

[DECK ch01-05 · 6 min · ~799 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~35s] Suppose your software reports that a one-predictor fitted model accounts for two percent of the variation in the outcome. Two percent is a small share, and a first reaction is to give up on the model and remove the predictor. Let us slow down, because two questions are hidden inside that one number. The first question asks two percent of what, and the second asks good for what. Once you answer both of them, R-squared stops being a grade and becomes something you can read. [NEXT]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
text:
All the spread there is









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Let us start with the first question, two percent of what? Set the predictor aside for a moment and look at the outcome on its own. These are GCSE exam scores for our Battersea students, and the dashed rust line marks their average. The scores do not all sit on that average; instead, they spread above and below it. [CLICK 1] Each grey segment shows how far one student falls from the mean. When you square those gaps and add them up, you get the total spread in GCSE, which is the whole pie. The notes call this the total sum of squares, written T-S-S. This is everything there is to explain, before the predictor has said anything at all. [NEXT]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
text:
Split it: explained, leftover









Figure 3














Figure 4















Figure 5








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~67s] We are still looking at the same total spread around the mean. Now bring reading score back in. The question is: how much of that spread can be described by the fitted line, and how much is still left over? [CLICK 1] The slate line is our regression, with reading at age eleven predicting the exam at age sixteen. The slate segments run from the mean line up or down to the slanted line, and they show the part of the spread described by the fitted linear model, that is, how far each prediction moves away from simply guessing the mean. When you square and add these, you get the explained part, written M-S-S. [CLICK 2] The line does not pass through every point, however. The rust segments run from each point to the line, and they are the leftover misses, the residuals you met when we studied least squares. When you square and add these, you get the unexplained part, written S-S-E. In this way the spread splits cleanly into two pieces, one explained and one left over. [NEXT]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
text:
The pie, sliced









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Here is the same total spread collapsed into one bar. For the moment, read the bar as one hundred percent: all the variability in GCSE scores. [CLICK 1] Now the bar is sliced. For this least-squares line with an intercept, the total spread splits exactly: T-S-S equals M-S-S plus S-S-E. These are Battersea’s real shares: the fitted linear model using reading score accounts for thirty-eight point one percent of the variation in GCSE scores, and sixty-one point nine percent remains residual variation. That explained slice is R-squared: zero point three eight one. It is not a grade; it is a share of the pie. [NEXT]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
text:
R-squared is that slice
For this in-sample least-squares model with an intercept:
\[ R^2 = \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{TSS}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{TSS}} \]

For this in-sample least-squares model with an intercept: a partition of variability, between 0 and 1 — not a quality grade. In simple regression, \(R^2 = r^2\).









Name the trap


R-squared is not how accurate each prediction is — it’s the share of total variability the model explains, a property of the spread, not of any one student.






[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~66s] Let us write it down. R-squared is the explained part, M-S-S, divided by the total, T-S-S. This is the same as one minus the leftover share. Either way, it is a proportion between zero and one. [CLICK 1] For this in-sample least-squares model with an intercept, R-squared is a partition of variability, never a quality stamp. In simple regression only, R-squared also equals the squared correlation. Our points correlate at about zero point six two, and when you square that value, you get R-squared. For now, read this as a quick bridge between correlation and explained variation. [CLICK 2] Now consider the trap that catches almost everyone. R-squared is not a measure of how accurate one prediction is. A model can report a healthy R-squared and still miss a single student badly. R-squared describes the spread, the share for the whole group, and never any one person. [NEXT]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
text:
Is 0.30 good? It depends
Pitfall







R-squared has no universal good/bad cutoff


Predicting individual behavior from one measure? 0.30 can be strong — one measure often leaves plenty unexplained. Judging a measurement instrument? You may want 0.80+. Testing a theoretical claim? Even 0.05 can matter if the relationship is important.





Caveat for later: compare R-squared only for models fit to the same response on the same transformed scale.


[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~68s] Let us return to that two-percent worry. Before using a cutoff, name the job. For individual prediction, thirty percent can be strong because one measure often leaves plenty of person-to-person variation unexplained. For a measurement instrument, you may want eighty percent or higher. For a theory test, even five percent can matter if the relationship is important. The question is not, is this number big? The question is, big enough for this use? [CLICK 1] Here is one caveat to keep in mind for later. Compare R-squared only for models fit to the same response on the same transformed scale. Do not compare raw-y R-squared with log-y R-squared; that changes the pie itself. [NEXT]

title: R-squared: Slicing the Variance Pie
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


R-squared is the explained share of variability — a partition, not a grade. The total spread splits exactly for a least-squares model with an intercept: TSS = MSS + SSE. R² = MSS/TSS: the share of variability explained by the fitted linear model. Whether that share is “good” depends on your purpose — predicting, measuring, or testing a claim.




Next: From description to inference — is the slope real? Notes, Ch 1 §Coefficient of Determination · Practice Quiz 1

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~28s] Take these two questions with you. First, what is the whole spread we are slicing? R-squared answers that exactly for this fitted least-squares model with an intercept: it is the explained part over the whole. Second, what purpose are we using the model for? Your context answers that. R-squared is a partition, not a grade. The notes and Practice Quiz One are linked just below. [END]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
Rerun the study — same 0.73?

Theory

Our Battersea slope estimate came out 0.73. Rerun the whole study on a fresh sample of students — would you get 0.73 again?


You wouldn’t — and that’s the point. The fitted slope estimate is a random quantity.


[DECK ch01-06 · 9 min · ~1128 words · clicks 8 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~62s] Let us return to Battersea, the single London school we studied earlier. We fit our line to those students, and the slope estimate came out to zero point seven three. Now consider a question that changes how you should think about that number. Suppose you reran the entire study at the same school, drawing a fresh sample of students, and fit the line again. Would the fitted slope estimate come out to zero point seven three a second time? [CLICK 1] You would not get exactly that value again. With a different sample of students, the fitted slope estimate would land a little differently every time. That observation is the whole idea of today’s lecture: the estimate is a random quantity, even though the target slope in the model is fixed. Because the estimate changes from sample to sample, it has a spread, which we describe with a sampling distribution. Once we picture that distribution, the standard error and the t-test follow directly from it. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
A cloud of slope estimates









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Let us watch this variation happen. The horizontal axis shows possible fitted slope estimates, one value for each sample we might have drawn. The single rust line marks the estimate we actually obtained, which is zero point seven three. [CLICK 1] Now I take our Battersea students, draw a resample from them, refit the line, and record the new fitted slope estimate, and I repeat that process five thousand times. This bootstrap cloud estimates how b would vary across repeated samples: it piles up most thickly around zero point seven three and spreads from roughly zero point four to one. The lesson is not that the target slope is drifting. The fitted slope estimate is one realization from the sampling process, and a different sample would have handed us a different estimate. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
section: SE is the width of that cloud
text:
SE is the width of that cloud









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~73s] Naturally, we want to measure how wide that cloud is, and we summarize its spread with one number, the standard error of the coefficient. The curve shown here is a plug-in approximation to the sampling distribution: we center it at our observed estimate so we can focus on its width. [CLICK 1] The standard error is the width of this bell. If you step out by one standard error on each side of the center, you reach zero point six four on the left and zero point eight three on the right, which marks the typical distance a fitted slope estimate wanders from the center. Two traps are worth naming. The standard error is not the spread of the students’ scores; it is the spread of fitted slope estimates. And the standard error is one number, not an interval. The confidence interval is the separate range we will build from it shortly. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
Three levers shrink it
How to read this

A more precise slope — a smaller SE of the coefficient — comes from three places:



Smaller residual scatter — residual SD is one ingredient in the slope SE.
Larger sample — more students.




More spread in x — a wider range of reading scores.



[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 2 · ~55s] It is worth asking what would make that standard error smaller, since a smaller standard error means a sharper, more precise estimate of the slope. Three levers control it. [CLICK 1] The first lever is smaller residual scatter: the residual standard deviation is one ingredient in the slope SE, so tighter points make the fitted slope estimate wobble less. The second lever is a larger sample, because adding more students gives the line more evidence to rest on. [CLICK 2] The third lever is more spread in the values of x, meaning a wider range of reading scores. When the design permits it, and when the goal is slope precision, including an appropriate wider range of x gives the line more information about how the response changes across the predictor. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
t = estimate over SE









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~72s] We are now ready to test the linear slope. The question is whether the true linear slope could be zero, meaning no linear relationship in this straight-line model. The curve drawn here shows where a t statistic would tend to sit under that zero-slope model. It is a t-distribution with eighty-nine degrees of freedom, that is, n minus two, or ninety-one minus two for this line. [CLICK 1] Now we build the signal-to-noise ratio. The signal is the fitted slope estimate, zero point seven three. The noise is its standard error, zero point zero nine nine. Using the unrounded values that the L M function keeps internally, estimate divided by standard error gives the observed t statistic, seven point four. When we place seven point four on the null curve, it lands far out to the right, well past the shaded rejection tail. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
Evidence against zero linear slope?







Rule of thumb


In a decent sample, the slope test is significant at the 5% level when |t| > 2. Our t = 7.4 clears that easily — strong evidence against a zero linear slope, with p < .001.





(In simple regression the F-test asks the same thing — there, F = t². The full F story comes in Chapter 3.)


[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~57s] So do we have evidence that the linear slope differs from zero? A useful rule of thumb is that, in a reasonably sized sample, a t statistic larger than two in magnitude is significant at the five percent level. Our t statistic is seven point four, which clears two by a wide margin. As a result, we have strong evidence against a zero linear slope, and the p-value is correspondingly tiny, far below zero point zero zero one. [CLICK 1] You will also encounter an F-test that asks the same zero-linear-slope question in a different form; in simple regression, the F statistic is simply t-squared, so the F-test and the t-test reach the same conclusion here. The full story of the F-test waits for Chapter Three. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
The CI adds magnitude









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~73s] There is one final piece, and it is the one many people skip. The test we just ran gives evidence against a zero linear slope, but it does not tell us the size of the slope very clearly. To recover the magnitude and precision, we read the confidence interval. [CLICK 1] For Battersea, the confint function returns an interval from zero point five four to zero point nine three. This interval supports two readings at once. First, the dashed line at zero sits well outside the interval, so a zero linear slope is not plausible, which agrees with the test we already ran. We also learn the magnitude: the slope plausibly runs from about a half to roughly nine-tenths of a GCSE point per LRT point. In short, the test gives evidence against the zero-slope model, and the interval tells you how large the linear slope could plausibly be. [NEXT]

title: The Slope Estimate Is a Random Quantity
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway



The fitted slope estimate varies across samples.
Its SE of the coefficient measures the spread of those estimates.
t = estimate / SE with df = n − 2 tests a zero linear slope.
The CI adds magnitude and precision.





Next: One dummy, two means — a coefficient becomes a group difference. Notes, Ch 1 §Statistical Inference · Practice Quiz 2

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~49s] Let us gather the main ideas before we close. First, the fitted slope estimate varies across samples. Second, its standard error of the coefficient measures the spread of those estimates; it is one number, not an interval, and not the spread of the students’ scores. Third, the t statistic compares the estimate to its standard error, using n minus two degrees of freedom to test a zero linear slope. Finally, the confidence interval adds the magnitude and precision that the test alone leaves out. Quiz Two applies this same chain of reasoning in a new setting, and it is linked below. [END]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
section: Can a line compare two groups?
text:
Can a line compare two groups?

Theory

You already know how to read a slope when the predictor is a number.

But what if the predictor is just a label — two schools, Battersea and Chelsea?

Can a regression line really compare two groups? It can — and the answer is hiding in the coefficients.


[DECK ch01-07 · 6 min · ~797 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~42s] You already know how to read a slope when the predictor is a number, such as a reading score that climbs point by point. Today the predictor is different, because it is a label that simply records which school a student attends, either Battersea or Chelsea. [CLICK 1] This raises a natural question: can a straight line really compare two whole groups? A line may seem like the wrong tool for that job, yet it works, and the comparison turns out to sit directly in the coefficients. Let us build it step by step. [NEXT]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
text:
Code it zero or one
A dummy variable codes a label as a single 0/1 column:

Battersea → 0 (the reference group) · Chelsea → 1


Then lm(gcse ~ chelsea) fits \[\text{average GCSE} = \alpha + \beta \times \texttt{chelsea}\] — two predictor values in, so only two predictions out.


[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~54s] We begin with the simple trick that lets a label enter a regression. We build a dummy variable, and the name is less mysterious than it sounds. It is just a switch that equals one when the student is in Chelsea and zero when the student is in Battersea. Battersea takes the value zero because we chose it as the reference group, the baseline that every other group is compared against. [CLICK 1] Now we feed that column into the L M function. The average GCSE score equals alpha plus beta times the dummy. Notice that the predictor takes only two values. Two values go in, so only two predictions come out, which means the model reduces to fitting two means. [NEXT]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
section: Two clouds, two means, one line
text:
Two clouds, two means, one line









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~66s] Let us look at the picture. Along the bottom is the dummy, with Battersea at zero and Chelsea at one. Up the side are the GCSE scores, and each faint dot is one student. Notice that the two clouds overlap a great deal, so many Battersea students score higher than many Chelsea students. [CLICK 1] The line cares about only one thing from each group, namely its mean. We therefore place a diamond at each mean: the Battersea diamond sits lower, and the Chelsea diamond sits higher. [CLICK 2] Now we connect the two diamonds with a straight segment, and that segment is the regression line. With only two possible values of x, the line has nowhere else to go, so it passes through exactly the two group means and produces just two fitted values. The next question is where the coefficients live in this picture. [NEXT]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
section: Read the table off the picture
text:
Read the table off the picture









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6















Figure 7








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 3 · ~69s] This single picture holds the whole lecture, but let us be precise about what is in the table. The two coefficient rows, plus the comparison mean we compute from them, all have locations in this picture. [CLICK 1] Consider the intercept, which is negative zero point four one. Let us name the common trap directly: this is not the grand mean of everyone. It is the reference group’s own mean, marked by the Battersea diamond, the predicted value when the dummy is zero. [CLICK 2] Next consider the slope, which is plus nine point eight five. This is not the Chelsea mean either. It is the gap shown by the arrow, equal to Chelsea minus Battersea. In other words, it is a difference in means, measured in GCSE points rather than percent. [CLICK 3] Finally, take the intercept plus the dummy coefficient. That sum lands on the right diamond, the Chelsea mean of nine point four five. The idea worth remembering is scoped to this kind of model: with dummy variables, the intercept is the reference mean, and each non-reference dummy coefficient is a difference from that reference. [NEXT]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
text:
Recover Chelsea: just add









Figure 8














Figure 9








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Here is a move you will use often, so let us rehearse it. The table gives you the reference mean for free, because that is simply the intercept, the Battersea value of negative zero point four one. The table does not hand you the other group’s mean directly, so you build it yourself. [CLICK 1] You build it by adding the coefficients. Using the full-precision values behind the table, the intercept plus the dummy coefficient rounds to nine point four five, and that value lands exactly on the Chelsea diamond. The displayed decimals are rounded, so read the printed sum as approximate. The reference mean plus the difference gives the comparison mean. For a zero-one dummy, the non-reference group mean equals the intercept plus its dummy coefficient. [NEXT]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
text:
A gap, not a cause







Name the trap


The 9.85-point gap is what we observed between two schools — an association, never a “school effect.” School assignment was not randomized; intake, neighborhood, resources, and other differences move with the label.





[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 0 · ~53s] Before we close, name the boundary. The gap of nine point eight five is an observed association, not a school effect. Students were not assigned to schools. Intake, neighborhood, resources, and other differences move together with the school label. The dummy compares the two groups, but it does not explain why they differ. [NEXT]

title: One Dummy, Two Means
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


With a 0/1 dummy, the line connects two group means: the intercept is the reference group’s own mean (Battersea), the dummy coefficient is the difference in means (Chelsea − Battersea, in points), and intercept + dummy coefficient is the comparison group’s mean (Chelsea). Only two fitted values; for dummy variables, coefficients are differences from the reference.




Next: Why this dummy regression matches the pooled two-sample t-test under the usual equal-variance setup. Notes, Ch 1 §Dummy Variables · Practice Quiz 2

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Let us carry the main idea forward in three pieces. A zero-one dummy makes the line connect two means. The intercept is the reference group’s own mean rather than the grand average. The dummy coefficient is the non-reference group’s difference from that reference, measured in points, and you add intercept plus dummy coefficient to recover the comparison mean. There are only two fitted values. Quiz Two reads a coefficient table much like this one, built on a PISA model, and it is linked below. [END]

title: The t-Test Was Regression All Along
section: Your first regression was a t-test
text:
Your first regression was a t-test

Theory

Back in intro stats, you compared two group means with a t-test.


You’d already run your first regression — you just didn’t know it.


[DECK ch01-08 · 6 min · ~745 words · clicks 7 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~42s] Let us begin with something you already know without realizing it. Back in introductory statistics, when you wanted to compare two group means, you reached for the two-sample t-test. At the time it felt like its own separate tool, with no connection to regression. [CLICK 1] Here is the surprising part. The pooled two-sample t-test was a regression all along. You ran your very first regression years ago, and no one pointed it out. Today you will see that regression contains the pooled two-sample t-test as one special case inside a much larger framework. [NEXT]

title: The t-Test Was Regression All Along
text:
Same question, two notations
The two tests ask the same question about the two schools:









The hypothesis it tests




pooled two-sample t-test
\(H_0:\ \mu_{\text{Battersea}} = \mu_{\text{Chelsea}}\)


dummy regression
\(H_0:\ \beta = 0\)





“Means equal” ⟺ “dummy coefficient is zero.” Same null. Same data. Same assumptions.


[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 2 · ~74s] Many students assume that these are two different tests, but they are not. [CLICK 1] Consider the pooled two-sample t-test first. I will say pooled every time, because the equal-variance version is the one that matches regression, not the unequal-variance version. The pooled t-test asks a single question: are the two school means equal? The dummy regression seems to ask something else: is the dummy coefficient, which we call beta, equal to zero? Notice that this zero is a hypothesized value we are testing, not a number measured from the data. [CLICK 2] Now read the two questions together. Saying the means are equal is the same as saying the gap between them is zero. Recall that the slope from the previous lecture, plus nine point eight five, is exactly that gap between the two means. So both tests use the same null, the same two schools, and the same assumptions, which means you would expect their answers to agree. Let us confirm that they do. [NEXT]

title: The t-Test Was Regression All Along
text:
Run both — the numbers match









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 2 · ~69s] Here are both procedures run side by side. On the left is the pooled two-sample t-test. On the right is the dummy regression, the L M function of GCSE on the chelsea dummy. When students read the two t-values, they notice one is negative and one is positive, and they conclude that the methods disagree. [CLICK 1] They do not disagree. In size, the t-statistic is the same on both sides, seven point one one either way. The degrees of freedom also match, one hundred sixty-nine on each, which is simply n minus two. [CLICK 2] The p-value tells the same story: both procedures produce the same p-value, shown here as below zero point zero zero one. The absolute t is the same, the degrees of freedom are the same, and the p-value is the same. This is not a near miss. It is the identical test, printed twice. [NEXT]

title: The t-Test Was Regression All Along
text:
Only the sign can differ









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~66s] There is one honest detail to address, and it is a second place students stumble. The sign of t can indeed come out differently, and some readers take that as the tests disagreeing. It is not a disagreement. It is bookkeeping, namely which group you subtract first. The axis is ready; now watch where the two signed values land. [CLICK 1] The pooled t-test computes Battersea minus Chelsea, so its marker lands at minus seven point one one. The dummy regression codes Chelsea as the one, so its marker lands at plus seven point one one. That sign is the only thing that moves. The absolute t is the same, the degrees of freedom are the same, and the p-value is the same; only the sign can differ. If you switch the reference group, you switch a plus to a minus, and the evidence itself is untouched. Do not let a sign mislead you. [NEXT]

title: The t-Test Was Regression All Along
text:
One trick, three links









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~53s] This result is more than a curiosity; it is the first link in a chain. Link one is now lit: under the pooled equal-variance setup, the two-sample t-test matches the dummy regression. That result is yours to keep. [CLICK 1] Here is one more matching fingerprint. Using the unrounded t-statistic, t-squared equals the regression F-statistic, which is fifty point five four. We will study F in more detail later. The chain continues from there. With three or more groups, the same regression framework becomes one-way ANOVA F in Session Five, and with two factors it becomes two-way ANOVA in Session Eight. Those grey links are promises for the lectures ahead. [NEXT]

title: The t-Test Was Regression All Along
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


For this pooled two-sample t-test and 0/1 dummy regression: same null, same data, identical |t|, df, and p; only the sign can differ with the direction of subtraction. Using the unrounded statistic, t² = F. More broadly, the two-group comparison, ANOVA, and interaction models we use in this course fit inside the regression framework we will build.




Next: Root MSE — the model’s typical miss. Notes, Ch 1 §Dummy Variables · Practice Quiz 2

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~45s] Let us gather the key points to carry forward. In this pooled two-sample case, the t-test is a regression on a zero-one dummy variable, with the same null, the same absolute t, the same degrees of freedom, and the same p-value; only the sign can differ. Using the unrounded statistic, t-squared equals F. You did not learn a brand-new tool today; you discovered that a familiar one was regression all along. Practice Quiz Two applies the same trick to fresh data, and it is linked below. [END]

title: In a 0/1 Dummy Model, Root MSE Is Pooled Within-Group SD
text:
A near-miss pair

Theory









Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch01-09 · 5 min · ~654 words · clicks 4 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us begin with a short puzzle. We will work with the same two-school model you have been building, comparing Battersea with Chelsea, and we will compare two numbers that look almost identical: ten point two eight, and nine point zero four. One of these is the regression’s own standard deviation, its Root MSE, and the other is the ordinary standard deviation of all the scores. The natural temptation is to reach for that familiar all-scores standard deviation, ten point two eight. [CLICK 1] That choice is the wrong one. The model’s standard deviation is nine point zero four, the smaller of the two numbers. Explaining why is the work of this entire lecture. [NEXT]

title: In a 0/1 Dummy Model, Root MSE Is Pooled Within-Group SD
section: A residual goes to YOUR group’s mean
text:
A residual goes to YOUR group’s mean









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us see where nine point zero four comes from. The key fact about a zero-one dummy variable is that the fitted value for every student is simply that student’s own group mean. As a result, a residual, which is the gap from a point to the regression line, is really the gap from each student to the mean of their own school. [CLICK 1] Notice the segments that now appear. Each one runs from a student to their own group’s mean diamond, drawn in navy inside Battersea and in rust inside Chelsea, and never reaching across to the other school. This is the scatter that remains after the model already knows which school a student attends, and Root MSE is built entirely from these short within-group gaps. [NEXT]

title: In a 0/1 Dummy Model, Root MSE Is Pooled Within-Group SD
text:
Average them, take the root









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~62s] Let us now compress those within-group gaps into one number. First, square each within-group residual. Second, add those squared residuals; that total is SSE, the sum of squared errors. Third, divide by n minus two. We use n minus two because this two-group dummy model estimated two group means, leaving one hundred sixty-nine residual degrees of freedom. That average squared residual is the MSE, and here it equals eighty-one point seven six. [CLICK 1] Now take the square root. Root MSE is nine point zero four, back in GCSE-score units. It is a standard deviation: the typical size of a residual around each group’s own mean. [NEXT]

title: In a 0/1 Dummy Model, Root MSE Is Pooled Within-Group SD
section: Why the other one is bigger
text:
Why the other one is bigger









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~56s] Let us now see why the other number, ten point two eight, comes out larger. That quantity ignores the schools entirely and measures every score against a single grand mean of four point two zero, shown here as the dashed line. [CLICK 1] Watch the segments swing across to that one line. The Battersea segments barely change, because Battersea sits close to the grand mean. The Chelsea segments, however, stretch far downward, since Chelsea’s mean sits about nine point eight five points above Battersea’s. In this example, the grey reach is plainly longer, because each gap now spans the scatter within a school plus the jump between the two group means. The combined standard deviation absorbs that extra gap, and that is why it lands larger here. [NEXT]

title: In a 0/1 Dummy Model, Root MSE Is Pooled Within-Group SD
text:
Find it in the output


Figure 9

[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 0 · ~48s] You never need to compute this quantity by hand, because the software prints it for you. The output calls this value the residual standard error: nine point zero four on one hundred sixty-nine degrees of freedom. Here, nine point zero four is sigma-hat. In this zero-one dummy model, sigma-hat is the Root MSE: the typical within-school residual standard deviation. One common trap is to call it the standard deviation of all scores with the groups ignored. That description points to the larger number, ten point two eight, not to this one. [NEXT]

title: In a 0/1 Dummy Model, Root MSE Is Pooled Within-Group SD
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


In this zero-one dummy model, Root MSE = sigma-hat = the square root of SSE/(n − 2) — the pooled within-group SD, the typical residual around each group’s own mean. It is not the SD of all the scores ignoring the groups. In this example, that group-blind combined SD is bigger, because it also absorbs the gap between the groups.




Next: Two intervals, two questions — CI versus PI. Notes, Ch 1 §Estimating the Residual Variance · Practice Quiz 2

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Let us close with the idea worth carrying with you. In this zero-one dummy model, Root MSE, written as sigma-hat and equal to the square root of the sum of squared errors over n minus two, is the pooled within-group standard deviation: the typical residual around each group’s own mean. It is not the larger standard deviation that ignores the groups. The two are near-twins, yet only the smaller of them is the model’s own. You will rely on this quantity again in later course work. The notes and Practice Quiz Two are linked just below. [END]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
section: Predict the average, or predict Maria?
text:
Predict the average, or predict Maria?

Theory

A reading score comes in. You can predict the average student at that score — or predict Maria, one real new student.


Same x in. Two completely different questions — and two different intervals.


[DECK ch01-10 · 7 min · ~860 words · clicks 8 · advances 9] [SLIDE 1/9 · clicks 1 · ~53s] This question is secretly two questions, and it is worth separating them carefully. A reading score arrives, and you are asked to predict the GCSE score that goes with it. The difficulty hides in the word predict. You might mean the average student who sits at that reading score, or you might mean Maria, one real new student who walks in tomorrow. These are not the same target. [CLICK 1] The same number goes in, yet we are answering two different questions, and each question has its own interval. If you confuse the two, you have made one of the most common mistakes in all of regression, so the goal today is to keep them clearly apart. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
text:
Two questions, two intervals
How to read this

CI — confidence interval for the mean response E(y | x): where is the line at this x?



PI — prediction interval for an individual y: where will one new student actually land?



[SLIDE 2/9 · clicks 1 · ~46s] Let us name the two intervals carefully. The confidence interval brackets the mean response, which is the average value of y at a given x. It answers the question of where the line sits, and that is fundamentally a question about alpha and beta, the two numbers we estimated from the data. [CLICK 1] The prediction interval brackets one individual instead. It is not about her group’s average but about Maria herself. The intuition is that an average is calm and steady, while any single person is noisy. Let us watch what that difference does to the width of each interval. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
section: The CI: where is the mean?
text:
The CI: where is the mean?









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 3/9 · clicks 1 · ~47s] Let us return to Battersea, where the rust line is our fitted model. The slope is zero point seven three, but today our attention is on the uncertainty rather than on the slope itself. [CLICK 1] Notice the narrow band that now snaps tightly around the line. This is the confidence interval for the mean. Our fitted slope is an estimate. If we drew a new sample, the estimated intercept and slope would move a little, so the fitted line would move too. This narrow band shows sample-to-sample uncertainty in the mean line. It stays tight because averages are comparatively easy to pin down. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
section: The PI: where will Maria land?
text:
The PI: where will Maria land?









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 4/9 · clicks 1 · ~45s] Here is that same narrow confidence band again, telling us where the line sits. Let us now turn to the other question and ask where Maria herself will land. [CLICK 1] Notice that a second, much wider band now floods out around the first. This is the prediction interval for one individual student at this x. It is the honest answer to Maria’s question. Knowing where the average sits tells us much less about where one particular student lands, because real students scatter widely on both sides of the line. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
section: Why the PI is always wider
text:
Why the PI is always wider
The PI carries two sources of uncertainty; the CI carries only the first:


CI = uncertainty in the line (estimating α, β). PI = that + σ², the individual scatter around the line.



[SLIDE 5/9 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Let us see why the prediction interval is always the wider of the two, and never the other way around. [CLICK 1] The confidence interval carries uncertainty in the fitted line. The prediction interval starts with that same uncertainty and then adds individual scatter, sigma-squared, on top of it. Even if we knew the true line perfectly, Maria would not sit exactly on it. More students can pin down the fitted line, but they do not remove individual scatter. That is why the prediction band stays wider than the confidence band. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
section: Narrow at the mean, flaring out
text:
Narrow at the mean, flaring out









Figure 5














Figure 6















Figure 7








[SLIDE 6/9 · clicks 2 · ~56s] Notice now the shape of these bands. They are not a pair of parallel rails, because they pinch inward in the middle. [CLICK 1] Right at the mean of x, which is Battersea’s reading average of two point seven, both bands are at their narrowest, because this is where we know the most. At this point the prediction band is nine point six times as wide as the confidence band. [CLICK 2] As we push x toward the edge of the data, both bands flare open. We know the least where the data thin out, which is the same flaring we met in the lesson on extrapolation. The ratio falls to three point nine here, yet the prediction band still never reaches the confidence band. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
text:
Go drag it yourself

The CI vs PI Band Explorer on the real Battersea GCSE data — not simulated. Drag the reference point x0 from the mean toward an edge and watch both bands pinch and flare; the gold dashed marker is x0, and the PI / CI pill stays well above one.Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 7/9 · clicks 0 · ~47s] This is the explorer from the chapter, built on the real Battersea students rather than on simulated data. In the live version, drag the gold reference point, x-zero, from the mean toward an edge. The bands pinch and flare just as we saw, and the prediction-to-confidence ratio stays above one. That stubborn gap is the individual scatter refusing to disappear. The live version is linked in the notes. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
text:
The trap, named out loud
Pitfall

❌ “A 95% confidence interval contains 95% of the students.”



✅ The CI brackets the mean — where the line is. For a 95% interval for one new student’s outcome at this x, use the PI. Different question, wider band.



[SLIDE 8/9 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Let us name the trap out loud, so that you will catch yourself making it. People see a ninety-five percent confidence interval and conclude that it holds ninety-five percent of the students. It does not. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] The confidence interval is about the mean, that is, about where the line sits. The prediction interval gives a model-based ninety-five percent interval for one new student’s outcome at this x; it is not a claim that this band contains ninety-five percent of all students. So ask what the interval is for: the mean response, or one new individual response. If it is the mean response, you want the confidence interval; if it is one new individual at this x, you want the prediction interval. [NEXT]

title: Two Intervals, Two Questions: CI vs PI
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Two intervals, two questions. CI: where is the mean response at x? PI: where will one new student land? The PI is wider because it adds σ², the individual scatter, to fitted-line uncertainty. A 95% CI does not hold 95% of the people.




Next: The improvement that isn’t — regression to the mean. Notes, Ch 1 §Prediction Intervals

[SLIDE 9/9 · clicks 0 · ~38s] Let us close with the central idea in three beats. CI: where is the mean response at this x? PI: where will one new student land? Trap reminder: a ninety-five percent CI is not a container for ninety-five percent of people. Keep those questions separate, and the two bands make sense. The prediction-interval notes are linked just below. [END]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
text:
The low-scoring group improved










Figure 1







[DECK ch01-11 · 6 min · ~722 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Let us begin with a result that looks like a clear success. A school identifies its lowest-scoring students for possible tutoring, and here we see their before-and-after picture, with the same students measured twice: the pretest is on the left and the posttest is on the right. Most rust lines tilt upward, and the group mean climbs from about thirty-five point two to about forty-one point nine, a rise of nearly seven points. It is tempting to treat that rise as the kind of evidence a program would want. [PAUSE] But in this simulation, no tutoring has happened, and the group still rose. Let us see why. [NEXT]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
text:
We picked the extremes









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~53s] The key to this puzzle lies in how the students were chosen. This is the whole sample, with the pretest along the horizontal axis and the posttest along the vertical axis, where each dot is one student. The points form a loose, noisy cloud. [CLICK 1] Now we watch the school identify students for possible support. We take the bottom twenty percent on the pretest, which are all the rust dots to the left of the dashed line. That is a reasonable rule. Notice, though, that we picked these students using a single noisy test, and some of them landed that low partly because of bad luck. Keep that point in mind. [NEXT]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
text:
Retest them — they rise









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~59s] Let us follow that selected group on its own and view their scores as a distribution. Here is the pretest, shown in rust, with a mean of about thirty-five point two, which sits well below the population mean of fifty marked by the dotted line. [CLICK 1] Now we retest the same students and place their posttest distribution on top, in teal. Notice that the whole curve slides to the right, toward fifty, with its mean now at about forty-one point nine. [CLICK 2] That shift is the rise, about six point seven points. Here is the part that should give you pause: in this simulation, no student was tutored. I built the posttest to have the same expected average as the pretest, and yet the group still climbed toward the middle. [NEXT]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
text:
Why? The math of z-scores
The standardized prediction relation is exact:

\[ \hat{z}_y = r\,z_x \]
For noisy, imperfect remeasurement, \(|r| < 1\).










Figure 7














Figure 8















Figure 9








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 3 · ~65s] Let us switch from raw scores to z-scores, where zero means average. In this language, regression to the mean has one moving part: how much of the first z-score carries over to the prediction. [CLICK 1] The rule is z-hat-y equals r times z-x. With noisy remeasurement, the correlation is imperfect, so the absolute value of r is below one. That multiplier shrinks extreme z-scores toward zero. [CLICK 2] The dashed forty-five-degree line is the no-shrink fantasy: r equals one, so two standard deviations out would stay two standard deviations out. The rust line is shallower because r is less than one. [CLICK 3] Now follow one extreme case. Plus two on the first test predicts only about plus one point four on the second. Since zero is the mean, that inward pull is regression to the mean. [NEXT]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
text:
See it move yourself

Regression-to-the-Mean explorer screenshot at the default correlation. A student 15 points above the mean on the first test is predicted only about 8.3 above on the second.Simulated data. Try the live version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 0 · ~43s] This screenshot shows the explorer from the chapter at its default setting. The correlation is zero point five five, and the arrow shows the key idea: a student fifteen points above the mean on the first test is predicted only about eight point three above on the second. In the live version linked in the notes, moving the correlation toward one makes this pull shrink; moving it toward zero makes extremes collapse back toward the mean. [NEXT]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
section: So you need a control group
text:
So you need a control group







The rule this buys you


A pre-post gain in an extreme-selected group is not proof a program worked — regression to the mean produces that gain with no treatment at all.












To claim an effect


Compare the tutored group against a control group selected the same way. Estimated effect, under a comparable or randomized control design = how much the tutored group beats the control’s own rise.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~57s] This brings us to the moral, which matters for your final projects. Recall that the selected low-scoring students were chosen far out on the low side. On retest, they were predicted back toward the middle, and that movement accounts for the whole climb, with no tutoring required. So a pre-post gain in an extreme-selected group is not enough evidence that a program worked. [CLICK 1] What helps separate a program effect from regression to the mean is a control group selected in the same way: low scorers who do not receive tutoring. They will drift upward too. Under random assignment, or under a credible comparable-control design, the program signal is the tutored group’s gain beyond that control group’s own rise. [NEXT]

title: The Improvement That Isn’t: Regression to the Mean
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


When remeasurement is noisy and imperfectly correlated, extreme scores are predicted closer to the mean: \(\hat{z}_y = r\,z_x\). So a low-selected group can “improve” with no treatment. Treat an uncontrolled pre-post gain as suspect; estimate the program signal against a same-selection control group, ideally randomized.




Next: Chapter 2 — ANCOVA: one slope, two intercepts. Notes, Ch 1 §Regression to the Mean

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~33s] Carry three checks with you. First, extreme-selected groups tend to move toward the mean on remeasurement. Second, that movement can create a convincing pre-post gain with no treatment. Third, a program effect is the gain beyond a same-selection control group, ideally randomized. The notes are linked below; next we move to ANCOVA. [END]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
text:
Chelsea wins by 9.85 — case closed?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch02-01 · 7 min · ~938 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~63s] Recall the two London schools from the previous lecture, Battersea and Chelsea. A natural first question is which school has the higher GCSE scores. The raw means point to Chelsea, whose average is nine point four five against Battersea’s negative zero point four one, so Chelsea is ahead by nine point eight five. It is tempting to stop here and say the matter is settled. [CLICK 1] Before we conclude, let us add one more column, the LRT, which is a reading score taken at age eleven. Battersea’s students arrive with a mean LRT of two point seven, while Chelsea’s arrive at six point three eight. This means that Chelsea did not only finish ahead; it also started ahead. As a result, we cannot yet tell whether a school-associated difference remains after accounting for measured intake, or whether the raw gap mostly reflects stronger intake. [NEXT]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
text:
Same intake — then ask again
The same-intake comparison

The raw gap of 9.85 isn’t wrong — it answers one question: “who’s ahead with all students lumped together?”


But that’s an apples-to-oranges race — Chelsea started ahead.


The more comparable question: for two students who arrived with the same LRT, how far apart are the schools? To ask it, we hold LRT equal.


[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~66s] Let us be careful here, because this is a common point of confusion. The gap of nine point eight five is not wrong. It answers one specific question, namely which school is ahead when all of the students are pooled together. The difficulty is that this compares two groups that are not alike to begin with. [CLICK 1] Think of it as a footrace in which the two teams do not begin at the same place. Chelsea’s runners started further down the track, so a raw finish-line comparison is less comparable. [CLICK 2] The more comparable question is this one. If we take a Battersea student and a Chelsea student who arrived with the same reading score, how far apart are the two schools then? To answer that question, we hold LRT equal, and that is exactly what the next model does. [NEXT]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
section: Add the covariate to the dummy
text:
Add the covariate to the dummy
Take last time’s dummy regression and bolt on the covariate LRT:

average GCSE = α + β₁ · LRT + β₂ · chelsea



β₁ — the LRT slope, shared by both schools
β₂ — the chelsea coefficient: the shift between schools



In this additive, parallel-slopes ANCOVA model: two parallel lines.


[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~68s] The way to hold LRT equal is to place it inside the model. Recall the dummy variable, which is one for Chelsea and zero for Battersea.
