We take that dummy regression and add the covariate to it. The model says that average GCSE equals alpha, plus beta-one times LRT, plus beta-two times the dummy. This is the additive, parallel-slopes ANCOVA model we are using here: a dummy regression with a covariate added and no interaction term. [CLICK 1] The two coefficients play different roles. Beta-one is the LRT slope, and it is shared, so both schools rise at the same rate as LRT increases. Battersea is the reference group; Chelsea is the dummy-coded group. Beta-two sits on that dummy, and it measures the shift between the two schools. [CLICK 2] When we draw this additive, parallel-slopes model, it produces two lines with the same slope, so the lines are parallel. This leaves us with one thing to explain, which is why beta-two is the only quantity that separates them. [NEXT]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
text:
One line per school
Set the dummy to 0, then 1, and the one equation splits in two:

Battersea (chelsea = 0): average GCSE = α + β₁ · LRT



Chelsea (chelsea = 1): average GCSE = (α + β₂) + β₁ · LRT

Same β₁ on LRT both times — only the intercept moved.


[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Let us separate the two lines, one school at a time. For Battersea the dummy is zero, so the last term drops out, and average GCSE equals alpha plus beta-one times LRT. That is the navy line. [CLICK 1] For Chelsea the dummy is one, so beta-two turns on. Now average GCSE equals alpha plus beta-two, which is the new intercept, still plus beta-one times LRT. Notice the slope on LRT, which is beta-one in both lines, exactly the same. The slope did not change at all. The only thing that moved is the intercept, which rose by beta-two. This is the meaning of one slope and two intercepts. [NEXT]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
text:
Subtract: the LRT cancels







The ten-second proof


Chelsea − Battersea at the same LRT:
(α + β₂ + β₁·LRT) − (α + β₁·LRT)
The α’s cancel. The β₁·LRT’s cancel. Left standing: β₂.





The gap is β₂ — and LRT is gone, so in this parallel-lines model it is the same gap across the modeled LRT range.


[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~55s] Here is a short proof that is worth ten seconds of your attention. Let us subtract the two lines, Chelsea minus Battersea, at the same LRT. Consider the alpha terms first; they are the same in both lines, so they cancel. Consider next the beta-one times LRT terms; they are identical, because we have fixed LRT to be equal, so they cancel as well. [CLICK 1] What remains is just beta-two. The gap is therefore beta-two, and notice that LRT has disappeared from the answer. Within this parallel-lines model, the gap does not depend on LRT, so it is the same vertical distance across the modeled LRT range. That is what beta-two represents: the model-adjusted difference, holding LRT equal. [NEXT]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
text:
See it: parallel, gap = β₂









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~68s] Here is the whole story in one picture. Battersea is in navy and Chelsea is in rust, plotted against their LRT, with two fitted lines that share the same slope and run parallel. The two dots on the vertical axis mark the two intercepts. Let us name the trap that is easy to fall into: some people read beta-two as a second slope. It is not a second slope. A second slope would tilt the rust line, but beta-two tilts nothing; it only shifts the line straight up. The rule to remember is shift, not tilt. [CLICK 1] Now we measure the gap. This arrow, drawn at the average LRT, has a height of seven point five one four. That value is beta-two, the model-adjusted difference. Because these fitted lines are parallel, imagine drawing this arrow anywhere within the shared modeled LRT range; its height stays the same every time. [NEXT]

title: ANCOVA: One Slope, Two Intercepts
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


This additive, parallel-slopes ANCOVA model = a dummy regression plus a covariate → two parallel lines: one shared LRT slope (β₁), intercepts β₂ apart. Across the modeled/common-support LRT range, the dummy coefficient β₂ = 7.51 is the constant vertical gap — the model-adjusted difference, not a second slope. It shifts the line; it never tilts it.




Next: ‘Controlling for’ changes the question. Notes, Ch 2 §The ANCOVA Model · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~54s] Let us close with the points worth carrying forward. In this additive, parallel-slopes ANCOVA model, a dummy regression with a covariate added produces two parallel lines: one shared LRT slope, with intercepts that sit beta-two apart. That coefficient, seven point five one, is the constant gap across the modeled LRT range, and it is the model-adjusted difference. It shifts the line; it never tilts it. Notice the contrast that sets up the next lecture: the raw gap was nine point eight five, while the adjusted gap is seven point five one, and these answer two different questions. In the next lecture, we will see which question controlling for actually asks. The notes and Practice Quiz Three are linked just below. [END]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
text:
“Controlling for” is a vertical slice









Figure 1














Figure 2















Common trap


Controlling is not physical balancing, and not deleting x. You keep x so the comparison is made at the same x.






[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 2 · ~71s] Let us define controlling for x visually. First, lump everyone together. The dashed lines compare group averages, but the groups sit in different parts of x, so the raw gap mixes group with x. [CLICK 1] Now hold x still. The gold line marks one shared x. The arrow is the group gap at that same x. That is the adjusted comparison. [CLICK 2] Common trap: controlling does not balance people by hand, and it does not delete x. We keep x so the model can compare like with like. [NEXT]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
text:
Same data, two estimands

Unadjusted: “Who’s ahead, everyone lumped together?” — it conflates the group difference with the difference in x.



Model-adjusted difference: “For two cases with the same x, how far apart are the groups?” — a more comparable contrast, as if both had the same average x.



[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~56s] Two distinct questions live inside one dataset, and each has its own estimand. The unadjusted question asks who is ahead overall, but in doing so it conflates the group difference with the difference in x, without separating those two sources. [CLICK 1] The adjusted question asks instead how far apart the groups are for two cases that share the same x. This gives a more comparable contrast, as if both groups had the same average x. The mindset shift is important here. The adjusted number is not a correction of the raw number, as though the raw number were a mistake. It is the answer to a different question. Once the question changes, the number is allowed to move, whether it shrinks or flips. [NEXT]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
text:
Case 1: the gap shrinks









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~51s] Let us look at real data, the two London schools from last time. Lumped together, Chelsea’s raw lead in GCSE over Battersea is nine point eight five points. [CLICK 1] Now let us control for LRT and compare the schools at the same intake. The lead drops to seven point five one four. It shrank because the raw gap was conflating intake with school, and this gentle case is called attenuation, since the sign held. Notice that nine point eight five is not wrong: it answers who is ahead overall, while seven point five one four answers who is ahead at equal intake. For that reason, you should report both numbers. [NEXT]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
text:
Case 2: the gap reverses









Figure 5














Figure 6








Same group z, two estimands — +1.6 ignoring x, −0.4 holding x fixed. The sign flipped, and neither model is broken.


[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 2 · ~63s] Shrinking was the gentle case, and now we turn to a sharper one. These are fresh generic numbers, with a predictor x and a zero-one indicator z. In the first panel we ignore x, so y-hat equals ten plus one point six z. Turning z on lifts group one above group zero, giving a positive gap of one point six. [CLICK 1] Now we bring x into the model. The adjusted fit is y-hat equals four plus zero point nine x minus zero point four z. At any fixed value of x, turning z on subtracts zero point four, so group one now sits below group zero. [CLICK 2] The sign did not merely shrink; it flipped. That is exactly the puzzle we opened with: a positive raw gap became a negative adjusted gap. [NEXT]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
section: A flipped sign is not a bug
text:
A flipped sign is not a bug







Common trap


A reversed sign does not mean the model is broken. Both answers can be right — to different questions: +1.6 compares groups overall; −0.4 compares them at the same x.






One careful limit: we did not assign units to groups. Statistical control is not causal control — call it model-adjusted, not automatically causal.



[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~56s] Let us calm the panic reflex. When the sign flips, your instinct says that something is broken, but nothing is. A reversal is simply the two estimands disagreeing, as they are entitled to: positive for the groups overall, and negative at the same x. [CLICK 1] There is one careful limit to keep in mind. First, we did not assign units to groups. Second, we may not have measured every common cause of group and outcome. Third, the causal claim would require a strong assumption, often called strong ignorability. For that reason, call this the model-adjusted difference, not automatically a causal effect. [NEXT]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
section: Go try it: drift versus stability
text:
Go try it: drift versus stability

The Adjusted-versus-Unadjusted-Means explorer in its default state — two bars compare the unadjusted group difference against the ANCOVA-adjusted one under a confounding covariate. In the live version, raising confounding strength makes the unadjusted bar drift while the adjusted bar stays much closer to the true gap.Simulated data. Try the live version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 0 · ~41s] Here is the explorer from the chapter, and it is worth trying for yourself. It is simulated on purpose, because demonstrating that adjustment reduces bias requires a known true gap to aim at, which a single real dataset cannot supply. In the live version, push the Confounding-strength slider from zero point five up toward zero point nine. Notice that the unadjusted bar drifts away from the truth while the adjusted bar stays much closer, apart from finite-sample noise. The lump-together comparison drifts, and the same-x comparison is much more stable. The live version is available in the notes. [NEXT]

title: ‘Controlling For’ Changes the Question
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Controlling for x means comparing groups at the same x.
That changes the estimand, so the adjusted gap can shrink or reverse the raw gap.
Report both, name the estimand, and call the adjusted one model-adjusted, not causal.




Next: Adjusted means — same yardstick, new gap. Notes, Ch 2 §Estimation & Interpretation · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~36s] Let us carry this away in three beats. First, controlling for x means comparing the groups at the same x, a statistical slice rather than balancing by hand or deleting a variable. Second, because it is a different question, the adjusted gap can shrink the raw difference or even reverse sign. Third, report both, name the estimand, and say model-adjusted difference rather than automatic cause. Next we turn to adjusted means, the same yardstick giving a new gap. [END]

title: Adjusted Means: Same Yardstick, New Gap
section: Read both schools at one LRT
text:
Read both schools at one LRT










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch02-03 · 5 min · ~664 words · clicks 8 · advances 5] [SLIDE 1/5 · clicks 1 · ~72s] We return to the same two London schools from the last two lectures, Battersea and Chelsea. Here are their raw mean GCSE scores. Battersea sits low, at negative zero point four one. Chelsea sits much higher, at nine point four five. That raw gap is real, but the two schools did not begin at the same intake level: Chelsea enrolled students with higher LRT scores. So before adjustment, the raw means are less comparable. [CLICK 1] The idea of adjustment is simple. Instead of reading each school at its own intake level, let us read both schools at one shared LRT, namely the grand mean, the average reading score across all of these students, which is four point four two two. In this two-school ANCOVA, we ask what each school would predict if every student had that same average reading score. The two values produced this way are the adjusted means. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Means: Same Yardstick, New Gap
text:
Plug in the grand mean









Figure 3














Figure 4















Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 2/5 · clicks 3 · ~58s] To obtain those means, we predict from the fitted lines at the grand-mean LRT. Start with Battersea, the reference school. For that reference line, put the grand-mean LRT into alpha plus beta-one times LRT. [CLICK 1] Carrying out that arithmetic, Battersea’s adjusted mean comes to zero point six eight eight. [CLICK 2] Now turn to Chelsea, the comparison group. The shared part is still Battersea’s adjusted mean. The extra piece is beta-two, the Chelsea coefficient, so Chelsea’s adjusted mean is zero point six eight eight plus seven point five one four. [CLICK 3] That sum lands on eight point two zero two, which is Chelsea’s adjusted mean. Both schools are read on the same yardstick, and adding the Chelsea coefficient to the reference prediction is the whole computation. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Means: Same Yardstick, New Gap
text:
Watch the means move









Figure 7














Figure 8















Common trap


Adjusted means are model predictions at one shared LRT, not the raw means.






[SLIDE 3/5 · clicks 2 · ~70s] This is the clearest single picture of what adjustment does, so let us watch it closely. On the left stand the raw means: Battersea low, Chelsea high, with a wide gap between them. [CLICK 1] When we hold LRT at its grand mean, each raw mean slides to its adjusted value, and you can trace the movement along the dashed connectors. Battersea climbs upward, from negative zero point four one to zero point six eight eight, while Chelsea drops downward, from nine point four five to eight point two zero two, so the two points pull closer together. [CLICK 2] Notice the common trap: adjusted means are not the raw means. The dots moved because these are new model predictions at one shared LRT. In this example, part of the raw gap was intake-related, because Chelsea arrived with higher LRT. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Means: Same Yardstick, New Gap
text:
The new gap is β₂
So the gap between the adjusted means is exactly β₂ — the same yardstick, one new gap.









Figure 9














Figure 10









Careful interpretation: this is model-adjusted, not automatically causal.



[SLIDE 4/5 · clicks 2 · ~65s] We can now ask what the gap is between the two adjusted means. [CLICK 1] Measuring it directly, eight point two zero two minus zero point six eight eight comes to seven point five one four. This is no coincidence. Recall that Chelsea’s adjusted mean was simply Battersea’s adjusted mean plus beta-two. When we subtract one from the other, Battersea’s shared part cancels cleanly, so the difference is beta-two by construction. It is therefore the very same seven point five one four that you first met as the constant gap between the parallel lines. [CLICK 2] One careful word about what this number is: it is the model-adjusted difference, the school contrast at the same LRT. By itself, it is not a verified causal claim, because we never assigned anyone to a school. It is a more comparable model-based contrast, not causal proof. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Means: Same Yardstick, New Gap
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Here, an adjusted mean is a school’s predicted GCSE at the grand-mean LRT — both schools read on the same yardstick. With this 0/1 dummy, get the other group by adding the coefficients: adjusted Chelsea = adjusted Battersea + β₂. The gap between the adjusted means is exactly β₂ = 7.514, the model-adjusted difference — not the raw means, which still mix in the intake gap.




Next: Omitted variable bias — when a lurker lies to you. Notes, Ch 2 §Adjusted Group Means · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 5/5 · clicks 0 · ~59s] Let us close with the ideas worth carrying with you. An adjusted mean is a school’s predicted GCSE at one shared LRT, the grand mean, so both schools sit on the same yardstick. You obtain the second school by adding the Chelsea coefficient: adjusted Battersea plus beta-two. The gap between the adjusted means is exactly beta-two, seven point five one four, the model-adjusted difference rather than a verified causal claim. Remember above all that the adjusted means are not the raw means, because the raw means still carry the intake gap inside them. The same yardstick gives one new gap. In the next lecture, we will see how an omitted variable can change the coefficient: omitted variable bias. [END]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
section: Could a variable left out of your model be writing your results?
text:
Could a variable left out of your model be writing your results?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch02-04 · 8 min · ~1082 words · clicks 10 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~68s] Let us begin with a question that is worth taking seriously. A variable you do not include in your model can quietly shape the results you report. Consider our two London schools once more. When we compare them at the same reading score, the gap in GCSE between the two schools is seven point five one four. That is the model-adjusted difference, comparing the two schools at equal LRT. [CLICK 1] Now suppose we drop a single variable, namely LRT, the reading score, from the comparison. The gap grows to nine point eight five. Notice that nothing in the data itself changed; we simply stopped accounting for one variable. A lurking variable slipped out of the model and distorted the answer by two point three three eight. The question for the rest of this lecture is precisely when a missing variable behaves this way. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
section: Condition 1 — the lurker moves the outcome
text:
Condition 1 — the lurker moves the outcome









Figure 3














Figure 4














Common trap


A lurker that relates to y alone is not enough. One condition down — but it takes two.





[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~50s] Omitting a variable only matters when two conditions hold together, and most students recall only one of them. Consider the first condition, which is that the lurking variable must move the outcome. We are asking whether the LRT predicts the GCSE score. The horizontal axis shows the LRT, and the vertical axis shows the GCSE. [CLICK 1] When we add the fitted line, it climbs steadily, so a higher LRT goes with a higher GCSE. The effect of the lurking variable on the outcome is positive, and the first condition is met. Notice, however, the trap that follows: relating to the outcome by itself biases nothing. A second link is required. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
section: Condition 2 — the lurker tracks the predictor you kept
text:
Condition 2 — the lurker tracks the predictor you kept









Figure 5














Figure 6














Common trap, continued


The lurker must also relate to the predictor you kept — here, school. Relate to only one? No bias.





[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~59s] The second condition is the one people most often forget. The omitted variable must also track the predictor you chose to keep. Our kept predictor is the school itself, so we ask whether LRT differs from one school to the other, and the distributions of intake reading scores let us see this directly. [CLICK 1] When we mark the two means, Battersea enters at two point seven zero and Chelsea at six point three eight. Chelsea’s students therefore started ahead in reading, which means that LRT is correlated with the school, and this correlation is positive as well. Both links are now in place: the omitted variable touches the outcome, and it also tracks the predictor we kept. With both conditions met, we can see what the omission does. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
section: Both links live — so the raw gap is inflated
text:
Both links live — so the raw gap is inflated









Figure 7














Figure 8















Common converse trap


“Any extra variable changes the coefficient” — false. If either link is absent, there is no systematic OVB. A single sample can still wiggle.






[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 2 · ~78s] Both links are now live, so let us deliberately omit LRT and simply compare GCSE between the two schools, school against school. [CLICK 1] When we mark the means and read the gap, it measures nine point eight five. This is the distortion in plain view. Chelsea arrived with higher reading scores, and higher reading lifts GCSE, so part of this raw gap is associated with intake differences rather than the same-LRT school contrast. The model-adjusted gap, holding reading equal, was seven point five one four, and omitting LRT inflated the raw comparison. [CLICK 2] Notice also the converse trap. It is not true that any extra variable changes a coefficient. Only a variable related to both the outcome and the kept predictor creates systematic omitted-variable bias. If a variable is tied to neither, or to only one link, expected omitted-variable bias is zero, although a single sample estimate can still wiggle. The natural next question is by how much the omission shifts the estimate. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
text:
The formula — shown, not derived
What bias does to your coefficient

\[\text{E}(\hat{b}) \;=\; \underbrace{b}_{\text{target coefficient}} \;+\; \underbrace{\,r \times \dfrac{s_{\text{omitted}}}{s_{\text{included}}} \times \gamma\,}_{\text{the systematic bias}}\]









Read the symbols (the trap)


r — the lurker’s correlation with the predictor you kept (condition 2). γ — the lurker’s own effect on y (condition 1) — not the school gap β₂. Make either factor zero → the bias is zero.






[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Here is the bias expressed as a formula, shown rather than derived. What you would estimate equals b, the target coefficient, plus a bias term, and that bias term is r times the ratio of the two spreads times gamma. [CLICK 1] Let us read the symbols carefully, because one of them is a trap. The letter r is the lurking variable’s correlation with the predictor you kept, which is condition two. The letter gamma is the lurking variable’s own effect on the outcome, which is condition one; gamma is the omitted variable’s coefficient, not beta-two, the school gap from earlier lectures. A different variable calls for a different symbol. The reassuring part is that the bias is a product. If you can drive either factor to zero, the entire bias becomes zero. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
section: Predict the direction from two signs
text:
Predict the direction from two signs
The two signs are all you need — bias direction = sign(r) × sign(γ).









Figure 9














Figure 10















Figure 11








[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 2 · ~65s] You do not even need the numerical values to determine the direction of the bias; you only need to multiply two signs. The columns of this table give the sign of gamma, and the rows give the sign of r. [CLICK 1] When we fill in the grid, the pattern is clean. Matching signs, where both are positive or both are negative, push the coefficient upward. Opposite signs pull it downward. That is the entire rule. [CLICK 2] Now apply it to our schools. Reading lifts GCSE, so gamma is positive. Chelsea’s intake runs higher, so r is positive. A positive times a positive gives upward, which is the top-left cell, ringed in gold. This matches exactly what we observed, since the gap grew too large. The formula therefore called the direction correctly. The remaining question is whether it can also nail the size. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
section: The payoff — the formula predicts the bias
text:
The payoff — the formula predicts the bias









Figure 12














Figure 13








[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~46s] Here is the moment that should give you pause. On the left, we feed the two relationships into the formula, namely r, the two spreads, and gamma, and it predicts a bias of two point three three eight. [CLICK 1] On the right, we look at what we actually observed: raw gap minus adjusted gap. Use the full-precision coefficients here: nine point eight five one eight minus seven point five one four two equals two point three three seven six, which rounds to two point three three eight. The displayed nine point eight five and seven point five one four are rounded labels, so we do not subtract the labels. In expectation, the product is the systematic omitted-variable bias; in this fitted sample, the same FWL decomposition gives the exact observed coefficient difference. [NEXT]

title: Omitted Variable Bias: When a Lurker Lies to You
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


OVB needs both links: the lurker relates to the outcome (γ ≠ 0) and to the predictor you kept (r ≠ 0). If either link is absent, there is no systematic OVB.

Direction is sign(r) × sign(γ); size is r × (s of lurker / s of predictor) × γ. Random assignment is designed to break the systematic treatment-lurker link in expectation; a realized study still needs design checks. With observational data, keep it model-adjusted, not automatically causal.





Next: Collinearity — wide intervals, not wrong answers. Notes, Ch 2 §Omitted Variable Bias · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 1 · ~64s] Let us carry a few clear lessons forward. A left-out variable creates systematic omitted-variable bias only when it relates to both the outcome and the predictor you kept. If either link is absent, expected omitted-variable bias is zero, even though a single sample estimate can still move a little for ordinary sampling reasons. [CLICK 1] When both links hold, the two signs give you the direction, and the formula gives you the size. Randomization is the design escape hatch because random assignment is meant to break the systematic link between pre-treatment lurkers and treatment in expectation. A realized study still needs balance, compliance, attrition, and implementation checks. Without randomization, report the result as model-adjusted, not automatically causal. This same logic returns in Session twelve on the odds scale. Next we turn to collinearity, which widens your intervals rather than giving you wrong answers. [END]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
section: Two predictors telling the same story
text:
Two predictors telling the same story

The setup

Two predictors that tell almost the same story — height and arm span, say. Does feeding the model both break it?


Your gut panics. In the near-overlap case, the answer is not automatically wrong. It just gets fuzzy.


[DECK ch02-05 · 7 min · ~876 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~47s] Here is a situation you will meet again and again. Imagine two predictors that move almost together and tell almost the same story, such as a person’s height and arm span. They are not identical, but they overlap a lot. Because they carry shared information, you might worry that handing both of them to the model will break your regression. [CLICK 1] Your intuition raises an alarm, and here is the key distinction. If one predictor were an exact copy or conversion of another, the two separate coefficients would not be separately estimable. But with near-collinearity, the model can still estimate the coefficient; it simply becomes less precise, or fuzzy. Our whole task today is to tell a wrong answer apart from a merely fuzzy one. [NEXT]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
text:
Crank up the correlation









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3







Simulated data — the true slope is 1.0 for both predictors.

[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~78s] Let us see this in action with data where we already know the target. We simulate two predictors and set the true coefficient to exactly one point zero for both of them. We simulate on purpose, because to talk about bias clearly we need a known target to compare against. The dashed line marks that target. Begin gently, with a target correlation of zero point three zero between the predictors. The estimate lands close to the line, and the interval is tight, with a width of zero point three zero. [CLICK 1] Now raise the target correlation to zero point nine zero. The interval is already taller, with a width of zero point six six, yet the point estimate remains close to one point zero. [CLICK 2] Finally, push it to the edge, with a target correlation of zero point nine nine. The interval balloons to a width of one point eight two, roughly six times as wide as before, and it still covers the target. The interval expands, but in this draw the point stays close. [NEXT]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
section: What does not happen: no bias
text:
What does not happen: no bias


Figure 4








Important caveat


Unbiased in expectation — on average across samples. Any one sample can still drift off 1.0; the wider interval is exactly the model admitting it.






[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~43s] Let us now name what did not happen. When we strip the intervals away and look only at the points, all three stay near the target in this simulated draw. Across repeated samples, collinearity has not created systematic bias; it has inflated the uncertainty. [CLICK 1] There is one important footnote to keep in mind. Unbiased means unbiased in expectation, that is, on average across many samples. Any single sample can still drift away from one point zero. The model is not hiding this fact, because the wide interval is precisely the model telling you that it is unsure. [NEXT]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
section: Put a number on it: VIF
text:
Put a number on it: VIF


Figure 5

[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 0 · ~75s] The natural next question is whether we can measure that overlap, and the answer is yes, through the variance inflation factor, written as VIF. VIF measures variance inflation. The standard error multiplier is the square root of VIF. For example, a VIF of two doubles the coefficient variance, so the standard error is about one point four one times as large. Near zero correlation the VIF sits at one, and as the predictors close in on each other it shoots upward. The three teal marks correspond to our three frames from earlier, with standard errors multiplied by about one point oh, two point three, and seven point one. As rules of thumb, VIF cutoffs beyond five signal moderate collinearity, and beyond ten signal serious collinearity, and that VIF value of ten is reached near a correlation of zero point nine five. How to compute VIF in practice is a task we save for Session eight. [NEXT]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
text:
Two diseases, two cures

Omission → your estimate moves. That’s bias. The cure is putting the confounder in.



Collinearity → your interval widens. That’s variance. Same estimate, just less precise.










The opposite levers


In this example, adding the true confounder reduces systematic bias but can raise uncertainty when predictors overlap. For causal questions, we usually keep the confounder and live with the wider interval.






[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~78s] Let us turn to the trap I most want you to avoid. In the previous lecture, omitting a confounder moved the answer away from the target, and that shift is bias, which we fix by putting the missing variable back into the model. [CLICK 1] Collinearity is the opposite kind of failure. It widens the interval, and that wider interval reflects variance, not bias. The estimate is less precise, not automatically wrong. A different disease calls for a different cure. [CLICK 2] Here is the part that ties the two together. In this example, adding the true confounder reduces systematic bias but can also increase uncertainty when that confounder overlaps with other predictors. For a causal question, this means you usually keep the confounder and accept the wider interval. Let one sentence stay with you: collinearity widens your uncertainty, while omission moves your answer. [NEXT]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
text:
Go try it yourself

The Collinearity Explorer on simulated data, where the true coefficient is 1.0 for both predictors. Drag the predictor-correlation slider up toward 0.99: the coefficient confidence intervals balloon, while the point estimates stay near the true 1.0 line in this draw.Simulated data. Try the live version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Here is the explorer from the chapter, so that you can build the intuition for yourself. It is simulated once more, because seeing bias clearly requires a known value to aim at, and here both true coefficients are one point zero. The tool hands you the slider. As you drag the correlation toward zero point nine nine and watch, both intervals balloon and the VIF reading climbs toward severe, while the estimates remain near the true line in this draw. You will find the live version linked in the notes. [NEXT]

title: Collinearity: Wide Intervals, Not Wrong Answers
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Correlated predictors inflate coefficient variance. The SE grows by √VIF, so the confidence intervals widen. Point estimates stay unbiased in expectation: collinearity is a precision problem, not a bias problem.
Collinearity widens your uncertainty; omission moves your answer. VIF > 5 and VIF > 10 flag trouble; computing VIF comes later.




Next: Confounder or mediator? When NOT to control. Notes, Ch 2 §Collinearity · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~45s] Let us close with the main idea. Correlated predictors inflate each coefficient’s standard error, by a factor of the square root of VIF, so the confidence intervals widen. The estimate, however, stays unbiased on average. This is a precision problem, not a bias problem: wide intervals, not wrong answers. Say it back to yourself: collinearity widens your uncertainty, while omission moves your answer. In the next lecture we will ask whether a variable is a confounder or a mediator, and when you should not control for it. The notes and the practice quiz are linked just below. [END]

title: Confounder or Mediator? When NOT to Control
section: More controls means a better model, right?
text:
More controls means a better model, right?


More controls = a better model?


Not always. One kind of control can quietly change the question.


[DECK ch02-06 · 7 min · ~909 words · clicks 8 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~48s] There is an instinct that almost everyone shares, and it turns out to be mistaken. The instinct says that adding more control variables always gives you a better model, so you should add every variable you measured and play it safe. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] In fact, this is not always true. There is one kind of variable that, the moment you control for it, quietly changes the effect you are estimating. So the real question is not how many controls you add, but which ones you add, and a single picture is enough to decide. Let us look at the two shapes that matter. [NEXT]

title: Confounder or Mediator? When NOT to Control
text:
The fork: control it









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 1 · ~69s] The first shape is the fork. You met it in the last lecture, where we called it the lurker. At the top sits z, a common cause, and two arrows run downward from it, one into x, your predictor, and one into y, your outcome. The important point is that the same variable drives both of them. The dotted line drawn between x and y marks the effect you actually want to estimate. [CLICK 1] When you leave z out of the model, its influence leaks into the coefficient on x, and that leakage is the bias we discussed last time. The remedy is to control z: you hold it fixed, which closes the back path it opened between x and y. For a fork, then, adding the control is exactly the right move. Now let us look at a shape that appears very similar yet behaves in the opposite way. [NEXT]

title: Confounder or Mediator? When NOT to Control
text:
The chain: leave it alone









Figure 3














Figure 4















Common trap


A confounder opens a noncausal backdoor path — control it. A mediator carries part of the assumed causal pathway — for a total effect, leave it alone.






[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 2 · ~78s] The second shape is the chain. Here the arrows run straight across, from x to m to y. To picture it, think of a father’s education, then the son’s education, and then the son’s income. The variable m in the middle is the mechanism, because it is how x reaches y; it stands directly on the road between them. [CLICK 1] If your target is the total effect of x on y, controlling m blocks part of the road through which x reaches y. As a result, you would remove part of the effect you came to study. If your target were a direct effect net of m, that would be a different estimand, and you would need to say so explicitly. [CLICK 2] Here is the clean distinction. A confounder opens a noncausal backdoor path, so you control it. A mediator carries part of the causal pathway under the diagram you are assuming, so for a total effect you leave it alone. One word, common cause versus consequence, flips the verdict. [NEXT]

title: Confounder or Mediator? When NOT to Control
text:
What controlling a mediator does









Figure 5














Figure 6







Simulated data — a pure chain where all of x’s effect runs through m (true direct effect 0).

[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~68s] Let us make the mechanism concrete, using simulated data where I built the chain myself, so the target is known in advance. I wired x to move y only through m, with no direct path between them. When I estimate the effect of x with the mediator left out of the model, the total effect appears clearly at one point eight zero, and all of it is flowing down the chain. [CLICK 1] Now I add m to the model, which means I control the mediator, and I watch the bar. It drops to zero point zero zero on the display. More precisely, the fitted coefficient is essentially zero; in a finite sample it need not be exactly zero. That happens because this simulation has no direct x-to-y path. Every bit of x’s total effect ran through m, and conditioning on m left only the near-zero direct path. The whole danger sits in one picture: for a total effect, controlling a mediator can throw away the mediated part you meant to count. [NEXT]

title: Confounder or Mediator? When NOT to Control
text:
A ten-second decision rule









Figure 7














Figure 8















Figure 9








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 2 · ~64s] Here, then, is your ten-second rule. Before you add any variable to the model, ask one question about it: what is its shape? Point at the variable and trace the arrows. [CLICK 1] If the arrows point into both x and y, the variable is a fork, a common cause, and you control it. That is the confounder you need to fix. [CLICK 2] If instead the variable sits on the road, with x pointing into it and it pointing into y, the variable is a chain, a mediator. For a total effect, leave it alone. If you want a direct effect net of that mediator, name that estimand explicitly. Common cause means control; consequence means pause and ask what effect you want. One brief aside: there is a third shape, called a collider, where controlling actually creates a problem rather than fixing one, but set that aside for now, because the fork-versus-chain distinction will carry you a long way. [NEXT]

title: Confounder or Mediator? When NOT to Control
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


“More controls” is not always better. Control a confounder — a common cause, the fork x ← z → y — because leaving it out biases your answer. For a total effect, do not control a mediator — a consequence of x, the chain x → m → y — because conditioning on it removes part of the pathway you meant to count. If the estimand is a direct effect net of that mediator, say so explicitly.

Your adjustment set is a research decision: it follows from the causal diagram you’re assuming, not from a throw-everything-in default. And even then it is causal only under strong ignorability — no unmeasured common causes, with adequate overlap — which observational data rarely guarantee. That’s why randomized experiments stay the gold standard.





Next: Are the lines really parallel? Notes, Ch 2 §Confounding · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 1 · ~78s] Let us carry this out the door together. Adding more controls is not automatically better. You should control a confounder, which is a common cause in the shape of a fork, because leaving it out biases your answer. For a total effect, you should not control a mediator, which is a consequence of your predictor in the shape of a chain, because controlling it removes part of the pathway you meant to count. If you want the direct effect net of that mediator, say that estimand explicitly. [CLICK 1] Your adjustment set is therefore a research decision: it follows from the diagram you are assuming, not from a habit of including everything you measured. And it is worth staying humble, because even the right set is causal only under no unmeasured common causes and adequate overlap. Randomization is preferred because it breaks the confounder-treatment link by design, apart from chance imbalance or implementation problems. In the next lecture, we will ask whether the lines are really parallel. [END]

title: Are the Lines Really Parallel?
section: We drew the lines parallel — who said we could?
text:
We drew the lines parallel — who said we could?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch02-07 · 6 min · ~796 words · clicks 7 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~64s] In the ANCOVA model from last time, we drew one line for each school and made them parallel. That choice was convenient, but it was an assumption rather than something the data had confirmed, so today we will put it to the test. On the screen are our two familiar schools, with Battersea shown in navy and Chelsea shown in rust, the GCSE score running up the vertical axis and the reading score running along the horizontal axis. [CLICK 1] Now we relax the constraint and let each school keep its own line, allowing the two slopes to be estimated independently. Notice that the two lines come out nearly parallel. The picture certainly looks close, but a picture alone is not proof, so we need a formal way to decide whether that small visible difference is real. [NEXT]

title: Are the Lines Really Parallel?
text:
Let the slopes go free
How to test it

Compare two models: one constrained, one freer.



Without it: gcse ~ lrt + school — one shared slope (the parallel lines)




With it: gcse ~ lrt × school — a separate slope per school (lines free to tilt apart)










Common trap


The parallel-slopes assumption has a named check — fit both models, then ask whether the extra slope improves the fit enough to keep.






[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 3 · ~86s] To test the assumption, we compare two models that differ in exactly one way. The key move is to add the product term, the LRT score multiplied by school, because that single term is what allows the two slopes to differ from each other. [CLICK 1] Without the product term, the model is GCSE on LRT plus school, and here both schools are forced to share one common slope. This is the stricter, parallel model, which draws two lines that can sit at different heights but must rise at the same rate. [CLICK 2] With the product term, the model is GCSE on LRT times school, and now each school is given its own slope, so the lines are free to tilt apart. In other words, one model is simply the other model with a single constraint lifted. [CLICK 3] This is why the parallel-slopes assumption is not mere decoration: it has a named check. We fit both models, and then the F-test asks whether the extra slope buys enough improvement to justify the added complexity. [NEXT]

title: Are the Lines Really Parallel?
text:
Ask the F-test









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~68s] Because the two models are nested, with the parallel model sitting inside the freer one, we can compare them with a single F-test. The question that test answers is whether the freer model, the one with a separate slope for each school, fits significantly better than the simpler parallel model. [CLICK 1] The result is that F is two point one four and p is zero point one five, which is not significant. The way we phrase this conclusion matters a great deal, so let us be careful: we say that we find no evidence that the slopes differ. Notice what we deliberately did not say, namely that the slopes are equal, because we cannot prove that. The test may simply lack the power to detect a small difference, and so we say only that we failed to reject the equal-slopes hypothesis, which is the humbler and more honest claim. [NEXT]

title: Are the Lines Really Parallel?
text:
Verdict: keep the parallel lines


Figure 5

[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 0 · ~49s] This brings us to the verdict. The F-test found no evidence that the product term improved the fit enough to justify the added complexity, so we keep the simpler model. These are the parallel lines from last time: one shared slope and a constant vertical gap between the schools. Parallelism is tenable here, but tenable is not the same thing as proof. It means the simpler model is a reasonable working model after this check, not proof that the slopes are exactly equal. [NEXT]

title: Are the Lines Really Parallel?
text:
But what if they weren’t?









Figure 6














Figure 7







Schematic — not a data fit.

[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~67s] Before we move on, let us sit with the other possibility for a moment and ask what we would have seen if the lines had not been parallel. This sketch shows that world, drawn by hand rather than fitted from data. Here the slopes genuinely differ, so the two lines fan apart instead of staying a fixed distance from each other. [CLICK 1] Watch the vertical gap between the lines as we move across the axis. The gap is large at low LRT and small at high LRT, which means the school difference is no longer a single number but instead depends on the value of LRT. A difference that depends on another variable has a name: it is an interaction, and that idea is the whole story of Session Seven. That is the bridge to interactions: the group difference changes depending on LRT. [NEXT]

title: Are the Lines Really Parallel?
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


ANCOVA assumes one shared slope — parallel lines. Don’t take it on faith: add the product term (lrt × school) and F-test the two models. Here F = 2.14, p = 0.15 — not significant, so no evidence the slopes differ. The parallel model is a reasonable working model, not proof that “the slopes are equal.”

And if a test like this ever did fire? The gap would depend on the covariate — the world of interactions.





Next: One-way ANOVA — comparing several group means. Notes, Ch 2 §Parallel Slopes · Practice Quiz 3

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Let us carry this away with us. The ANCOVA model leans on one shared slope, and we do not simply assume that slope is justified; instead, we add the product term and run the F-test. In our case, F was two point one four and p was zero point one five, so we find no evidence that the slopes differ. That makes the parallel model a reasonable working model, not proof that the slopes are equal. [CLICK 1] Had the test fired instead, the school gap would have depended on LRT, which is the world of interactions in Session Seven. In the next lecture, however, we move from comparing two groups to comparing several groups at once, through one-way ANOVA. [END]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
text:
Just code the groups 1, 2, 3?

The shortcut

Three groups – Low, Medium, High teacher prep. You want them in a regression.


Easy, right? Just number them 1, 2, 3 and feed that one column in?


[DECK ch03-01 · 8 min · ~1118 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] In the previous lecture, we fit a single zero-one dummy, which let us compare two groups through their two means. Now we have three groups instead of two. The groups come from teacher preparation, which we have binned into Low, Medium, and High, and our goal is to predict each student observation’s math gain from that group label. [CLICK 1] Here is the shortcut that almost everyone reaches for first. We could simply number the groups, calling Low one, Medium two, and High three, and then feed that single column into the model. The idea looks clean and economical, but it quietly imposes assumptions that the data never agreed to. Let us see exactly what it costs. [NEXT]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
text:
Says who?









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~100s] These three diamonds are the observed group means, plotted directly from the data with no model imposed yet. Notice the shape they trace. Low sits lowest, Medium rises to the highest point, and High then drops back down below Medium. The pattern climbs and then falls, which means that Medium has the highest observed sample mean. [CLICK 1] Now suppose we treat that numeric code as an ordinary number and fit a straight line through it. A single predictor entered this way gives one slope, and one slope can only draw one straight line. Notice that the Medium diamond floats above that line while the High diamond falls below it, because no single straight line can pass through all three means at once. [CLICK 2] Here is where the real damage shows. One slope forces the steps between consecutive groups to be equal, so the line rises by plus three point nine three from Low to Medium and then by exactly the same amount again from Medium to High. As a result, the numeric-code fit gives High the highest fitted mean, while the observed sample means put Medium on top. The numeric code did not merely mismeasure a gap; it reversed the story by deciding in advance that the High-minus-Medium step must equal the Medium-minus-Low step. Nothing in the data justifies that decision. [NEXT]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
text:
Code each label 0 or 1
For this nominal factor with 3 levels, use 2 dummies – not a single numeric score unless the linear trend and spacing are intentional and defensible. Pick Low as the reference:

d_Medium = 1 if Medium, else 0 · d_High = 1 if High, else 0





Group
d_Medium
d_High




Low (reference)
0
0


Medium
1
0


High
0
1





[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~72s] Let us set the numeric shortcut aside and use the dummy idea you already know, only now applied twice. Instead of ranking the groups, we switch each one on and off with its own indicator. We define one dummy for Medium, equal to one when a student observation carries the Medium teacher-prep label and zero otherwise, and a second dummy for High, defined the same way. There is deliberately no dummy for Low, because Low serves as our reference group, the baseline that the others are measured against. [CLICK 1] Read the table down its rows to see how the coding works. Low takes zero and zero, with both switches off, so it rides on the baseline alone. Medium turns the first switch on, and High turns the second switch on. Three nominal groups therefore need only two dummies, and because each switch is either off or on, the coding does not impose an order or equal spacing on these labels. [NEXT]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
section: What R builds under the hood
text:
What R builds under the hood









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~66s] In practice, you do not type these dummy columns by hand. You hand the factor to the el-em function, and R constructs the indicator columns for you automatically. To understand what the model actually fits, it helps to lift the hood and look at those columns directly. [CLICK 1] What you see here is the design matrix, the exact set of columns R fits behind the scenes. Each row is one student observation carrying a teacher-prep label, and I have sampled two observations per group so the pattern is visible. The Intercept column is entirely ones, because every observation shares the same baseline. The dummy columns then light up exactly as the table promised: the Low rows read zero and zero and lean on the intercept alone, each Medium row lights the d-Medium column, and each High row lights the d-High column. The whole mechanism is now visible in one picture. [NEXT]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
section: The model reads off two gaps
text:
The model reads off two gaps
\[\text{math gain} = \alpha + \beta_1\,\texttt{d\_Medium} + \beta_2\,\texttt{d\_High} + \varepsilon\]









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~75s] With the dummies in place, the equation simply reads those switches off. Math gain equals alpha, plus beta-one times d-Medium, plus beta-two times d-High, plus an error term. Because Low carries zeros in both dummy columns, alpha, the intercept, is the Low mean at full precision, displayed here as fifty-six point zero eight and marked by the dotted baseline. [CLICK 1] Each coefficient then measures a gap from that Low baseline. Beta-one, the coefficient on d-Medium, is plus seven point four eight, the rust arrow that rises from Low up to the Medium mean. Beta-two, the coefficient on d-High, is plus five point two six, the shorter teal arrow up to the High mean. These two gaps are estimated as separate, unconstrained differences from Low, so they are free to be unequal, and here they plainly are. At full precision, adding the intercept to a group’s own coefficient lands on that group’s mean; the displayed values close only up to rounding. In this way the data choose the shape, and the model simply records what it finds. [NEXT]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
text:
Two dummies, never three







The dummy trap


With 3 groups you use 2 dummies – one fewer than the levels. Add a third (one for every level) plus the intercept and the columns become perfectly redundant – R silently drops one.












One honest caveat


We binned a continuous prep score into three boxes – tidy for teaching, but it discards information and reduces power. A real analysis would weigh keeping it continuous.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] Two cautions are worth carrying with you. The first is the opposite mistake, which many people make. Faced with three groups, it feels only fair to give each its own dummy, all three of them. You should not. The intercept already accounts for Low, so if you add a dummy for every level and keep the intercept as well, the columns become perfectly redundant and R will silently drop one of them. This is the dummy trap. Too few columns, as with the one-two-three code, fakes an order and an equal spacing, while too many columns create perfect redundancy. [CLICK 1] The second caution is an honest one about our own setup. We chopped a smooth, continuous preparation score into three bins, which is convenient for a lecture but not free. Binning a continuous variable discards information and reduces statistical power. In a real analysis, you would weigh that cost against the convenience and consider keeping the variable continuous. [NEXT]

title: Three Groups, Two Dummies
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A factor with k + 1 levels enters as k zero-one dummies against a reference – not a single 1/2/3 number unless that scoring is the intended linear model, and never one for every level (the dummy trap). The intercept is the reference mean; each coefficient is that group’s difference from it – gaps set freely, no spacing assumed.





Next: A coefficient is a difference, not a level. Notes, Ch 3 §ANOVA as Regression · Practice Quiz 4


[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 1 · ~59s] Let us gather the key points to carry forward. A categorical predictor with k plus one levels enters a regression as k zero-one dummies measured against a reference group. For nominal labels, you should not replace the factor with a single one-two-three number unless a defensible linear score is the model you mean to fit, because that score forces order and equal spacing onto the groups. You should also not give every level its own dummy, because that creates the dummy trap. The intercept is the reference mean, and each coefficient is one group’s gap from that reference. [CLICK 1] This same dummy logic will return for a categorical predictor such as department on the logit side in Session twelve. In the next lecture, we will see that a coefficient is a difference, not a level. The notes and Practice Quiz Four are linked just below. [END]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
section: Which row is the High group’s mean?
text:
Which row is the High group’s mean?





Table 1: lm(mathgain ~ prep_level) – the coefficient table (n: Low 926, Medium 169, High 95)






Term
Coefficient
p-value




(Intercept)
56.08
< .001


prep_levelMedium
+7.48
0.010


prep_levelHigh
+5.26
0.157
















Trick question


Not the High row. The intercept row is the Low/reference mean. The High row, +5.26, is the High − Low difference.






[DECK ch03-02 · 7 min · ~853 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us begin with a quick trick question. Here is the coefficient table from our preparation-group model, with Low, Medium, and High predicting math gain. Point at a single row and ask which row is the High group’s mean. The High row reads plus five point two six, so it is tempting to call that the mean for the High group. [CLICK 1] That reading is wrong. The intercept row is the Low mean, not the High mean. The High row’s plus five point two six is not a level at all; it is a difference, High minus Low. As we will see, this single confusion, treating a dummy coefficient as a level rather than as a difference, is the most expensive misreading in this course. [NEXT]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
text:
The intercept is the reference









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~64s] Let us picture what the numbers mean. The three diamonds are the three group means, and the dotted line marks the Low mean, fifty-six point zero eight. Here is the first half of the rule: the intercept is exactly that dotted line, the reference group’s own mean. Because Low is the reference, the intercept simply is the Low diamond. [CLICK 1] Now notice where the coefficients live. The Medium coefficient, plus seven point four eight, is this arrow, the gap upward from the baseline to Medium. It is not the height of Medium; it is the climb. [CLICK 2] In the same way, the High coefficient, plus five point two six, is this shorter arrow up to High. We have two gaps measured from one baseline, and they are not equal. Each one tells you how far that group sits above the reference. [NEXT]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
text:
Every row, one sentence
How to read it


Intercept = the reference mean (Low). · Each non-reference dummy coefficient = that group − reference.




So Medium coef is Medium − Low, High coef is High − Low. There’s no “Medium vs High” row — that arrow simply isn’t in the table.



[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~44s] Let us say the whole table in a single sentence, the one sentence worth memorizing for this dummy-coded model. [CLICK 1] The intercept is the reference mean, which here is Low. Each non-reference dummy coefficient is that group minus the reference. That statement describes the entire table. [CLICK 2] This means the Medium coefficient is Medium minus Low, and the High coefficient is High minus Low. Now notice the trap. There is no row and no arrow that compares Medium with High, because each dummy speaks only to the reference. If you want Medium versus High, that comparison is simply not in this table. [NEXT]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
text:
Recover a level? Just add









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~59s] If a coefficient is a gap, how do you recover an actual level? You add. Let us drill the practice numbers from the notes. Suppose the output said the intercept is fifty point three, the Medium coefficient is plus eight point seven, and the High coefficient is plus fifteen point two. Low comes for free, because it is just the intercept, fifty point three. [CLICK 1] Now add. Medium is fifty point three plus eight point seven, which gives fifty-nine point zero. High is fifty point three plus fifteen point two, which gives sixty-five point five. Notice the trap highlighted in gold: for High you add only the High coefficient, and never the Medium one as well. Each group is the intercept plus its own dummy, and nothing else. [NEXT]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
text:
It checks out, exactly









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~42s] This is not a quirk of that example; it holds exactly, on our real data. On the left are the plain group means, computed straight from the data: fifty-six point zero eight, sixty-three point five seven, and sixty-one point three five. [CLICK 1] Now look at the right column, the model-implied mean. For Low, that is the intercept alone; for Medium and High, it is the intercept plus that group’s own dummy coefficient. The two columns are identical, line for line. That is the deeper fact: in one-way analysis of variance, the regression is simply handing you the group means, expressed as a baseline plus a set of gaps. [NEXT]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
text:
What each p-value tests




Table 2: Each p tests that gap against Low – and only against Low






Term
Coefficient
p-value




(Intercept)
56.08
< .001


prep_levelMedium
+7.48
0.010


prep_levelHigh
+5.26
0.157
















Not significant ≠ equal


High’s gap is +5.26, p = 0.157 — we just can’t rule out zero. That’s absence of evidence, not evidence the groups are equal.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~55s] Let us return to the real table and read the p-values. The logic is the same: each p-value tests that group against the reference, Low. The Medium p-value, zero point zero one zero, provides evidence that Medium differs from Low at alpha point zero five, before any multiple-comparison correction. The High p-value, zero point one five seven, tells us that High versus Low is not significant. [CLICK 1] Here is the important trap. Not significant does not mean equal. The High gap is still plus five point two six; it did not vanish, we simply cannot rule out zero. In other words, absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. Notice also that these p-values are uncorrected for testing several groups at once, and a later pairwise-comparisons lesson handles that correction. [NEXT]

title: A Dummy Coefficient Is a Difference, Not a Level
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


In a dummy-coded model the intercept is the reference mean, and each non-reference dummy coefficient is a difference from that reference — never a level, never one dummy versus another. Recover a non-reference group’s level by adding the intercept and that group’s own dummy coefficient; the reference level is the intercept. A non-significant coefficient means can’t rule out zero — not “equal.” Change the reference and the coefficients change, but the fit and the group means never do.




Next: Where F comes from — between-group vs within-group variation. Notes, Ch 3 §Interpreting the Coefficients · Practice Quiz 4

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Let us close with the points worth remembering. In this dummy-coded model, the intercept is the reference mean, and each non-reference dummy coefficient is a difference from that reference, never a level and never one dummy against another. To recover a non-reference level, add the intercept and that group’s own dummy coefficient; the reference level is the intercept itself. A non-significant coefficient means you cannot rule out zero, not that the groups are equal. Finally, if you change the reference, the coefficients change, but the fit and the group means do not move. The same reference-category logic will return in logistic regression on the log-odds or odds-ratio scale. Next, we use these same group means to see where the F test comes from: between-group versus within-group variation. [END]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
text:
Same target means, two verdicts

The puzzle

Two simulations. Same target group means. One screams “significant!” — the other shrugs.


How can the same planned gaps give opposite verdicts? One word: noise.


[DECK ch03-03 · 6 min · ~831 words · clicks 7 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~41s] Consider a puzzle. We have two simulated studies, each with three groups, and we set them up with the same target group means, the same planned gaps, and the same overall signal. Yet one study reports a clearly significant result while the other reports nothing of the kind. The question we want to answer is how the same planned averages can lead to opposite verdicts. [CLICK 1] One word accounts for the difference, and that word is noise. Understanding how the group differences compare against that noise is exactly where the F-statistic comes from. [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
text:
One grand mean, three groups









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Let us build the picture on real data, using math gains for the three teacher-preparation groups you have already met. At this stage we see only a faint cloud of students, with no summary drawn yet. [CLICK 1] Now gather everyone into a single pile and take one overall average, which we call the grand mean, equal to fifty-seven point six. The gold line marks that value and ignores the groups entirely, so it serves as our anchor. The question the F-statistic asks is this: of all the spread around that line, how much is due to the groups, and how much is simply due to individual people? [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
section: Between: the group means reach out
text:
Between: the group means reach out









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~42s] The first piece is the signal, and it comes from the fact that each group has its own mean. [CLICK 1] Notice how the rust bars reach from the grand mean out to each group’s mean. This is the between-group part, which measures how far the groups pull away from the centre. Long bars indicate that the groups sit far apart, and that separation is what looks like a difference between them. The notes call this piece the model sum of squares, written M-S-S. The bars on their own, however, can still mislead us. [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
section: Within: people scatter around their own mean
text:
Within: people scatter around their own mean









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~45s] The second piece is the noise. [CLICK 1] Notice how widely the students scatter around their own group’s mean. This is the within-group part: the residual spread left after each group gets its own mean. In standard one-way ANOVA, we pool those within-group spreads into M-S-E, the denominator of F. The notes call the underlying sum the error sum of squares, written S-S-E, and it is the very same residual left over from R-squared. The essential idea is that the between-group bars impress us only when they are large compared with this scatter. A small step between groups means very little if the individuals are spread widely. [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
text:
F is signal over noise
The F-statistic
\[ F = \frac{\text{MMS}}{\text{MSE}} = \frac{\text{between-group mean square}}{\text{within-group mean square}} = \frac{\text{signal}}{\text{noise}} \]


Same split as R-squared — total spread = between + within. For our prep groups: F = 3.977 on F(2, 1187), p = 0.019.



[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~42s] Now we put the two pieces into the ratio we call F. This is the same split as R-squared, only now the model is the group means. We turn each sum of squares into a mean square. On the top sits M-M-S, the between-group signal carried by the bars. Below it sits M-S-E, the pooled within-group noise carried by the scatter. That ratio is signal over noise. [CLICK 1] For our prep groups, F equals three point nine seven seven, on two and eleven eighty-seven degrees of freedom, with p equal to zero point zero one nine. [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
text:
Watch separation and noise fight









Figure 7














Figure 8















Figure 9








[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 2 · ~62s] Let us see this in action with three simulated worlds, each drawn just like our picture. In the first, the null case, all three groups share the same target mean, so F is zero point nine seven, right around one, with p equal to zero point three eight one. There is no planned signal here. [CLICK 1] Now spread the target means apart and keep each group tight. With clear target separation and low noise, F leaps to forty point zero seven, with p below zero point zero zero one, which gives clear evidence of group differences. [CLICK 2] Here is the crucial case. Keep those target means exactly where they were, but increase the noise. F collapses to two point seven six, with p equal to zero point zero six seven, which is not significant. The intended gaps are the same, and the main design change is the scatter. [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
text:
Go try it yourself

The between-vs-within app: a strip plot with the grand-mean line and group means, beside the variance split and the live F-statistic. Drag the group-mean sliders apart and F climbs; drag the within-group noise slider up and F falls. (simulated data)Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 0 · ~46s] You can explore this directly in a live app in the chapter, built on simulated data, so you may bend it any way you like. Drag the three target-mean sliders apart and watch F climb as the numerator grows and the bars stretch. Then leave those target means alone and push the within-group noise slider upward, and watch F fall even though the intended group separation has not changed, because the denominator is swelling. Separation raises F, while noise pulls it back down. This puts the F-test directly in your hands, and the link is in the notes. [NEXT]

title: Where F Comes From: Between vs Within
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


ANOVA first splits the outcome’s spread into between-group signal and within-group noise — the same split as R-squared, with the model being the group means. F then compares their mean squares: MMS / MSE. Big separation makes F large — but only if the within-group scatter is small. A big mean difference does not guarantee significance: noise is the denominator.




Next: Reading the ANOVA table — and what F doesn’t say. Notes, Ch 3 §The ANOVA Table · Practice Quiz 4

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~45s] Let us summarize the central idea. ANOVA first splits the spread in your outcome into between-group signal and within-group noise. The F-statistic then compares the two mean squares: M-M-S over pooled M-S-E. It is the same split as R-squared, with the model being the group means. A large gap between groups does not by itself guarantee significance, because noise is the denominator. In the next lecture, we will read the ANOVA table in which this F lives, and we will see what F does not tell you. The notes and the practice quiz are linked just below. [END]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
section: Half a table — fill it in
text:
Half a table — fill it in

Complete the table


Figure 1

[DECK ch03-04 · 8 min · ~1015 words · clicks 10 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~56s] This is a table that you will complete again and again in course output, in software output, and throughout your work. It is the one-way ANOVA table. The setup is the same example we have been using, with three groups of teacher preparation, Low, Medium, and High, used to predict math gain. For now the numeric cells are left blank. The table has five columns: the sum of squares, the degrees of freedom, the mean square, and then F, and then p. By the end of this lecture you will be able to fill the cells needed to read F and p, and you will know why some Total-row cells stay blank here. Let us fill it in. [NEXT]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
section: Degrees of freedom: k − 1 and n − k
text:
Degrees of freedom: k − 1 and n − k









Figure 2














Figure 3















Figure 4








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~84s] The two sums of squares are already in place, because you built them in the previous lecture. Let us therefore move straight to the column that students most often get wrong, the degrees of freedom. [CLICK 1] Let us call k the number of groups, which here is three. The between-groups degrees of freedom is k minus one, which gives two. The reason we lose one is that the degrees of freedom count how many group means are free to vary once the grand mean is fixed. Once the overall mean is held in place, the final group mean is determined by the others, so it is not free to move. [CLICK 2] The within-groups degrees of freedom is n minus k, that is eleven ninety minus three, which gives eleven eighty-seven. The total degrees of freedom is n minus one, which gives eleven eighty-nine. One footnote is worth noting. The course notes count the dummy variables instead of the groups, and three groups produce two dummies, which are exactly the same two degrees of freedom. Either way of counting lands in the same place. [NEXT]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
section: Mean square is SS over its df
text:
Mean square is SS over its df









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~45s] These two sums of squares cannot be compared directly, because one rests on two degrees of freedom while the other rests on eleven eighty-seven. So we put them on equal footing. [CLICK 1] A mean square is simply a sum of squares divided by its own degrees of freedom. We do this for each row. The between mean square, written M-M-S, comes to four thousand seven forty-one point three. The within mean square, written M-S-E, comes to eleven ninety-two point one, and that within mean square is the pooled within-group variance. We now have two numbers on the same scale. [NEXT]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
text:
F, and where it lands









Figure 7














Figure 8















Figure 9








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 2 · ~82s] Now we reach the headline of the table. The F statistic is the between mean square divided by the within mean square, that is M-M-S over M-S-E, which you can read as signal over noise. Carrying out that division gives F equal to three point nine seven seven. I am leaving the p cell blank for one more beat, because p only makes sense after we compare F to its reference curve. [CLICK 1] This reference curve is the F-distribution on two and eleven eighty-seven degrees of freedom, under H0 and the usual one-way ANOVA assumptions: independent observations, roughly normal residuals within groups, and similar within-group variances. Under those conditions, the signal and the noise are on average the same size, so F clusters near one. The shaded right tail is the rejection region at the five percent level. Our observed F, marked by the dashed line, lands inside that shaded region, and the right-tail area is zero point zero one nine. [CLICK 2] Now that tail area becomes the p-value in the table. Because zero point zero one nine is below five percent, we reject the null hypothesis, with the usual assumptions in the background. [NEXT]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
text:
One null, two notations
The null hypothesis F tests
\[ H_0:\ \beta_{\text{Medium}} = \beta_{\text{High}} = 0 \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad \mu_{\text{Low}} = \mu_{\text{Medium}} = \mu_{\text{High}} \]


Regression voice: both non-reference dummy coefficients are zero. ANOVA voice: all three group means are equal. Same null. Reject it, and the means are not all equal.



[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~48s] Let us be precise about what we just rejected. For this three-group model, the null hypothesis can be written in two concrete ways. On the left is the regression form: the Medium dummy coefficient and the High dummy coefficient are both zero. On the right is the ANOVA form: the Low, Medium, and High group means are all equal. It is one statement written in two notations. [CLICK 1] Now consider what rejecting it means. We have rejected the claim that all three means are equal. Here is the subtle point: not all equal does not mean every pair is different. It only means at least one pair differs. Keep that phrase in mind. [NEXT]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
text:
“So all three groups differ?”
A colleague reads the same table

“F is significant — so all three prep levels differ from each other.”









What a significant F does NOT say


A significant F means the group means are not all equal — equivalently, at least one pair differs. NOT which pair · NOT that all pairs differ.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~79s] Here is the trap that people fall into the moment an F-test comes back significant. Imagine a colleague glancing at our table and concluding that, because F is significant, all three preparation levels differ from one another. The conclusion sounds reasonable, but it is wrong. The colleague has quietly turned not all group means are equal into all pairs are different, and the F-test never said that. [CLICK 1] Here is the careful reading. A significant F tells you that the means are not all equal, equivalently that at least one pair differs, and that is all it tells you. It does not tell you which pair differs, and it does not tell you that every pair differs. For example, it is entirely possible that Medium differs from Low while High and Low are essentially tied. Finding out which groups actually differ requires follow-up comparisons, and that is exactly where we are headed next. [NEXT]

title: Reading the ANOVA Table — and What F Doesn’t Say
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway
