Read the table left to right.





df/MS: df = k − 1 between groups and n − k within groups; MS = SS ÷ its own df.


F/p: F = MMS ÷ MSE = signal ÷ noise; p is the right-tail area under H0 plus the usual assumptions.


Omnibus: a small p rejects the null that all group means are equal, but it does not say which pair differs or that all pairs differ.

Next: Same F, two printouts — ANOVA is regression. Notes, Ch 3 §The ANOVA Table · Practice Quiz 4

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 3 · ~72s] Let us gather the main ideas to carry forward. Read the table from left to right. [CLICK 1] First, degrees of freedom and mean squares. The between row uses k minus one; the within row uses n minus k. Each mean square is that row’s sum of squares divided by its own degrees of freedom. [CLICK 2] Second, F and p. F is the between mean square over the within mean square, which is signal over noise. The p-value is the area in the tail beyond that F, using the F reference distribution under the null and the usual assumptions. [CLICK 3] Third, interpretation. A small p rejects the null that all group means are equal, but F is an omnibus verdict. It does not say which pair differs, and it does not say that all pairs differ. In the next lecture, we will see that same F appear again, this time read straight from the regression printout. The notes and the practice quiz are linked just below. [END]

title: Same F, Two Printouts: ANOVA Is Regression
section: Session 3’s trick, now with three groups
text:
Session 3’s trick, now with three groups

Continuity payoff

Back in Session 3, one trick: the pooled two-sample t-test was a regression on a 0/1 dummy.


Two groups. But what about three? Same trick — just a bigger design matrix.


[DECK ch03-05 · 6 min · ~800 words · clicks 6 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Let us pick up a thread from Session three. The lesson there was that the pooled two-sample t-test you learned earlier was in fact a regression, fitted on a single zero-one dummy variable that simply marked which of the two groups each observation belonged to. [CLICK 1] That example involved only two groups, but now you have three: Low, Medium, and High teacher preparation. You might expect that three groups call for a brand-new test, but they do not. Instead of a new test, you simply need a larger design matrix. One additional dummy variable carries the same idea forward, and we will work through it together. [NEXT]

title: Same F, Two Printouts: ANOVA Is Regression
text:
“Surely they could disagree?”
A fair objection

“ANOVA tests group means. Regression fits coefficients. Two different procedures — surely they could give different answers.”



No. lm(mathgain ~ prep_level) is the one-way ANOVA — same fit, two printouts.



[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 1 · ~53s] Here is a reasonable objection. The ANOVA procedure tests whether the group means are equal, while regression fits coefficients. Because the two use such different language, it is natural to suspect that they could give different answers. [CLICK 1] That suspicion is mistaken, because there are not two procedures at work here. There is a single fit, the linear model of math gain on preparation level, and there are two ways to print it. When you call anova on that fit, you obtain the ANOVA table. When you call summary on the very same fit, you obtain the regression output. The model underneath is the same fitted model, so the two cannot disagree. Let us confirm this side by side. [NEXT]

title: Same F, Two Printouts: ANOVA Is Regression
text:
Two printouts, four matching numbers









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 2 · ~78s] This is one fit shown in two layouts: the anova table on the left and the regression summary on the right. Notice how the rows line up, because there are four values to compare. [CLICK 1] Consider the headline statistics first. The F value, three point nine seven seven, appears on the anova side and again on the regression’s F-line. The p-value, zero point zero one nine, is likewise the same on both. These are not merely close to each other; they are the exact same test reported twice. Let that row comparison settle before we move to the quieter pair. [CLICK 2] Now consider the quieter pair, and notice the dashes. This is not another left-right equality row. The error sum of squares, one million, four hundred fifteen thousand, seventy-eight, is printed on the anova side. The Root MSE, thirty-four point five three, is printed on the summary side. These may look unrelated, yet they describe the same residual scatter on different scales. Recall that the Root MSE is simply the square root of the MSE, and the MSE is the error sum of squares divided by its degrees of freedom. That is four numbers from one model. [NEXT]

title: Same F, Two Printouts: ANOVA Is Regression
section: Why? The F tests all the dummies at once
text:
Why? The F tests all the dummies at once









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Let us see why the two printouts must match by looking closely at what the F actually tests. Three groups give you two dummy variables, Medium and High, with Low serving as the reference. The Medium coefficient is the Medium-minus-Low difference, and the High coefficient is the High-minus-Low difference. The omnibus F does not test these dummies one at a time. In the regression voice, it poses a single joint question: are all of the dummy coefficients equal to zero, every one of them at once? [CLICK 1] Now read that same question in the ANOVA voice. If every dummy coefficient is zero, then no group differs from the reference, which is exactly the statement that every group mean is equal. This means it is one statement written in two notations, that is, one test carrying two names. Recall as well that this Root MSE is the pooled within-group standard deviation, the same quantity you first met back in Session one. [NEXT]

title: Same F, Two Printouts: ANOVA Is Regression
section: One trick, three links — link 2 is yours
text:
One trick, three links — link 2 is yours









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~45s] Recall this equivalence chain from Session three. Here it is again. Link one, which states that the t-test equals the dummy regression, is already established. [CLICK 1] Today you have lit link two: the one-way ANOVA F is the dummy regression with three groups or more. The F and p match across the two printouts, and the SSE and Root MSE are residual summaries from that same fit. That is two links completed. The grey box, which represents two-way ANOVA and interactions in Session eight, is a promise for a later lecture. The trick stays the same every time; you simply keep widening the design matrix. [NEXT]

title: Same F, Two Printouts: ANOVA Is Regression
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


One-way ANOVA is a dummy regression — one fit, two printouts. The F and p match, while error SS and Root MSE are linked residual summaries from the same fit. The omnibus F tests all the dummy coefficients = 0 at once, which is “all group means equal.” And Root MSE is the pooled within-group SD — the same one from Session 1. You never needed a new test — just a bigger design matrix.




Next: After a significant F — which pairs actually differ? Notes, Ch 3 §The ANOVA Table · Practice Quiz 4

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~54s] Let us close with the essentials. One-way ANOVA is a dummy regression, a single model printed two ways. The F and p match across those printouts, and the error sum of squares and Root MSE are linked residual summaries from the same fit. The omnibus F tests whether every dummy coefficient equals zero jointly, which is the same as stating that all group means are equal. That Root MSE is the pooled within-group standard deviation, the same one from Session one. You never needed a new test, only a larger design matrix. Notice, however, that the F tells you only that the means are not all equal, so in the next lecture we will ask which pairs actually differ. [END]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
text:
“More tests, more wins”?

A colleague, fishing

“The F was significant — so let’s just test every pair and report whatever pops.”


Test six groups pairwise — that’s fifteen comparisons — and you’ll “discover” something about 54% of the time. From pure noise.


[DECK ch03-06 · 8 min · ~1041 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~57s] In the previous lecture, your omnibus F test came back significant. Recall that this result tells you at least one group mean differs from the others, but it does not tell you which pair is responsible. A colleague now leans over with a tempting shortcut, suggesting that since the F was significant, you should simply test every pair and report whatever turns up. The reasoning seems to be that more tests will buy you more discoveries. [CLICK 1] Here is the catch. If those fifteen tests behaved like independent five-percent tests, the benchmark chance of at least one false alarm would be about fifty-four percent, even when the data are pure noise. Pairwise tests that share groups are dependent, so the exact rate is not that formula, but the warning survives: many uncorrected tests create false alarms. More tests do not buy you truth; they buy you opportunities to fool yourself. [NEXT]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
section: Every test is another coin flip
text:
Every test is another coin flip









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~75s] Let us look at the engine that drives this problem. Each individual test runs at a five percent error rate, so the independent-tests benchmark asks a simple question: if you ran m independent five-percent tests, what is the chance that at least one cries wolf? That benchmark is one minus zero point nine five raised to the power m. With three groups you have three pairs, and the benchmark is already fourteen percent. [CLICK 1] With six groups you have fifteen pairs, and the independent benchmark climbs to fifty-four percent. In actual pairwise comparisons, the tests share groups, so they are dependent and the exact family-wise error rate can differ. But the teaching point stays the same: uncorrected all-pairs testing inflates false alarms. [CLICK 2] When you connect the dots, the independent benchmark races past ninety percent by the time you reach eleven groups. Notice the point that many people miss: the inflation is driven by the number of tests you run, not by the size or quality of your data. [NEXT]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
section: Watch the false alarms litter the grid
text:
Watch the false alarms litter the grid









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~65s] Let us make that idea concrete with a simulation. Here are ten groups and forty-five pairs, with one square for each pair. The key detail is that all ten groups were drawn from the exact same population, so there are zero real differences between them. As a result, every rust square you see is a guaranteed false alarm. The subtitle gives the independent-tests benchmark, about ninety percent. Because these pairwise tests share group means, a repeated shared-group simulation gives a lower exact rate, but it is still far above five percent. Either way, uncorrected all-pairs testing is not a five-percent procedure. [CLICK 1] Now apply the Bonferroni correction to the very same noise. The rust disappears in this run, and every square turns teal. The goal is not to promise that every corrected run is empty; the goal is to keep the family error target near five percent by accounting for how many tests we ran. [NEXT]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
text:
The fix: raise the bar
Pick a correction, then read the adjusted table

Bonferroni · test each pair at α / m — five percent, split across the m tests Sidak · Tukey · Holm — same goal, sharper bookkeeping









The trap that catches everyone


A bigger sample does NOT fix this. The family-wise error rate rides on the number of tests, not on n — fifteen tests are fifteen tests whether you have fifty students or fifty thousand.






[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us see how a correction actually works. The most straightforward method is Bonferroni. You begin with a five percent error budget for the whole family of tests, and then you split it among them. With m comparisons, you judge each pair at alpha divided by m, which is a smaller and tougher threshold. Because each test is harder to pass, fewer false alarms slip through. The Sidak, Tukey, and Holm methods pursue the same goal with sharper bookkeeping. [CLICK 1] Now consider the trap that catches almost everyone. A bigger sample does not rescue you here. The error rate depends on how many tests you run, not on how much data you have. Fifteen uncorrected pairs are fifteen tests whether you have fifty students or fifty thousand, so collecting more data does nothing to lower that rate. [NEXT]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
section: Read the adjusted table: who survives?
text:
Read the adjusted table: who survives?
Pairwise on our prep groups · Sidak-adjusted (emmeans)









Figure 6














Figure 7















Figure 8








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~68s] Here is the payoff: all three pairwise contrasts on our prep groups, computed through emmeans. Look first at Medium versus Low. The gap is plus seven point four eight points, and its raw, unadjusted p-value is zero point zero one zero. That looks solid on its own, but the header is deliberately raw p, because this is not the value we should report after looking across the family. [CLICK 1] Now read the column that actually matters for exploratory all-pairs follow-up: the Sidak-adjusted p-value. After adjustment, Medium versus Low rises to zero point zero two nine. High versus Low becomes zero point four oh one, and High versus Medium becomes zero point nine four three. [CLICK 2] Now we see which pairs survive. Only Medium versus Low holds up under the five percent bar, and the other two drop out. The F test told us that something differs; now we know it is Medium standing ahead of Low. The F test opened the door, and the table named the room. [NEXT]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
text:
Go try it yourself

The multiple-comparisons app on simulated data — every group is drawn from one common distribution, so every null is true and every flagged pair is a known false positive. The screenshot shows k = 5, ten comparisons, an expected uncorrected benchmark of 40.1%, and an observed 100-run FWER of 23%; the strip below shows one column per simulation, and rust ticks are false alarms. (simulated data)Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 0 · ~54s] You do not have to take my word for this, because a live app in the chapter lets you see it yourself. It runs on simulated data in which every group secretly shares one distribution, so nothing is ever truly different and every flag is a known mistake. This screenshot shows five groups, which means ten comparisons. The app’s independent benchmark is forty point one percent, and this particular one-hundred-run display observed twenty-three percent. Those numbers will bounce from run to run because the app is simulating. Try changing the number of groups and then switching from None to Bonferroni or Holm; the point to watch is how correction pulls the family error rate back toward the intended target. [NEXT]

title: After a Significant F: Which Pairs?
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


For exploratory all-pairs follow-up, start with the omnibus F, then read corrected pairwise comparisons. Uncorrected, the independent benchmark balloons — 3 pairs is 14%, 15 pairs is 54% — so apply a correction (Sidak / Bonferroni / Tukey / Holm, with Bonferroni testing each pair at α / m) and read the adjusted p-values. For our prep groups, only Medium − Low survives (Sidak p = 0.029).





Three traps to leave behind: more tests means more discovery (it means more false alarms) · a big sample fixes the error rate (it doesn’t — the count of tests does) · the omnibus F already told us which pairs (it never did). Pre-planned contrasts can be tested directly when the family and error-control plan are defined before looking at the results.
Next: What significance does — and doesn’t — say. Notes, Ch 3 §Pairwise Comparisons · Practice Quiz 4


[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 1 · ~66s] Let us close with the points worth carrying with you. For exploratory all-pairs follow-up, the omnibus F opens the door, and corrected pairwise comparisons name the pairs. Without correction, the independent benchmark balloons, since three pairs already reach fourteen percent and fifteen pairs reach fifty-four percent. For that reason, you should apply a correction such as Sidak or Bonferroni and read the adjusted p-values. On our data, only Medium beats Low. If a contrast was planned before looking at the results, you can test it directly within the family and error-control plan you defined in advance. [CLICK 1] Finally, leave three myths behind: more tests do not mean more discovery, a bigger sample does not fix the error rate because the count of tests does, and the omnibus F never told you which pairs differ. In the next lecture, we will examine what a significant result really does, and does not, say. [END]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
section: p below point-oh-five — so that’s enough, right?
text:
p below point-oh-five — so that’s enough, right?

The trap

The omnibus F came back significant — F(2, 1187) = 3.977, p = 0.019, below .05. So the group means are detectably different — but is the effect important?



Hold on. The effect size: η² = 0.0067. Negligible.



[DECK ch03-07 · 8 min · ~1070 words · clicks 11 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~57s] This is the last lecture in Chapter Three, and it carries an important twist. Our omnibus F-test came back significant, with an F value of three point nine seven seven and a p-value of zero point zero one nine, which falls below point-oh-five. The narrow test conclusion is that the group means are not all equal. The tempting overread is to treat that as proof that the effect is important. [CLICK 1] Before we accept that overread, let us look at the effect size, which is eta-squared, equal to zero point zero zero six seven. By the usual standards, that value is negligible. The result is therefore statistically detectable and yet tiny at the same time, and the purpose of this lecture is to explain how both of those statements can be true together. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
text:
The sliver that “matters”









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~61s] This is the picture that the p-value never shows you. The whole bar represents all of the spread in math gain, that is, the total variation we are trying to account for. Recall the idea of R-squared, because it asks the very same question here: how much of that spread do the groups actually explain? [CLICK 1] When we slice the bar by preparation level, the thin slate sliver is everything the groups account for, which is just zero point seven percent. The large rust portion, at ninety-nine point three percent, is variation among people within the same group. That small sliver has a name: it is eta-squared, which in this one-way setting equals R-squared exactly, and its value is zero point zero zero six seven. The effect is statistically significant and yet almost invisible. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
text:
Eta-squared is R-squared
\[ \eta^2 = \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{between}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{total}}} = R^2 \qquad (\text{one-way ANOVA}) \]

p asks is there evidence that at least one mean differs? · η² = R² asks how big? — two different questions.









One caveat


In factorial designs (two-plus factors), you may see partial η² = SS_effect / (SS_effect + SS_error), so partial η²s aren’t additive.






[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 2 · ~80s] Let us make the connection between these two ideas explicit. The sliver we just saw, eta-squared, is defined as the between-group spread divided by the total spread. That ratio is precisely R squared, the explained share of variance you met back in Chapter One. It is one single number wearing two different names. [CLICK 1] This reveals the split that really matters. The p-value asks whether the data provide evidence that at least one population mean differs. Eta-squared, which is R squared here, asks how much variance prep level explains. A small p-value never speaks to the second question, so you must not let one of these numbers stand in for the other. [CLICK 2] Here is one caveat to keep for later. When a design has two or more factors, you may see partial eta-squared, where the denominator is SS effect plus SS error. It isolates one effect relative to that effect plus error, so partial eta-squared values do not add up cleanly across effects. Today we have only one factor, so eta-squared and R squared are the same quantity. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
text:
The diamond lands in “Negligible”









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~73s] The natural next question is where this effect falls on a standard ruler of effect sizes. These are Cohen’s bands for eta-squared, which mark off conventional ranges. An effect below point-oh-one counts as negligible, from there up to point-oh-six is small, then comes the medium range, and anything past point-one-four is considered large. [CLICK 1] Now we drop our own effect onto the ruler. At zero point zero zero six seven, the diamond lands over here, well inside the negligible region, not even reaching the small band. So the F-test says there is evidence of a difference, while eta-squared says the difference explains less than one percent of the variation. That is statistical significance without practical importance. One honest qualification is worth adding: in education, a small effect can still matter when the program is inexpensive or easy to scale. This particular effect, however, sits below even the small band, so it is genuinely tiny. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
section: Four things p does NOT say
text:
Four things p does NOT say
Don’t let a small p tell you…


❌ a big effect — size is η² = R², not p




❌ the groups are equal when n.s. — absence of evidence ≠ evidence of absence




❌ every pair differs — a significant omnibus = at least one differs, not all     ❌ causation — significance is not a causal claim



[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 3 · ~70s] Let us now name the traps directly, the four things that a small p-value does not license you to claim. [CLICK 1] The first is a large effect. We have just seen that the result is significant and yet eta-squared is only zero point zero zero six seven. For the size of an effect, you read eta-squared, never the p-value. [CLICK 2] The second is the flip side of the first. A non-significant result does not mean that the groups are equal. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence, which means you have failed to detect a difference, not that you have proven there is none. [CLICK 3] The third is that a significant omnibus test tells you at least one mean differs, not that every pair of means differs. And the fourth is that significance is not causation: these groups were not randomly assigned, so even a real difference would not prove that preparation caused it. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
text:
Report it honestly
Instead of a lone p-value or a row of stars…


Report the estimate + a confidence interval + an effect size.



The p-value tells how incompatible the data are with a no-difference model. These three tell direction, size, and precision.


[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 2 · ~53s] Given all of this, the natural question is what you should report in place of a bare p-value, and certainly in place of a row of little significance stars. [CLICK 1] The recommendation is to report three things every time. Report the estimate, which states how large the difference actually is. Report a confidence interval, which expresses your uncertainty about that estimate. And report an effect size, such as eta-squared, which places the result on a practical scale. [CLICK 2] The p-value tells you how incompatible the data are with a no-difference model under the test assumptions. These three quantities together tell you the direction of the estimate, the size of the effect, and the precision of the evidence. That is the difference between printing a star and reporting a genuine finding. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
text:
Why big samples fool you
The mechanism behind the trap

A bigger sample shrinks the noise in the test — so even a trivial nonzero effect can look “significant.”









Tiny effect, giant sample


In a large synthetic study with thousands of students, a group difference could explain less than 1% of variance and still produce a tiny p-value. A giant sample makes tiny nonzero effects easier to detect; it never makes them important.






[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~56s] One reason a negligible effect can register as significant is sample size. A larger sample tightens the test by reducing the noise in the estimate, so a tiny nonzero difference can become easy to detect. [CLICK 1] Picture a synthetic study with thousands of students. Even if group membership explained less than one percent of the variance, the p-value could still become very small. Notice the lesson: the effect size can remain tiny while the evidence against exact equality becomes strong. A giant sample makes tiny effects easier to detect, but it never makes them important. [NEXT]

title: What Significance Does (and Doesn’t) Say
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A p-value answers one question: are the data hard to reconcile with equal means, under the test assumptions? It does not measure size — that’s η² = R² — does not prove groups are equal when non-significant, and does not license all-pairs or causal claims. Our own F is significant (p = 0.019) yet η² = 0.0067, negligible: significance without importance. Report an estimate + CI + effect size, never a lone p.




Next: Chapter 4 — continuous predictors and multiple regression. Notes, Ch 3 §Effect Size Measures · Practice Quiz 4

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~56s] Take this rule with you: significant means the data are hard to reconcile with equal means under the test assumptions; it does not mean important. In our example, p equals zero point zero one nine cleared the test, but eta-squared was only zero point zero zero six seven, so prep level explained less than one percent of the variation in math gain. When you report a result, give the estimate, the confidence interval, and the effect size. The p-value earns one sentence; the effect size carries the interpretation. Next, we leave group predictors and move to continuous predictors and multiple regression. [END]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
text:
A table, no data attached



Figure 1

[DECK ch04-01 · 6 min · ~826 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~36s] Much of published research arrives in exactly this form, a regression table on the screen. This is a published-style faculty-pay table adapted for reading practice, with salary measured in dollars. Notice that no dataset is attached, so you cannot refit this model yourself. All that you are given is this grid of numbers, and your task is to read it. That is the skill for today, namely to approach a published-style table cold and state what every number means. [NEXT]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
text:
Anatomy of the table









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Almost every table presents roughly six things, in about this order, so let us walk down through them together. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] First is N, the sample size, which here is one thousand two hundred five. Second is R-squared, zero point four one, the share of the variation in salary that the model explains. Third is a coefficient, and here we will use the Campus type row. Fourth is its standard error, sitting right beside it, which measures the uncertainty in that estimate. Fifth are the stars, which flag statistical significance. Sixth is the reference group, given in the footnote as Campus type equals flagship. Hold on to that last item, because it sets what every dummy variable is compared against. [NEXT]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
text:
The continuous template
\[\small \widehat{\text{salary}} = \alpha + \beta_1(\text{experience}) + \beta_2(\text{teaching score}) + \dots + \beta_k(\text{campus type})\]
How to read a continuous row


For each additional [unit of x], predicted [y] differs by [slope] [units], on average, holding the other predictors fixed.




For each additional 1 year of experience, predicted salary differs by $162.40, on average, holding the other predictors fixed.



[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~65s] Now let us read one row. Behind the table sits a single equation: predicted salary equals an intercept, plus a slope on experience, plus a slope on productivity, and so on. Each slope is one column that you read. This is where Chapters two and three pay off. Recall that controlling for a variable means a comparison at the same value of x, and that a coefficient is a difference. [CLICK 1] So here is the template you already know, with one phrase added on the end: holding the other predictors fixed. That makes it an adjusted association, not a bare effect. [CLICK 2] Now pour in the experience row. For each additional one year of experience, predicted salary differs by a hundred sixty-two dollars and forty cents, on average, holding the other predictors fixed. If you drop that clause, the reading is simply wrong. [NEXT]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
section: The dummy template — mind the reference
text:
The dummy template — mind the reference

The [group] is predicted [coef] [units] [higher/lower] than the [reference], holding the others fixed.



Regional-campus faculty are predicted $1,420.50 lower than flagship-campus faculty (the reference), holding the others fixed.










The dummy speaks only to its reference


−$1,420.50 is regional vs flagship — because flagship is the reference. No reference, no meaning. (Chapter 3: a coefficient is a difference from the reference.)






[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 2 · ~55s] A dummy variable reads a little differently, because it describes a gap between two groups rather than a per-unit slope. For that reason it gets its own card, and the generic template is already on screen. [CLICK 1] Let us fill it in. Regional-campus faculty are predicted one thousand four hundred twenty dollars and fifty cents lower than flagship-campus faculty, the reference group, holding the others fixed. This is the same backbone as the slope, a difference, now phrased for two groups. [CLICK 2] A negative value means below the reference. The reading carries no meaning until you know who the reference is, exactly as in Chapter three, where a dummy speaks only to its baseline. No reference, no meaning. [NEXT]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
text:
Two traps


Figure 4







Trap 1


Dollars, not percent. $162.40 is dollars per year, never a percentage.












Trap 2


The sign rides the coding. Recode Campus type as 1 = flagship and −$1,420.50 becomes +$1,420.50 — same adjusted comparison, regional still predicted below the flagship reference.






[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~62s] There are two traps to watch for here. The first trap is dollars, not percent. Because the outcome is salary measured in dollars, the experience coefficient is in dollars per year, and not a percentage raise. For a continuous slope, the units are y-units per one x-unit; for a dummy gap, the units are y-units. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] The second trap is that the sign rides the coding. The left bar shows the table coding, where one means regional campus, and the value is negative, so regional-campus faculty are predicted below the flagship reference. Now recode so that one means flagship campus. The bar flips to the very same amount, now positive. The adjusted comparison is unchanged, and regional-campus faculty are still predicted lower than the flagship reference. Only the label has moved. For that reason you must read the sign and the coding together, or you will state the result backward. [NEXT]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
text:
Read one, cold
The Teaching score coefficient: $487.20, *** (p < .001).


For each additional teaching-score unit, predicted salary differs by $487.20, on average, holding the other predictors fixed.










Stars ≠ effect size


The stars say $487.20 is distinguishable from zero — not that it’s big or important. Tiny $162.40 also wears ***. Significance is not magnitude. (Recall ML-03.7.)






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 2 · ~52s] Now it is your turn to read one cold. Consider the teaching-score row, which carries three stars. [CLICK 1] Apply the continuous template. For each additional teaching-score unit, predicted salary differs by four hundred eighty-seven dollars and twenty cents, on average, holding the others fixed. That is a clean read. [CLICK 2] Now consider the last trap, the stars. Three stars can feel as though this coefficient is the large one, but it is not. Notice that experience, a smaller hundred sixty-two dollars and forty cents, also carries three stars. The stars say only that the coefficient is distinguishable from zero, which is significance, not size. Use stars for evidence against zero, not for practical size. [NEXT]

title: Reading a Published Regression Table
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Published-table coefficient = adjusted association.
Continuous row: predicted y change per unit of x.
Dummy row: gap from the reference.
Name units, coding, and stars separately.
For this observational table, read the coefficient as an adjusted association, not as a causal claim.




Next: More education, more crime? The sign reversal. Notes, Ch 4 §The Model

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Let us close with the points to carry forward. A coefficient in a published-style table is an adjusted association: per-unit for a continuous predictor and a gap from the reference for a dummy, always holding the other predictors fixed. Read continuous slopes in y-units per x-unit, read dummy gaps in y-units, read the sign together with the coding, and never let the stars stand in for size. For this observational table, read the coefficient as an adjusted association, not as a causal claim. You can do all of this from the table alone. In the next lecture, we will meet a coefficient that does not merely shrink, but flips its sign. [END]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
text:
More education, more crime?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch04-02 · 7 min · ~886 words · clicks 6 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~51s] This is data from sixty-seven Florida counties, and each navy dot represents one county. The horizontal axis shows the county’s education, measured as its share of high-school graduates, and the vertical axis shows its crime rate. Let us fit the simplest possible line and read what it tells us. [CLICK 1] The line slopes upward, with a slope of plus one point four nine, which is positive. Read carelessly, it sounds as if more schooling itself raises crime. That causal reading is our warning sign. Something here is misleading us, and the rest of this lecture explains what. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
section: Who else is in the room?
text:
Who else is in the room?









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Recall the lurker from Session four. A confounder distorts a relationship only when it touches both sides of it, so let us go looking for one. On the screen we have education and crime, joined by that puzzling positive link. [CLICK 1] Here is the suspect, urbanization. Urban counties tend to have more schools, so urbanization and education move together, with a correlation of zero point seven nine, which is strong. Urban counties also tend to have more crime, so that link is positive as well. Because urbanization reaches both education and crime, it is a textbook confounder, and it has been hiding inside that simple slope all along. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
section: Control for it — the sign flips
text:
Control for it — the sign flips









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~51s] Let us do what Session four told us to do, and put urbanization into the model. First, here is where we began: education against crime with urbanization ignored, giving that rust slope of plus one point four nine. [CLICK 1] Now we strip urbanization out of both variables, which is how we hold it fixed, and we refit the line. Notice how the line rolls over. The slope is now minus zero point five eight, pointing downward. The counties are the same and only one variable has been added, yet the sign has reversed. This is not a glitch; it is what omitting a confounder can do when the bias is strong enough to overwhelm the adjusted relationship. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
section: See the flip with its error bars
text:
See the flip with its error bars









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~65s] This is the picture worth fixing in your memory. Each dot is the education coefficient, and each whisker is its ninety-five percent confidence interval, the range of values that are consistent with the data. The dashed line marks zero, the divide between a positive and a negative coefficient. At the top sits the bivariate estimate, the one that omits urbanization, shown in rust. [CLICK 1] Below it is the adjusted estimate, shown in teal. Notice where each one lands. The bivariate interval sits entirely above zero, while the adjusted interval crosses zero, so it no longer points clearly in either direction. In a single picture you can see the whole story, as the estimate jumps across the zero line, driven by a confounder strong enough to reverse a sign. Keep your eye on that teal bar, because it matters in a moment. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
text:
The backdoor path









Figure 9














Figure 10








[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Here is why this happens. If this diagram is correct, urbanization pushes education up and pushes crime up, which are the two slate arrows. That creates an indirect path running from education, back through urbanization, and over to crime, which we call a backdoor path. The simple slope walks straight through that path and mistakes it for the coefficient we meant to isolate. The direct arrow from education to crime is drawn as negative, but notice that the diagram only posits this; it is an assumption about how the world works, not something these data have proven. [CLICK 1] Conditioning on urbanization blocks that backdoor, which is the gold band. Once the path is shut, what remains is the adjusted slope. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
text:
Negative — but be honest









Figure 11














Figure 12








[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~51s] This is the part that is most often misread, so let us state it plainly. Yes, the adjusted slope is negative, at minus zero point five eight. Its p-value, however, is zero point two two, so it is not statistically significant. Treat the sign flip as evidence that the positive bivariate association was confounded, not as reliable evidence of a negative adjusted association. [CLICK 1] Here is the honest claim, and only this. The win is not that education lowers crime, because we did not show that. The win is that the misleading positive sign disappeared once we controlled for urbanization. If you swap those two claims, you simply trade one wrong conclusion for another. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
section: Go try it: turn the confounder on
text:
Go try it: turn the confounder on

The omitted-variable-bias explorer in its default state: Include Z is unchecked, the simulated scatter shows the biased education-only fit, and the readout pills report the true slope, biased slope, and OVB amount.Simulated data. This screenshot is the starting state; create the sign reversal in the course notes.

[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 0 · ~39s] Here is the explorer from the chapter, and it is worth trying yourself. This screenshot shows the starting state: the confounder is still left out, so you see the biased education-only fit. It is simulated on purpose, because to demonstrate a sign reversal you need to set the true X-to-Y slope and dial the confounder’s strength, and those are knobs that no real dataset gives you. In the live version, change the settings until the biased slope points the wrong way, then turn Include Z on to recover the adjusted slope. The screenshot starts the experiment; the live toggle shows the reversal. [NEXT]

title: More Education, More Crime? The Sign Reversal
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway — name the trap


Never trust a bivariate sign for an adjusted question. A simple slope can point the wrong way when a confounder is omitted — here, ignoring urbanization made the education coefficient look positive (+1.49); controlling for it flipped the sign (−0.58). The omitted-variable logic from Session 4 warns why this can happen: urbanization is positively tied to both education and crime, so leaving it out biases the slope upward. And the honest read: the misleading positive sign disappears — this is not a finding that education lowers crime (adjusted p = 0.22, not statistically significant). Same mechanism returns with a categorical predictor later — that is Simpson’s paradox.




Next: “Holding constant,” geometrically — added-variable plots. Notes, Ch 4 §The Sign Reversal

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~38s] Let us carry this lesson with us. Never trust a bivariate sign when your question requires adjustment, because a left-out confounder tied to both sides can bias it, and when the bias is strong enough it can even flip it, here from plus one point four nine to minus zero point five eight. At the same time, stay honest, because the positive sign vanished but this is not a claim that education lowers crime. Later, this same mechanism returns under the name Simpson’s paradox. Next, we make holding constant visible through added-variable plots. [END]

title: Holding Constant, Geometrically: Added-Variable Plots
text:
What does “holding urbanization constant” DO?



Figure 1

[DECK ch04-03 · 7 min · ~903 words · clicks 4 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 0 · ~96s] In the previous lecture, the slope on education changed sign once we added urbanization to the model, moving from positive to negative, and we watched that reversal happen in a plot of residuals against residuals. That plot has a name: an added-variable plot. Today we will learn to read it. Look carefully at this cloud of points. It shows the same crime and education as before, but now each point is shaded by how urban its county is, with grey marking the more rural counties and rust marking the more urban ones. The shading varies as you move up the crime axis and also as you move across the education axis. That tells us urbanization is mixed into both the outcome and the predictor. Holding it constant does not mean deleting counties or pretending urbanization vanished. In the OLS geometry, it means removing the part linearly associated with urbanization from each variable, then comparing what remains. That is the whole recipe: two residuals and one slope. Let us build it step by step. [NEXT]

title: Holding Constant, Geometrically: Added-Variable Plots
section: Step 1: strip urbanization out of crime
text:
Step 1: strip urbanization out of crime









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 1 · ~72s] Step one is to strip urbanization out of crime. For the moment, set education aside entirely and look only at crime and urbanization. We fit a straight line through these points, and that rust line represents the part of a county’s crime that urbanization alone can account for, since more urban counties tend to have more crime. [CLICK 1] Now comes the key move. We keep the vertical gap between each county and the fitted line, then re-center those gaps on a horizontal axis at zero. Each gap is a residual, and together they form a new column of points. This new cloud is crime with urbanization removed, meaning the part of crime left after accounting for urbanization linearly. The notes write this as crime given urbanization, with a vertical bar, and that bar simply means after removing. Step one is done, and we have completed our first subtraction. [NEXT]

title: Holding Constant, Geometrically: Added-Variable Plots
section: Step 2: strip urbanization out of education
text:
Step 2: strip urbanization out of education









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~78s] Step two repeats the same move, but now on education instead of crime. We regress education on urbanization, which gives us that rust line once again. The reason is that more urban counties also tend to have more education, so part of a county’s education is linearly associated with urbanization, and we want to remove that shared part as well. [CLICK 1] We apply the same move: keep the residuals and re-center them around zero. What remains is education with urbanization removed, written as education given urbanization, using that same vertical bar. Read the bar carefully, because it means after removing, and it does not mean division. In the usual coefficient interpretation, holding urbanization fixed means comparing counties at the same urbanization level. In this OLS picture, that same idea is represented by removing the part of each variable linearly associated with urbanization, then comparing what remains. Both subtractions are now complete, so we are ready to plot the results against each other. [NEXT]

title: Holding Constant, Geometrically: Added-Variable Plots
section: Step 3: plot the leftovers — the slope IS the coefficient
text:
Step 3: plot the leftovers — the slope IS the coefficient









Figure 6














Figure 7















Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 2 · ~85s] Step three is where the reasoning pays off. We take the two piles of leftovers and plot them against each other, placing crime given urbanization up the vertical side and education given urbanization across the bottom. These are nothing but the residuals from the two earlier steps, and both clouds sit centered on zero because residuals always average to zero. This picture is the added-variable plot. [CLICK 1] Now we fit a single line through these residuals. Its slope is minus zero point five eight, and the line tips downward, which tells us that once urbanization has been removed from both variables, higher residualized education is associated with lower residualized crime. [CLICK 2] Here is the result worth holding onto. That slope is exactly the coefficient on education from the full multiple regression. It is not merely close; it is identical to machine precision, and the Match badge on the figure confirms it. This exact agreement is guaranteed by a theorem known as Frisch-Waugh-Lovell. As a result, your adjusted coefficient is the slope of these leftovers, which means that controlling for a variable was never magic at all. [NEXT]

title: Holding Constant, Geometrically: Added-Variable Plots
section: Go try it: raw view first, then toggle to the AVP
text:
Go try it: raw view first, then toggle to the AVP

The Added-Variable Plot Builder in its default raw-view state: the Show added variable plot toggle is off, the raw scatter is visible, and the readout already shows that the AVP slope matches the multiple-regression coefficient.Simulated data. The readout already shows the match; use the live version in the course notes to toggle on the residual plot.

[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 0 · ~58s] This is the builder from the chapter, and you can use it to run the recipe yourself. The data here are simulated so you can change the predictor structure and watch the recipe update. The exact match itself is not a simulation trick: in OLS, using the same rows and controls, the focal predictor’s added-variable-plot slope equals its full-model coefficient, assuming no perfect collinearity. This screenshot begins with the toggle switched off, so the raw scatter is visible, and the readout already shows the Match check. In the live version, flip the toggle on to see the leftovers-on-leftovers picture behind that readout. The simulation lets you experiment; the equality is the theorem. [NEXT]

title: Holding Constant, Geometrically: Added-Variable Plots
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


For a focal predictor in OLS, the multiple-regression coefficient is the slope of residuals on residuals. Holding urbanization fixed means comparing cases at the same urbanization value; the added-variable plot shows the geometry of that comparison: strip urbanization out of the outcome, strip it out of the predictor, then plot the leftovers. The slope of that added-variable plot equals the adjusted coefficient exactly; here both round to −0.58.




Next: Anatomy of the project — research questions and the seven parts. Notes, Ch 4 §Partial Regression Plots

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~66s] Let us carry this idea forward. For a focal predictor in OLS, the regression coefficient is the slope of residuals on residuals. The added-variable plot gives the OLS geometry behind holding a variable fixed: compare at the same covariate value by removing that covariate’s linear part from the outcome and the focal predictor, then reading the residual-on-residual slope. That slope is the adjusted coefficient exactly, which here is minus zero point five eight. It is not magic, and it is not a matter of throwing the variable away; it is a recipe you can now narrate back in your own words. In the next lecture, we will map out the whole project, including your research questions and its seven parts. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
text:
Forty percent, one skeleton


Project 1 = 20%. Project 2 = 20%.

That’s 40% of your grade — two documents, one 7-part skeleton.

[DECK ch04-05 · 8 min · ~1045 words · clicks 5 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~65s] Before you fit a single model, let us talk about strategy. In this course you will write two project reports, and together they carry a large share of your grade. Project One uses linear regression and is worth twenty percent of your grade, and Project Two uses logistic regression and is worth another twenty percent. That means forty percent of the course rests on two written reports. The reassuring part is that these are not two different assignments that you must learn separately. They are one genre written twice, built on the same seven-part skeleton. Once you learn the shape of that report, you know the structure that supports both major projects. For that reason, today is not a statistics lecture but a map: it shows you where the points are, and where students quietly lose them. [NEXT]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
text:
One report, seven parts









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Here is the entire report on a single screen, organized into seven fixed parts. Part one frames the research and states your questions. Part two describes the data and the variables. Part three lays out the analysis plan. Part four presents the descriptive analyses. Part six is the discussion, and part seven covers reproducibility. Notice that both projects follow exactly this skeleton, so the structure you learn here applies to each of them. [CLICK 1] Now part five, the regression results, appears in the centre, and it visibly dwarfs every other box. The reason is its weight: this single part is worth thirty points out of one hundred, and it holds the table, the fit, the diagnostics, and the interpretation together. That is nearly a third of the project rubric concentrated in one place. Everything above it is setup that prepares this part, and this part is where the work finally pays off. [NEXT]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
text:
Part 5 is the boss fight









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] These are the same one hundred points, now drawn as a bar chart so you can compare the parts directly. Reading down the bars, you see ten points, fifteen, ten, and fifteen, and then you reach part five. Part five is worth thirty points, and its bar towers over the others. This is the part to prepare for most carefully, because you do not want to face the largest block of points unprepared. [PAUSE] [CLICK 1] Here is the move that most students miss. Parts one through three, which are the framing, the data, and the analysis plan, together come to thirty-five points, and they are due first, as the Proposal, before you have fitted anything. Compare the two totals: thirty points of results against thirty-five points of setup. Careful planning protects a large share of the available project points, and these are the points most often left on the table. [NEXT]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
text:
Project 1: number your questions
Project 1 — linear regression

2–3 research questions, and number the ones you use: RQ1, RQ2, and, if you include a third, RQ3. You’ll refer back to those numbers all report long.



Template: “Is there a relationship between [outcome Y] and [predictor X], after controlling for [covariates]?”
Not “What predicts income?” — too vague. → RQ1: “Is there a relationship between income and education, after controlling for age and experience?”



[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~75s] Let us turn to the research questions, which is the part of the report taught almost nowhere else. For Project One, you write two or three research questions, and you number the ones you use: R-Q one, R-Q two, and, if you include a third question, R-Q three. You will point back to those numbers throughout the report, so each question keeps a stable name from start to finish. [CLICK 1] There is a template so that you do not have to invent the wording yourself: is there a relationship between some outcome Y and some predictor X, after controlling for covariates? Consider how the template repairs a weak question. The phrase what predicts income is not yet a research question, because it names no predictor. Sharpen it with the template, and you get a usable question: is there a relationship between income and education, after controlling for age and experience? That version is specific and can be numbered, whereas a vague question is the single most common way that part one loses points. [NEXT]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
section: Project 2: exactly one, name the event
text:
Project 2: exactly one, name the event
Project 2 — logistic regression

Exactly ONE focused research question, on a binary outcome — and name the event (which group is Y = 1).









The named pitfall: don’t misnumber


A model sequence is Model 1, Model 2, Model 3 — not RQ1, RQ2, RQ3. Project 2 has one question; the models are stages building evidence for that one.






[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Project Two reverses this rule, so it is worth stating plainly. The logistic project gets exactly one research question, not two or three. You must also name the event, which means saying clearly which group counts as the Y-equals-one group. For an outcome such as manager versus not a manager, you state out loud which of the two the event refers to. [CLICK 1] Now consider the named pitfall, which is worth circling. Students often copy Project One and write R-Q one, R-Q two, and R-Q three for Project Two, and that is incorrect. You will indeed fit a sequence of models, but you label them Model one, Model two, and Model three. The distinction matters because there is still only one question; those models are stages that build evidence for that single question, rather than separate questions of their own. Labelling the sequence correctly is what keeps you clear of this pitfall. [NEXT]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
text:
Proposal, Draft, Final









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~72s] The report does not arrive all at once; it grows across three milestones. The Proposal comes first, and it contains your research questions, your variables, and your planned sequence of models, all written before you fit anything. For Project One, the Proposal is due in Session seven. Notice that this places the planning early, so it becomes deliberate work rather than a last-minute scramble. [CLICK 1] The Draft then fills the middle of the report with the descriptives, the regression, and the diagnostics. The Final is the polish, together with a source file that re-runs cleanly from start to finish. Consider the dataset that feeds all three milestones, and lock it in before anything else: it needs at least two hundred complete cases and at least four explanatory variables, including at least one continuous predictor and at least one categorical predictor with three or more categories. This gate decides the whole project up front, because a dataset that does not clear it cannot support the report. [NEXT]

title: Anatomy of the Project: RQs and the 7 Parts
text:
Write the RQs first







Key takeaway


Both projects are one fixed genre — the same 7 parts, with Part 5 worth 30 of 100. Project 1: 2–3 numbered RQs. Project 2: exactly one, name the event — models are Model 1/2/3, not RQ1/2/3. A good Proposal is RQs + variables + a planned model sequence — written before any fitting.




Next: Interactions — when the effect depends on another variable. Project guide: the 7-part structure · Notes, Ch 4 §The Model

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~56s] Let us gather the strategy into a few sentences worth remembering. Both projects share one skeleton, and within that skeleton part five is worth thirty of your one hundred points, so it is the part to over-prepare. Project One asks for two or three numbered questions, while Project Two asks for exactly one, with the event named, and its stages labelled Model one, Model two, and Model three, never R-Q one, two, and three. The single most valuable thing you can do for your grade is to write the research questions first, before you fit a single model. In the next lecture we return to the statistics with interactions, which describe what happens when the effect of one variable depends on another. [END]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
section: Does the slope work the same in every school?
text:
Does the slope work the same in every school?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch05-01 · 8 min · ~959 words · clicks 6 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~62s] In the previous lecture, a question was left hanging: what if the lines for two schools were not parallel? Today we answer that question. The horizontal axis shows the reading test, and the vertical axis shows the later exam score, for two London schools, Hackney in navy and Chelsea in rust. Reading predicts the exam, but the question we now ask is whether it predicts the exam in the same way within each school. [CLICK 1] Let us fit each school its own line and compare them. The Chelsea line climbs faster, with a steeper slope, while the Hackney line is gentler. This suggests that the effect of reading may depend on which school a student attends. Hold that picture in mind, because it is the central idea of the entire chapter. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
text:
The additive world: one answer









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Every model we have built so far lives in this world, the additive one, where the two lines are forced to stay parallel. There is a single slope, drawn just once, and every school receives that same tilt. [CLICK 1] Notice the phrase one slope for both, because it names the quiet assumption underneath every additive model. That is, the effect of reading does not depend on the school. The school can shift a line up or down, but it can never tilt it, so the rate of rise is the same for everyone and one answer fits all. The question we now raise is whether that assumption should hold. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
text:
The interaction world: it depends









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~51s] Let us now look at the other world. The data are exactly the same, but here each school keeps its own fitted slope, so the two lines are allowed to fan apart rather than being forced to run parallel. That fanning is what an interaction model allows us to represent: the slope can be different under different conditions. [CLICK 1] Now let us put numbers on the picture. Hackney’s fitted line rises at zero point three zero exam points per reading point, while Chelsea’s fitted line rises at zero point five four, almost twice as steeply. In this fitted interaction picture, the reading-GCSE relationship is no longer summarized by a single slope; it is summarized by conditional slopes, and which one applies depends on the school. An interaction, stated in a single word, means that it depends. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
text:
Parallel, or fanning?









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~53s] Here are the two worlds placed side by side. On the left is the additive world, where the lines are parallel, share one slope, and keep the same gap everywhere. On the right is the interaction world, where the fitted lines are allowed to fan, so the model estimates different slopes and a changing gap as you move across. The formal test asks whether that extra flexibility is worth trusting. [CLICK 1] Let us also clear up one common misunderstanding right away. Many people believe that an interaction requires the lines to cross, but that is not so. Notice that these lines fan apart and never meet. For this continuous-by-school example, different fitted slopes are enough; crossing is not required. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
section: Go try it: drag the interaction
text:
Go try it: drag the interaction

The interaction app on the real Chelsea-versus-Hackney GCSE data — drag the β₃ slider and watch the two school lines tilt apart or snap back to parallel. Real GCSE data, not simulated.Live in the course notes — toggle the interaction off and the lines flatten to parallel.

[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 0 · ~48s] Here is the chapter’s interactive app, running on these exact students, Chelsea and Hackney, with real data rather than made-up numbers. This screenshot shows the interaction turned on, and the live version provides a slider for changing it. In the live version, when you drag the slider up, the fitted lines tilt apart and Chelsea climbs more steeply. When you drag it down to zero, the two lines flatten until they are perfectly parallel, which is the additive world reappearing live in front of you. The live version is available in the course notes, and you are encouraged to experiment with it yourself. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
section: It’s not confounding — it changes the slope
text:
It’s not confounding — it changes the slope









Figure 9














Figure 10








[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~49s] Let us now head off a confusion that trips up almost everyone. An interaction is not the same thing as confounding, because the two play very different roles. Recall that a confounder distorts an effect, which is the controlling-for story from earlier chapters, and that a mediator transmits an effect, passing it along to the outcome. [CLICK 1] A moderator is the variable that makes an effect conditional, and the interaction term represents that moderation. This is the line worth remembering: it changes the slope itself, not the adjustment. Confounding moves your estimate of one single effect, whereas an interaction model asks whether one common effect is too simple. If the interaction is supported, the model estimates condition-specific slopes. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
text:
The new question: for whom?

Not “what is the effect?” — but “the effect for whom, under what conditions?”









Test first — honesty at the gateway


Here the slope difference is not significant: F(1, 126) = 2.36, p = .13. So: no evidence the slopes truly differ — not proof they’re equal. We’re learning the mechanics; whether this fanning is real, we test in a later session (ML-05.5).






[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~65s] An interaction therefore rewrites the question itself. You stop asking what is the effect of reading, and you begin asking what is the effect for whom, and under what conditions. That shift, from a single number to a conditional one, is the heart of this chapter. [CLICK 1] Let us be fully honest right at the gateway. For these particular schools, the difference between the slopes is not statistically significant: F is two point three six, and p is zero point one three. The careful verdict is therefore that we have no evidence the slopes truly differ. Notice that this is not the same as saying the slopes are equal; we simply cannot tell them apart with these data. For now we are learning the mechanics, and whether this particular fanning is real is something we will test in a later session. [NEXT]

title: ‘It Depends’: What an Interaction Is
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


An interaction means the effect of one predictor depends on the level of another — additive world: parallel lines, one slope for all; interaction world: fanning lines, a slope each. A moderator changes the slope (not the adjustment), and the lines need not cross. The question shifts from “what’s the effect?” to “for whom?” — but test before you trust it.




Next: One model, two lines — the anatomy of every interaction coefficient. Notes, Ch 5 §Continuous × Categorical · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~51s] Let us carry a few ideas out the door. An interaction means it depends: the effect of one predictor depends on the level of another. In the additive world the lines are parallel, with one slope for all, while in the interaction world the lines fan, with a slope for each group. A moderator changes the slope itself, not the adjustment, and the lines need not cross. The question turns from what is the effect into the effect for whom, and you should always test before you trust it. In the next lecture, we will open up a single interaction model and give a name to every coefficient. [END]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
text:
One model, two lines










Figure 1






\[ y = \alpha + \beta_1\,x + \beta_2\,d + \beta_3\,(x \times d) \]

[DECK ch05-02 · 9 min · ~1078 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~57s] In the previous lecture, two school lines fanned apart on the same plot, and we promised to open the model up and see what holds them. That model appears here, beneath the slide. The predicted value of y equals alpha, plus beta-one times reading, plus beta-two times the dummy, plus beta-three times the product of reading and the dummy. Those four coefficients carry both lines at once, the navy line for Hackney and the rust line for Chelsea. By the end of this lecture, you will be able to point at any feature of either line and name the coefficient responsible for it. There is no new mathematics here; the entire task is learning to read what is already in front of you. [NEXT]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
section: Plug in d = 0: the reference line
text:
Plug in d = 0: the reference line









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~78s] Let us begin with the simpler of the two lines. The dummy variable d acts as a switch: it equals zero for Hackney and one for Chelsea. Set it to zero and follow what happens to each term. Beta-two times zero is zero, so that term disappears. Beta-three times reading times zero is also zero, so that term disappears as well. Two of the four terms vanish, and what remains is alpha plus beta-one times reading, a plain and complete straight line. This is the reference line, drawn in navy for Hackney. [CLICK 1] Here is the point that is easiest to misread. When you see beta-one, it is tempting to call it the slope, as if it described every group. It does not. Beta-one is the slope of the reference group only. The intercept is negative three point two five, marked by the navy dot, and beta-one is zero point three zero, marked by the teal arrow. Both of these numbers belong to Hackney and to no one else. [NEXT]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
section: Plug in d = 1: the focal line
text:
Plug in d = 1: the focal line









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~71s] Now flip the switch the other way and set d equal to one, for Chelsea. This time nothing vanishes; instead, two new terms switch on. Many people expect the only effect to be that the line lifts upward. [CLICK 1] The line does indeed lift. Beta-two switches on, and the line jumps upward by nine point two eight. Notice, however, that this jump is the gap between the two lines only at reading zero, where the vertical axis sits. It is not the gap everywhere along the line, and it is only half of the story. [CLICK 2] Beta-three switches on as well, and it rides along with the slope. As a result, the line does more than rise: it also tilts, becoming steeper. Recall that the ANCOVA model only shifted the line up or down. Once we add the interaction, the line shifts and tilts together, and that tilt is the very reason this lecture exists. [NEXT]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
text:
The focal slope = b1 + b3









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~66s] Let us now determine exactly what Chelsea’s slope is. This is the single most-missed point in the chapter, so let us be careful. Chelsea’s slope is not beta-three; beta-three is only the gap between the two slopes. To obtain Chelsea’s actual slope, you add: beta-one plus beta-three, the reference slope plus the extra amount. [CLICK 1] Zero point three zero plus zero point two four lands on zero point five four, which is Chelsea’s slope, shown in the rust box. Notice what you just did: you added two coefficients together to recover a quantity that has real meaning. This is the same move you use to recover a group mean, taking a reference piece and adding an offset to it. Beta-three, then, is the step you take, not the place you arrive; it is a difference between slopes, and never a slope on its own. [NEXT]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
text:
One model, four jobs









Figure 9














Figure 10















Figure 11















Figure 12








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 3 · ~78s] Here is the entire anatomy gathered into one frame, together with a four-word handle for it: anchor, tilt, lift, fan. Alpha anchors. It is negative three point two five, the navy dot where the reference line crosses x equals zero. [CLICK 1] Beta-one tilts. It is zero point three zero, shown by the teal arrow, and it is the slope of that reference line. With the reference intercept and the reference slope named, two of the four coefficients are settled. [CLICK 2] Beta-two lifts. It is nine point two eight, shown by the rust arrow pointing straight up at x equals zero, and it is the gap between the two intercepts, measured just at that point. [CLICK 3] Beta-three fans. The gold dotted guide shows where Chelsea would be if it kept the same slope as Hackney after the lift. The actual rust line tilts above that guide, and the extra tilt is beta-three, zero point two four. So beta-three is not the vertical distance between the two lines at one reading score. A vertical gap at a score is beta-two plus beta-three times that score. Beta-three is the slope difference, the rate at which the school gap changes as reading moves. Anchor, tilt, lift, fan: point at the picture, and name the piece. [NEXT]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
text:
One term, two readings









Figure 13














Figure 14








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] There is one more thing worth knowing about beta-three. The product term is symmetric, which means that beta-three can be read in two fitted-model ways. Read it across the groups: in this fitted model, the reading slope changes by school, and the point estimate makes Chelsea’s fitted line steeper than Hackney’s. That is the reading we have used throughout this lecture. [CLICK 1] Now read the same number down: in this fitted model, the school gap changes as reading increases. It is the same product term and the same value of zero point two four, told as two fitted stories. Choose whichever one your question is asking, and keep the later test in mind before treating the pattern as evidence. [NEXT]

title: One Model, Two Lines: Coefficient Anatomy
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


One model, four coefficients, two complete lines. Plug in d = 0 → the reference line α + β₁x; plug in d = 1 → the focal line (α + β₂) + (β₁ + β₃)x. So β₁ is the reference group’s slope (not “the” slope), β₂ is the gap at x = 0 only, and β₃ is a difference of slopes (not a slope) — read across or down. The focal slope is β₁ + β₃ — the same add-the-pieces move that recovers a group mean.




Next: The fine print — why “main effects” become conditional. Notes, Ch 5 §Continuous × Categorical · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~77s] Let us gather the main ideas to carry forward. One model holds four coefficients and two complete lines. With the dummy off, you have the reference line; with the dummy on, both features shift, the intercept by beta-two and the slope by beta-three. As a result, beta-one is the reference slope rather than the slope for everyone, beta-three is a difference between slopes, and the focal slope is beta-one plus beta-three. One honest caveat is in order: this slope difference is not statistically significant, with an F value of two point three six and a p value of zero point one three, so there is no evidence that the slopes truly differ. You will meet this same anatomy on a salary table before long, again with two group slopes built from a reference plus an interaction. In the next lecture, we will read the fine print and see why so-called main effects quietly become conditional. The notes and Practice Quiz Five are linked just below. [END]

title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
text:
Same four numbers, new meanings










Figure 1














Figure 2







\[ y = \alpha + \beta_1\,x + \beta_2\,d + \beta_3\,(x \times d) \]

[DECK ch05-03 · 8 min · ~961 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~50s] In the previous lecture, we named every coefficient in an interaction model. Today we read the fine print, which contains the single most dangerous reading in this course. The output in front of you carries four numbers, and the one we will watch most closely is the school coefficient, b-two, which equals nine point two eight. [CLICK 1] It is tempting to call that number the Chelsea advantage, but that reading is not safe. Once a product term enters the model, that coefficient does not mean what it appears to mean. The same four symbols you have read a hundred times have quietly changed their meaning. Let us see exactly how. [NEXT]

title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
text:
Same symbol, two meanings









Figure 3














Figure 4















Figure 5








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~65s] The heart of this lecture fits into a single table. On the left side sits the additive world, the ANCOVA model. In that world, b-one is the LRT slope, shared by both schools, and b-two is the gap at every LRT value, one constant advantage. Both of these are clean, overall meanings. [CLICK 1] Now add the product term, and the same b-one changes. It is no longer everyone’s slope; instead, it is Hackney’s slope only, the slope of the reference group, and it says nothing directly about the other school. [CLICK 2] The same b-two changes in the same way. It is no longer the gap everywhere; instead, it is the gap at one specific value, where LRT equals zero, which is the value when the other variable equals zero. The symbols on the printout are identical, yet their meanings are fundamentally different. [NEXT]

title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
section: b2 is the gap at zero — only there
text:
b2 is the gap at zero — only there









Figure 6














One number can’t summarize a moving gap


WRONG: b2 = 9.28 is the Chelsea advantage. RIGHT: b2 = 9.28 is the fitted Chelsea-Hackney gap at reading score zero. The model estimates gaps from about 6.9 to 11.7 at these probes, but b3 is not significant, so the changing-gap pattern is mechanics, not evidence.






[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~68s] Let us now see what the gap at zero only really looks like. The two fitted lines fan apart, and the school gap is drawn at three reading scores. At a low score the gap is six point eight eight, at zero it is nine point two eight, marked by the gold arrow as b-two, and at a high score it is eleven point six nine. Notice that nine point two eight is simply the middle one of the three. [CLICK 1] Compare the wrong reading with the right one. The wrong reading says that b-two equals nine point two eight is the Chelsea advantage. The right reading says that it is the fitted Chelsea-Hackney gap at reading score zero, which is a below-average score. The fitted interaction model estimates gaps of about six point nine, nine point three, and eleven point seven at these probe scores. That is the model mechanics. Because b-three is not significant, we do not yet treat the changing-gap pattern as established evidence. A single number cannot summarize a fitted gap that moves, but inference still decides whether that movement is worth trusting. [NEXT]

title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
section: b1 is Hackney’s slope — only Hackney’s
text:
b1 is Hackney’s slope — only Hackney’s









Figure 7








β₁ = 0.30 is Hackney’s rate of climb — not “the LRT effect.” In this fitted interaction model, Chelsea’s fitted slope is 0.54. There is no single “LRT slope” here.



[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] The same trap waits at the other coefficient. The LRT slope, b-one, equals zero point three zero, and it is tempting to call that the LRT effect, but it is not correct. [CLICK 1] It is Hackney’s fitted slope, the slope of the reference group, and only theirs. In this fitted interaction model, Chelsea’s fitted slope is zero point five four, the steeper of the two lines. As a result, the fitted effect of reading is two different numbers, and b-one reports only one of them, the slope of the group coded zero. Read it as the reference slope, which is conditional once again, never as the overall effect. [NEXT]
