title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
text:
“Not significant” is not “no effect”









Figure 8














Figure 9








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~66s] Now we reach the trap that costs the most points. On this data, the one coefficient that is not significant is the interaction, b-three, with an F of two point three six and a p-value of zero point one three. [CLICK 1] It is tempting to conclude that the schools do not differ, but that conclusion is too broad. There is evidence of a school gap at the conditioning point, LRT equals zero, because b-two is highly significant there. The fitted gap estimates at the three probe scores come from the model mechanics; the non-significant b-three means only that we cannot show the gap changes with reading score. Here is the principle stated carefully. For b-three, non-significant means no detectable slope difference, or gap-change rate. For a lower-order conditional coefficient, non-significant means no detectable effect at that conditioning value. Neither statement means no effect anywhere. For that reason, be precise, and say that the slope difference is not significant rather than that everything is. [NEXT]

title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
section: Where is reading score zero, anyway?
text:
Where is reading score zero, anyway?









Figure 10














Figure 11








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~55s] One loose end remains. Recall that b-two reports the gap at LRT equals zero, so it is worth asking where zero actually sits among these students. It turns out to be a strange anchor. [CLICK 1] Zero sits down in the lower tail. About three in ten students fall below it, which makes it a below-average reading score. The average student sits far to the right, near plus four point seven. As a result, b-two gives the gap for a below-average student rather than for a typical one. The fix is to center the reading score, sliding zero to the average, so that b-two reports the gap for the average student. We will do exactly that a couple of decks from now. [NEXT]

title: The Fine Print: ‘Main Effects’ Become Conditional
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Once a product term is in the model, the lower-order coefficients are conditional, not average: read each as conditional on zero for the other predictor, or as the reference group. b2 = 9.28 is the fitted gap at LRT = 0 only – not the universal Chelsea advantage. The interaction model can estimate changing gaps; inference asks whether b3 supports that change. b1 = 0.30 is Hackney’s slope only. For b3, non-significant means no detectable gap-change rate; for lower-order terms, significance is local to the conditioning value.




Next: The gap that grows — difference as a function. Notes, Ch 5 §Interpreting Each Coefficient · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~63s] Let us close with the lesson worth carrying with you. The moment a product term enters the model, every lower-order coefficient turns conditional, so you read each one as the effect when the other variable is zero, or as the reference group, never as an average. In the same way, a result that is not significant never means that there is no effect anywhere. One honest caveat belongs here: the slope difference is not significant, so in practice we would report the simpler additive model, and we read these coefficients only to learn the mechanics. You will meet this same reading soon on a salary table, where a coefficient that looks like the gender gap is really the gap at one value of the other predictor. In the next lecture, we turn to the gap that grows. [END]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
section: How big is the gap? Wrong question
text:
How big is the gap? Wrong question










Figure 1







[DECK ch05-04 · 8 min · ~923 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~56s] Here are our two schools once more, with Chelsea on top and Hackney below. A natural question is how big the gap between them is, and you would like a single number to answer it. Notice, though, that the fitted gap is not the same everywhere. At a low reading score the fitted gap is small, in the middle it is larger, and at the high end it is larger still. Because the two fitted lines fan apart rather than stay parallel, the fitted gap changes as you move across the reading scores. This means that asking how big the gap is, as if it had one size, is the wrong question. There is no single number in this fitted interaction model, because the fitted gap itself moves with the reading score. [NEXT]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
section: Subtract one line from the other
text:
Subtract one line from the other









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~65s] To see where that changing gap comes from, we simply subtract one line from the other, taking the top line minus the bottom line. It helps to say the direction out loud: this is Chelsea minus Hackney, the focal group minus the reference group. If you reverse the subtraction, every sign reverses with it. Now carry out the subtraction in symbols and watch the terms cancel. Both equations contain the alpha term, so alpha cancels. They also contain the shared baseline slope on reading, so that part cancels too. What remains are two terms: beta two, plus beta three times reading. [CLICK 1] The convenient part is that there is nothing new to compute. That intercept and that slope are coefficients you have already fit: beta two, which is nine point two eight, and beta three, which is zero point two four. [NEXT]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
section: The gap is a line: a height plus a slope
text:
The gap is a line: a height plus a slope









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~45s] Consider what this expression actually is: the gap is itself a straight line, with its own intercept and its own slope. Beta two, nine point two eight, is the starting height of that line, which is the gap when the reading score is zero. [CLICK 1] Beta three, zero point two four, is the slope of that line, which tells you how fast the fitted gap widens for each additional reading point. Notice what this gives you. A vague question, such as whether the Chelsea-Hackney gap is estimated to be larger at higher LRT scores, becomes a precise one that you can check directly: is beta three positive? [NEXT]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
section: See the gap as one moving line
text:
See the gap as one moving line









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~55s] Let us now draw the gap, and read the axis carefully first, because this is not the two-line scatter from before. The vertical axis here is the gap between the schools, not a test score; it shows Chelsea minus Hackney. This means that the single rust line is the fitted gap itself, and it tilts upward. The dashed line marks zero, and the fitted gap stays well above it across the observed LRT range, so the two fitted lines never meet in the data window. Notice, however, that the confidence band around the line is quite wide. [CLICK 1] In fact, a perfectly flat line, representing a gap that never changes, fits comfortably inside that band. This is the honest caveat drawn out: we cannot rule out a constant gap. [NEXT]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
section: Read the gap at three reading scores
text:
Read the gap at three reading scores









Figure 8














Figure 9















Figure 10








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~60s] Let us read three worked probe values straight off the line. At a low probe score of minus ten, the fitted gap is six point eight eight. At zero, the fitted gap is nine point two eight. At a high probe score of plus ten, the fitted gap is eleven point six nine. [CLICK 1] Now notice the steady rhythm. With each step to the right, the fitted gap climbs by the same amount, which is zero point two four for each reading point. Ten more reading points therefore add two point four to the fitted gap. That steady climb is exactly beta three. [CLICK 2] The middle value, nine point two eight, is circled in gold, and that value is beta two. It is special only because it is the gap at zero. It is simply one point on a moving line. [NEXT]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
text:
So: does the gap widen?







The substantive read


The point estimate says the fitted Chelsea-Hackney gap widens by 0.24 GCSE points per LRT point — from about 6.88 at LRT = -10 to 11.69 at LRT = +10 over the worked probe window. Across the observed LRT range, the fitted lines never cross.












But only suggestive


The slope of the gap, b₃ = 0.24, is not significant — \(F(1,126)=2.36\), \(p=.13\). There is evidence of a school gap at LRT = 0 (b₂ is highly significant) — only the change in the gap is unproven. “The fitted gap widens” is the point estimate, not a proven fact.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~65s] Is the Chelsea-Hackney gap estimated to be larger at higher reading scores? On the point estimates, yes: the fitted gap widens by zero point two four GCSE points for each reading point. Over our worked minus ten to plus ten probe window, that is a move from about six point nine to eleven point seven. The full observed LRT range is wider than that probe window, so do not treat those two numbers as the dataset endpoints. Across the observed range, the fitted gap also never closes; the two fitted lines would only meet near a reading score of minus thirty-nine, far below the data window. [CLICK 1] Here is the honest statement, said precisely. That slope, beta three, is not significant, with an F of two point three six and a p of zero point one three. Notice that it is the slope difference that is not significant, not everything in the model. There is evidence of a school gap at LRT equals zero; only the change in the gap is unproven. A flat, constant gap is still on the table. [NEXT]

title: The Gap That Grows: Difference as a Function
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


In an interaction model a group difference is not a number — it’s a function: gap(x) = b₂ + b₃x. Read b₂ as the gap’s starting height (at x = 0) and b₃ as its slope. Mind the direction — focal minus reference. Then ask the substantive questions: where is the fitted gap zero? where is it estimated to widen? — and stay honest: here the widening is only suggestive (F(1, 126) = 2.36, p = .13), not proven.




Next: Test first — is the interaction even real? Notes, Ch 5 §The School Difference · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~43s] Let us carry this away with us. When you fit an interaction, a group difference becomes a function: the gap of x equals beta two plus beta three times x. Read beta two as the starting height and beta three as the slope, then ask where the fitted gap is zero and where it is estimated to widen. You will apply this same machinery to a pay gap before long. Because we kept describing that slope as only suggestive, the obvious next question is whether the slope difference is statistically supported. That is what we test next time. [END]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
section: Three decks reading beta-three — what supports that read?
text:
Three decks reading beta-three — what supports that read?

Theory

The last three lectures interpreted the fitted interaction — read its slope, drew its growing gap.


One question we never asked: what evidence supports that read?


[DECK ch05-05 · 6 min · ~816 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~43s] Let us begin with an honest admission. In the last three decks, we opened up this interaction and read every coefficient. We named beta-three, we watched the fitted gap between the two schools grow, and we treated that difference as if it were statistically supported. [CLICK 1] There is one question we never paused to ask, which is whether the evidence supports that reading. We never checked whether that difference in slopes is statistically supported, and a point estimate alone is not enough for an inferential claim. We need its uncertainty, so today we will run the check that we skipped earlier. [NEXT]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
section: Two models, one nested in the other
text:
Two models, one nested in the other









Figure 1














Figure 2








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~53s] The test compares two models, and the key idea is that one model is nested inside the other. On the left is the parallel model, the ANCOVA. Both schools share one common slope. The two lines sit at different heights, but they cannot fan apart, because this is the same nested F-test setup you first met back in Session One. [CLICK 1] On the right is the interaction model, fitted to the same data. Here we give the model one extra parameter, beta-three, which lets the two lines fan apart. This means the parallel model is simply the interaction model with beta-three fixed at zero. The question is whether that one extra freedom is worth keeping. [NEXT]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
section: Run the test — and read the verdict
text:
Run the test — and read the verdict









Figure 3














Figure 4















Figure 5








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~64s] We run the test with a single line, an anova of the two models, and then read the table. Freeing the slope barely lowers the leftover error: the sum of squares falls only from five thousand eight hundred eighty to five thousand seven hundred seventy-two. The F-statistic that weighs that drop against chance is two point three six. [CLICK 1] Now look at the p-value. It is zero point one three, which is larger than zero point zero five, so we fail to reject. The careful wording is exactly this: there is no evidence of differing slopes. [CLICK 2] Here is one quick connection. Because this is a single term, the F-test and the t-test on the coefficient are really one test. The interaction t is one point five four, and t-squared equals F. They are the same test seen from two sides. [NEXT]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
section: What an honest report actually says
text:
What an honest report actually says









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~61s] So what do you actually report? Because the test came back null, you report the simpler additive model. There is one constant gap of ten point oh nine, which is the same Chelsea lead at every reading score, and it is highly significant, with a p-value below zero point zero zero one. The two schools do differ. There is also one common slope of zero point four six. [CLICK 1] Here is the common trap to avoid. That ten point oh nine is the gap from the additive model. Do not reach for the nine point two eight from the earlier gap deck, because that number was the gap at a reading score of zero only. We showed how you would read the interaction if the slope difference were statistically supported, but this test did not support that reading here. [NEXT]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
text:
One picture for every interaction









Figure 8














Figure 9








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~53s] Here is the whole routine on a single card, and it works for every interaction you will fit, not only this one. Start at the gold box: you fit the model, and you test whether beta-three equals zero. The verdict then chooses your path. [CLICK 1] If the test is significant, go left: we have statistical evidence that the slopes differ, so conditional effects are usually the cautious report, and we probe them. If it is not significant, which is our case, go right: the additive model is usually the cautious report, with one gap and one slope. Test first, because the answer tells you which reading the evidence supports. Notice the footnote, though, because a p-value is not an automatic keep-or-drop rule. [NEXT]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
section: When you keep an n.s. interaction anyway
text:
When you keep an n.s. interaction anyway







A p-value is not an automatic keep/drop switch


A pre-specified, theory-driven interaction may be kept and reported even when n.s. — you planned it, and absence of evidence isn’t evidence of absence. Conversely, don’t go fishing through many interactions and keep only the one that turned up significant.












And when the factor has 3+ levels


Several product terms at once? Test them together with one omnibus F — not separate t’s. The tidy t² = F shortcut works for a single term only.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Here is one honest complication, so that you do not over-learn the rule. If theory told you to expect this interaction, and you specified it in advance, you may keep it even when it is not significant. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. The other side of this is that you should not go fishing through many interactions and then report only the one that happened to be significant. [CLICK 1] One point is worth keeping in mind for later. When your factor has three or more levels, the interaction is several terms at once, so you test them together with one omnibus F. The tidy shortcut that t-squared equals F holds for a single term only. [NEXT]

title: Test First: Is the Interaction Supported?
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Test before you interpret. Compare the parallel and interaction models with a nested F (for one term, t² = F). Here F(1, 126) = 2.36, p = .13 — fail to reject, no evidence of differing slopes — so report the additive model (gap 10.09, slope 0.46). A significant interaction usually points us toward the conditional read; a null one usually points us toward the simpler model — but theory and design get a vote.




Next: Probing this same null interaction — conditional slopes with their own CIs. Notes, Ch 5 §Testing the Interaction · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~38s] Let us close with the main idea. Test the interaction before you interpret it, by comparing the parallel model against the full one with a nested F. Here the p-value is zero point one three, so there is no evidence of differing slopes, and we report the additive model. Next time, we will probe this same interaction anyway, not because it is significant, but to learn how conditional slopes are reported and how probing differs from significance. The notes and Practice Quiz Five are linked just below. [END]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
section: b₃ said the slopes differ — but how big is each one?
text:
b₃ said the slopes differ — but how big is each one?

Theory

Last deck the test came back null: b₃ is not significant — no strong statistical evidence that the slopes differ.
But that one coefficient still hides a question: how big is each school’s slope on its own?


[DECK ch05-06 · 7 min · ~960 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~48s] Let us pick up exactly where the previous deck left off. In that deck we tested beta three, and the test came back null, which means we did not find strong statistical evidence that the two slopes differ. It is tempting to stop there, but notice what that coefficient actually is. It is a single number describing a gap, and a gap is silent about the two quantities it sits between. For that reason, today we ask the more useful question of how steep each school’s own slope is, and what confidence interval surrounds that slope. That move has a name, and we call it probing. [NEXT]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
section: Probing: ask the slope at each level
text:
Probing: ask the slope at each level
The move

Probing turns one product coefficient into substantive conditional effects — each group’s own slope, with its own SE, CI, and p.









You probe a non-significant interaction too


You don’t probe only when the interaction is significant. You probe to learn how and where effects differ — so we’ll probe this null one anyway, just to watch the machinery run.






[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~69s] Let us be precise about what probing means. Probing takes that one product coefficient and turns it into quantities you can actually report, which we call conditional effects. Each group receives its own slope, and each slope arrives with its own standard error, its own confidence interval, and its own p-value. In other words, instead of a single coefficient about a difference, you obtain one fully described slope per group. [CLICK 1] There is one common misunderstanding worth disarming right away. Many students believe you may probe only after the interaction itself is significant, as though probing were a reward that significance unlocks. That is not correct. You probe in order to learn how effects differ and where they differ, regardless of the test result. Our interaction is not significant, and we will probe it anyway, simply so that we can watch the machinery run. This is exactly what the notes do. [NEXT]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
section: Each school’s own slope, with its own CI
text:
Each school’s own slope, with its own CI









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~67s] A single command does the work. We call avg-slopes with the by argument set to school name, and the result is a table with one row per school. [CLICK 1] Take Hackney first, the reference school. Its slope is zero point three zero, with its own interval from zero point zero four to zero point five five, and its own p-value of point zero two. That interval clears zero, so Hackney’s slope is significant. [CLICK 2] Now take Chelsea, which is steeper. Its slope is zero point five four, its interval runs from zero point three six to zero point seven one, and its p-value is below point zero zero one, so it is significant as well. Notice the structure: Hackney’s row is beta-one, the reference slope, Chelsea’s row is beta-one plus beta-three, and the gap between the rows is beta-three. Probing has turned one coefficient into two readable slopes. [NEXT]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
section: Two points, two whiskers, one zero line
text:
Two points, two whiskers, one zero line









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~51s] Let us picture the same result. We have two points, each with its ninety-five-percent whisker, and a dashed line at a slope of zero, which marks no LRT-GCSE relationship at all. Both whiskers sit entirely above that line, so the fitted LRT-GCSE association is positive within both schools. Chelsea’s point sits higher because it is the steeper school in this fitted interaction model, and the vertical distance between the two points is beta-three, the gap. [CLICK 1] Now look at the gold band. The two intervals overlap, because Chelsea’s lower bound dips below Hackney’s upper bound, so they share some territory. Keep this in mind, because this overlap is exactly what a non-significant difference looks like. [NEXT]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
section: Two significant slopes ≠ a significant difference
text:
Two significant slopes ≠ a significant difference

For Hackney, each additional LRT point is associated with a 0.30-point GCSE increase (p = .02). For Chelsea, 0.54 points (p < .001). The difference in slopes (b₃ = 0.24) is the interaction; it is not significant (F(1, 126) = 2.36, p = .13), so we do not have strong evidence the LRT–GCSE relationship differs between the schools.









Two SIGNIFICANT slopes is NOT a SIGNIFICANT difference


Both slopes individually clear zero — yet that does not prove they differ from each other. The difference has its own test (b₃), and here it’s n.s.






[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~90s] The sentence on screen is the reporting sentence to practice, and its shape is worth studying. It reports each conditional slope with its p-value, and it leans on one phrase that is doing real work, namely associated with, because this is observational data and we never say causes. It then closes honestly: the difference, beta three, is the interaction, and it is not significant, with an F of two point three six and a p-value of point one three. [CLICK 1] Here is the move that is missed most often in this entire chapter. We have two slopes, and both are significant, so it feels as though they must differ. They need not. Each slope being significant means only that each one clears zero on its own. Whether the two slopes differ from each other is a separate question, answered by its own separate test, which is beta three, and that test is null. This means two individually significant slopes can still be statistically indistinguishable from one another. The overlapping whiskers are consistent with that conclusion, but remember that an eyeball comparison of overlap is only a rough guide; the formal F-test is what decides. [NEXT]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
text:
One framework, three interaction types


Figure 6








Copy carefully


For continuous-by-continuous interactions, use slopes(..., newdata = datagrid(...)) to get one slope at each chosen moderator value.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~72s] Here is the payoff, a map card that covers every interaction you are likely to fit. Today’s case was continuous by categorical, and we handled it with avg slopes and the by argument, which returns a slope at each level. When the interaction is categorical by categorical, you switch to avg comparisons with the by argument, which returns the group gap at each level; that is the simple-effects move you saw earlier on the faculty two-way side. [CLICK 1] The row to copy carefully is continuous by continuous. There, use slopes with newdata set to a data grid, so you get the slope at each chosen moderator value. The idea is the same every time: read the effect where it actually matters. It is all one framework, the marginaleffects package, so you learn the move once. An alternative package, ggeffects, also exists, and the worked example shows it as well, but if you learn this one approach you already have all three cases. [NEXT]

title: Probing: Conditional Slopes with Their Own CIs
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Probing turns one coefficient into each group’s own conditional effect — avg_slopes(by=) hands you a slope with its own SE, CI, and p. Report both: the conditional slopes (Hackney 0.30, Chelsea 0.54) and the difference test. Here the difference — b₃ = 0.24 — has its own test and is n.s. (F(1, 126) = 2.36, p = .13). Two significant slopes is not a significant difference.




Next: Centering — put zero where the data live. Notes, Ch 5 §Probing Interactions · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Let us close with a few sentences worth remembering. Probing turns one coefficient into each group’s own conditional effect, giving you every slope with its own confidence interval. Report both pieces: the conditional slopes and the difference test. Here both slopes were significant, yet the difference between them was not. The lesson to remember is that two significant slopes do not amount to a significant difference. In the next deck we will take up a quieter fix, one that finally makes your intercept mean something, and that fix is centering. [END]

title: Centering: Put Zero Where the Data Live
section: Zero is real, but not typical
text:
Zero is real, but not typical










Figure 1







[DECK ch05-07 · 6 min · ~744 words · clicks 4 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 0 · ~66s] Let us begin with a simple question about our two-school model. The intercept in that model was the predicted GCSE score of a Hackney student whose LRT reading-score value is zero, so let us picture that student carefully. The figure shows the reading scale running from low scores on the left to high scores on the right, and each tick mark along the bottom is one real student. Notice where a value of zero falls: it is below average and not typical, but still inside the observed data. That means an LRT value of zero belongs to a below-average reader, not to the typical student we usually want as a reference. Let us see how to put zero in a more meaningful place. [NEXT]

title: Centering: Put Zero Where the Data Live
section: Where is reading score zero, really?
text:
Where is reading score zero, really?
Common misread — “the gap at zero is just the gap, right?”









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Here is a common misread. We earlier met a school gap of nine point two eight, and it is tempting to call that simply the gap. In fact it is the gap at zero, so we should ask again where zero lives. The navy line marks it, and it sits low: about three in ten students fall below it, which places it near the thirtieth percentile. [CLICK 1] The teal line now marks the typical student, the mean reading score, at about four point seven. Notice that zero is still part of the observed range, but it is not the typical value. So the problem is not that the gap at zero is impossible; the problem is that it answers a less useful question than the gap at the average student. [NEXT]

title: Centering: Put Zero Where the Data Live
section: Put zero where the data live
text:
Put zero where the data live









Figure 4







[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 0 · ~53s] The fix for this is remarkably simple. We center the reading score, which means that we subtract the mean, about four point seven, from the value of every student. We will write the centered predictor as lrt-underscore-c; the underscore-c suffix reminds us that this variable has been centered. Notice what happens on the number line. The students themselves do not move, because the data are exactly the same. Only the label zero relocates: the old raw zero shifts left of the new reference point, and the new zero lands squarely on the typical student. That is the whole idea, captured in five words: put zero where the data live. [NEXT]

title: Centering: Put Zero Where the Data Live
section: Before and after: what moves, what stays
text:
Before and after: what moves, what stays









Figure 5














Figure 6















Figure 7








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 2 · ~70s] Now compare the two fits. We refit the same model on the centered score and lay the two columns side by side, raw first and then centered, and we ask which numbers move. [CLICK 1] These two change, shown in gold. The intercept goes from negative three point two five to negative one point eight eight, and the school gap changes from nine point two eight to ten point four zero, which is now the gap for the average student. Both quantities are measured at zero, and zero is exactly what just moved, so both are reported by the centered model as new coefficients. [CLICK 2] These three, shown in teal, stay the same. The slope is still zero point three zero, the interaction beta-three is zero point two four, and the R-squared is zero point five three in both columns. These estimates are numerically the same after centering, because centering changes the reference point, not the fitted relationship. [NEXT]

title: Centering: Put Zero Where the Data Live
text:
What centering does — and doesn’t









Figure 8














Figure 9








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~57s] There are two common worries about centering, and each misses a distinction. The first worry says that centering changed the model. It did not. The slope is the same, beta-three is the same, and the R-squared is zero point five three either way, with every prediction matching up to numerical rounding. Centering is a re-expression, not a re-model. [CLICK 1] The mirror worry says that centering is therefore only cosmetic. That is not the whole story. The school gap moved from nine point two eight, the gap for a below-average student, to ten point four zero, the gap for the average student. Centering did not change the model; it changed which question the coefficient answers, and that is precisely the point. [NEXT]

title: Centering: Put Zero Where the Data Live
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Centering relocates zero to where the data live. Subtract the mean, and the lower-order coefficients — the intercept and the group gap — read off at the typical case, not a below-average one: the school gap becomes 10.40, the gap for the average student, not 9.28. And the fit is untouched — slope 0.30, β₃ = 0.24, R² = 0.53, every prediction matches up to numerical rounding. A re-expression, not a re-model.




We will use this again in Ch 6: center first, then build squared terms from the centered variable.
Next: Cell means and interaction plots. Notes, Ch 5 §Centering · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~55s] Let us close with the central idea. Centering puts zero where the data live, so that the lower-order terms read off at the typical case. The school gap becomes ten point four zero, the gap for the average student, instead of nine point two eight, while the slope, beta-three, and the overall fit stay the same. It is a re-expression, not a re-model. One note looks ahead to Chapter Six: we will use this again when we center first and then build squared terms from the centered variable. Next time, we will move to cell means and interaction plots. The notes and Practice Quiz Five are linked just below. [END]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
Six means, one question










Figure 1







[DECK ch05-08 · 8 min · ~992 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~52s] In this lecture we work with two factors at the same time, gender and academic rank. Rank has three levels, namely Assistant, Associate, and Full. When you cross the two factors, you do not obtain a single number; instead you obtain a grid with two rows and three columns, which gives six cells in total. Each cell corresponds to one combination of gender and rank, and each cell has its own predicted average salary. Running underneath all six cells is a single question: is the gender gap constant across ranks, or does it change as rank rises? Let us fill in the boxes and find out. [NEXT]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
A grid of cell means









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~69s] Let us see where those six numbers come from. Each cell is built in the same way, as the reference cell plus the coefficients that apply to it. Look first at the formulas printed above each dollar figure. The Female-Assistant cell in the corner is simply alpha, which is the reference cell, and its predicted salary is thirty-seven thousand five hundred ninety-three. The Male-Assistant cell adds beta one to that reference. The far corner, Male-Full, carries four pieces: the reference plus a gender term, a rank term, and an interaction term. [CLICK 1] The gold ring marks the anchor of the whole grid, which is alpha, the reference cell. This gives us the rule for the entire table, namely that a cell mean is the reference plus the coefficients that apply. Notice that we are not plugging in any coefficient values here; we are only naming which coefficients land in which cell. [NEXT]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
Read one simple effect









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~61s] Now let us practice the reading pattern for a simple effect, meaning the gender gap at one single rank. We will work through the gap at Full: choose one rank, compare the two gender cells, then subtract Male minus Female. [CLICK 1] First, choose the cells, which means the same rank for both genders. We stay inside one rank, here Full, and we take that pair of bars. We do not reach across to a different rank. [CLICK 2] Then subtract Male minus Female. That is, we take sixty-one thousand seven hundred forty-five and subtract fifty-four thousand nine hundred fifty-three, which leaves six thousand seven hundred ninety-two. A simple effect is the difference between two cell means. [NEXT]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
Plot the profiles









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~63s] Here are the same six means drawn a second way, as the interaction plot. Rank runs along the bottom and salary runs up the side, and each gender’s three cells are joined into a profile, with Female in navy and Male in rust riding above it the whole way. [CLICK 1] Here is the key way to read this plot. At each rank, the vertical distance between the two lines is the gap at that rank. Measure it across the ranks: the distance is three thousand nine hundred sixteen at Assistant, it narrows to three thousand four hundred thirty-five at Associate, and then it jumps to six thousand seven hundred ninety-two at Full. In this sample, the gap changes across ranks, so the profiles are interaction-shaped rather than parallel. The next question is whether that visible pattern is statistically supported. [NEXT]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
Two common misreads









Figure 9














Figure 10








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~72s] There are two common misreads that are worth disarming. The first is the belief that the lines must cross before we can talk about an interaction-shaped pattern. The second is the belief that if both groups move in the same direction, there is no interaction-shaped pattern. [CLICK 1] Both readings miss the point. The Male line sits above the Female line at every rank, so the two lines never cross, and yet the gap still changes across the ranks, from three thousand nine hundred sixteen, to three thousand four hundred thirty-five, to six thousand seven hundred ninety-two. Crossing is one possible non-parallel shape, but it is not required. In the same way, both salaries rise with rank, which is the same direction, but they rise at different rates. One rule settles both misreads: visually, what matters is not crossing and not direction, but whether the profiles are non-parallel. Then we still test the pattern formally. [NEXT]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
Test the pattern









Figure 11














Figure 12








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~84s] Now let us apply the discipline of testing. The profiles appear non-parallel, but a picture is not inference, so we must test the pattern formally. Because rank has three levels, the interaction is carried by two terms, and we test those two terms together with a single omnibus F. Consider the verdict. The F statistic on two and five-oh-eight degrees of freedom is zero point nine zero one, and the p value is zero point four one, which is not significant. The evidence-based conclusion is that we have no statistical evidence that the population gender gap varies by rank, and the apparent fanning is well within what sampling noise would produce. As a result, we report the additive model, which estimates one common gender gap across ranks. [CLICK 1] This does not mean interactions never matter. The schematic case on the right shows strongly non-parallel profiles together with a significant test, which you would then read cell by cell, and you will meet a statistically supported example later in the course. The lesson is to test first, before you report from the picture. [NEXT]

title: Two-Way Cell Means and the Interaction Plot
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A two-factor design is a grid of cell means — each cell is the reference plus the coefficients that apply. A simple effect is a difference of two cells: choose one rank, then subtract Male minus Female. On the interaction plot the vertical gap is the simple effect, the gender gap at that rank; non-parallel profiles are interaction-shaped in the sample — but crossing isn’t required, and a shared direction doesn’t rule out interaction. And always test before you report from the picture: here F(2, 508) = 0.901, p = .41, so report the additive model — one common estimated gap across ranks.




Next: Recovering common model quantities by adding the coefficients that apply. Notes, Ch 5 §Categorical × Categorical · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~71s] Let us gather the main ideas to carry with you. A two-factor design is a grid of cell means, and each cell is the reference plus the coefficients that apply to it. A simple effect is a difference of two cells, found by choosing one rank and subtracting Male minus Female. When you plot the cells you obtain two profiles, and profiles that are non-parallel are interaction-shaped in the sample, but remember that the lines need not cross, and that both profiles going up does not rule out interaction. Above all, test before you report from the picture, because here the omnibus test was not significant, so we report one common estimated gap across ranks. Notice one final connection: a cell mean and a simple effect were both the same move of adding the coefficients that apply. In the next lecture, we will use that move to recover common model quantities. [END]

title: Recover Model Quantities by Adding Coefficients
text:
Five recoveries, one move










Figure 1







[DECK ch05-09 · 6 min · ~763 words · clicks 7 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 0 · ~39s] One idea connects several computations we have already used. Each recovery uses the same move: start at the reference, and add the coefficients that apply. A group mean, an adjusted mean, a group slope, a cell mean, and a simple effect are five different names for that kind of sum. Once you see this, five separate computations become a single routine. Let us work through the most detailed case, the two-way simple effect, and then connect all five. [NEXT]

title: Recover Model Quantities by Adding Coefficients
text:
Rebuild a cell mean









Figure 2














Figure 3















Figure 4








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 2 · ~76s] Let us take the target cell, the Male, Full professor cell in the far corner. We start at the reference. Alpha is the reference cell, which is Female and Assistant, and it helps to name both references every time, because every other coefficient is measured relative to them. [CLICK 1] Now we add only the coefficients whose dummies switch on. Because the cell is Male, we add beta-one. Because it is Full rank, we add beta-three. And because it is Male and Full at the same time, we also add the interaction, beta-five. A common error is to add every coefficient in the table. Instead, add only the terms that match this cell. The two coefficients we do not add, beta-two and beta-four, belong to Associate. [CLICK 2] Adding the applicable terms gives the predicted cell, sixty-one thousand seven hundred forty-five dollars. Notice that we never substituted a coefficient value; we only named which coefficients define this cell. [NEXT]

title: Recover Model Quantities by Adding Coefficients
text:
Simple effect = main + interaction









Figure 5














Figure 6















Figure 7








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 2 · ~59s] Now consider a simple effect. It combines a main effect and an interaction. We want the gender gap at Full. In symbols that is beta-one plus beta-five, and we will build it from the cells you can see. [CLICK 1] Start at the reference rank, with the gender gap among Assistants. Forty-one thousand five hundred nine minus thirty-seven thousand five hundred ninety-three gives three thousand nine hundred sixteen. [CLICK 2] Now add how much the gap changes from Assistant to Full, which is two thousand eight hundred seventy-six using the rounded displayed cells. Three thousand nine hundred sixteen plus two thousand eight hundred seventy-six equals six thousand seven hundred ninety-two, the gap at Full. This is a main effect plus an interaction: the reference gap, plus the change in that gap. [NEXT]

title: Recover Model Quantities by Adding Coefficients
text:
Difference of differences









Figure 8














Figure 9








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~67s] Let us look more closely at that two thousand eight hundred seventy-six, because it is a difference of differences. Recall that the Full gap is six thousand seven hundred ninety-two, and the Assistant gap is three thousand nine hundred sixteen. [CLICK 1] Subtracting one from the other gives two thousand eight hundred seventy-six using the rounded displayed cells. Symbolically, this is the Male-by-Full beta-five contrast; at full precision, the coefficient rounds to about two thousand eight hundred seventy-seven, so the one-dollar difference is just rounding. This is what an interaction contrast represents: how much the Full gap exceeds the Assistant gap. There is an honest caveat that matters. This rank-to-rank change is not significant: the F statistic on two and five hundred eight degrees of freedom is zero point nine zero one, with a p-value of zero point four one. The gaps differ in this particular sample, but we have no statistical evidence that the population gender gap varies by rank. We are learning the machinery here, not claiming a statistically supported population change. [NEXT]

title: Recover Model Quantities by Adding Coefficients
text:
Same move, four guises









Figure 10














Figure 11















Figure 12








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 2 · ~62s] This recap shows the same move across four settings. Start with the simplest one. A group mean is the reference group mean plus the group offset, which is alpha plus beta-j. You have been doing this since Chapter One. [CLICK 1] An adjusted mean has the same skeleton, but now you also add beta times x-bar, which simply means you evaluate every group at the same value of x. A group slope is the reference slope plus the interaction, which is beta-one plus beta-three. Each of these is still a sum. [CLICK 2] And the simple effect we just built is beta-one plus beta-five, the Male-Full cell minus the Female-Full cell, giving that faculty Full gap of six thousand seven hundred ninety-two dollars. Four guises, one move: start with the relevant reference quantity, and add what applies. [NEXT]

title: Recover Model Quantities by Adding Coefficients
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Start at the reference — add the coefficients whose dummies switch on. That one sum recovers a group mean, an adjusted mean, a group slope, a cell mean, and a simple effect. Add only what applies (Male · Full is α + β₁ + β₃ + β₅, not the whole table), and name the reference — Female, Assistant — every time. A simple effect is a main effect + an interaction; the interaction contrast is a difference of differences. Using rounded displayed cells, that contrast is $2,876; at full precision, the coefficient rounds to about $2,877. Here the rank-to-rank change is not significant (F(2, 508) = 0.901, p = .41), so report one common estimated gender gap across ranks.




Next: How t-tests and ANOVA can be written as matching regression models. Notes, Ch 5 §Categorical × Categorical · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~48s] Let us carry one idea away from this lecture. A single move connects these models: start at the reference, and add the coefficients whose dummies switch on. That sum is a group mean, an adjusted mean, a group slope, a cell mean, and a simple effect. Add only what applies, and name the reference every time. Remember as well that here the rank-to-rank change is not significant, so we report one common estimated gender gap across ranks. In the next lecture, we will show how the t-test, one-way ANOVA, and two-way ANOVA can be written as matching regression models. [END]

title: One Regression Engine, Three Procedures
text:
Three procedures, one engine










Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[DECK ch05-10 · 8 min · ~1005 words · clicks 7 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 2 · ~77s] Let us close a loop that has been open since Session one. Over three weeks we met a t-test, then one-way ANOVA, then two-way ANOVA, and each one felt like a brand-new procedure. The point of this lecture is that they use the same regression engine, with a different design matrix at each rung. Picture a ladder, where each rung pairs a procedure with its matching regression representation. The bottom rung is the pooled two-sample t-test, which compares two groups using a single dummy variable, and its test statistic is t. [CLICK 1] Step up one rung to one-way ANOVA, which handles three groups or more. Notice that the only thing that changed is the design matrix: it is now wider, holding k minus one dummies, and its test statistic is F. [CLICK 2] Step up once more to two-way ANOVA, which adds dummies plus products. Its test statistic is still F. The design matrix is different at each rung, so the fitted values and fit statistics can differ across rungs, but each procedure has a matching regression version. Let us now trace that equivalence carefully. [NEXT]

title: One Regression Engine, Three Procedures
text:
“Different procedures, surely?”
Common misconception

“A t-test, an ANOVA, a regression — different names, different output tables. They must give different answers.”










Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 1 · ~79s] Here is the objection, stated in a student’s voice. A t-test, an ANOVA, and a regression can feel like different procedures because the output tables look different, so surely they must disagree. This is a common misconception, and it is worth unpacking carefully. Begin with the smallest rung, comparing male and female salary. We run the pooled two-sample t-test, and I will say pooled every time, because this is the equal-variance version with var-dot-equal set to TRUE, never the Welch version. We then run a regression of salary on a single gender dummy. [CLICK 1] Read the t statistic first. It is eight point seven seven, and it is identical on both sides, with only the sign able to flip depending on the contrast direction. When you square the full-precision t, you get the regression’s overall F, which is seventy-six point nine six after rounding. That is, t-squared equals F as an exact identity at full precision, not something you confirm by squaring the rounded eight point seven seven. The pooled t-test has a one-dummy regression representation. [NEXT]

title: One Regression Engine, Three Procedures
text:
Rung 2: the receipt









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~81s] Rung two moves to the three faculty ranks, and this slide is the receipt. On the left is the anova table built from the model, and on the right is the summary of the very same fit. [CLICK 1] Notice the numbers as they line up. The overall F, on two and five hundred eleven degrees of freedom, is two hundred sixty-two point five four, and it reads the same on both sides. The R squared is zero point five one, and the Root MSE, which is the residual standard error, is eight thousand nine hundred seventeen. These values are not merely close; they are identical to the decimal within this one-way rank model. Rank is a strong effect here, with a p-value below point zero zero one, but the point is not significance. The point is that the one-way ANOVA table and the regression summary are the same fitted model printed twice. Let us also be precise about which quantities match: within this matched pair, the fitted values, F, p, R squared, and Root MSE all agree, while the coefficient meanings do not, because those depend on the coding. [NEXT]

title: One Regression Engine, Three Procedures
section: Rung 3: and you get effects back
text:
Rung 3: and you get effects back









Figure 8














Figure 9















Figure 10








[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 2 · ~78s] Rung three is the full two-way model, with gender crossed with rank. This gives six cells, built from dummies plus products, with its own overall F and its own R squared. Because it is still a regression, you can read the effects right back out of it. [CLICK 1] Consider the Full professors. The predicted salary for women is fifty-four thousand nine hundred fifty-three, and the predicted salary for men is sixty-one thousand seven hundred forty-five. [CLICK 2] Subtract one from the other and you recover the Full gender gap, which is six thousand seven hundred ninety-two dollars. This is a simple effect, recovered as beta-one plus beta-five. One honest flag belongs right on the chart, however. Whether the population gender gap changes from rank to rank, which is the interaction, is not statistically supported: F on two and five hundred eight is zero point nine zero one, with a p-value of point four one. The equivalence claim is separate from that verdict: the two-way ANOVA representation and the regression representation match whether or not an effect is statistically supported. [NEXT]

title: One Regression Engine, Three Procedures
text:
The chain is complete









Figure 11














Figure 12








[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~57s] Here is the chain we have been building since Session three. Link one, the pooled t-test matches a dummy regression, is done. Link two, the one-way ANOVA F, was lit in Session five. And today we add link three: the two-way ANOVA, interactions and all, now lit. [CLICK 1] With that, the chain is complete. Within each rung, the named procedure and its matching regression representation share the same fitted values, F, p, R squared, and Root MSE. Across rungs, those values can change because the design matrix changes. So when someone says that ANOVA and regression must disagree just because the output tables have different names, the careful answer is this: in these OLS versions, each procedure has a matching regression representation with the same fitted-model results. This is precisely why we taught regression first: once you learn the general engine, methods that look new become design matrices you already know how to read. [NEXT]

title: One Regression Engine, Three Procedures
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The t-test, one-way ANOVA, and two-way ANOVA all use the same OLS engine. Within each rung, the named procedure and its matching regression representation give the same fitted values, F, p, R², and Root MSE. Across rungs, the design matrix changes, so those fit statistics can change. The t-test rung matches the pooled two-sample t-test, and t² = F. That is why the course taught regression first.




Next: Reading the Type-III table — when the interaction matters, the effect for whom? Notes, Ch 5 §Categorical × Categorical · Practice Quiz 5

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~52s] Let us close with a few sentences worth remembering. The t-test, one-way ANOVA, and two-way ANOVA all use the same OLS engine. Within each rung, the procedure and its matching regression representation share the same fitted values, F, p, R squared, and Root MSE. Across rungs, those values can change because the design matrix changes. The coefficient meanings also depend on the coding, so those need careful interpretation. Recall that the t-test rung is the pooled one, and that t-squared equals F. This is why we taught regression first. Next time, we will see that when an interaction is significant, a pooled main effect is incomplete, so we read the Type-three table and ask the sharper question of the effect for whom. The notes and chapter practice are linked just below. [END]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
section: Which row do you read first?
text:
Which row do you read first?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch05-11 · 7 min · ~944 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~60s] This is a table you will meet on every two-way model, with one row for gender, one row for rank, and one row for their interaction. The natural question is which row you should read first. Almost every eye travels straight to the top, to the two main-effect rows, and treats the interaction row at the bottom as a kind of footnote. [CLICK 1] The correct order is the reverse of that instinct. You read the interaction row first, even though it sits last in the table. The reason is that this row decides whether the rows above it actually mean what you think they mean. Until you know how the interaction behaves, you cannot safely interpret the main effects, and so the interaction row sets the terms for everything else. [NEXT]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
text:
Read the interaction row first









Figure 3














Figure 4















Figure 5








[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~76s] Let us read that row for the faculty model. The interaction row reports an F on two and five hundred eight degrees of freedom of zero point nine zero one, with a p-value of zero point four one. [CLICK 1] It is better to build those degrees of freedom than to memorize them. The numerator is a minus one times b minus one, where a is the number of gender levels and b is the number of rank levels. With two genders and three ranks, that is one times two, which gives two. The denominator is the residual degrees of freedom, namely five hundred eight, drawn from five hundred fourteen faculty in total. [CLICK 2] Here is why this row comes first. In this full two-way model, the interaction F is the same in the Type Two and Type Three checks we are using, so it gives us a stable anchor before we touch the more contrast-sensitive main-effect rows. In this case the p-value sits above zero point zero five, which means there is no evidence that the gender gap varies with rank. [NEXT]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
section: If it’s significant: the effect for whom?
text:
If it’s significant: the effect for whom?









Figure 6














Figure 7








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~62s] Now suppose that same row had come back significant instead. Consider what that changes. Picture two groups, where Group A barely moves across the levels of the moderator and Group B climbs steeply. The gap between them is not a single number, because it changes depending on where you stand. [CLICK 1] Now look at the main-effect row, which reports one pooled number, drawn here as the dashed average line. The risk is that students read this single number as the whole answer. A significant interaction does not mean the average is impossible to compute; it means that the pooled average hides the group-specific pattern and should not be the headline. As a result, you stop asking only what the effect is, and you ask the more precise question instead: the effect for whom. The honest answer is to report the effect separately for each group. [NEXT]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
text:
The reporting rule
Two roads — the interaction F row picks one








Read the interaction first — then report


Significant? The pooled main-effect rows are incomplete — report per-group (simple) effects: the effect for whom? If you must show main effects, use Type III — but they’re conditional (at-the-mean), not the headline; the plotted simple slopes communicate better.
Not significant? (our faculty model) — drop the interaction, fit the additive model, read one gap directly. Type II and Type III now agree.






[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~56s] Let us state the reporting rule, with both roads laid out on one card. [CLICK 1] When the interaction is significant, the pooled main-effect rows are incomplete, so you report the simple effects, the effect for whom. If you must show Type Three main effects, it is important to know what they are. They are conditional effects, measured at the mean, rather than the overall average that people tend to assume. The plotted simple slopes communicate the story far better. When the interaction is not significant, as in our faculty case with a p-value of zero point four one, you drop the interaction, fit the additive model, and read one gap directly. With the interaction gone, Type Two and Type Three now agree. [NEXT]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
section: Type II vs Type III, in one breath
text:
Type II vs Type III, in one breath









Figure 8














Figure 9








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~57s] Let us compare Type Two and Type Three in one breath. Both control each main effect for the other main effect. Type Three additionally controls for the interaction. The key point, worth saying carefully, is that the interaction F is the same in both, which is exactly why you anchor on it. [CLICK 1] Now consider the R setup point. To obtain a Type Three table aligned with the Stata and S-P-S-S convention, you first fit with sum contrasts, written contr dot sum, and only then call car-Anova with type three. If you skip that step, the main-effect rows can answer a different coding-dependent question. The useful boundary is that this affects the main-effect rows, not the interaction test we anchored on. [NEXT]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
text:
The rule on one card









Figure 10














Figure 11








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~57s] Here is the whole habit on a single card, and you have seen this exact diagram before, when we tested the interaction. Begin at the gold box and read the interaction F row first. The verdict you find there chooses your road. [CLICK 1] If the row is significant, you take the left road, where the main effects are incomplete and you report the effects per group, the effect for whom. If the row is not significant, you take the right road, where the pooled main effects are fine and you report the additive model. Notice the footnote, however, because a p-value is not a blind on-and-off switch. The table is one diagnostic, not the headline, and theory and design have a vote as well. [NEXT]

title: Reading the Type-III Table: The Effect for Whom?
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Read the interaction row first. Its df are (a−1)(b−1) over the residual df, and in this full two-way model the interaction F agrees under Type II and Type III — so anchor on it. Significant → the pooled main effects are incomplete; report per-group (simple) effects — the effect for whom? Not significant → drop it, fit the additive model (faculty: F(2, 508) = 0.901, p = .41). For a Stata/SPSS-aligned Type-III table in R, fit with contr.sum first, then car::Anova(type = 3) — otherwise the main-effect rows can answer a different coding-dependent question.




Optional extension: continuous × continuous interactions live in the Chapter 5 notes. Next: Chapter 6 — four ways to use a log. Notes, Ch 5 §Type II vs Type III · Chapter 5 practice

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~51s] Let us carry the rule out the door. Read the interaction row first, because in this full two-way model it gives you a stable anchor before the main-effect rows. When that row is significant, report the effect per group, the effect for whom. When it is not significant, as with the faculty model at a p-value of zero point four one, fit the additive model. For a Type Three table in R that matches the Stata and S-P-S-S convention, fit with contr dot sum first and only then call car-Anova. Continuous by continuous interactions are available as an extension in the Chapter Five notes; next, Chapter Six begins with four ways to use a log, and we ask when changing the scale makes a relationship easier to read. [END]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
Income-style data often bends



Figure 1

[DECK ch06-01 · 7 min · ~929 words · clicks 9 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 0 · ~35s] Many income-style variables bend on the raw scale, so the first move is to check the scatter. When the bend is multiplicative, a straight line cannot keep up, sitting too high through the middle and too low at the ends. A common remedy is to take a logarithm, but a logarithm quietly changes how you read the slope. If you misread it, you will misstate your result, and this is one of the most common reporting slips in this chapter. Let us make that reading automatic. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
A log straightens the bend









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] To see why a logarithm helps, look at this real data, which plots crime against urbanization. Many quantities grow by percentages rather than by fixed amounts, and that kind of growth traces a curve that no straight line can sit on. Notice how the rust line bows away from the dashed smoother. [CLICK 1] Now take the logarithm of the outcome, which keeps the same data and only rescales the vertical axis. Notice that the cloud snaps into a band, so the straight line and the smoother now lie right on top of each other. A logarithm turns multiplying into adding, and a straight line is exactly what adds. That is why we log. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
Four cells, four readings









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6















Figure 7















Figure 8








[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 4 · ~80s] Here is the map. Across the top we ask whether x is raw or logged, and down the side we ask whether y is raw or logged, which gives four cells and four readings. [CLICK 1] In the top-left cell nothing is logged. This is the level-level case, the ordinary slope, where one more unit of x goes with beta units of y. You already know this one. [CLICK 2] Next, log the outcome. This is the log-level case, where one more unit of x goes with about a hundred beta percent in y, so the slope is read as a percentage of the outcome. [CLICK 3] Now log the predictor instead. This is the level-log case, where we work in percents on x, so a one-percent rise in x goes with about beta over a hundred units of y. [CLICK 4] Finally, log both. This is the log-log case, where a one-percent rise in x goes with about a beta-percent change in y, which we call the elasticity. The same beta is read four different ways; you simply pick the cell. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
Say the log-y slope right
Common log-y misread










Figure 9















The one to remember


A log-y coefficient of about 0.69 (= ln 2) does not mean +69%. Since exp(0.69) ≈ 2, the outcome roughly doubles — about +100%.






[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 2 · ~66s] This next point matters directly for clear reporting. When the outcome is logged, calling beta the percent change is only the quick rule, which is a hundred times beta. The exact rule is a hundred times the quantity e-to-the-beta minus one. [CLICK 1] Notice the gap between the two. For an illustrative beta of zero point two zero, the quick rule gives plus twenty percent, while the exact rule gives plus twenty-two point one. For a small beta the two nearly agree, but for a larger beta they split apart. [CLICK 2] Here is the one to remember. A log-y coefficient near zero point six nine does not mean plus sixty-nine percent. Because e-to-the-zero-point-six-nine is about two, the outcome roughly doubles, which is about plus one hundred percent. As a result, for a large beta you should always quote a hundred times e-to-the-b minus one. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
The doubling rule for log-x
Common log-x misread

With log(x), a doubling of x moves y by β·ln2 ≈ 0.69β units — not β.










Figure 10







[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~55s] There is one more common slip, and now it is x that is logged. People often call the log-x coefficient the change in y when x doubles, but that change is not beta. The reason is that doubling x adds ln two to the logged predictor, and ln two is about zero point six nine. As a result, a doubling moves y by beta times ln two, which is roughly zero point six nine beta units. [CLICK 1] Take an illustrative beta of two. The naive read says two, but the honest number is two times ln two, which is about one point three nine units, not two. So whenever x is logged, multiply by ln two to read the effect of a doubling. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
Go straighten one yourself

The Log-Transformation Explorer from the Chapter 6 notes. Toggle the four specifications and watch the curved cloud straighten when the right log is applied. The app’s data is simulated.Try the live version in the course notes — flip each switch and watch the cloud straighten.

[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Now it is your turn to drive it, using the Log-Transformation Explorer in the chapter. You have one scatterplot and four switches, for logging x, logging y, both, or neither. Its data are simulated, so you may play freely. As you flip a switch, the cloud either bends or snaps straight, and a pill states the reading rule for that choice. Hunt for the toggle that lays the line near the smoother, because that is the candidate specification to investigate, and the pill tells you exactly how to say it. Go and straighten one. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
One trap left: R-squared
R² scale warning







Never compare R² across response scales


A y model and a log(y) model explain variance on different scales — different total sums of squares. Their R² are not comparable, even when they look close.





Compare R² only within the same outcome — level-level vs level-log (both y), or log-level vs log-log (both log y). Across scales, judge by how straight the cloud gets or by back-transformed prediction error — never raw R².


[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~44s] Here is the last point, and it closes a loop from Chapter One. When you have a raw-y model and a log-y model, do not pick the winner by R-squared. [CLICK 1] The reason is that the two models explain different outcomes, with different total variation underneath them, so their R-squared values are not measured on the same ruler, and a larger one means nothing across scales. Within a single outcome the comparison is fine. Across the logarithm, judge instead by which picture straightens the cloud, or by back-transformed prediction error, and never by the raw R-squared. [NEXT]

title: Four Ways to Use a Log
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A log changes how you read β, not how hard it is. Pick the cell: raw → units; log y → ≈ 100β% (exactly 100[exp(β) − 1]%); log x → ≈ β/100 units per 1%, and β·ln2 ≈ 0.69β per doubling; log–log → elasticity, β%. And never compare R² across response scales.




Next: The slope that changes — reading a quadratic. Notes, Ch 6 §Log Transformations

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~38s] Here is the whole idea. A logarithm does not make beta harder; it changes how you read it. If nothing is logged, beta is in units of y. If y is logged, beta is approximately a percent change, and the exact percent change is one hundred times e-to-the-b minus one. If x is logged, a one-percent rise in x is beta over one hundred units of y, and a doubling is beta times ln two. If both are logged, beta is an elasticity. And never rank R-squared across response scales. Next we will study a curve where no single number is the slope: the quadratic. [END]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
section: So there’s a U-shape in my data, right?
text:
So there’s a U-shape in my data, right?



Figure 1

[DECK ch06-02 · 7 min · ~919 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~48s] Suppose that you fit a quadratic and the x-squared coefficient comes back significant. The natural reflex is to conclude that there must be a U-shape in your data, and that conclusion is exactly the trap we want to avoid. Look carefully at this curve. It is a parabola, but your data, the shaded strip in the middle, sit entirely to the right of its minimum. The bottom of the U lies off-screen, far to the left. A significant x-squared coefficient is evidence that the fitted quadratic is curved; it does not tell you that the bottom of the U lies inside the observed range of your data. [NEXT]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
section: The slope is not one number
text:
The slope is not one number


Figure 2








The trap in your output


You get two coefficients — β₁ and β₂. Neither one is “the effect.” Never read β₁ or β₂ in isolation.






[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~67s] Let us understand why a single significant number does not give you a U. The reason is that on a curve there is no single slope to report. As you walk along this parabola, the steepness keeps changing: on the left arm the curve is steep and falling, at the bottom it is momentarily flat, and on the right arm it is steep and rising. It is the same curve at three different points, yet each point has a different slope. [CLICK 1] This is where the trap appears in your output. The software hands you two coefficients, beta-one and beta-two, and it is tempting to call beta-one the effect of x. Notice, however, that beta-one is only the slope at one particular spot, namely where x is zero. That is why the reporting rule is firm: never read beta-one or beta-two on its own. [NEXT]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
text:
The slope is β₁ + 2β₂x

\[\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \beta_1 + 2\beta_2 x\]



The slope is a function of x, not a number. Plug in an x, get the slope there. \(\beta_2 > 0\) → convex (a minimum); \(\beta_2 < 0\) → concave (a maximum).



[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~72s] Since no single coefficient is the slope, we need a way to express the slope itself, and there is one formula worth committing to memory. [CLICK 1] The slope at any point is beta-one plus two beta-two x. Notice that beta-two carries the factor of two and multiplies x, so as x moves, the slope rises or falls along with it. That is precisely why the steepness changed as we walked across the curve. [CLICK 2] Read this expression as a function of x, never as a single number: you choose a value of x and it returns the slope at that value. In a quadratic model, the sign of beta-two tells us which way the parabola opens. When beta-two is positive, the curve is convex and its turning point is a minimum; when beta-two is negative, the curve is concave and its turning point is a maximum. For our data, beta-two is positive, so the curve is convex and the bottom is a minimum. [NEXT]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
text:
Worked: the slope climbs 0.47 → 0.73









Figure 3














Figure 4








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~64s] Let us now pour our own numbers into that formula and draw the slope of GCSE on LRT across the data. As the picture shows, for a quadratic the slope function is a line rather than a flat constant. [CLICK 1] At the lowest LRT the slope is about zero point four seven, and at the highest it is about zero point seven three, with a value of about zero point six for the average student. The slope is positive throughout and grows slightly larger as LRT increases. The pointwise ninety-five-percent band stays above zero across the observed range, giving evidence that the slope remains positive there. The honest description is that GCSE increases with LRT, very slightly faster at higher LRT. This is not a case of diminishing returns, and it is not a U-shape that you can actually see in the data. [NEXT]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
section: The turning point is outside the data
text:
The turning point is outside the data







Check before you claim a U


The turning point sits at \(-\beta_1/(2\beta_2)\) — here a minimum at about −141. The data only runs −29.35 to 30.16. The bottom of the U is nowhere near the data, so over the observed range the relationship is effectively monotone increasing.





And the curvature is tiny: the quadratic term lifts R² by only about 0.001 — “significant” mostly because n = 4,059 is large. A pure significance-vs-importance echo (ML-03.7).


[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~61s] Now we ask the question that settles the matter, which is where the turning point lies. The turning point is located at minus beta-one over two beta-two, and here that is a minimum at about minus one forty-one. Our LRT scores, however, only run from minus twenty-nine to thirty, so the bottom of the U sits far off to the left. Within the range of the data, the curve simply rises, so the relationship is monotone increasing. [CLICK 1] Here is the honest beat. The curvature is tiny: the x-squared term lifts R-squared by only about zero point zero zero one, and it reaches significance mainly because n is four thousand fifty-nine, a very large sample. Recall the distinction between significance and importance. This is exactly that case, where significant does not mean important. [NEXT]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
text:
First, hygiene — and never extrapolate







Center x before you square it


Center first (\(x_c = x - \bar{x}\)): it cuts the correlation between \(x\) and \(x^2\), and it makes \(\beta_1\) the slope at the mean — not the slope at \(x = 0\), which is often outside the data.





(Here LRT is already about mean-zero, so centering barely moves this fit — but it’s the rule. In applied models, center the predictor before adding its squared term for this same reason.)


[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~57s] There are two habits to practice before fitting any polynomial. The first is to center x before you square it. Centering reduces the correlation between x and x-squared, and it makes beta-one the slope at the mean of x, which is an interpretable spot, rather than the slope at x equals zero. [CLICK 1] One honest caveat belongs here. In this example, LRT is already close to mean-zero, so centering barely moves this particular fit; it remains the rule nonetheless. In applied models, center the predictor before adding its squared term for this same reason. The second habit is never to extrapolate. Beyond the range of the data, a polynomial can swing in any direction, as we saw with that minimum at minus one forty-one. [NEXT]

title: The Slope That Changes: Reading a Quadratic
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


In \(y = \alpha + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2\), no single number is “the effect.” The slope is \(\beta_1 + 2\beta_2 x\) — it changes with x. So never read \(\beta_1\) or \(\beta_2\) alone. Find the turning point \(-\beta_1/(2\beta_2)\) and check it’s inside the data before you claim a U. A “significant” curve can still be unimportant — significance is not importance (ML-03.7).




Next: Detect, then choose — a curve decision guide. Notes, Ch 6 §Polynomial Regression

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~51s] Let us gather the whole idea into a single breath. In a quadratic, no single number is the effect, since the slope is beta-one plus two beta-two x, which changes with x; for that reason you must never read beta-one or beta-two on its own. Always locate the turning point and check that it lands inside your data before you claim a U-shape. Here it did not, so within our data the curve simply rises. Remember as well that a significant curve can still be unimportant. In the next lecture, we will see how to spot a bend, and then let theory choose the form. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
section: Your scatter may bend. Now what?
text:
Your scatter may bend. Now what?










Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch06-03 · 8 min · ~1021 words · clicks 4 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Your scatter has a faint bend in it, and the temptation is to reach for a curve right away, perhaps an x-squared term or a log. Notice the reflex we want to catch here, which is adding a curve simply because it makes the model look more sophisticated. That reasoning runs backwards.
