The curve needs a justification; it is not the justification by itself. [CLICK 1] For that reason, we ask the data first. This teal smoother is called LOWESS. It does not impose one global formula; it gives a flexible diagnostic trace of the local pattern in the points. The plan is to detect, then choose, and it takes five moves that end in a single sentence. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
text:
Step 1 — detect, don’t assume


Figure 3

[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 0 · ~58s] The first move is to see the bend before you model it. LOWESS is a smoother that does not impose a single global shape; it follows local patterns in the data. You lay it over your scatter and ask whether it wanders away from a straight line. Look at the two curves now. The slate line is the straight fit, and the teal dashed curve is the LOWESS. Notice that they hug each other across the whole range. This suggests the curvature is negligible, so a straight line is honest here, and that is what a relationship with little visible bend looks like. If the LOWESS had peeled away from the line, sagging in the middle or arching upward, then you would have diagnostic evidence of curvature to check against theory. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
section: Step 2 — let theory name the form
text:
Step 2 — let theory name the form


Figure 4


No strong theory? Use LOWESS as a diagnostic — choose a simple candidate that matches the stable pattern it suggests.



[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~66s] Suppose now that LOWESS suggests a stable bend. The next question is which form to fit, and your theory answers that question, because the shape you expect names the tool you reach for. Consider diminishing returns, where the first five tutoring hours matter far more than hours twenty-five to thirty; that shape calls for a log. Consider an inverted-U, where both small and large classes underperform the mid-sized ones; that shape calls for a quadratic. Consider a threshold, where crossing a proficiency benchmark lifts a student up a level; that shape calls for a piecewise fit. In every case the form follows the story you can tell, not the asterisks on a coefficient. [CLICK 1] When you have no strong theory to lean on, that is precisely when LOWESS leads as a diagnostic: you read the shape it draws and then match the parametric form to it cautiously. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
section: Step 3 — compare honestly, not by p
text:
Step 3 — compare honestly, not by p


Figure 5







Don’t let raw R² decide


R² only ever climbs as you add terms — it always “prefers” the wigglier model. For nested same-response models on the same rows, use an F-test (anova()). Use AIC / BIC only for same-response, same-row comparable likelihoods.





On gcse the criteria genuinely disagree — AIC picks the cubic, BIC picks the linear — and the cubic term’s p = 0.052, a hair above .05. No single number decides.


[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~70s] The third move is to choose between the candidates. Here the visual keeps the two main shapes on screen, linear and quadratic, over the LOWESS benchmark. The criteria were computed over linear, quadratic, and cubic fits. Notice that the quadratic tracks the LOWESS closely while the straight line drifts away at the ends. The question is which one truly wins. The answer is not raw R squared, because R squared only ever climbs as you add terms, so it always crowns the wigglier model. Instead, compare fairly: use an F-test for nested models fit to the same response and rows, and use AIC or BIC only for same-response, same-row comparable likelihoods. Across response scales, compare predictions on a common outcome scale. [CLICK 1] Now look closely at what they say. AIC picks the cubic, while BIC picks the linear, because BIC penalizes complexity more harshly at large n. That is, the two criteria genuinely disagree. As for the cubic term, its p-value is point oh five two, which is a hair above point oh five. This means the p-value is no cliff edge, and even AIC and BIC cannot agree. As a result, no single number decides this, and theory breaks the tie. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
text:
More degrees is not better


Figure 6

[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 0 · ~51s] You might still wonder why not simply raise the degree until R squared looks beautiful. This is simulated data, the same eleven points fitted three different ways. At degree one we have a flat line that misses the hump entirely, which is underfitting. At degree two we have a clean curve passing through the cloud, which is sensible. At degree eight the curve threads every single point, and notice how wildly the right edge swings upward, which is overfitting. That swing is the Runge problem: high-degree polynomials oscillate violently near the boundaries. The data here are simulated, but the lesson is real, namely that more degrees is not better. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
text:
Go slide it yourself

The Polynomial Degree Chooser in the course notes — drag the degree d and watch the fit go underfit → sensible → wildly overfit. Its data is simulated.Try the live version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 0 · ~45s] Here is that same idea in a form you can experiment with yourself, the Polynomial Degree Chooser from the chapter, which also runs on simulated data. As you drag the slider, you can watch the fit respond. At degree one the fit is too stiff to follow the bend; around degree two or three it sits just right; and as you push it toward degree eight it goes wild at the edges, which is the Runge problem appearing live in your hands. The interactive version lives in the notes, so you can go and slide it for yourself. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
section: Steps 4 and 5 — stay in, center first
text:
Steps 4 and 5 — stay in, center first







4 · Stay inside the observed range


A polynomial does anything past your data — never extrapolate a curve. Our gcse LRT only runs −29.35 to 30.16; a fit inside that says nothing outside it.












5 · Center predictors before squaring


\(x_c = x - \bar{x}\) — it cuts the collinearity between \(x\) and \(x^2\), and makes \(\beta_1\) the slope at the mean, not at \(x = 0\).






[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Two habits remain, and you have in fact already met both of them. The fourth move is to stay inside the observed range, because past the edge of your data a polynomial will do whatever it wants. Our LRT only runs from minus twenty-nine to thirty, so a fit that behaves inside that band tells you nothing outside it. Recall that minimum sitting far off at minus one forty-one from before; that is exactly the danger. [CLICK 1] The fifth move is to center your predictor before you square it. Centering cuts the collinearity between x and x-squared, and it makes beta-one the slope at the mean of x rather than the slope out at x equals zero. Two habits, and together they give you one honest curve. [NEXT]

title: Detect, Then Choose: A Curve Decision Guide
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Look for a bend with LOWESS · let theory pick the form · compare fair candidates, never raw R² · stay inside the data · and justify the curve in one sentence:

“Theory predicts diminishing returns, LOWESS suggested a stable bend, and a level-log beat the linear candidate on comparable AIC — so I used a log.”





Next: Chapter 7 — model comparison. Notes, Ch 6 §Choosing Among Approaches

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~51s] Let us draw the whole guide together. You detect the bend with LOWESS. You let theory pick the form. You compare fair candidates, and never with raw R squared. You stay inside your data, and you center first. Then comes the reporting sentence, like the one shown on the slide: theory predicted it, LOWESS suggested a stable bend, and this form won on comparable AIC. A significant x-squared term is never the justification; the reasoning is. For optional extensions, the notes cover steps, splines, and GAMs. Next, we move to Chapter 7 model comparison. [END]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: This week: building and checking models
text:
This week: building and checking models

Session 10

This week is about how professionals assemble and check a model – not one predictor at a time, but a planned sequence of models compared head to head.



Today’s one question: does adding this block of predictors actually help?



[DECK ch07-01 · 9 min · ~1141 words · clicks 8 · advances 9] [SLIDE 1/9 · clicks 1 · ~58s] This week we turn to a skill you will use in almost every analysis. The work shifts from fitting one model and reading it to assembling and checking a model as a planned sequence, compared head to head rather than one predictor at a time. [CLICK 1] Today we narrow that to one practical question. You have a model, and you are weighing whether to add a block of predictors. Does that block actually help? By the end you will know how to answer, and why a higher R-squared, on its own, is never the answer. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: “Model 2 has a higher R-squared, so it is better, right?”
text:
“Model 2 has a higher R-squared, so it is better, right?”


Figure 1

[SLIDE 2/9 · clicks 0 · ~56s] Here are three models fit to the same students. The first uses socioeconomic status; the second adds an indicator for being female; the third adds two more predictors. The R-squared climbs every time, from zero point one one eight nine, to zero point one three zero five, to zero point two one one nine. That looks decisive, but it is a trap. Adding a predictor can never lower R-squared; it only rises or stays flat, even when the predictor is pure noise. So a higher R-squared alone can never prove a model is better. We need a real test, the incremental F-test. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: “Nested” means one model sits inside the other
text:
“Nested” means one model sits inside the other


Figure 2


Reduced = the complete model with the new coefficients set to zero. The null hypothesis is that those extra betas are all zero – the new block adds nothing.



[SLIDE 3/9 · clicks 1 · ~64s] The incremental F-test only works on nested models. The inner navy box is the reduced model, holding the predictors you already had; the outer rust box is the complete model, those same predictors plus the new block. The reduced model lives entirely inside the complete model. [CLICK 1] You get the reduced model by setting the coefficients on the new block to zero. The null hypothesis is that those extra coefficients are all zero, that the new block adds nothing once your existing predictors are in place. Models with different, overlapping predictors are not nested. For those, use A-I-C or B-I-C only inside a comparable candidate set: same response, same rows, same outcome scale, and same likelihood family. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: The test asks: how much error did the block remove?
text:
The test asks: how much error did the block remove?


Figure 3


\[ F = \frac{(\text{SSE}_{\text{reduced}} - \text{SSE}_{\text{complete}})\,/\,h}{\text{SSE}_{\text{complete}}\,/\,(n - g - h - 1)} \]



[SLIDE 4/9 · clicks 1 · ~62s] The residual sum of squares is the leftover error after fitting. The reduced model leaves seven thousand seven hundred eighty-eight point one; the complete model leaves seven thousand six hundred eighty-five point two. The gap, one hundred two point nine, is the extra sum of squares the block removed. [CLICK 1] The F-test turns that gap into a verdict. Recall from the one-way ANOVA lecture that an F statistic is a ratio of two mean squares. The numerator spreads the extra sum of squares over h, the number of added coefficients or restrictions in the block, so h is the numerator degrees of freedom. The denominator is the complete model’s leftover error per degree of freedom. A large F means the block removed far more than sampling variation under the null would usually explain. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: Read one anova() comparison, line by line
text:
Read one anova() comparison, line by line









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 5/9 · clicks 2 · ~70s] The el-em and anova functions compare Model 1, socioeconomic status alone, with Model 2, which adds female. The residual degrees of freedom fall from one hundred eighty-six to one hundred eighty-five, and the residual sum of squares from seven thousand seven hundred eighty-eight point one to seven thousand six hundred eighty-five point two. [CLICK 1] The test row fills in. The Df column is one, so h equals one, and the Sum of Sq is one hundred two point nine. The F statistic is two point four eight, and the p-value, under Pr greater than F, is zero point one one seven. [CLICK 2] Because zero point one one seven sits above the five percent threshold, we do not reject the null hypothesis. We do not have statistically sufficient evidence that adding female improves fit beyond socioeconomic status. R-squared rose when we added female, yet the observed increase is compatible with sampling variation under the null. That contrast is the whole reason the test exists. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: How much better? Report the change in R-squared too
text:
How much better? Report the change in R-squared too









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 6/9 · clicks 1 · ~70s] The F-test says whether a block helps, not how much. For that, report the change in R-squared, the complete model’s R-squared minus the reduced model’s. Adding female raised it by only zero point zero one one six, about one point two percent, which was not significant. [CLICK 1] Now add minority status and school sector together, a block of two added coefficients, so h is two, with two numerator degrees of freedom. It raises R-squared by zero point zero eight one four, about eight point one percent, with an F of nine point four five and a p-value below zero point zero zero one, which is significant. As a yardstick, Cohen puts a small change near point zero two, a medium near point one three, and a large near point two six. When your model includes a planned multi-coefficient block, report the whole-block test and adapt the structure to your own data. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: The silent killer: lm() drops NA rows
text:
The silent killer: lm() drops NA rows
Pitfall

Build one analysis dataset first, then fit every model on it: analysis_df <- drop_na(data, all_of(vars))









Same response, same observations


lm() silently drops any row with a missing value on a predictor. If the complete model has extra missing values, the reduced and complete models are fit on different rows – and the extra sum of squares is no longer valid. Fix the rows once, up front.






[SLIDE 7/9 · clicks 1 · ~74s] One mistake quietly invalidates the comparison. The el-em function silently drops any row with a missing value on a predictor. If the new block has gaps the reduced predictors do not, the two models end up fit on different rows, and the difference in their residual sums of squares is no longer a valid extra sum of squares. Nothing warns you. [CLICK 1] The fix is to build one analysis dataset up front, keeping only the rows complete on every predictor any model uses, and fit every nested model on it. Confirm with the nobs function that the models share the same number of observations. Both models must be fit to the same response and the same observations. Then the comparison is honest. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
section: Go try it: real signal versus pure noise
text:
Go try it: real signal versus pure noise



The Nested Model Comparison explorer from the Chapter 7 notes, on simulated data. Toggle predictors on and watch the R-squared rise with each one – even for a predictor that is pure noise – while the incremental F-test stays honest about which additions are real.











Figure 9







[SLIDE 8/9 · clicks 1 · ~64s] The chapter’s interactive explorer makes this concrete. It runs on simulated data, which is deliberate: only when we set the true coefficients ourselves can we label some predictors as genuine and others as pure noise. The outcome is built from two real predictors, with true coefficients of one point zero and zero point eight, plus two more whose true coefficients are exactly zero. [CLICK 1] These static panels use the same coefficient pattern as the app simulation. The real predictor gives a clear upward slope; the noise predictor gives a flat line. Yet toggle on the noise predictor and the R-squared still ticks upward, because R-squared can never fall when a predictor is added. That empty rise is why we trust the F-test, not a higher R-squared. The live version is linked in the notes. [NEXT]

title: Does Adding a Block Help? The Incremental F-Test
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


To ask “does this block help?”, compare two nested models with anova(reduced, complete). The F-test says whether the block helps; the change in R-squared says how much. A higher R-squared alone never decides it – R-squared can only rise. And it is valid only on the same rows: build one analysis dataset, fit every model on it. A factor with k categories contributes k - 1 degrees of freedom; test those added coefficients as one joint block. Chapter 1’s overall F is the special case where the reduced model is intercept-only.




Next: Theory First – the five-step confirmatory strategy. Notes, Ch 7 §Comparing Models

[SLIDE 9/9 · clicks 0 · ~62s] To ask whether a block helps, compare two nested models with the anova function, where the reduced model is the complete model with the new coefficients set to zero. The F-test tells you whether the block helps; the change in R-squared tells you how much; report them together. A higher R-squared alone never settles it, because R-squared can only rise. The comparison is valid only when both models are fit on the same rows, so build one analysis dataset first. A factor with k categories contributes k minus one degrees of freedom, so test those added coefficients as one joint block. Chapter 1’s overall F-test is the special case where the reduced model is just the intercept. Model building is a planned sequence, not a fishing expedition. Next, the five-step confirmatory strategy. [END]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
text:
Where Models 1, 2, and 3 come from

Session 10


Figure 1


Adapt the sequence to your research question and data; do not discover it afterward by searching.




Model rows are fitted models. Linearity and diagnostics are required checks around that sequence.



[DECK ch07-02 · 9 min · ~1115 words · clicks 12 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 2 · ~68s] In a confirmatory analysis, you write a planned sequence of models before you fit anything. Many students wonder where that sequence comes from and how to decide what belongs in each model. Look at the screen, because this is the lecture in miniature. Model One holds the focal predictors your research question is about. Model Two adds plausible confounders. Model Three adds a prespecified interaction if theory predicts one. [CLICK 1] The structure is fixed in advance by the question, not discovered afterward by searching. This is the five-step confirmatory strategy, the central idea of the chapter, and we will treat it as a reusable workflow rather than as one specific analysis. To make it concrete we will watch it run once on a teaching dataset, but the structure is something you adapt to your own project data. [CLICK 2] One more distinction matters before we start: model rows are fitted models. Linearity and diagnostics are required checks around that sequence, even when they do not create separate model rows. [NEXT]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
section: First decide why you are modeling
text:
First decide why you are modeling
Three reasons to build a model


Explanation. Test a theory-specified relationship; the coefficients and their interpretation are what matter.




Exploration. Theory is weak, so you search the data to generate hypotheses; results are tentative until confirmed on new data.




Prediction. Forecast new cases; out-of-sample accuracy matters more than interpreting any single coefficient.



[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 3 · ~92s] Before you choose a single variable, it helps to be explicit about your goal, because three goals are easy to confuse and they impose different standards on the same model. [CLICK 1] The first goal is explanation, or confirmation. Here you test a relationship that your theory has specified in advance, and getting the coefficients and their interpretation right is what matters. [CLICK 2] The second goal is exploration. When theory is weak, you search the data to generate hypotheses rather than to confirm them, and those results stay tentative until they are confirmed on new data. [CLICK 3] The third goal is prediction. Here you forecast new cases as accurately as possible, and out-of-sample accuracy matters more than interpreting any single coefficient. The goal changes what you keep, so decide it before you start. This chapter uses the explanation goal, so the five steps that follow are the confirmatory workflow. [NEXT]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
text:
The five-step confirmatory strategy









Figure 2














Figure 3















Figure 4















Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 4 · ~120s] Here is the strategy as five ordered steps, and the order matters. The research question, not a p-value, decides what goes into the model. Step one is to include the focal predictors, the variables whose effects you care about; they go in by design to answer the research question, regardless of their significance. [CLICK 1] Step two is to include possible confounders: plausible background or pre-treatment variables associated with both the focal predictor and the outcome. They enter regardless of their own p-value, because their job is to reduce omitted-variable bias. [CLICK 2] Step three is to check linearity for your continuous predictors, and to relax that form when the evidence suggests the straight-line form is not adequate. [CLICK 3] Step four is to test interactions suggested by theory. A prespecified, central interaction is reported regardless of significance, because it allows the effect to depend on context. [CLICK 4] Step five is to run diagnostics and adjust accordingly. P-values should not decide inclusion in Steps 1 and 2; after the model is specified, they help with prespecified tests and interpretation, while diagnostics check assumptions. [NEXT]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
section: Step 1 to Step 2: the SES estimate shifts
text:
Step 1 to Step 2: the SES estimate shifts









Figure 7














Figure 8








[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~80s] Let us watch step two do its work on the High School and Beyond extract, which has one hundred eighty-eight students. The research question asks whether socioeconomic status, which we abbreviate as S-E-S, predicts math achievement, and step one holds S-E-S and school sector. The estimate for S-E-S is about two point four five, meaning that two students who differ by one unit of S-E-S differ by about two point four five points of math achievement, on average. [CLICK 1] Now step two adds plausible background variables, minority status and being female, and the S-E-S estimate shrinks to about one point six eight. After conditioning on them, the S-E-S coefficient is smaller. In this observational teaching example, that adjusted coefficient is conditional and less confounded, not proof of a causal pathway. The numbers belong to this teaching dataset; this is the kind of adjustment you make when your project includes plausible confounders. [NEXT]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
section: A confounder can stay even when p is large
text:
A confounder can stay even when p is large









Figure 9














Figure 10








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~78s] This is exactly where many people stumble. A student might say: female is not significant, so I dropped it from my model. Let us look at what the data actually show. In the step two model, the coefficient for female is about negative one point five five, and its p-value is about zero point one zero, which sits above the usual zero point zero five threshold. [CLICK 1] It is tempting to delete it, but we keep it, because its job is bias control, not passing its own significance test. Recall that a plausible confounder earns its place by helping reduce bias in the estimates you care about, and removing it can push your focal estimates back toward the less-adjusted values you were trying to avoid. As a result, a confounder stays in the model for a substantive reason, regardless of its own p-value. This is one of the most common and most costly mistakes in model building. [NEXT]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
section: Report every step, not just the winner
text:
Report every step, not just the winner









Figure 11














Figure 12








[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~84s] Transparency requires that you show the whole model-building process, not only the model you end up keeping. The table reports fitted model rows; linearity and diagnostics are required checks around the sequence even when they do not create separate rows. Step one, the focal model, has an R-squared of zero point one four five six. Step two, with the confounders, has an R-squared of zero point two one one nine. [CLICK 1] Step four adds the prespecified interaction between S-E-S and sector, and its R-squared is zero point two one eight nine. The interaction test gives an F statistic of one point six two and a p-value of about zero point two zero. That is insufficient evidence that the S-E-S slope differs by sector. Because the interaction was prespecified and central to the question, the result is still useful to report rather than quietly dropping it. By contrast, letting a stepwise algorithm choose your predictors and then calling the result theory-driven is a form of hypothesizing after the results are known. Reporting the whole sequence is what keeps the analysis transparent. [NEXT]

title: Theory First: The Five-Step Confirmatory Strategy
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The research question specifies the model, not p-values: focal predictors by design, confounders for bias control regardless of p, check linearity, test prespecified interactions and report them either way, then run diagnostics. Report the whole sequence, not just the model you kept. A planned Model 1, Model 2, Model 3 sequence is this workflow adapted to your research question.




Templates from Anatomy of the Project (ML-04.5): Project 1 · Project 2 Next: Why R-squared always goes up — and what to use instead. Notes, Ch 7 §The Confirmatory Approach

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~63s] Let us close with the principle that ties it together. The research question specifies the model, not the p-values: you include the focal predictors by design, you keep confounders for bias control regardless of their p-value, you check linearity, you test prespecified interactions and report them either way, and you finish with diagnostics. You then report the whole sequence, not only the model you kept, because the estimates moving across Model One, Model Two, and Model Three are the evidence, not a single polished final table. A planned Model One, Model Two, and Model Three sequence is this same workflow adapted to the research question and data. The project-guide templates linked on the slide give concrete versions for linear and logistic projects. In the next lecture we will see why R-squared always goes up as predictors are added, and what to use instead. [END]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
text:
A column of random numbers

Session 10









Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch07-03 · 9 min · ~1160 words · clicks 8 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us begin with a small experiment that should feel unsettling. Here is a real model of math achievement explained by socioeconomic status, and its raw R squared is zero point one one nine. Now we add a column of pure random numbers, a predictor with no connection to anything. The raw R squared does not fall, and it does not even hold still: it rises to zero point one two two. [CLICK 1] This is not a fluke of one data set. In a same-response, same-row nested OLS comparison, raw R squared can never go down when you add a predictor; it either holds steady or rises. The honest measure on the right tells a different story in this example: adjusted R squared falls from zero point one one four to zero point one one three, because the sample gain does not beat the complexity penalty. Today we will see why raw R squared is so optimistic, and what to use instead. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
text:
Least squares is an optimist


Figure 3








The trap: “a bigger R-squared means a better model”


Raw R-squared measures fit in the sample at hand. Least squares spends every extra predictor chasing sample-specific wiggles, so in same-row nested OLS, adding one can only hold R-squared steady or push it up — never down.






[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~50s] Let us see why this happens, because the reason is mechanical, not mysterious. This picture is simulated, which lets us feed in columns of pure noise one at a time and watch the raw R squared in navy. Notice that it never steps back down; in this run, it climbs in small steps as the noise piles on. [CLICK 1] The reason is that raw R squared measures the variance explained in the sample at hand, not in the population. When the response and rows stay fixed, least squares can turn that extra knob to chase the sample’s own random wiggles, so the fit can only hold or improve. That is what we mean by structurally optimistic, and it is why you cannot use raw R squared alone to compare models of different sizes. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
text:
Adjusted R-squared: the honesty tax









Figure 4






\[ R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)\,(n - 1)}{\,n - k - 1\,} \]



[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~55s] Adjusted R squared writes a penalty into the formula to undo that optimism. On the same simulated picture, the navy line is the raw R squared from before, and the rust line is the adjusted version. Notice how differently the rust line behaves: it can rise at first when the sample fit improves enough to beat the penalty, then turns over and falls steadily as the noise predictors accumulate. That downward turn is the honesty tax made visible. [CLICK 1] The formula shows where the tax comes from. Notice the term n minus k minus one in the denominator, where k is the number of predictors. As k grows, that term shrinks and the whole quantity is pulled down. So a new predictor now has to earn its keep, lifting adjusted R squared only when it explains more than the penalty costs. In the opening example, the noise column does not clear that bar, so the measure falls. More generally, a noise predictor can still raise adjusted R squared by chance; unlike raw R squared, adjusted R squared is not guaranteed to rise. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
text:
See the tax for yourself

The Nested Model Comparison explorer in the Chapter 7 notes — toggle this pure-noise predictor on and watch raw R-squared rise while the adjusted-R-squared pill drops. Its data is simulated.Try the live version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Here is that same idea in a form you can operate yourself, the Nested Model Comparison explorer from the chapter, which runs on simulated data so we can add a predictor that is pure noise by construction. In this setup, as you flip on the toggle that adds the noise predictor, the two readout pills move in opposite directions. The raw R squared pill ticks upward, because least squares cannot resist the extra slack, while at the very same moment the adjusted R squared pill drops, because the honesty tax outweighs the meaningless gain. The live version is linked in the notes, and it is well worth trying. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
section: AIC and BIC: lack-of-fit plus penalty
text:
AIC and BIC: lack-of-fit plus penalty
\[ \text{AIC} = \underbrace{-2\log L}_{\text{lack-of-fit score}} + \underbrace{2q}_{\text{penalty}} \qquad \text{BIC} = -2\log L + \underbrace{q\log(n)}_{\text{heavier penalty}} \]


First term: how badly the model misses — lower is a better fit. Second term: a charge for the \(q\) estimated parameters. Lower AIC or BIC is better.




BIC’s penalty grows with \(\log(n)\), so on large samples it charges more per parameter than AIC.



[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 2 · ~62s] Two more criteria, AIC and BIC, are the ones your project fit tables will report. Read each formula as two pieces added together. The first piece, negative two log L, you can simply treat as a lack-of-fit score that grows when the model misses the data by more, where lower is better. [CLICK 1] Now label the two pieces together: first, the lack-of-fit score, and second, the penalty, a charge for the q estimated parameters. So each criterion balances fit against complexity, and lower AIC and lower BIC are both better. This is genuinely more than a fancier R squared, because the complexity charge is explicit rather than hidden. [CLICK 2] The two differ only in how hard they charge. AIC uses two per parameter, while BIC uses log of n per parameter. On a large sample, where log of n is well above two, BIC charges considerably more per parameter, and that difference is what sometimes makes them disagree. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
section: On this data, all four agree
text:
On this data, all four agree









Figure 5














Figure 6








[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Let us put these criteria to work on four real nested models, each adding predictors to the last. The first panel shows raw R squared alone, and it climbs at every single step, from zero point one one nine at the simplest model up to zero point two two three at the fullest. By this panel alone the biggest model always wins. [CLICK 1] Now look at all four criteria together, with the best on each panel ringed in rust. Adjusted R squared peaks at the full model, at zero point two zero two, AIC reaches its lowest value there at one two two three point nine, and BIC reaches its lowest there too at one two four six point six. On this particular data set all four criteria agree and point to the full model. Let me be careful, though: the criteria do not always agree, they simply did this time. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
text:
When they disagree, report both
Two purposes

AIC aims at lower expected prediction error for new data. BIC targets model identification under standard assumptions, if the true candidate is in the set.









The trap: “lower AIC=the true model”


On the full gcse data (n = 4,059), AIC picks the cubic fit while BIC picks the linear — and the cubic term’s p = 0.052, just above .05. AIC targets prediction error, not truth. Report both.






[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~54s] We carry two criteria instead of one because they answer two different questions. AIC is aimed at lower expected out-of-sample prediction error. BIC is aimed at model identification under standard assumptions, if the true model is actually among your candidate models. [CLICK 1] Most of the time they agree, as they just did, but consider a case where they split. On the full G-C-S-E data, with four thousand and fifty nine students, AIC picks the cubic fit while BIC picks the simpler linear fit, and the cubic term’s p-value is zero point zero five two, a hair above the usual threshold. This is the trap behind the idea that a lower AIC reveals the truth, because AIC targets prediction error, not truth. No single number settles it, so the honest move is to report both criteria and state the trade-off. [NEXT]

title: Why R-squared Never Steps Down — and What to Use Instead
text:
Take this with you







The trap: “AIC is just a fancier R-squared”


No — AIC and BIC charge an explicit complexity penalty, and they are comparable only within a common candidate set: same response, same outcome scale, same rows, same likelihood family. Never compare an AIC for mathach with one for log(mathach), or models fit on different rows.





The takeaway: raw R-squared is structurally optimistic and cannot compare models of different sizes; adjusted R-squared, AIC, and BIC charge a penalty — AIC tilts toward prediction, BIC toward identification under assumptions — and when they disagree, report both.

Next: GIGO — stepwise finds signal in pure noise. Notes, Ch 7 §Model Fit Criteria

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Let us gather the whole idea, starting with the last trap to defuse. AIC is not just a fancier R squared, because it charges an explicit complexity penalty, and because an AIC value carries no meaning on its own. These criteria are comparable only within a common candidate set: the same response, the same outcome scale, the same rows, and the same likelihood family. In particular, never compare an AIC for math achievement with an AIC for the log of math achievement. [CLICK 1] So here is the whole picture. Raw R squared is structurally optimistic and cannot compare models of different sizes, while adjusted R squared, AIC, and BIC each charge a complexity penalty, with AIC tilting toward prediction and BIC toward identification under assumptions, and when they disagree you report both. In the next lecture, we will see how an automatic search can find impressive signal in pure noise. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
text:
Twenty columns of pure noise

Session 10









Figure 1














Figure 2








[DECK ch07-04 · 9 min · ~1138 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] Let us run an experiment that is designed to have no right answer. We build a data set in which the outcome and twenty candidate predictors are all independent random numbers, so the data-generating process has zero true effects. We hand all twenty columns to an automatic stepwise search, ask it to search for a lower-A-I-C subset, and repeat the whole exercise two hundred times, recording how many predictors it keeps each time. [CLICK 1] Here is what it kept. If the search were honest about empty data, almost every bar would stand at zero. Instead the bars pile up around three. It kept at least one predictor in nearly every run, even though we know, by construction, that there is nothing real to select. This is the trap we are here to disarm: the belief that because the algorithm found it, it must be real. [NEXT]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
text:
Zero effects by design









Figure 3







[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 0 · ~48s] Let us be precise about how the data are built, because the design is what gives the demonstration its force. The navy column on the left is the outcome, which we call y, and it is simply a column of random numbers. Beside it stand twenty predictors, the rust chips labelled x-one through x-twenty, and each is another column of random numbers. Every column is drawn independently of every other, so the true data-generating process has zero effects. Random sample patterns can still appear, but the honest design answer to which predictors belong in a model of y is none of them. As a result, any predictor the search keeps is what we will call a spurious selection: a pattern that happens to appear in this one sample, not a real effect that would return in fresh data. [NEXT]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
text:
What the search “found”







The trap: “it selected three predictors, so three are real”


Across 200 simulations of pure noise, stepwise selection kept 3.2 predictors on average, selected at least one in 192 of 200 runs (96.0%), and as many as 10 in a single run.






Selected is not significant. The search keeps predictors by a fit score, not by a hypothesis test — so a kept predictor is a candidate to investigate, never a finding to report.



[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~56s] Let us read the count carefully, because the language we attach to it matters. Across the two hundred simulations of pure noise, stepwise selection kept three point two predictors on average, so call it about three spurious predictors per data set. It kept at least one in one hundred ninety-two of the two hundred runs, which is ninety-six point zero percent, and only eight runs kept none, matching the zero-effect design. In its most extreme run it kept as many as ten. The tempting conclusion is that if the search selected three predictors, then three must be real. [CLICK 1] Here is the discipline that defuses that conclusion. Selected is not the same word as significant. The search did not test any predictor; it simply kept whichever columns lowered a fit score in this one sample. So a selected predictor is a candidate worth investigating, never a finding worth reporting. Keeping that distinction straight protects you from mistaking chance for evidence. [NEXT]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
section: It is not a significance test
text:
It is not a significance test
Why “the algorithm chose it” proves nothing

R’s step() selects by AIC — at each step it keeps whatever lowers the AIC score, a measure of fit penalized for complexity. It is not running a hypothesis test at each step.









The classic textbook rule uses p-values — and it is no safer


The older procedure adds the predictor with the smallest p-value and drops the largest, against a threshold like 0.05. Both rules — AIC and p-values — chase the best fit in this one sample, so both can manufacture spurious selections. The noise chart above was built with AIC.






[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] There is an important detail in how the search chooses. The procedure we just used is R’s step function, and at each step it selects by A-I-C, a measure of fit penalized for complexity, keeping whatever change lowers the A-I-C score. A-I-C selection is not a hypothesis test, so it makes no significance decision at any step. That is why we say spurious selection rather than reaching for the language of significance. [CLICK 1] You might wonder whether the older version of stepwise is any safer. The classic textbook procedure, including the version in this course’s earlier material, uses p-values: at each step it adds the predictor with the smallest p-value and drops the largest, against a threshold such as zero point zero five. But both rules, A-I-C and p-values alike, chase the best fit in the one sample at hand, so both can manufacture spurious selections. The noise demonstration was run with A-I-C, a formal fit criterion, and it still kept about three predictors from pure noise. Switching the rule does not save you. [NEXT]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
text:
In this split, stepwise loses









Figure 4














Figure 5















Figure 6








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~48s] You might expect the data-driven model to predict best, since it was tuned to the data. Let us test that honestly on the High School and Beyond extract of one hundred eighty-eight students. We compare three models by five-fold cross-validation, judged on data they have not seen, where a lower error is better: the stepwise-selected model, a parsimonious theory-driven model, and the full additive model. The detail that makes this honest is that the selection is re-run inside each training fold; selecting once on the whole sample would let the held-out rows help choose the predictors and flatter the search. [CLICK 1] Here are the three mean errors, the diamonds. They land close together, all near six point three, so we read them carefully. [CLICK 2] The arrow points to the result that should surprise you in this HSB five-fold split. The stepwise model has the highest mean error, at six point three seven two. The theory-driven model, though far simpler, comes in at six point three one zero, and the full additive model at six point two eight five. In this honest comparison, the selected procedure did not outperform the theory-driven or full additive models, while the lean theory model kept pace with the full one. Here, parsimony did not sacrifice prediction. [NEXT]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
section: A selected model is a hypothesis
text:
A selected model is a hypothesis







The core lesson


A selected model is a hypothesis, never a confirmation. The research question — not step() — should drive the model, because the algorithm has no understanding of your phenomenon. Calling a stepwise result “theory-driven” is HARKing: hypothesizing after the results are known.












Used honestly, search has its place


Exploratory selection is fine for generating hypotheses or screening a large predictor set, as long as you label it exploratory and confirm any explanatory keepers on new data. For prediction-only tasks, validate out-of-sample performance. What you may never do is present a selected predictor as confirmed.






[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us gather these threads into the lesson that governs all of model building. A selected model is a hypothesis, never a confirmation. The search has no understanding of your phenomenon; it only optimizes a score on one sample, so the research question, and not the step function, should decide what goes into your model. Taking a stepwise result and presenting it as theory-driven is a recognized error called HARKing, which means hypothesizing after the results are known. [CLICK 1] Let me be fair, though, because automatic selection is not forbidden. It has genuine uses: generating hypotheses when theory is thin, screening a very large set of candidate predictors, or building a model whose only job is prediction. For explanatory work, you label the search as exploratory and confirm any selected effects on new data. For prediction-only work, the standard is out-of-sample performance. The one thing you may never do is present whatever a search selected as if it were already confirmed. [NEXT]

title: GIGO: Stepwise Finds Signal in Pure Noise
text:
Take this with you







The trap: “the algorithm found it, so it is real”


No — automatic selection finds structure in pure noise (about 3 of 20 predictors, in 192 of 200 runs), and in this HSB five-fold split it had the highest RMSE. A selected model is a hypothesis, not a confirmation.





Let the research question drive the model, treat any selected set as exploratory, and confirm explanatory claims on new data. In this comparison, parsimony did not sacrifice prediction — the theory model kept pace with the full one.

Next: Model diagnostics — one plot, two assumptions: the residual pattern dictionary. Notes, Ch 7 §Problems with Automatic Selection

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Let us close with the trap stated plainly and then disarmed. The belief that the algorithm found it, so it is real, does not survive what we have seen. Automatic selection found structure in pure noise, keeping about three of twenty predictors in one hundred ninety-two of two hundred runs, and in this HSB five-fold split it had the highest mean error of the three models. A selected model is therefore a hypothesis, not a confirmation. [CLICK 1] Carry three habits with you. Let the research question, not the algorithm, drive the model. Treat any selected set as exploratory until new data confirms explanatory claims. And remember that in this comparison, parsimony did not sacrifice prediction, since the small theory model kept pace with the full one. In the next lecture we turn to model diagnostics, learning to read a single residual plot as a dictionary of two assumptions at once. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
section: The first plot a grader looks at
text:
The first plot a grader looks at

Chapter 8 · Diagnostics

One scatterplot. Two assumptions, read at the same time.


Residuals on the vertical axis, fitted values on the horizontal axis.


[DECK ch08-01 · 8 min · ~1032 words · clicks 6 · advances 9] [SLIDE 1/9 · clicks 1 · ~50s] When you hand in a fitted regression, the first plot a careful grader looks at is the scatterplot of residuals against fitted values. The notes call it the single most important diagnostic plot, and the reason is simple: this one picture lets you read two of the model’s assumptions at the same time. [CLICK 1] The setup is always the same. The residuals go on the vertical axis, and the fitted values, the model’s own predictions, go on the horizontal axis. In the next few slides we will learn to read the two signals this plot carries, and we will learn the fingerprint that each kind of violation leaves behind. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
Two channels in one picture









Figure 1














Figure 2















Figure 3








[SLIDE 2/9 · clicks 2 · ~76s] Here is the healthy case, drawn from the real working model rather than a simulation, so this is what a clean diagnostic actually looks like. Right now the grey points are just a shapeless cloud. [CLICK 1] Read the plot as a dashboard with two separate channels. The first channel is the shape of the LOESS curve, the smooth line drawn through the residuals, and it checks whether a straight-line mean function looks adequate. A flat curve sitting near zero is evidence of no obvious mean-shape concern. A curved smooth would suggest systematic over-prediction in some regions and under-prediction in others. [CLICK 2] The second channel is the width of the point cloud, and it checks whether the variance looks roughly constant. An even band is evidence of no obvious variance concern. A band that widens or narrows would point to unequal error variance. One plot, two assumptions, read independently, as diagnostic evidence rather than a proof certificate. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
section: Pattern one: a curve in the smooth
text:
Pattern one: a curve in the smooth


Figure 4

[SLIDE 3/9 · clicks 0 · ~56s] The first concern shows up in channel one, and from here on the panels are simulated data, so each pattern is a clean textbook case. Notice that the LOESS curve is no longer flat; it bends into a U-shape, dipping below zero in the middle and rising above it at both ends. This curvature suggests that the straight-line mean function is missing shape, because there is a curved relationship the model has failed to capture. Mean-function misspecification can distort fitted values and coefficients, especially for affected predictors and correlated covariates. A curved term, such as a quadratic, may be the right next model check when the bend traces to a specific predictor, but this plot is a clue rather than an automatic answer key. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
Pattern two: a widening band


Figure 5

Condition first: mean model + exogeneity. Main worry: conventional inference.


[SLIDE 4/9 · clicks 1 · ~70s] The second concern shows up in channel two. Here the LOESS curve stays roughly flat, so the mean shape does not look like the first problem, but the band is no longer even. The spread is narrow on the left and widens steadily to the right, forming what people often call a funnel. [CLICK 1] Say the condition carefully: if the mean model is correctly specified and predictors are exogenous, unequal variance by itself does not bias OLS coefficients. The first concern is inference, because conventional standard errors, tests, and intervals can be unreliable. The common remedy is robust standard errors, or a model that better captures the variance pattern. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
Pattern three: both at once


Figure 6

[SLIDE 5/9 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Real data is rarely tidy, so the two signals often appear together. In this panel the LOESS curve is curved and the band widens at the same time. Because the channels are separate, you read them separately: the curved smooth raises a mean-shape concern, and the widening band raises a variance concern. Each signal points to a different next question, so you address each one in turn. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
What this plot cannot see
Name the trap

The residuals-vs-fitted plot reads linearity and constant variance — and only those two.









Wrong plot for normality


Normality is not read here. You read it on the Q-Q plot. Independence is also outside this plot’s scope.






[SLIDE 6/9 · clicks 1 · ~64s] It is just as important to know what this plot cannot tell you. The residuals-versus-fitted plot reads linearity and constant variance, and only those two assumptions. [CLICK 1] In particular, it does not show normality. Whether the residuals follow a normal distribution is read on a different picture, the Q-Q plot, which we will meet in a later lecture. This is a common trap, so state it plainly: if someone asks whether the residuals look normal, the residuals-versus-fitted plot is the wrong plot, and the Q-Q plot is the right one. Independence is likewise outside this plot’s scope. The safer habit is to ask which channel the evidence speaks to: mean shape, variance width, or a different diagnostic plot altogether. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
The damage is not equal








Signal
Coefficient target
Usual SEs / tests




Curved smooth (mean shape)
Can be distorted
Often unreliable too


Widening band (variance)
Not by itself, if mean/exogeneity hold
Unreliable




Mean-shape concerns target the fitted mean; variance-shape concerns mainly target conventional inference.


[SLIDE 7/9 · clicks 1 · ~76s] The two channels matter differently, and the consequences table in the notes makes the difference clear. Read the visible rows as a contrast. In the first row, a mean-shape problem can distort fitted values and coefficients, and that can also make the usual tests misleading. Because the target of the regression mean is at stake, this is usually the first problem to fix. [CLICK 1] Now reveal the summary. In the second row, a widening variance band does not by itself move the OLS coefficient target when the mean model and exogeneity assumptions hold, but it does make conventional standard errors, tests, and intervals unreliable. One more row from the notes is worth keeping in mind: non-normality mainly matters for small-sample inference, and it belongs on the Q-Q plot, not here. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
Go play with the patterns

The Assumption Violation Detector from the Chapter 8 notes, running on simulated data — pick a violation type and watch the residuals-vs-fitted panel and the Q-Q panel respond together.Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 8/9 · clicks 0 · ~50s] The chapter includes an interactive app, the Assumption Violation Detector, that lets you switch between violation types and watch the diagnostic plots respond. It runs on simulated data, so each case is a clean textbook example. As you flip from one case to the next, watch the residuals-versus-fitted panel and the Q-Q panel change together, and connect each shape back to the channel it speaks to. The live version is linked in the notes, and a few minutes spent there will lock the diagnostic habit into memory far better than any single slide. [NEXT]

title: One Plot, Two Assumptions
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


One plot, two channels. The LOESS shape reads linearity — a flat smooth is evidence of no obvious mean-shape concern; a curved smooth points to possible missing curvature. The band width reads constant variance — an even band is evidence of no obvious variance concern; a widening band points to unequal variance: if the mean model and exogeneity assumptions hold, unequal variance mainly breaks conventional inference. Normality is read on the Q-Q plot, not here. Mean-shape concerns usually come first.




Next: VIF — compute it, read it, do not fear it. Notes, Ch 8 §Residuals vs Fitted

[SLIDE 9/9 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Let us close with the whole idea in one breath. The residuals-versus-fitted plot is a dashboard with two channels. The shape of the LOESS curve reads linearity: a flat smooth is evidence of no obvious mean-shape concern, while a curved smooth points to possible missing curvature. The width of the band reads constant variance: an even band is evidence of no obvious variance concern, while a widening band says conventional inference needs care. Remember that normality is read on the Q-Q plot, not here, and that mean-shape concerns usually come first because they can distort fitted values and coefficients. In the next lecture we will turn to a different diagnostic and learn to compute the variance inflation factor, read it, and not fear it. The chapter notes are linked just below. [END]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
How bad is 0.82?

The question

Suppose one predictor is 82% explained by the others. Has it broken your model — or just made one coefficient harder to pin down?


The number that answers this is the variance inflation factor, or VIF.


[DECK ch08-02 · 9 min · ~1162 words · clicks 10 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Here is a question that comes up the moment you fit more than one predictor at a time. Suppose one of your predictors is eighty-two percent explained by all the others put together, so they carry a great deal of the same information. The worry is natural: has that overlap broken your model, or has it only made one coefficient harder to pin down? These are two very different situations, and today we will learn to tell them apart. [CLICK 1] The single number that answers the question is the variance inflation factor, which everyone writes as V-I-F. Over the next few minutes we will compute it, read it against its thresholds, and see exactly what it does and does not mean. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
Where the number comes from









Figure 1














Figure 2









In the working model, regressing SES on the others gives R-squared ≈ 0.283, so its VIF is 1 / (1 − 0.283) ≈ 1.4 — the formula, confirmed on real data.



[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 2 · ~60s] Let us see where VIF comes from, because the idea is simpler than the name suggests. Take one predictor and set the outcome aside. This whole navy bar is that predictor’s variance. Now run an auxiliary regression, in which this predictor is the response and every other predictor explains it. [CLICK 1] That auxiliary regression returns an R-squared, the auxiliary R-squared sub j. Here it is zero point eight two, so the plum section, eighty-two percent of this predictor’s variance, is information the other predictors already contain, and only the teal slice of zero point one eight is unique. The more the others can reproduce a predictor, the less unique information is left for its coefficient. [CLICK 2] This holds on real data, not just in a picture. In our working model, regressing socioeconomic status on the others gives an auxiliary R-squared of about zero point two eight three, and one over one minus zero point two eight three is about one point four, exactly the VIF you will see for it in a moment. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
One over one minus R-squared











Figure 3














Figure 4










\[\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_j}\]



\[R^2_j = 0.82\]
\[\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - 0.82} \approx 5.6\]










What a 5 costs you


The SE inflates by √VIF. Here √5.6 ≈ 2.4, so the SE and interval are more than doubled.








[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 3 · ~82s] The formula turns that overlap into a single number. VIF for predictor j is one over one minus its auxiliary R-squared. The curve shows the shape: near zero overlap the VIF sits at one, then it rises slowly at first and very steeply as the auxiliary R-squared approaches one. [CLICK 1] Let us trace our value on the curve. The dashed guides show that an auxiliary R-squared of zero point eight two lands just above the first threshold, at a VIF of about five point six. [CLICK 2] Now put the same value through the formula. One minus zero point eight two leaves zero point one eight, and one divided by zero point one eight is about five point six. [CLICK 3] Finally, read what that costs your inference. The standard error of a coefficient is inflated by the square root of its VIF, and the square root of five point six is about two point four. An eighty-two percent overlap therefore more than doubles that coefficient’s standard error, widening its confidence interval by the same factor. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
Rules of thumb, not tests



VIF
Read it as
Tolerance = 1 / VIF




< 5
acceptable
above 0.20


5 to 10
investigate
0.10 to 0.20


≥ 10
serious
below 0.10











The trap: “VIF under 10 means nothing to discuss”


False. The 5 line already earns a look. And these are conventions, not a test — there is no p-value here, only judgment. Tolerance (just 1 / VIF) flags the same thing flipped over, with 0.20 as the matching line.






[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Here are the thresholds you will actually use. A VIF below five is generally acceptable. A VIF between five and ten asks you to investigate, and a VIF of ten or more is treated as serious, a sign that the coefficient is hard to trust on its own. You will also meet tolerance, which is simply one divided by VIF and carries the same message turned over, so a tolerance below zero point two zero is the equivalent flag, matching a VIF of five. [CLICK 1] One caution matters here. These cut-points are rules of thumb, conventions that the field has settled on, not a hypothesis test with a p-value attached. As a result, do not wait for the value of ten before you pay attention, because a VIF already at five deserves a look. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
What VIF does not mean







The trap: “high VIF means wrong coefficients”


False, if the model specification and exogeneity assumptions are held fixed. Collinearity by itself inflates standard errors and makes coefficients less stable — it makes a coefficient imprecise, not automatically wrong.






Wide interval, same coefficient target under the maintained model. You are just less certain where the estimate landed.



[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~63s] This is the point most worth getting right, because it is the most common misreading. People see a large VIF and conclude that their coefficients must be wrong. Say the condition out loud: holding model specification and exogeneity fixed, collinearity by itself inflates standard errors and makes coefficients less stable. It does not, by itself, move the coefficient target. In other words, VIF is an imprecision warning, not automatic evidence that the coefficient is wrong. [CLICK 1] You can picture it as a wider interval around the same coefficient target under the maintained model. You are simply less certain where the estimate actually landed. This is the locked theme of the whole chapter: high VIF means less precision and more instability, while omitted confounding is a different problem because it can move the estimate rather than just widening it. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
The real model is quiet









Figure 5








SES 1.4, Minority 1.1, Sector 1.4 — tolerances 0.72, 0.90, 0.71. All three sit far below 5. For this model, VIF raises no collinearity concern.



[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Let us run the computation on a real model, the same working model we have used throughout this chapter, with math achievement predicted from socioeconomic status, minority status, and school sector. One function call returns a VIF for each predictor, and here are the three bars, with both threshold lines drawn in for scale. [CLICK 1] Every bar is quiet. Socioeconomic status comes in at one point four, minority status at one point one, and sector at one point four, with tolerances of zero point seven two, zero point nine zero, and zero point seven one. All three sit far below the line at five, so VIF raises no collinearity concern for this model. That does not certify the rest of the regression; it only says this diagnostic is not warning us about predictor overlap. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
section: When it is high: fix or forgive
text:
When it is high: fix or forgive
If a VIF really is large

Select one of the overlapping predictors, combine them into a single index, or transform them to remove the redundancy.









Sometimes a high VIF is expected


A polynomial or interaction term is correlated with its own pieces by construction, so it can flag a high VIF on purpose. Centering often reduces this nonessential collinearity and improves interpretation, but it does not change fit or cure essential overlap among distinct predictors. Center thoughtfully, don’t panic.






[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Suppose a VIF really is large. You have three honest moves. You can select the one predictor among the overlapping set that is most meaningful for your question, you can combine the correlated predictors into a single index, or you can transform them to remove the redundancy, such as using a per-capita measure instead of a raw total. [CLICK 1] There is one important case where a high VIF can be expected. A squared term or an interaction term is, by its very definition, correlated with the pieces it is built from, so it can show nonessential collinearity by construction. In that situation, do not automatically throw the term away. Centering predictors before you form the squared or interaction term often reduces that nonessential overlap and improves interpretation, but it does not change the model’s fitted values and it does not cure essential overlap among distinct predictors. The rule to remember is simple: center thoughtfully, do not panic. [NEXT]

title: VIF: Compute It, Read It, Don’t Fear It
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


VIF = 1 / (1 − R²ⱼ) turns predictor overlap into one number; the SE inflates by √VIF. ≥ 5 earns a look, ≥ 10 is serious — but these are rules of thumb, not tests. A high VIF is an imprecision warning when the maintained model is otherwise sound. And a high VIF is sometimes expected by construction — for a polynomial or interaction, center thoughtfully.




Next: Leverage is not influence — Cook’s distance. Notes, Ch 8 §Collinearity Diagnostics

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Let us gather the whole idea into a few sentences. VIF, which is one over one minus the auxiliary R-squared, turns predictor overlap into a single number, and the standard error of a coefficient inflates by the square root of that VIF. A value at five earns a look and a value at ten is serious, but treat both as rules of thumb rather than as a formal test. Above all, remember that VIF is an imprecision warning when the maintained model is otherwise sound; it does not check the rest of the regression for you. And when a polynomial or interaction term raises the VIF by construction, centering often helps interpretation and nonessential collinearity, but it is not a cure-all. In the next lecture we will separate leverage from influence using Cook’s distance. The chapter notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
section: One school can flip your slope
text:
One school can flip your slope

Chapter 8 · Diagnostics

Drop in one extra school and the slope barely twitches. Drop in another and it swings.


Not how far out it sits. Whether it sits far out and off the line.


[DECK ch08-03 · 10 min · ~1245 words · clicks 7 · advances 9] [SLIDE 1/9 · clicks 1 · ~38s] Imagine you add one more school to your data and refit the line. Sometimes the slope barely twitches, and sometimes that single school swings it. A natural guess is that the troublemaker is whichever point sits farthest out along the x axis, and that guess is the trap this lecture defuses. [CLICK 1] The real answer is that distance along x is only half the story. In this experiment, the point that changes the slope sits far out in x and also lies off the trend the other points follow. Today we will name those ingredients, leverage and residual size, and then watch one number, Cook’s distance, combine them into a deletion-impact screen. By the end, you should be able to distinguish leverage from influence, screen with Cook’s D, and report a sensitivity refit. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
text:
Leverage: far out in x









Figure 1







Leverage = how far the point’s x is from the centre. The hat value \(h_{ii}\).


[SLIDE 2/9 · clicks 1 · ~44s] Let us begin with the first ingredient, using simulated data throughout this lecture. Leverage measures how far a point’s predictor value sits from the centre of the other x values, and software reports it as the hat value, written h sub i i. The gold point here is far out to the right, near a value of sixteen, while the rest of the cloud sits between zero and ten, so its leverage is high, with a hat value of about zero point three six. [CLICK 1] Recall that the average leverage is k plus one over n, and a common rule flags a point as high when its hat value exceeds two times k plus one over n. Here is the careful word. Leverage depends on the predictor side alone and says nothing about y, so a high-leverage point only has the potential to pull the line. In this simple plot that means an unusual x value; in multiple regression it means an unusual predictor profile. It is potential, not proof. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
text:
The surprise: it barely moves









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 3/9 · clicks 1 · ~48s] Now let us put that high-leverage point to the test. First, here is the clean simulated fit with thirty points and its rust least-squares line. [CLICK 1] I add the point far out to the right, but I place it right on the line the others already follow, and then I refit. The dashed slate refit line lands almost on top of the original, the slope changes by only plus zero point zero two, and its Cook’s distance is zero point zero six five, well below the screen. This disproves the most common misconception in the whole topic. An extreme x value is not automatically a problem, because a high-leverage point that agrees with the trend barely moves this fitted slope. Low Cook’s distance is not permission to ignore the case; you still verify that the x value is real and inside the model’s intended scope. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
text:
Influence is deletion impact
Influence = what changes when the case is removed


A point is influential when deleting it would materially change fitted values or coefficients. Cook’s D often gets large when unusual x combines with poor fit. A large residual alone is not a deletion rule.



[SLIDE 4/9 · clicks 1 · ~48s] So let us state the definition first. Influence is the property we actually care about, because an influential point is one whose removal would materially change fitted values, coefficients, or the conclusion you report. [CLICK 1] Cook’s distance gives a common way to screen for that deletion impact. In ordinary least squares, it often becomes large when an unusual x value gives the point a long lever arm and a large residual supplies the force at the end of the lever. But do not turn that into a rigid two-threshold checklist. The definition is deletion impact; leverage and residual size explain why Cook’s D often reacts. A large residual alone is not a deletion rule. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
text:
Cook’s distance fuses the two
\[ D_i = \underbrace{(e_i^{*})^2}_{\text{big residual}} \cdot \frac{1}{k+1} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}}}_{\text{high leverage}} \]








Screening rules of thumb — not tests


Investigate a point when D > 1 (absolute), or, for size-adjusted screening, when D > 4/n or the stricter D > 4(k+1)/n. For this fixed app screen, 4/30 = 0.133; with one added point, 4/31 = 0.129, and the classification is unchanged.






[SLIDE 5/9 · clicks 1 · ~38s] Cook’s distance puts the residual-size and leverage ingredients into one number, and you do not need to derive it. You only need to read the labels under the formula. The first piece is the squared studentized residual, the poor-fit ingredient, and the last piece grows as the hat value approaches one, the leverage ingredient. Because they multiply, Cook’s distance is especially sensitive when poor fit and unusual x travel together. [CLICK 1] To use it, you compare each point’s Cook’s distance against a cutoff. An absolute cutoff flags a value above one, and a size-adjusted cutoff flags points above four over n. In this app we keep the fixed base screen at four over thirty, which equals zero point one three three; the refit-specific four over thirty-one screen is zero point one two nine and gives the same classification here. Please hear the words carefully. These are screening rules of thumb, not tests. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
section: The whole story in one grid
text:
The whole story in one grid









Figure 4














Figure 5








[SLIDE 6/9 · clicks 1 · ~50s] This grid is the whole lecture in one picture, from the same simulated data. Read it in pairs. Panel B versus panel C holds the far-out x value roughly fixed and changes whether the point fits the trend. Panel C versus panel D gives both points a large residual, but only panel C has the long lever arm. That is why only panel C tilts the slope, swinging it by minus zero point three two. [CLICK 1] The bar chart summarizes that logic. The gold dashed line is the fixed app screen at four over thirty, which equals zero point one three three here, and only the deletion-impact case towers above it. The grey reference bar is just the no-added-point baseline. Leverage alone is not influence, a large residual alone is not a deletion rule, and Cook’s distance is screening for cases whose deletion could matter. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
section: Go try it: the Influence Explorer
text:
Go try it: the Influence Explorer

The Influence Explorer from the Chapter 8 notes, on simulated data (n = 30, fixed app screen 4/30 = 0.133). Tick a box to add a high-leverage point, an influential outlier, or a low-leverage outlier, then watch the slope change and the Cook’s-distance bars.Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 7/9 · clicks 0 · ~44s] The chapter includes an interactive version of this experiment, the Influence Explorer, running on simulated data with thirty points, so the fixed app screen sits at four over thirty, which is zero point one three three. At rest, with no box ticked, the full-model R-squared reads zero point seven five nine, the slope change reads plus zero point zero zero zero, the largest Cook’s distance is zero point zero six nine, and zero of thirty points are flagged. Each checkbox adds one of the points we just studied, and watching the slope change and the Cook’s-distance bars respond will lock the distinction into memory. The live version is linked in the notes. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
section: What to actually do with a flag
text:
What to actually do with a flag
Course reporting protocol







When a point is flagged



Screen with Cook’s \(4/n\) — flag, do not judge.
Inspect the point: a coding error, or a real but rare case?
Sensitivity-refit with and without it; report whether conclusions change.
Report what changed: sign, size, interval or p-value, substantive decision, and whether the case was an error or a real rare case.







Never auto-delete. A large residual alone never justifies deletion.



[SLIDE 8/9 · clicks 1 · ~44s] Suppose Cook’s distance flags a point. What you do next is the course reporting protocol. You screen with the four over n rule, flagging candidates rather than judging them. You inspect each flagged point and decide what it is, a coding error or a real but rare case that genuinely belongs in your data. Then you run a sensitivity refit, fitting the model with the point and again without it, and report whether your conclusions actually change: the sign, size, interval or p-value, substantive decision, and whether the case was an error or a real rare case. [CLICK 1] Notice the one thing this protocol never does, which is delete a point automatically. A large residual alone never justifies deletion, because the point you are tempted to throw away might be the most informative observation you have. One related tool deserves a mention. DFBETAS measures influence on one coefficient at a time, so it tells you which coefficient a point is bending, where Cook’s distance summarizes all of them at once. [NEXT]

title: Leverage Is Not Influence: Cook’s Distance
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Leverage is not influence. Leverage is an unusual x; influence means deletion impact, and Cook’s distance screens for cases where residual size and leverage may make that impact large. Screen at \(4/n\) — a rule of thumb, not a test — then investigate and sensitivity-check, never auto-delete.




Next: Heteroscedasticity — fix the inference, keep the model. Notes, Ch 8 §Influential Observations

[SLIDE 9/9 · clicks 0 · ~38s] Let us close with the whole idea in one breath. Leverage is not influence: leverage is only an unusual x value, while influence means deletion impact on fitted values, coefficients, or conclusions, and Cook’s distance screens for cases where residual size and leverage may make that impact large. You screen with the four over n rule, remembering that it is a rule of thumb and not a test, and then you investigate and sensitivity-check the flagged point, never deleting it for a large residual alone. In the next lecture we turn to heteroscedasticity, where the goal is to fix the inference while keeping the model. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
section: Your standard error may be wrong
text:
Your standard error may be wrong

Chapter 8 · The remedy

“My slopes look fine, so my inference is fine.”


A funnel can leave the estimates unchanged while quietly making conventional standard errors too small or too large — and the coefficient table will not warn you.


[DECK ch08-04 · 9 min · ~1174 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~48s] A natural thought after fitting a model is that the slopes look reasonable, so the inference must be sound. That feeling is exactly the trap this lecture defuses. An earlier residual-pattern deck taught you to recognize a funnel in the residual plot, the fingerprint of heteroscedasticity. Today we deal with what to do about it.
