[CLICK 1] The fact to absorb is that a funnel can leave your point estimates unchanged while quietly making conventional standard errors too small or too large, and nothing in the coefficient table will warn you. In this example, the understatements happen for the intercept and ses. By the end, you should be able to diagnose the funnel, name the Breusch-Pagan evidence, compare ordinary and H-C-three standard errors, and report the boundary of the repair. [NEXT]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
text:
The funnel, one more time









Figure 1







Flat LOESS — the mean function looks adequate here. Widening band — the variance is not constant.


[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] This is the funnel from the earlier residual-pattern lesson, drawn on simulated data so the pattern is the clean textbook case. Notice the two signals it sends at once. The slate smoothing curve stays roughly flat along the dashed zero line, which tells you the straight-line mean function looks adequate in this panel. At the same time the vertical spread is narrow on the left and widens steadily to the right, which is the signature of heteroscedasticity, the error variance changing across the range of fitted values. [CLICK 1] That split is the whole reason the remedy can be gentle when the usual regression conditions are otherwise in place. The flat curve says the model shape is not the visible problem here. The widening band says the spread is misbehaving, so the conventional uncertainty calculation needs repair. In other words, this picture is evidence for an inference problem, not a license to ignore mean-model or dependence problems. [NEXT]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
section: What breaks, and what does not
text:
What breaks, and what does not







If the mean model is right, inference is not


With a correctly specified mean model, exogenous predictors, and independent observations, heteroscedasticity by itself does not bias OLS coefficients.






But conventional standard errors, confidence intervals, and p-values can be wrong. In this example, OLS understates the intercept and ses SEs, so those intervals are too narrow and those p-values are too small.



[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~76s] Let us be precise about what a funnel breaks and what it leaves alone, because the distinction is the whole point of this lecture. The first half is reassuring, but only under the right conditions. If the mean model is correctly specified, predictors are exogenous, and observations are independent, heteroscedasticity by itself does not bias ordinary-least-squares coefficients. In that setting, the average relationship is still being estimated by the same fitted line; the funnel is not the thing that moves the slope. [CLICK 1] The second half is the warning. Conventional standard errors, confidence intervals, and p-values can be wrong, and the direction is not guaranteed. In this working example, ordinary least squares understates the standard errors for the intercept and ses, so those intervals are too narrow and those p-values are too small. The minority and sector standard errors move slightly the other way under H-C-three. In one honest sentence: with the mean model and independence conditions in place, heteroscedasticity mainly breaks conventional inference, not the coefficients. [NEXT]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
section: Let the plot and the test co-decide
text:
Let the plot and the test co-decide







The Breusch-Pagan test — a supplement, not the arbiter


For the working model, lmtest::bptest(): statistic 8.808, df 3, p = 0.032. Significant at 0.05 — consistent with the funnel.






Formal tests grow over-sensitive at large n — they can flag a trivial funnel, and two reasonable tests can even disagree. So the residual plot co-decides: report the evidence, trust no single number alone.



[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] You do not have to judge the funnel by eye alone, because there is a formal companion. The Breusch-Pagan test asks whether the spread of the residuals depends on the predictors. For the studentized result reported here, use lmtest::bptest(). For our working model the test statistic is eight point eight zero eight, on three degrees of freedom, with a p-value of zero point zero three two. That is significant at the zero point zero five level, which agrees with what the funnel already suggested. [CLICK 1] Treat this test as a supplement, not as the arbiter. Formal tests of constant variance grow over-sensitive as the sample size grows, so with enough data they can flag a funnel far too small to matter. Two reasonable tests can even disagree on the same model: the performance helper reports a different one-degree-of-freedom score-test result and reads this fit as non-significant, while lmtest::bptest() reads it as significant. That is precisely why the residual plot co-decides. You report the named test and the picture, trust no single number on its own, and here the picture plus lmtest::bptest() make the case for repairing the inference clear. [NEXT]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
text:
HC3: same estimates, honest SEs









Figure 2














Figure 3








[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~88s] Here is the repair. We re-estimate the standard errors with the H-C-three robust formula, the course’s chosen flavor for its good finite-sample properties, and lay them beside the ordinary ones. Read the columns left to right. The estimates come first: eleven point seven four eight for the intercept, one point seven five three for ses, negative three point three eight one for minority, and three point seven seven four for sector. Notice that the H-C-three column does not contain a second set of estimates. This disarms the second misconception, that robust standard errors change my coefficients. They do not, because by construction the robust method changes the covariance of the estimates, never the point estimates, so these four estimates are exactly the same as the ordinary least-squares estimates. [CLICK 1] Now the two ratios above one are ringed. The ratio is the robust standard error divided by the ordinary one. For the intercept it is one point one six two, so the ordinary standard error was understated by about sixteen percent. For ses it is one point zero four nine, understated by about five percent. The minority and sector ratios sit just below one, so their conventional standard errors were not understated here; H-C-three makes them slightly smaller. Notice that the ratio is a reported column, computed at full precision, not a division you should redo on the rounded standard errors. The result is the same coefficients with a sensitivity read on the standard errors. [NEXT]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
section: Report it as a sensitivity check
text:
Report it as a sensitivity check


Figure 4


“We re-ran inference with HC3 robust standard errors and our conclusions were unchanged.” — a clear reporting sentence. HC3 repairs heteroscedasticity inference only — not nonlinearity, omitted variables, endogeneity, or clustering/dependence.



[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~62s] Once you have both columns, you report the comparison as a sensitivity check rather than swapping models in secret. The bars show each ratio against the dashed line at one, where the robust and the ordinary standard error would agree. The intercept and ses reach past the line, while minority and sector sit just under it, so the correction is real but modest. [CLICK 1] A clear reporting sentence sounds like this. We re-ran inference with H-C-three robust standard errors, and our conclusions were unchanged. If a borderline p-value had flipped, you would say so just as plainly. One boundary keeps you honest. Robust standard errors repair heteroscedasticity inference only. They do not fix a wrong mean function, so they are no remedy for nonlinearity; they do not stand in for omitted variables or endogeneity; and they do not handle clustering or other dependence. If the LOESS curve had been bent, H-C-three would not have saved you, because that is a problem with the model itself. [NEXT]

title: Heteroscedasticity: Fix the Inference, Keep the Model
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


With a correct mean model, exogenous predictors, and independent observations, heteroscedasticity by itself does not bias slopes. HC3 does not change coefficients — it adjusts conventional SEs, tests, and intervals only. Let the plot and lmtest::bptest() co-decide, then report HC3 as a sensitivity check.




Next: Q-Q plots — normality is about residuals. Notes, Ch 8 §Robust Standard Errors

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Let us gather the whole idea in one breath. When the mean model is correct, predictors are exogenous, and observations are independent, heteroscedasticity by itself does not bias your slopes. The robust fix does not change your coefficients; H-C-three adjusts the conventional standard errors, tests, and intervals while leaving the estimates exactly where they were. You let the residual plot and lmtest::bptest() co-decide whether a funnel is real, and then you report H-C-three as a sensitivity check, stating whether your conclusions held. Remember the boundary: this repairs heteroscedasticity inference, not a wrong mean function, not omitted variables or endogeneity, and not clustering or other dependence. In the next lecture we will turn to the Q-Q plot and see that normality is about the residuals. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
section: My income is skewed – is my regression dead?
text:
My income is skewed – is my regression dead?

Chapter 8 · Diagnostics

“My income variable is heavily skewed – is my regression dead?”


The honest answer: the skew of a raw variable is the wrong thing to check.


[DECK ch08-05 · 9 min · ~1214 words · clicks 11 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] Here is a worry that surfaces in almost every student project. You plot a predictor or the outcome, perhaps an income variable, you see a long right tail, and a small alarm goes off. You have heard that regression assumes normality, and this variable is plainly not normal, so the fear is that the whole regression is dead before it has even been fit. This is the single most common piece of needless panic in applied work. [CLICK 1] The honest answer is that the skew of a raw variable is the wrong thing to check. In this lecture we will fix the misconception at its root, learn to read the one plot that matters, the Q-Q plot, and finish with the reassuring fact that normality is the assumption you should worry about least. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
section: It is the residuals, not the variables
text:
It is the residuals, not the variables
The misconception, named

The formal assumption is about unobserved errors; in practice we assess it with residuals.



Raw predictors and outcomes do not need to be normal for this assumption. Still inspect them for scale, outliers, and model-form clues.










The rule


Assess error normality with the residual Q-Q plot, not raw-variable histograms.






[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 2 · ~74s] Let us state the misconception plainly so we can retire it. The formal normality assumption concerns the model’s unobserved errors. Because those errors cannot be seen, we assess the assumption through the residuals, which estimate them. That is why the diagnostic object is the residual Q-Q plot, not a histogram of a predictor and not a histogram of the outcome variable. [CLICK 1] This means raw predictors and outcomes do not need to be normal for this assumption. A skewed predictor is not a normality violation, and a skewed outcome is not automatically a normality violation. You may still inspect raw variables for scale, outliers, and model-form clues, but that is a different question from residual normality. [CLICK 2] So the rule to carry away is simple. Assess error normality with the residual Q-Q plot, not raw-variable histograms. Plotting every column and treating skew as a regression failure is effort spent on the wrong object, and it is the panic we are here to defuse. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
text:
What “normal residuals” looks like



Points hug the line -> normal enough. Minor isolated tail wiggles are usually not concerning.



[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~54s] This is the picture that decides the question, the normal Q-Q plot, and this one comes from a real working model rather than a simulation. The idea is simple. We sort the residuals from smallest to largest and, for each one, we ask where a perfectly normal distribution would have placed a value at that position. The horizontal axis carries those ideal normal positions, the vertical axis carries the residuals we actually have, and the rust line marks perfect agreement. [CLICK 1] When the residuals are close to normal, the points fall along that line, and notice how closely they hug it here, from the lower left all the way to the upper right. The small isolated wiggles right at the ends are expected in finite samples and are usually not concerning. Systematic tail departures are different; that is what the next panels teach us to recognize. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
section: Heavy tails bend into an S
text:
Heavy tails bend into an S
























S-shape → heavy tails. Left tail below, right tail above: more extreme values than a normal.



[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 2 · ~64s] From here on the panels are simulated data, three hundred points each, so every pattern is the clean textbook case. Start again from the healthy reference, with the points sitting on the line. [CLICK 1] Now swap in heavy-tailed residuals and watch the two ends. On the left the points fall below the line, and on the right they rise above it, so the whole pattern bends into a gentle S-shape. The lowest residuals are lower than a normal distribution would place them, and the highest are higher, which means the distribution simply produces more extreme values at both ends than a normal one does. [CLICK 2] That S-shape, with the left tail below the line and the right tail above it, is the fingerprint of heavy tails, and the middle of the plot can still look straight while the ends give the shape away. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
text:
Skew lifts one tail
























One tail lifting → skew. Right tail up is right skew; left tail down is left skew.



[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 2 · ~70s] Skew shows up differently. Instead of both ends bending, just one tail lifts away while the rest of the cloud stays near the line. Here is right skew, also called positive skew. The upper-right tail curves up and away, above the line, which tells you the largest values are larger than a normal distribution would predict. If residual skew is substantial and the outcome scale supports it, an outcome transformation can be a candidate, perhaps a square root or log scale, followed by before-and-after diagnostic checks. [CLICK 1] Left skew, or negative skew, is the mirror image. Now the lower-left tail curves down and away, below the line, telling you the smallest values are more extreme than expected. A transformation is not automatic; it has to preserve a useful interpretation and improve the diagnostics. We will work that ladder properly in a later lecture. [CLICK 2] So the reading is clean. The right tail lifting above the line means right skew, the left tail dipping below means left skew, while heavy tails bent both ends away at once. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
text:
When does this actually matter?
Defuse the panic

Normality matters most for small-sample inference.



In large independent samples, with a correct mean model, finite-variance errors, no dominant leverage, and appropriate SEs, coefficient estimates are often approximately normal.










Read the plot, not the test


Shapiro-Wilk over-flags at large n: it rejects trivial, harmless deviations. For samples up to 5,000 it runs by default. Trust the Q-Q plot.






[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 2 · ~70s] Now for the part that should lower your blood pressure, with the right conditions attached. Of the regression assumptions, normality is usually the least critical. Under the usual correct-mean, exogeneity, independence, and finite-variance conditions, ordinary least squares estimates do not require normally distributed errors. Normality is mainly needed for confidence intervals and tests to be exactly valid in small samples. [CLICK 1] In large independent samples, with a correctly specified mean model, finite-variance errors, no dominant leverage points, and appropriate standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem often makes the sampling distribution of the coefficients approximately normal. As a result, you do not need to chase small, isolated tail wiggles when your sample is large and the other regularity conditions look reasonable. [CLICK 2] There is one more trap worth naming. The Shapiro-Wilk test, the formal normality test, over-flags at large n, because with enough data it rejects trivial and harmless deviations. It runs by default for samples up to five thousand. So read the plot, not the test, and let the Q-Q plot have the final word rather than a p-value that turns significant on a deviation too small to matter. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
text:
Keep the hierarchy honest







Violation
What it costs




Mean shape (curved residuals)
Can bias estimates — usually the most damaging


Non-normal errors (skew, heavy tails)
Mostly small-sample test/interval accuracy — often the least damaging




So spend your worry where the damage is. A curved mean pattern changes the target; modest non-normality in a well-behaved large sample is usually secondary.


[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~58s] It helps to place normality in an honest hierarchy of consequences. A mean-shape problem, the curved-residual pattern from an earlier lecture, can bias the estimates themselves, which makes it usually the most damaging diagnostic failure, because the coefficients may be pointed at the wrong target. Non-normal errors sit closer to the other end, because under the usual regularity conditions they mostly cost small-sample accuracy in tests and intervals. [CLICK 1] So spend your worry where the damage actually is. A curved mean pattern demands attention, whereas modest non-normal residuals in a well-behaved large sample are usually secondary. This is why policing the distribution of every raw variable is misplaced effort, and why a calm look at the residual Q-Q plot is the right response. [NEXT]

title: Q-Q Plots: Normality Is Assessed With Residuals
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Normality is formally about errors, assessed with residuals, not raw predictors or outcomes — so a skewed variable is not, by itself, a normality violation. Read it on the Q-Q plot: points on the line is healthy, an S-shape is heavy tails, one tail lifting is skew. It matters most for small-sample inference. In large independent samples with regularity conditions in place, CLT approximations often help — so read the plot, not the test.




Next: From Plots to Verdict — check_model() and the adequacy paragraph. Notes, Ch 8 §Checking Normality

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~44s] Let us gather the whole lecture into a few sentences. Normality is formally about errors, and in practice we assess it with residuals, not raw predictors and not the outcome, so a skewed variable on its own is not a normality violation. You read it on the Q-Q plot, where points on the line are healthy, an S-shape means heavy tails, and a single lifted tail means skew. It matters most for small-sample inference. In large independent samples, when the mean model is right, variances are finite, no single case dominates, and standard errors are appropriate, CLT approximations often help. So read the plot rather than obeying the test, and keep non-normality in proportion. In the next lecture we will turn these separate diagnostics into a single verdict, using the check model function to assemble the evidence and writing the adequacy paragraph that states the conclusion. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Six panels, one paragraph

Chapter 8 · Diagnostics, brought together

One function prints six diagnostic panels. Your grade rests on one paragraph.


The dashboard is the map. The adequacy paragraph is the destination.


[DECK ch08-06 · 11 min · ~1414 words · clicks 10 · advances 10] [SLIDE 1/10 · clicks 1 · ~40s] This lecture brings the whole chapter together. A single function, called check model, prints six diagnostic panels at once, and over the last few lectures you have learned to read each one. The temptation now is to print all six, admire them, and move on, as though the plots were the deliverable. [CLICK 1] That is the misconception this lecture defuses. Diagnostics must end in a decision, not a plot gallery. The graded deliverable is a short model-adequacy verdict that names what the diagnostics showed and ends in an action. As you will hear throughout, the dashboard is the map, and the paragraph is the destination. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
The dashboard is a map












One call, check_model(model). Six panels: posterior-predictive · linearity · variance · normality · influence · collinearity.


[SLIDE 2/10 · clicks 1 · ~40s] Here is the whole dashboard for our working model, the math achievement regression on socioeconomic status, minority status, and school sector. A single call produces all six panels at once. Read the top-left panel as a quick example: it overlays the observed data, drawn in green, on the curves the model predicts, drawn in blue, and you want the two to broadly agree. This posterior-predictive panel is a broad model-fit check; if it looks off, revisit the outcome scale, missing structure, transformations, or model specification before trusting the rest of the verdict. At this size the fine detail is unreadable, which is exactly the point. [CLICK 1] The six panels are the posterior predictive check, linearity, homogeneity of variance, normality, influential observations, and collinearity. You read the dashboard as a map, scanning for any panel that looks unusual, and then you zoom into that one panel with the dedicated plot you already know. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Zoom: linearity


Watch out: a wavy curve does not mean the data are bad. A bend means the shape is wrong — add a term, never discard rows.


[SLIDE 3/10 · clicks 1 · ~40s] The first panel to zoom is linearity, the residuals-versus-fitted plot you met in the first lecture of this chapter. The reference line you want is flat and horizontal. Notice that the slate smoothing curve stays close to the dashed zero line, with no systematic bend, which gives no meaningful evidence of curvature and makes the straight-line mean function look adequate here. [CLICK 1] A common misconception is that a wavy curve means the data are bad. In fact, a bend tells you the shape of the model is wrong, not that the rows are wrong, so the remedy is to add a term, never to discard observations. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Zoom: variance


Watch out: with a correct mean model, exogeneity, and independence, a funnel does not by itself bias OLS coefficients. HC3 adjusts inference for nonconstant variance; it does not fix the model.


[SLIDE 4/10 · clicks 1 · ~42s] The second panel is homogeneity of variance, read on the scale-location plot. Recall that this same model’s raw residual band looked roughly even by eye in the first lecture; the scale-location plot is more sensitive, so it can surface a mild funnel the eye misses. Here the slate curve drifts gently upward, which is exactly that: the spread of the residuals grows a little as the fitted values grow, the fingerprint of heteroscedasticity. [CLICK 1] This panel hosts the most stubborn misconception in the chapter, that a funnel automatically ruins the slopes or that H-C-three fixes the model. The correct claim is narrower. If the mean model is correctly specified, predictors are exogenous, and observations are independent, heteroscedasticity by itself does not bias OLS coefficients, but conventional standard errors are unreliable. H-C-three adjusts inference for nonconstant variance; it does not fix misspecification, omitted variables, endogeneity, clustering, or other dependence. So this panel sends you to an inference sensitivity check, not a blanket model repair. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Zoom: influence


Watch out: a tall bar does not mean delete that case. Screens only flag points — inspect or sensitivity-check them, never auto-delete.


[SLIDE 5/10 · clicks 1 · ~40s] The third panel is influential observations, shown one Cook’s distance at a time. Cook’s distance fuses leverage and a large residual into one number. The dotted rust line is the looser four-over-n screen, and six cases cross it. The gold dashed line is the stricter size-adjusted screen, four times k plus one over n, which here equals zero point zero eight five one, and the tallest stem reaches only zero point zero four six. No point crosses the stricter screen, and none is near the absolute action cutoff of one, so there is no strong-influence signal. [CLICK 1] The misconception this panel invites is that a tall bar means delete that case. A screen only flags a point for a closer look; it never licenses deletion. The six loose-screen flags can be inspected or sensitivity-checked, while the stricter screen tells us no point is strongly influential. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Zoom: collinearity


Watch out: more predictors do not automatically mean trouble. VIF 5 is a rule-of-thumb screen; here all three are about 1.4 or less.


[SLIDE 6/10 · clicks 1 · ~42s] The fourth panel is collinearity, read through the variance inflation factors from the V-I-F lecture. The V-I-F for ses is one point four zero, for minority it is one point one one, and for sector it is one point four one, and the dashed lines mark the moderate threshold at five and the serious threshold at ten. Every bar sits far to the left of both. [CLICK 1] The misconception here is that adding predictors automatically creates a collinearity problem. In fact, five is a rule-of-thumb screen, not a hard boundary, and these three sit near one point four. That gives no meaningful evidence of redundancy, so each predictor appears to carry information the others do not. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
The detrended Q-Q, read right









Read the right reference line


In check_model() the normality panel is detrended: good residuals hug the horizontal line at zero, not a rising diagonal. The standard diagonal Q-Q plot lives in the previous lecture.






[SLIDE 7/10 · clicks 1 · ~44s] One panel must be read differently, and it surprises almost everyone. In check model, the normality panel is a detrended Q-Q display. In the standard Q-Q plot from the previous lecture, well-behaved residuals follow a straight diagonal. Here the diagonal has been subtracted out, so what you plot is each residual’s deviation from where normality would place it, and the reference is therefore a flat horizontal line at zero. [CLICK 1] That changes what good looks like. In this detrended panel, normal residuals scatter close to the horizontal line at zero, with no systematic pattern, rather than tracking a diagonal. Our points stay near that horizontal line, so there is no meaningful visual normality problem. With one hundred eighty-eight reasonably independent observations, a correctly specified mean model, finite variance, no dominant leverage point, and appropriate standard errors, that modest wobble is unlikely to drive the inference. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Writing the verdict
The graded deliverable

Diagnostics indicated [what], so [the consequence]; accordingly we [the action / limitation].









The verdict, worked for the math achievement model


Diagnostics indicated an adequate mean shape, a mild funnel (Breusch-Pagan 8.808, df 3, p = 0.032), six loose 4/n influence flags but no point past 4(k+1)/n = 0.0851, and approximately normal residuals; accordingly we report HC3 robust standard errors for nonconstant variance and treat ordinary p-values as tentative.
Use this as a model of structure, not a template to copy: replace every diagnostic claim, value, and action with evidence from your own fitted model.







Independence — state the boundary: students are nested in only five schools, so HC3 handles heteroscedasticity, not dependence; treat ordinary p-values as descriptive unless a cluster-aware or multilevel sensitivity is feasible.



[SLIDE 8/10 · clicks 2 · ~52s] Now we reach the payoff, the sentence the whole dashboard exists to produce. The structure has three slots: what the diagnostics showed, the consequence for your conclusions, and the action you took, in limitation language. [CLICK 1] Here it is filled in for our model. Diagnostics indicated an adequate mean shape, approximately normal residuals, and no point past the stricter influence screen, though six cases cross the looser four-over-n screen and are worth inspecting. The one real diagnostic concern is a mild funnel, flagged by the Breusch-Pagan test with a statistic of eight point eight zero eight on three degrees of freedom and a p-value of zero point zero three two. Accordingly, we report H-C-three robust standard errors for nonconstant variance and treat ordinary p-values as tentative rather than pretending the model itself was fixed. Use the paragraph as a model of structure, not a template to copy; your diagnostic claim, value, and action must come from your own fitted model. [CLICK 2] One assumption the plots cannot test deserves its own sentence, and the projects require it. Our students are nested within only five schools, so H-C-three handles heteroscedasticity but not dependence. With so few clusters, ordinary OLS p-values should be treated as descriptive unless a cluster-aware or multilevel sensitivity analysis is feasible. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
section: The decision guide as your reference
text:
The decision guide as your reference









Assumption
How you detect it
What you do




Posterior predictive fit
Observed vs predicted distributions disagree
Revisit scale, omitted structure, or model family


Linearity
Curved residuals-vs-fitted
Add a term; never discard rows


Homoscedasticity
Funnel; Breusch-Pagan significant
HC3 for inference; transform only if scale-supported


Normality
Detrended Q-Q departs from zero
Transform only if substantial and interpretable; modest large-sample departures often matter less


Independence
Design: clustering
Cluster-aware or multilevel sensitivity if feasible; otherwise treat p-values as tentative


Collinearity
VIF ≥ 5 or ≥ 10
Combine, center, or drop only when redundancy is substantive


Influence
Cook’s past 4/n or 4(k+1)/n
Inspect; sensitivity-check; never auto-delete





Actions have boundaries: HC3 repairs variance inference, not dependence or misspecification.


[SLIDE 9/10 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Keep this decision guide beside you as the reference that turns each flagged panel into an action. Each row pairs an assumption with how you detect it and what you do about it. The posterior predictive panel is a broad model-fit check, so if the observed and predicted distributions disagree, revisit the outcome scale, omitted structure, transformations, or specification. A funnel, confirmed by the Breusch-Pagan test, sends you to H-C-three robust standard errors for inference, while transformations require a scale and interpretation check. Cook’s distance past four over n, or the stricter four times k plus one over n, sends you to investigate and sensitivity-check, never to delete a point automatically. [CLICK 1] The boundary matters. H-C-three repairs variance inference; it does not repair dependence, misspecification, omitted variables, or endogeneity. Every detection has a matching action, and every action has a limit, so no panel is ever left as a plot without a sentence. [NEXT]

title: From Plots to Verdict: check_model() and the Adequacy Paragraph
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The dashboard is the map; the adequacy paragraph is the destination. Run check_model(), zoom the flagged panel, then write the verdict — “Diagnostics indicated …, so …; accordingly we ….” Read the normality panel as a detrended Q-Q, and state each remedy’s boundary: HC3 handles variance, not dependence.




Next: Chapter 9 — logistic regression: from straight lines to probabilities. Notes, Ch 8 §The check_model() Dashboard

[SLIDE 10/10 · clicks 0 · ~36s] Let us close with the whole idea in one breath. Diagnostics end in a decision, never in a plot gallery, so the dashboard is the map and the adequacy paragraph is the destination. You run check model to see the territory, you zoom into whichever panel looks unusual, and then you write a short verdict that states what you found, names the consequence, and adds limitation language. Read the normality panel as a detrended Q-Q, and state the boundary of every remedy: H-C-three handles variance, not dependence. What is graded is that paragraph written from your own diagnostics, not the number of plots you printed. In the next chapter we turn to logistic regression, where we model probabilities rather than means. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: A model that predicts a 148% chance
text:
A model that predicts a 148% chance



[DECK ch09-01 · 10 min · ~1244 words · clicks 7 · advances 9] [SLIDE 1/9 · clicks 0 · ~62s] Let us begin with a model that has clearly gone wrong. These are one thousand three hundred nine Titanic passengers, and we want to predict whether each one survived, coded one for survived and zero for died, which is why the points stack into two flat bands. Along the bottom is the fare each passenger paid, and the rust line is an ordinary least-squares fit, the same straight-line tool you have used since Chapter One. The teal band marks the only values a probability may take, from zero to one. Notice where the line ends up. For the passenger who paid the highest fare, it predicts a one hundred forty-eight percent chance of survival, which is impossible, because no probability can exceed one hundred percent. That impossible number is the reason this chapter exists. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
text:
“It’s a 0/1 outcome — just run lm()”
The tempting shortcut

“survived is just numbers, 0 and 1, and sex is a dummy. Why not regress one on the other, the way I always have?”













[SLIDE 2/9 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Let us start with the shortcut almost everyone reaches for first. The survived variable is already numeric, coded zero or one, and a predictor like sex is a simple dummy, so it is tempting to run lm(), the ordinary least-squares function, just as before. Same outcome, different predictor: fare exposed the failure, and sex reminds us what zero-one means. [CLICK 1] Here is the Titanic survival rate split by sex, where sex equals one for females and zero for males. Among the males, one hundred sixty-one of eight hundred forty-three survived, which is nineteen point one percent, and among the females, three hundred thirty-nine of four hundred sixty-six survived, which is seventy-two point seven percent. The average of a zero-one column is the proportion of ones, which is a probability. So we are really modeling a probability, a number that must stay between zero and one, and a straight line makes no such promise. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: The line walks out of bounds
text:
The line walks out of bounds








The trap, named


A linear probability model on a 0/1 outcome can predict impossible probabilities — especially with continuous predictors or extrapolation. Here the fare fit reaches a 148% chance of survival on real observed fares, and 4 passengers get a predicted probability above 1.






[SLIDE 3/9 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Think back to the line from the opening picture, because it shows the first and most visible crack. A straight line keeps the same slope forever, so it climbs without limit and has no idea that a probability must stop at zero and at one. [CLICK 1] Here is the trap stated plainly. A linear probability model on a zero-one outcome can predict impossible probabilities, especially with continuous predictors or extrapolation. On these data the fare fit reaches a one hundred forty-eight percent chance of survival, and four real passengers receive a predicted probability above one. These are not far-fetched extrapolations; they are predictions for passengers who actually sailed. A number above one hundred percent tells you that a straight line is not a safe general probability model. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: Two more cracks in the same wall
text:
Two more cracks in the same wall
Even in range, the line has probability-scale limits


1 · Non-constant variance. A 0/1 outcome has variance p(1 − p), which changes with the predictor — so the equal-variance assumption behind OLS breaks.




2 · Implausible linearity. A straight line forces a constant change in probability at every level — often too rigid as you approach 0 or 1.



[SLIDE 4/9 · clicks 2 · ~76s] The impossible prediction is the crack you can see, but a linear probability model has two further limits as a general probability model. That does not mean lm() is useless for every binary outcome; with a simple zero-one group comparison, it can describe group rates. The trouble is treating a straight line as the general probability model, especially with continuous predictors. [CLICK 1] The first crack is variance. For a zero-one outcome the variance equals p times one minus p, the probability times one minus the probability. Because that depends on the probability, the spread changes as the predictor changes, so the equal-variance assumption of ordinary least squares does not hold. [CLICK 2] The second crack is the shape. A straight line forces the same change in probability at every level, but that is often too rigid near the edges, where a probability must level off rather than keep climbing. So even setting the impossible values aside, a constant-slope line is usually too rigid as a general probability model. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: The fix: a curve bounded by design
text:
The fix: a curve bounded by design


































[SLIDE 5/9 · clicks 2 · ~66s] The fix is to replace the straight line with a curve that can never leave the legal range. The horizontal axis is the linear predictor, alpha plus beta x, and the vertical axis is the probability. The rust dashed line is the straight-line fit again, crossing below zero on the left and above one on the right. [CLICK 1] Now add the logistic function in slate, the iconic S-shape of this chapter. As the linear predictor grows large and negative the curve flattens toward zero, and as it grows large and positive it flattens toward one, bending toward each boundary rather than crossing it. [CLICK 2] It passes through one half at the midpoint, when the linear predictor is zero. Because the logistic function maps every value into the range between zero and one, its predictions are bounded by design, and no coefficients can produce an impossible probability. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: Same linear predictor inside, new scale
text:
Same linear predictor inside, new scale


[SLIDE 6/9 · clicks 0 · ~72s] Here is the reassuring part, and it defuses a second worry, namely that logistic regression is a totally new model you must learn from scratch. It is not. Same outcome again, now with age: this panel shows that the line and the logistic curve can look similar over the observed range. Look inside that S-curve and you will find the familiar linear predictor, eta equals alpha plus beta x. The model links probability to that predictor with the logit: eta equals logit of p. Then the inverse-logit, the logistic S-curve, maps eta back to a probability between zero and one. This picture also keeps us honest. It shows survival against age, where the straight line in rust and the logistic curve in slate track each other closely, and the curve is only gently S-shaped, because the dramatic S appears at the extremes rather than in the middle of real data. So we will not oversell it. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: The new engine: likelihood and log-odds
text:
The new engine: likelihood and log-odds
What changes, and what carries over

The engine is now maximum likelihood, not least squares; common tests are Wald z tests or likelihood-ratio tests, not OLS t-tests.



The workflow carries over, but the model changes: coefficients live on log-odds, predictions must return to probability, and diagnostics are logistic-specific.



[SLIDE 7/9 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Two things do change beneath the surface, and it helps to name them. Because a straight-line least-squares fit no longer applies, the coefficients are estimated by maximum likelihood. In basic coefficient tables you will often see Wald z-tests, and for model comparisons you will also see likelihood-ratio tests, rather than OLS t-tests. [CLICK 1] What carries over is the modeling workflow, not every OLS rule. You still define the outcome, choose predictors, think about confounding and interactions, and explain uncertainty carefully. But the target scale changes: coefficients live on log-odds, predictions must be translated back to probabilities, and diagnostics and inference belong to the Bernoulli-logit model rather than the OLS model. So the bridge is strong, but it has guardrails. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
section: Go try it: watch the line escape
text:
Go try it: watch the line escape

Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 8/9 · clicks 0 · ~54s] The chapter includes an interactive explorer that lets you feel this difference for yourself. It runs on simulated data, drawn from a known process so that the line can be pushed far enough to cross the boundaries. As you drag the slope upward, the dashed straight line, the linear probability model, punches through the boundaries at zero and one, and the red pills count up the invalid predictions it produces. The solid logistic curve, built from the very same intercept and slope, refuses to leave the valid range no matter how hard you push. The live version is linked in the notes, and a few minutes there makes the contrast stick far better than a single slide. [NEXT]

title: Why a Straight Line Can’t Predict a Probability
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A straight line on a 0/1 outcome can predict impossible probabilities — on Titanic fares, up to 148% on a real passenger — and as a general probability model its variance p(1 − p) and constant-change shape are poor fits. Logistic regression keeps the familiar linear predictor α + βx, but models log-odds, returns predictions through the inverse-logit S-curve, and uses likelihood-based logistic inference.




Next: One Number, Three Scales — probability, odds, log-odds. Notes, Ch 9 §From Linear to Logistic

[SLIDE 9/9 · clicks 0 · ~61s] Let us gather the whole idea into a single breath. A straight line on a zero-one outcome can predict impossible probabilities, as we saw at one hundred forty-eight percent on a real Titanic passenger, and as a general probability model its variance of p times one minus p and constant-change shape are poor fits. Logistic regression keeps the familiar linear predictor, alpha plus beta x, but that predictor lives on the log-odds scale. The inverse-logit S-curve brings predictions back to probabilities and keeps them bounded by design. So the workflow from earlier chapters helps, but the interpretation, inference, and diagnostics now belong to the Bernoulli-logit model. In the next lecture we will take one fitted number and read it on three scales: probability, odds, and log-odds. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
text:
Is “4 to 1” a probability?

The trap

“The odds of rain are 4 to 1 — so the chance is about 4, right?”



No. Odds of 4 to 1 mean a probability of 4 / 5 = 0.80. Odds are not a probability.



[DECK ch09-02 · 10 min · ~1200 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~71s] Here is a confusion that catches almost everyone the first time. A forecaster says the odds of rain are four to one, and because the phrase sounds like a chance, it is tempting to read that four as the probability of rain. That reading cannot be right, because a probability can never go above one, and four is far past one. [CLICK 1] So let us settle it now. Four to one means four chances in favor for every one chance against, which is four rainy days out of every five, a probability of four divided by five, or zero point eight zero. Notice that the two numbers differ: the odds are four, but the probability is zero point eight zero. That is the first idea to keep, that odds are not a probability. They are one of three ways to say the same thing, and we will line all three up side by side. [NEXT]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
text:
Scale one: probability, with walls










Scale
Range
Reads as




Probability (pi)
0 to 1
share of times it happens


Odds (pi / (1 - pi))
0 to infinity
wins per loss


Log-odds (log of the odds)
minus infinity to plus infinity
symmetric, centered at 0

















[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~78s] Here is the map for the whole lecture. The same fitted event probability can be written on three scales, and this table gives each one a name, a range, and a plain reading. Probability runs from zero to one. The odds are the probability over one minus the probability, running from zero to infinity. The log-odds are the log of the odds, running from minus infinity to plus infinity and symmetric about zero. [CLICK 1] Start with probability, the scale you already trust. Picture a track with a hard wall at zero and a hard wall at one, because a probability can never be negative and can never exceed one. On the Titanic, where sex one marks a female passenger, the male dot sits at zero point one nine one, well left of center, and the female dot sits at zero point seven two seven, over to the right. Those walls are exactly what makes this scale awkward for a straight regression line, which keeps climbing and walks right off the track. [NEXT]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
section: Scale two: odds, a ratio of chances
text:
Scale two: odds, a ratio of chances























Do not flip the ratio


Odds put the event on top: 0.80 / 0.20 = 4, not 0.20 / 0.80 = 0.25. Flip the numerator and denominator and you describe the opposite event.







Males, worked through: odds = 0.191 / (1 − 0.191) = 0.191 / 0.809 = 0.236  ·  Females, from counts: odds = 339 / 127 = 2.669





[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 2 · ~90s] The second scale is the odds, which compare the successes to the failures. Picture an eighty percent event: out of ten tries, eight happen and two do not, so the odds are eight divided by two, which is four. This is where people stumble, because the odds of an eighty percent event are four, not zero point eight. [CLICK 1] When you build the ratio, keep the event on top. Zero point eight zero over zero point two zero is four, but flip it to zero point two zero over zero point eight zero and you get zero point two five, the odds of the opposite event. [CLICK 2] Now run the same recipe on the Titanic. The male survival probability is zero point one nine one, and dividing by its complement gives the odds zero point two three six, below one because fewer than half the men survived. For the women, use the full counts so the arithmetic closes: three hundred thirty-nine survived and one hundred twenty-seven did not, so the odds are three hundred thirty-nine over one hundred twenty-seven, or two point six six nine. The women sit well above one, the dividing line between unlikely and likely. [NEXT]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
section: Scale three: log-odds, unbounded and symmetric
text:
Scale three: log-odds, unbounded and symmetric
The log-odds (the logit) take the natural log of the odds:
\[ \text{log-odds} = \log\!\left(\frac{\pi}{1 - \pi}\right) \quad\in\quad (-\infty,\ +\infty) \]


































[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 2 · ~93s] The third scale finishes the job. The log-odds, also called the logit, take the natural logarithm of the odds, written as the log of pi over one minus pi. To see why, compare the three shapes as the probability sweeps from zero to one. On the left, probability against itself is a diagonal, firmly capped between zero and one. [CLICK 1] In the middle, the odds remove the ceiling but keep the floor. They are pinned at zero and then climb without any upper limit, badly lopsided around the value of one. [CLICK 2] On the right, taking the log of the odds stretches the scale in both directions. The scale now runs from minus infinity to plus infinity and is symmetric about zero, with probabilities near one half landing near a log-odds of zero. This is the punchline, and the wording matters. The observed outcome stays zero or one; we do not take the logit of observed zeros and ones. Logistic regression makes the fitted probability linear after the logit transform: logit of P of Y equals one given x equals alpha plus beta x. You report predictions on the probability scale, but the model does its arithmetic on the log-odds of the fitted probability. [NEXT]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
text:
The one row to memorize























[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~71s] There is one row worth committing to memory, because it is the reference point for all three scales at once. Picture them as gauges standing side by side, probability on the left, odds in the middle, and log-odds on the right. [CLICK 1] Now draw one line straight across all three. A probability of zero point five zero sits at exactly one on the odds gauge and at zero on the log-odds gauge. Picture a fair coin flip, and you have the single place where the three scales line up cleanly. Above this line the event is more likely than not, and below it the reverse. In the Titanic example, the men’s negative log-odds and the women’s positive log-odds would fall on opposite sides of this anchor, even though this picture shows only the reference row. If you remember nothing else, remember that zero point five zero, one, and zero name the same middle point. [NEXT]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
section: Go try it: three gauges in lockstep
text:
Go try it: three gauges in lockstep

Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 0 · ~66s] The chapter includes an explorer that lets you feel how tightly these scales are tied together. It runs on simulated probabilities, and a single slider sets the fitted probability while all three gauges respond at the same instant, because the probability, the odds, and the log-odds are one quantity shown three ways. The picture here is the starting position, with the slider resting at the anchor we just memorized, where the probability is zero point five zero, the odds are one, and the log-odds are zero. Push the slider toward one and the probability gauge crowds against its wall while the log-odds gauge keeps gliding outward with room to spare. That contrast is why the logistic model puts the linear predictor on the log-odds scale for P of Y equals one given x, and the live version is linked in the notes. [NEXT]

title: One Number, Three Scales: Probability, Odds, Log-Odds
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


One fitted probability, three scales: probability in [0, 1], odds = π/(1 − π) in [0, ∞), log-odds in (−∞, +∞). They meet cleanly at the anchor 0.50 ↔︎ 1 ↔︎ 0. Odds are not a probability — convert with odds = p / (1 − p), the event on top. The observed outcome stays 0/1; the model is linear for logit[P(Y = 1 | x)].




Published logistic tables — like the Kalmijn study you meet in the practice set — usually report log-odds coefficients or odds ratios. These three scales are the translation kit for turning model effects into interpretable probabilities.
Next: exp(β) for Groups — the Odds Ratio and Its Reciprocal. Notes, Ch 9 §Probability, Odds, and Log-Odds · Practice Quiz 7

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~85s] Let us gather the whole idea into one breath. A single fitted probability can be spoken on three scales, probability, odds, and log-odds, and the three meet cleanly at one anchor, where a probability of zero point five zero is the same as odds of one and a log-odds of zero. Above all, remember that odds are not a probability, that you convert with odds equal to p over one minus p with the event on top, and that an eighty percent event has odds of four, not zero point eight. The observed outcome stays zero or one; logistic regression reaches for log-odds because logit of P of Y equals one given x is the scale where the fitted probability can be written as a line. One bridge before we close: published logistic tables usually report log-odds coefficients or odds ratios, and this three-scale vocabulary is the translation kit for turning those model effects into probabilities. In the next lecture we will exponentiate a slope and read the odds ratio that compares two groups. The notes and Practice Quiz Seven are linked just below. [END]

title: exp(β) for Groups: The Odds Ratio (and Its Reciprocal)
section: The table says 2.43 — is that the odds ratio?
text:
The table says 2.43 — is that the odds ratio?

The trap























[DECK ch09-03 · 8 min · ~1036 words · clicks 8 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Here is a trap that costs points on almost every logistic regression question. We fit a model of Titanic survival on sex, and the coefficient table for the sex term reads two point four three. Because that is the number staring back at you, it is tempting to report it as the odds ratio. That reading is wrong, and naming why is the whole job today. Two point four three is the coefficient on the log-odds scale, and the log-odds scale is not where we want to live. [CLICK 1] To get the odds ratio, you exponentiate. When you run model parameters with exponentiate set to true, the very same sex row now reads eleven point three, and that number, eleven point three, is the odds ratio. So the rule is simple to state. You never read the raw coefficient as the odds ratio; you exponentiate the log-odds coefficient first, and only then do you have an odds ratio. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) for Groups: The Odds Ratio (and Its Reciprocal)
text:
Exponentiate, then check the receipt


































[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 2 · ~84s] Start with the number the table gave you, the log-odds coefficient of two point four three, which is hard to feel on its own scale. [CLICK 1] Now apply the one operation that fixes that. Exponentiate the coefficient, that is, raise the constant e to the power of the coefficient, and you land on the odds ratio of eleven point three. Think of it as e to the b, written as a symbol, because e of the rounded two point four three does not quite reproduce eleven point three; the full-precision coefficient is what exponentiates cleanly to the locked value. [CLICK 2] To see where eleven point three comes from, recall the two group odds from the previous lecture. The odds of survival for the women are two point six six nine, and the odds for the men are zero point two three six. The odds ratio is the first divided by the second, two point six six nine over zero point two three six, which gives eleven point three. The odds ratio is nothing more than the ratio of the two groups’ odds, and it equals e to the b exactly. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) for Groups: The Odds Ratio (and Its Reciprocal)
section: Read it in one sentence — and name the event
text:
Read it in one sentence — and name the event
How to read this


The odds of survival (Y = 1) for women are about 11.3 times the odds for men. The OR is the factor multiplying the coded group’s odds versus the reference. Odds, not probability: this is not “11.3 times more likely.”



[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~58s] An odds ratio means little until you say it as a full sentence, so here is the template worth memorizing. An odds ratio is the factor that multiplies the odds for the coded group relative to the reference group. On the Titanic the women are the coded group, coded one, and the men are the reference, coded zero. [CLICK 1] Pour our numbers into it carefully. The odds of survival, the outcome coded one, for the women are about eleven point three times the odds for the men. Notice the two disciplines built into that one sentence. First, you name the modeled event every single time, here survival, because an odds ratio that does not say which event it describes is the project’s most expensive point loss. Second, you keep the direction straight, the coded group compared against the reference, women relative to men, which is exactly the reference-group logic you already know from dummy coding, now living on the odds scale. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) for Groups: The Odds Ratio (and Its Reciprocal)
section: When the OR is below 1, you may flip the comparison
text:
When the OR is below 1, you may flip the comparison


































[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 2 · ~92s] An odds ratio below one is correct: it says the coded group has lower odds than the reference. You can say that directly. For a clean demo, imagine a late-registration coefficient of negative zero point six nine three for course completion. Exponentiating gives an odds ratio of zero point five zero, read as the odds of completion for late registrants versus on-time registrants. [CLICK 1] If you want a greater-than-one sentence, then you may take the reciprocal. One divided by zero point five zero is two point zero, which is easier to say. [CLICK 2] Here is the subtlety the reciprocal hides, and it is the second most common trap. Taking one over the odds ratio does not simply tidy the number; it reverses which group you are comparing. The groups swap, from late-versus-on-time over to on-time-versus-late. So you must flip the sentence along with the number and, as always, name the outcome the table models. The clean reading is that on-time registrants have about two point zero times the odds of completion, with the comparison now running the other way. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) for Groups: The Odds Ratio (and Its Reciprocal)
section: A small map for any OR
text:
A small map for any OR


































[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 2 · ~84s] Let us gather the readings into one small map you can apply to any odds ratio. An odds ratio of one means no association, the coded group having the same odds as the reference. An odds ratio above one means the coded group has higher odds, and an odds ratio below one means lower odds. Use the reciprocal only when you also reverse the groups. [CLICK 1] Place the Titanic result on a number line and a clean symmetry appears. The women-versus-men odds ratio is eleven point three, well to the right of one, and the men-versus-women odds ratio is its reciprocal, one over the odds ratio, which is zero point zero eight eight, the same distance to the left. Eleven point three and zero point zero eight eight are mirror images across the no-association line at one, the very same association read from opposite directions. [CLICK 2] One last row completes the map, the intercept. Exponentiating the intercept does not give an odds ratio at all; it gives the baseline odds, the reference group’s own odds, which here is zero point two three six for the men. An odds ratio always compares two groups, while the exponentiated intercept is a single group’s odds standing on its own. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) for Groups: The Odds Ratio (and Its Reciprocal)
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The coefficient is on the log-odds scale — exponentiate to get the odds ratio; never read the raw coefficient as the OR. An OR is the factor multiplying the coded group’s odds versus the reference, and you name the modeled event every time (here, survival, Y = 1). For an OR < 1, read lower odds directly, or report 1 / OR only after reversing who is compared. The intercept exponentiates to the baseline odds, not an OR.




Next: exp(β) per Unit — Continuous ORs and % Change. Notes, Ch 9 §Odds Ratios · Practice Quiz 7

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Let us close with the points worth carrying forward. The coefficient lives on the log-odds scale, so you exponentiate it to reach the odds ratio, and you never report the raw coefficient as the odds ratio itself. An odds ratio is the factor multiplying the coded group’s odds against the reference, and you name the modeled event every time, here survival, the outcome coded as one. When an odds ratio falls below one, read it as lower odds, or report one over the odds ratio only after reversing which group is compared to which. And keep the intercept straight: exponentiated, it is the baseline odds, a single group’s odds rather than a comparison. In the next lecture we will exponentiate a slope for a continuous predictor and read the odds ratio per unit, along with the percent change it implies. The notes and Practice Quiz Seven are linked just below. [END]

title: exp(β) per Unit: Continuous ORs and % Change
section: An odds ratio of 0.992 — so age does not matter?
text:
An odds ratio of 0.992 — so age does not matter?

The trap























[DECK ch09-04 · 9 min · ~1128 words · clicks 7 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~62s] Let us begin with a number that looks like nothing. We fit a model of Titanic survival on age, exponentiate the coefficient, and the odds ratio comes back as zero point nine nine two per year. That value is so close to one that it is tempting to conclude age simply does not matter. Notice where it sits on this picture, just a hair to the left of the dashed line at one, which marks no association. [CLICK 1] Here is the honest reading of this particular result. The ninety-five percent confidence interval runs from zero point nine eight four to one point zero zero one, so it crosses one, and the p value is zero point zero seven three. As a result, there is no strong evidence that age changes the odds of survival on these data. We will still work through the arithmetic, because learning to read a continuous odds ratio is today’s lesson, and that reading is the same whether or not the effect is significant. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) per Unit: Continuous ORs and % Change
text:
One unit multiplies the odds
























Continuous: each additional one-unit increase multiplies the odds by the OR, holding the other predictors fixed.




Dummy: the odds for the coded group are the OR times the reference group’s odds.



[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 3 · ~94s] Recall what an odds ratio does for a continuous predictor. Exponentiating the coefficient gives the factor that multiplies the odds for each one-unit step. In this picture, every extra year multiplies the odds of survival by zero point nine nine two, the same factor at every step, which is why the boxes shrink by a constant proportion rather than a constant amount. The first temptation is to read that odds ratio like a regression slope and say the odds go down zero point zero zero eight points a year. That additive reading is wrong, because an odds ratio is multiplied into the odds, not subtracted from them. [CLICK 1] The correct reading is multiplicative. Each additional year multiplies the odds by zero point nine nine two, holding the other predictors fixed, and the quantity being multiplied is the odds, not the probability. [CLICK 2] That gives us the sentence template for a continuous predictor. Each additional one-unit increase multiplies the odds by the odds ratio, holding the other predictors fixed. That clause travels with every continuous odds ratio you report. [CLICK 3] Compare the template for a dummy predictor, where the odds for the coded group are the odds ratio times the reference group’s odds. Both run on the same engine, an odds ratio that multiplies, but a dummy compares two groups while a continuous predictor moves along a scale one unit at a time. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) per Unit: Continuous ORs and % Change
text:
Stack the units: effects compound























[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~72s] Because the effect multiplies, several units compound. Walking ten years forward is just ten of those one-year steps in a row, shown by the ten faint arrows from age a to age a plus ten. [CLICK 1] Now collapse those ten steps into a single decade. Multiplying by the same factor ten times means raising it to the tenth power, so the per-decade odds ratio is e to the beta, all raised to the tenth, which equals e to the ten beta, and that comes out to zero point nine two four. Notice that I am writing this as a symbol rather than a power of the rounded number, because both zero point nine nine two and zero point nine two four are computed from the full-precision coefficient. If you instead took the rounded zero point nine nine two and raised it to the tenth, you would land on zero point nine two three, which is not the same. So compound on the scale of the coefficient. This is exactly why an odds ratio that looks negligible per year can look more interpretable once the units pile up. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) per Unit: Continuous ORs and % Change
section: Say it as a percent change
text:
Say it as a percent change























[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~76s] An odds ratio near one is easiest to feel as a percent change, and the conversion is one short formula, one hundred times the quantity odds ratio minus one. Per year, one hundred times zero point nine nine two minus one is negative zero point eight percent, so the fitted point estimate corresponds to odds of survival about zero point eight percent lower for each additional year. Per decade, one hundred times zero point nine two four minus one is negative seven point six percent, or about seven point six percent lower odds. There is a useful magnitude rule here. When an odds ratio sits between zero point five and two, report it as a percent change; above two, say times the odds; and for an odds ratio below one, read lower odds directly. Use the reciprocal only if you reverse the comparison or direction, such as describing a one-year decrease instead of a one-year increase. [CLICK 1] One caution keeps these readings honest. The percent change describes the odds, and the odds are not the same thing as the probability. A separate quantity, the average marginal effect, is here negative zero point one nine percentage points per year. That number lives on the probability scale, in percentage points, while the odds ratio and its percent change live on the odds scale. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) per Unit: Continuous ORs and % Change
section: The whole picture on a log scale
text:
The whole picture on a log scale























[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~80s] Let us place all of this on the standard picture for odds ratios, the forest plot you will use throughout multiple logistic regression. This example combines illustrative rows from two separate simple models, not one adjusted model. Each row is one odds ratio with its confidence interval, and the dashed gold line marks one, the point of no association. The horizontal axis is on a log scale, which puts an odds ratio of two and an odds ratio of one half the same distance from one. The sex effect sits far to the right at eleven point three, women having dramatically higher odds of survival, with an interval well clear of one. Our two age terms sit close to one instead, the per-year odds ratio at zero point nine nine two and the per-decade odds ratio at zero point nine two four. [CLICK 1] Now read the age effect honestly. Both age intervals cross the line at one, and the p value for age is zero point zero seven three, so there is no strong evidence that age changes the odds. The arithmetic we just practiced is how you read any continuous odds ratio, and on this predictor it simply does not establish a real effect. [NEXT]

title: exp(β) per Unit: Continuous ORs and % Change
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


For a continuous predictor, exp(β) multiplies the odds per unit, holding the others fixed — the effect is multiplicative, not additive. Units compound: (eβ)k = e^(kβ), so the per-decade OR is 0.924, not the per-year value times ten. Convert with 100(OR − 1)%: −0.8% per year, −7.6% per decade. Match the words to the size — 0.5 to 2 → percent change, above 2 → times the odds; for OR < 1, lower odds first, reciprocal only if direction reverses. And stay honest: this OR’s CI includes 1 (p = 0.073) — no strong evidence of an effect.




Next: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio. Notes, Ch 9 §Odds Ratios · Practice Quiz 7

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~55s] Let us gather the points worth carrying forward. For a continuous predictor, the exponentiated coefficient multiplies the odds for each one-unit step, holding the other predictors fixed; the effect is multiplicative, not additive. Because it multiplies, units compound, so e to the beta raised to the kth equals e to the k beta, which is why the per-decade odds ratio is zero point nine two four rather than the per-year value times ten. To say any odds ratio in words, use one hundred times the quantity odds ratio minus one, and match the phrasing to the size: between zero point five and two, speak in percent change; above two, say times the odds; and for an odds ratio below one, read lower odds first. Use the reciprocal only when you reverse the comparison or direction. And remember the honest verdict here, that the interval crosses one, so we read the arithmetic without claiming a real age effect. In the next lecture we will see why an odds ratio is not a risk ratio. The notes and Practice Quiz Seven are linked just below. [END]

title: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio
section: True or false: women were 11 times more likely to survive?
text:
True or false: women were 11 times more likely to survive?

The trap























[DECK ch09-05 · 9 min · ~1129 words · clicks 6 · advances 6] [SLIDE 1/6 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Here is a claim you will read in newspapers, in abstracts, and in plenty of write-ups, and it is one of the most common misinterpretations in all of applied research. The statement is that women were eleven times more likely to survive the Titanic. The number is real and it came straight out of a logistic regression, so the sentence sounds completely authoritative. Let us treat it as a simple true or false. [CLICK 1] The verdict is false, and seeing why is the whole lesson today. Eleven times is the odds ratio, and an odds ratio is not a statement about how likely an outcome is. How much more likely is measured by the probability ratio, also called the risk ratio, and for these data that ratio is only about three point eight one. The gap between eleven times the odds and about four times the probability is the entire lesson of this micro-lecture, and keeping the two apart will protect your project interpretation throughout this chapter. [NEXT]

title: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio
section: Compute both: the risk ratio and the odds ratio
text:
Compute both: the risk ratio and the odds ratio
Two ratios, two answers


































[SLIDE 2/6 · clicks 2 · ~86s] Let us put both numbers side by side and see where each one comes from. Start with the probability ratio, also called the relative risk, which we can compute right here. It is simply the probability that a woman survived divided by the probability that a man survived. That is zero point seven two seven divided by zero point one nine one, which equals three point eight one, and that division closes cleanly. For a probability ratio, the everyday phrase times as likely is perfectly correct. [CLICK 1] Now recall the odds ratio from the previous lecture, where the chapter reported it as eleven point three. We are not recomputing it on the slide; we are carrying it forward as the value the chapter already established, so that we can set it beside the risk ratio. [CLICK 2] Here is the contrast that matters. Survival for women was about eleven point three times the odds, but only about three point eight one times the probability. These are very different numbers from the same comparison and the same data, and they measure genuinely different things. The word likely belongs to the probability ratio of about four, not to the odds ratio of about eleven. [NEXT]

title: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio
section: Why the two numbers pull apart
text:
Why the two numbers pull apart























[SLIDE 3/6 · clicks 1 · ~82s] The reason the two numbers pull apart is hiding inside one small denominator. Recall that the odds are the probability divided by one minus the probability, written as pi over one minus pi. Everything turns on that one minus pi underneath. When the outcome is common, the probability is large, so one minus pi becomes small, and dividing by a small number makes the odds shoot up well above the probability. In this odds ratio greater than one example, that makes the odds ratio larger than the risk ratio; more generally, common outcomes can make odds ratios sit farther from one than risk ratios. [CLICK 1] Now consider the opposite case, where the outcome is rare. When the probability is small, one minus pi is very close to one, so dividing by it barely changes anything. Notice that the odds are then about equal to the probability, and when that holds in both groups the odds ratio is about equal to the risk ratio. In other words, the two measures agree only when the event is rare, and they pull apart once it becomes common. [NEXT]

title: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio
section: The rule: rare in both groups
text:
The rule: rare in both groups























[SLIDE 4/6 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Let us watch that separation happen in one picture. Here we hold the true risk ratio fixed at exactly two point zero, the flat dashed line, and we sweep the baseline probability from very rare on the left to just under one half on the right. The solid rust line is the odds ratio that results. On the far left, where the outcome is rare in both groups, the odds ratio sits right on top of the dashed line at two point zero, so the two measures agree. As the outcome becomes more common, the odds ratio peels away and climbs, even though the true risk ratio never moves. That gives us a clean working rule: an odds ratio is a reasonable approximation to the risk ratio only when the probability is below zero point one zero in both groups, a condition often called the rare-disease assumption. In this picture, because the second probability is twice the baseline, that shaded region ends near zero point zero five. [CLICK 1] Now place the Titanic on this axis. Overall survival was thirty-eight point two percent, marked by the gold line far to the right, deep in the common region rather than the rare zone. Survival is the perfect non-rare case, which is exactly why the odds ratio sits much farther from one than the probability ratio here. [NEXT]

title: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio
section: Say it right: the phrasing that protects points
text:
Say it right: the phrasing that protects points























[SLIDE 5/6 · clicks 1 · ~84s] This last slide is the one that protects the interpretation. For an odds ratio, there is a single phrase you must never use, and that phrase is times as likely. It is struck through here for a reason, because times as likely is actually a risk-ratio phrase, and attaching it to an odds ratio is the central mistake we have just taken apart. Saying that women were eleven times as likely to survive treats the odds ratio as though it were the risk ratio. [CLICK 1] In its place, you have two safe templates that you may reuse word for word, and both are correct for an odds ratio. You may say times the odds, as in the odds of survival for women were about eleven point three times the odds for men. You may also say percent higher odds, when a percentage reads more naturally. To see why the discipline matters, take the misconception that an odds ratio of two means twice as likely. At a baseline of forty percent, an odds ratio of two actually gives a probability of about fifty-seven percent, not eighty percent; the odds doubled, but the probability did not. So reserve times as likely for a genuine risk ratio, such as our three point eight one, and for an odds ratio stay with times the odds. [NEXT]

title: An Odds Ratio Is Not a Risk Ratio
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


An odds ratio is not a risk ratio. On the Titanic the OR is 11.3 while the probability (risk) ratio is only 3.81 — very different numbers. Because odds = π / (1 − π), the OR can sit much farther from 1 than the risk ratio when the outcome is common; they agree only under the rare-outcome assumption (π < 0.10 in both groups). Survival is common (38.2%), so the OR sits much farther from 1 than the probability ratio here. For an OR, say “times the odds” or “percent higher odds” — never “times as likely.”




Next: Multiple Logistic Regression — adjusted odds ratios with several predictors. Notes, Ch 9 §The Critical Distinction · Next Notes, Ch 10 · Practice Quiz 7, after Ch10 CI material

[SLIDE 6/6 · clicks 0 · ~58s] Let us close with the one idea worth carrying out of Chapter nine. An odds ratio is not a risk ratio. On the Titanic the odds ratio is about eleven point three, while the probability ratio is only about three point eight one, and those are very different numbers describing the very same comparison. The reason is the denominator one minus pi, which can make the odds ratio sit much farther from one than the risk ratio whenever the outcome is common; the two agree only under the rare-outcome assumption, when the probability is below zero point one zero in both groups. Survival was common, at thirty-eight point two percent, so the odds ratio sits much farther from one than the probability ratio here. When you report an odds ratio, say times the odds or percent higher odds, and never say times as likely. In the next chapter we will carry odds ratios into multiple logistic regression, where several predictors are adjusted for one another at once. The notes links are below, and Practice Quiz Seven is labelled for after the Chapter Ten confidence-interval material. [END]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
