section: Berkeley, 1973: the pooled table looked damning
text:
Berkeley, 1973: the pooled table looked damning

The accusation























[DECK ch10-01 · 9 min · ~1130 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~50s] This is the most famous puzzle in applied statistics, and it is where Chapter ten begins. In the early nineteen seventies, the University of California at Berkeley was accused of discriminating against women in graduate admissions, and the reason is in front of you. This chart uses the berk-sub data, which holds one thousand one hundred sixty-nine applicants to two departments, pooled together. The women were admitted at about twenty-six point six percent, and the men at about fifty-four point one percent. [CLICK 1] Read together, the pooled bars look damning, because the men were admitted at roughly twice the rate of the women. Today we will turn that visual gap into a single number, the crude odds ratio, and then split the very same applicants by department and watch that number fall apart. Notice that we name the dataset on every slide, because the numbers depend on which applicants we include. [NEXT]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
section: Build the crude OR by hand: rate, then odds, then ratio
text:
Build the crude OR by hand: rate, then odds, then ratio









































Two traps to avoid


Odds are not the rate — odds = rate / (1 − rate), so they can exceed 1. The OR is a ratio of odds, not a ratio of rates. “Crude” is a flag, not an insult: it faithfully answers the pooled question, just a different one.






[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 3 · ~62s] Let us build the crude odds ratio by hand, the entry skill this chapter expects. Begin with the admission rate, which is simply the number admitted over the total. [CLICK 1] The second step is where people stumble. The odds are not the rate; they are the number admitted over the number rejected, which equals the rate over one minus the rate. For the men the odds are four hundred six over three hundred forty-five, which is one point one seven seven, and for the women one hundred eleven over three hundred seven, which is zero point three six two. A rate stays between zero and one, yet these odds climb above one. [CLICK 2] The third step is the crude odds ratio, a ratio of the two odds, not a ratio of the two rates. Dividing the men’s odds by the women’s, one point one seven seven over zero point three six two, gives three point two five, so the odds of admission for men are about three and a quarter times the odds for women, in the pooled berk-sub data. [CLICK 3] The box names two traps students often miss. Odds are not a rate, and the odds ratio is a ratio of odds, never a ratio of rates. The last line defends the word crude: crude is a flag, not an insult. The crude odds ratio is not wrong; it faithfully answers the pooled question, just a different one from the question we are about to ask. [NEXT]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
section: Now split the very same applicants by department
text:
Now split the very same applicants by department























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Here is the pooled picture once more, the same dramatic gap we started with. Keep it in your eye for a moment. [CLICK 1] Now we split these exact same applicants by the department they applied to, in the single most important pair of pictures in the chapter. In Engineering the women were admitted at sixty-eight point zero percent and the men at sixty-three point zero percent, so the women were actually admitted at the higher rate. In Psychology the two rates are close, twenty-three point nine percent for the women and twenty-seven point seven percent for the men. Nothing in the data changed between these two slides; we only stopped pooling, yet the dramatic aggregate gap does not appear inside either department. This pooled-versus-stratified disagreement, where an aggregate gap shrinks or flips once you split by a third variable, is Simpson’s paradox. [NEXT]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
section: Within each department, the advantage dissolves
text:
Within each department, the advantage dissolves























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~60s] Let us put exact odds ratios on what the panels showed, dividing the men’s odds by the women’s within each department. These are within-department odds ratios, not the single model-adjusted odds ratio we will estimate next. In Psychology the within-department odds ratio is about one point two two, and in Engineering it is about zero point eight zero. [CLICK 1] Now compare those with where we started. The pooled crude odds ratio was three point two five, yet that value appears in neither department; it has shrunk to about one point two two in Psychology and slipped below one, to about zero point eight zero, in Engineering, where the women’s odds actually edge ahead. The strong aggregate advantage for men has dissolved, and in one department it has even reversed. For descriptive context, a chi-squared test of independence on the pooled table returns about eighty-one point three, confirming that sex and admission are associated once the data are pooled. [NEXT]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
text:
The clue: who applied where













Engineering admits ~63%; Psychology admits ~25%. Men were concentrated at the easy door; women at the hard one.



[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~62s] The paradox is real, so the natural question is what produces it. The answer begins with a single clue, which is where each group’s applications were concentrated. [CLICK 1] Count the applicants by department and the asymmetry is striking. Engineering had five hundred sixty men but only twenty-five women, while Psychology had three hundred ninety-three women and one hundred ninety-one men, so the applications were concentrated differently by sex. Engineering also admitted roughly sixty-three percent of its applicants, while Psychology admitted only about twenty-five percent, so the men were concentrated at the easy door and the women at the hard one. One caution before we leave these numbers. They belong to berk-sub, the two-department subset with men coded one. A larger version of these data, with more departments and women coded as the group of interest, uses different constants, so always name the dataset before you compare. We will not yet explain the model mechanism behind this pooled-versus-stratified disagreement, because that is the payoff of the next lecture. [NEXT]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
text:
Step through it yourself

Simulated Berkeley-style explorer from the Chapter 10 notes: switch the View from Aggregate to Stratified, then step the Department control to watch the male advantage shrink or reverse within each stratum.Try the live interactive version in the course notes.

[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 0 · ~50s] The chapter includes a live explorer for exactly this effect, and it is worth a few minutes. You begin with the View set to Aggregate, where you see the same apparent male advantage we started with. You then switch the View to Stratified and step the Department control from one stratum to the next, watching the advantage shrink or reverse as you go. One honest detail matters here. The stepping departments inside the app are a clean Berkeley-style simulation, built so the mechanism is easy to see on demand, whereas every figure in this deck uses the real two-department berk-sub data, and a bridge panel ties the two together. The live version is linked in the notes, and stepping through it yourself makes the next lecture much easier. [NEXT]

title: Simpson’s Paradox: When the Aggregate Lies
text:
So who is lying?







Key takeaway


A pooled association can shrink or reverse once you stratify by a third variable. In berk_sub the crude OR of 3.25 dissolves within departments — Psychology ~ 1.22, Engineering ~ 0.80. The aggregate and the strata answer different questions — and “crude” is a flag, not an insult.




So who is lying — the aggregate, or the departments? Neither does, yet they disagree.
Next: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale. Notes, Ch 10 §Simpson’s Paradox · Practice Quiz 8

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Let us close on the question that frames the chapter. A pooled association can shrink, or even reverse, once you stratify by a third variable. In the berk-sub data the crude odds ratio of three point two five dissolves within departments, falling to about one point two two in Psychology and to about zero point eight zero in Engineering. Hold on to two ideas as you go. The aggregate and the strata answer different questions, and crude is a flag, not an insult. So who is lying, the aggregate or the departments? Neither one is lying, and yet they plainly disagree, which is what makes this a paradox rather than a mistake. In the next lecture we resolve why, by viewing this reversal as omitted variable bias on the logit scale. The notes and Practice Quiz Eight are linked just below. [END]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
section: You solved this mystery once before — in Session 4
text:
You solved this mystery once before — in Session 4
























[DECK ch10-02 · 9 min · ~1150 words · clicks 9 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~50s] You have already solved a puzzle shaped exactly like this one. Back in Session four, a raw school gap that looked convincing shrank once we held a reading score equal, because a lurking variable had quietly inflated it. That was omitted variable bias on the ordinary regression scale. Here, in the berk underscore sub data, the two-department Berkeley subset, the crude odds ratio comparing men and women comes out near three point two five, far to the right of the no-effect line, which looks like strong evidence that men are favored. [CLICK 1] The encouraging part is that this is a familiar story on a different scale. In this Berkeley example, the paradox behaves like omitted variable bias for a binary outcome, with the same two confounding conditions you already know, now operating on the logit scale. Let us walk through those two conditions with the Berkeley data, and then repair the estimate. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
text:
Condition 1 — department predicts admission























[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Recall that omitting a variable bites only when two confounding conditions hold together, so let us check them one at a time. The first condition is that the lurking variable, here the department, must be associated with the outcome. Pooling across sex in the two-department Berkeley subset, the two admission rates are easy to compare directly. [CLICK 1] The gap is large. Engineering admits about sixty-three percent of its applicants, while Psychology admits only about twenty-five percent. Department is strongly associated with admission, so the first condition is met. This fact alone, however, does not make department a confounder. On the odds-ratio scale, adding outcome-related covariates can still move an odds ratio because odds ratios are non-collapsible; for confounding, we still need the second link. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
text:
Condition 2 — department tracks sex























[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~58s] The second condition is the one people most often forget. The lurking variable must also track the predictor of interest, which here is sex. We therefore count how many men and women applied to each department. [CLICK 1] The asymmetry is striking. In the two-department Berkeley subset, Engineering had five hundred sixty men but only twenty-five women, while Psychology had three hundred ninety-three women and one hundred ninety-one men. Applications were concentrated differently by sex, with more men in the higher-admission department and more women in the lower-admission department, so department is correlated with sex. Both links now hold: department is associated with admission, and department tracks sex. When a variable satisfies both conditions, it is a confounder. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
section: The same two conditions you already know
text:
The same two conditions you already know























[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~56s] This is exactly the picture you drew for the two schools in Session four, with the labels changed for this problem. A confounder sits above both the predictor and the outcome. Department reaches across to sex, which is condition two, and down to admission, which is condition one, while the crude association we measured at the start runs along the bottom, contaminated by both links. [CLICK 1] Because both links hold, department confounds the crude sex comparison, and that is the source of the pooled-to-stratified shift. One honest caveat belongs here. On the logit scale the odds ratio is non-collapsible, which means it can change after adding a covariate even when there is no confounding, so comparing the crude and adjusted odds ratios does not isolate confounding quite as cleanly as it does for ordinary regression coefficients. Here, though, the structure is genuine confounding. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
section: The fix is a main-effect covariate, not an interaction
text:
The fix is a main-effect covariate, not an interaction


































[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 2 · ~62s] We repair the estimate by adding department to the model as its own term, fitting admitted on male plus engineering. This is a main-effect covariate, and it holds department constant while we read the sex effect. With department in the model, the male coefficient on the log-odds scale falls from one point one eight to zero point one two. [CLICK 1] A tempting detour is to reach instead for a product term, fitting male times engineering, on the reasoning that the sex effect may differ by department. That is a different question. A product term estimates effect modification, which asks whether the sex effect changes from one department to another. [CLICK 2] For a confounding question, start with the confounder as a main-effect covariate; add an interaction only when the question is effect modification. One reassurance is worth stating plainly: a coefficient that shifts after you add a genuine confounder can be adjustment doing its job, not a broken model. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
section: The forest plot — the signature of the paradox
text:
The forest plot — the signature of the paradox























[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~60s] A forest plot makes the whole story visible at a glance. The crude odds ratio for male sits far to the right of the no-effect line, near three point two five, which is the apparent advantage we began with. [CLICK 1] Adding department drops the adjusted odds ratio to about one point one three, with a confidence interval from zero point seven nine to one point six two that now crosses the no-effect line. Notice the honest way to describe this. The aggregate odds ratio attenuates dramatically toward one; it does not reverse. Only within Engineering does the odds ratio fall below one, at roughly zero point eight zero, where women’s odds edge slightly ahead. A move this large can be adjustment working, not a broken model, and the apparent advantage was mostly composition, consistent with how applications were distributed across departments. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
section: Lines, then odds — the crude-to-adjusted ledger
text:
Lines, then odds — the crude-to-adjusted ledger


































[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 2 · ~62s] It helps to see the same diagnostic habit repeated across the course: compare the crude estimate with the model-adjusted comparison. [CLICK 1] On the ordinary regression scale, in Chapter two, we saw a raw school gap shrink after holding reading equal, and another raw gap even change sign after adjustment. Those were line-model examples, measured in points of the outcome. [CLICK 2] Now read the logistic row, which is an odds ratio. In the two-department Berkeley subset, the crude sex odds ratio of three point two five attenuates to an adjusted one point one three. The lesson is not that adjustment magically finds truth. It is that, when the covariate is a justified confounder, the adjusted estimate answers the conditional comparison we meant to ask. [NEXT]

title: Why the Reversal? OVB on the Logit Scale
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


In this Berkeley example, the paradox is confounding on the logit scale: department predicts admission and tracks sex, so it confounds the crude sex effect. Start with a main-effect covariate (admitted ~ male + engineering) for the confounding question; add an interaction only for effect modification. The crude OR 3.25 attenuates dramatically toward 1 (adjusted OR ≈ 1.13, CI 0.79–1.62) — reversing only within Engineering — and a big shift can be adjustment working, not a broken model. The OR is non-collapsible, so always name the dataset and read the result as consistent with composition differences, never “department causes.”




Next: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale. Notes, Ch 10 §Why the Reversal · Practice Quiz 8

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~52s] Let us carry a few clear lessons forward. In this Berkeley example, Simpson’s paradox is confounding on the logit scale, arising when a third variable both predicts the outcome and tracks the predictor, exactly as department does for sex and admission. For a confounding question, start by adding that confounder as a main-effect covariate; an interaction is for effect modification. When we add department here, the crude odds ratio of three point two five attenuates dramatically toward one, landing at an adjusted one point one three, and a shift that large can be adjustment working, not a model breaking. Read the result as consistent with composition differences rather than as proof of cause, and because the odds ratio is non-collapsible, name the dataset every time. In the next lecture we make that adjusted odds ratio precise, by holding a covariate constant on the logit scale. The notes and Practice Quiz Eight are linked just below. [END]

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
section: Same data, two odds ratios — which should we report?
text:
Same data, two odds ratios — which should we report?
























[DECK ch10-03 · 9 min · ~1108 words · clicks 9 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~50s] In the previous lecture, we watched a single odds ratio change once we adjusted for department. Let us begin there. In the berk sub data, the two-department Berkeley subset of one thousand one hundred sixty-nine applicants, the crude odds ratio comparing men and women is three point two five, sitting far to the right of the no-effect line. [CLICK 1] Now we add the second estimate. After holding department constant, the adjusted odds ratio for the same comparison is one point one three, and its interval crosses the no-effect line. This is the central question of the chapter. One dataset has handed us two very different odds ratios, three point two five and one point one three, so which one should we report. The useful reply is that both are real, and the rest of this lecture explains why they answer two different questions. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
text:
Two odds ratios, two questions























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] The two odds ratios feel like rivals, but they are answers to two separate questions. The crude odds ratio of three point two five is the aggregate comparison. It pools everyone together and asks how the odds of admission for all men compare with the odds for all women, ignoring which department they applied to. [CLICK 1] The adjusted odds ratio of one point one three asks a narrower question. It compares men and women within the same department, holding department constant, and so it reports the sex gap once everyone is on equal footing. This distinction matters, because it is tempting to describe the adjusted number as the crude number simply cleaned up. That description is not quite right. The adjusted odds ratio is not a tidier version of the same comparison; it is the answer to a genuinely different, within-department question. This echoes a lesson from earlier in the course, where one raw number and one adjusted number answered two questions rather than one. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
section: The model puts department in — and the odds multiply

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
section: Crude versus adjusted odds ratios, side by side
text:
Crude versus adjusted odds ratios, side by side


































[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 2 · ~72s] Now let us read the crude and adjusted odds ratios side by side. Begin with sex on its own, the crude column, where the sex-only male odds ratio is three point two five. [CLICK 1] Once department joins the model, the adjusted column fills in. The adjusted odds ratio for male is one point one three, the odds multiplier for being male while holding department constant, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point seven nine to one point six two that crosses one. In the same model, the adjusted odds ratio for engineering is four point seven, the multiplier for applying to Engineering rather than Psychology while holding sex constant. Each adjusted column entry comes from the multiple model and is read by holding the other predictor fixed. [CLICK 2] Here is the distinction to keep. Each adjusted odds ratio is a within-stratum multiplier, not the crude odds ratio cleaned up. It answers the within-department question, which is the logit-scale analogue of holding a covariate constant in ANCOVA. A multiplier describes an association on the odds scale, never a cause, and the sex indicator is coded male equals one. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
section: A category is a label, not a number line
text:
A category is a label, not a number line
For a nominal multi-level predictor, use a factor → k − 1 dummies. A numeric code asserts a linear, equally spaced trend.


































[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~66s] Berkeley’s department had only two levels, but many categorical predictors have more, so let us see the general rule with Titanic passenger class, which has three levels. A numeric code of one, two, and three is allowed only if you intentionally want one linear, equally spaced trend in log-odds. That is a modeling assumption, not just a shortcut. [CLICK 1] For nominal categories like passenger class labels, use a factor. R then turns k levels into k minus one dummies. Three levels therefore make two dummies, one comparing second class with first and one comparing third class with first, with first class serving as the reference. [CLICK 2] Here is the idea to keep. A category is a label, not a number line. R will silently accept a one-two-three code, but acceptance does not mean it matches the question. Use a factor for nominal categories; use a numeric code only when a linear trend is the model you mean. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
section: The same phrase reads a published table
text:
The same phrase reads a published table

Every adjusted odds ratio you read — in this course or in print — carries the same phrase.









One bridge to the literature


A published logistic table reports an adjusted odds ratio for each predictor — each one holding the others fixed. You read every row with the same sentence you just used for Berkeley.








predictor
adjusted OR
read as




(X_j)
(e^{_j})
holding the other modeled predictors fixed









[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] The reason this matters beyond our example is that published research reports its results in exactly this form. [CLICK 1] When a study fits a logistic model and prints a table of adjusted odds ratios for the modeled event, each row is one predictor’s multiplier, holding the other predictors fixed. You read every line with the same sentence you just used for Berkeley: this predictor multiplies the odds of the outcome by its odds ratio, comparing like with like on everything else in the model. The phrase holding the others fixed travels from our two-department example straight to a printed research table without changing its meaning. [NEXT]

title: Adjusted Odds Ratios: Holding Constant on the Logit Scale
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A multiple logistic model factors the odds into multipliers: in a no-interaction model, each e to the beta is one adjusted odds ratio — the within-stratum multiplier for that predictor, holding the others fixed (the ANCOVA idea on the logit scale). The crude 3.25 and adjusted 1.13 are both real; they answer two different questions (aggregate vs within-department), not a tidied one. A multi-level predictor enters as a factor → k − 1 dummies when categories are nominal. It is an association, never a cause; the sex indicator is coded male = 1.




Next: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First. Notes, Ch 10 §The Multiple Logistic Regression Model · Practice Quiz 8

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Let us gather the key points to carry forward. A multiple logistic model factors the odds into a product of multipliers. In a no-interaction model, e to the beta sub j is one adjusted odds ratio: the within-stratum multiplier for that predictor while the others are held fixed. The crude odds ratio of three point two five and the adjusted odds ratio of one point one three are both real, because they answer two different questions, the aggregate comparison and the within-department comparison. For nominal categories, use a factor and let R make k minus one dummies; use numeric coding only when you mean a linear trend. Throughout, an odds ratio reports an association and not a cause, and the sex indicator is coded male equals one. Next we make these odds ratios precise with confidence intervals, working on the log scale first. The notes and Practice Quiz Eight are linked just below. [END]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
section: This interval is not centred on the estimate
text:
This interval is not centred on the estimate

The trap























[DECK ch10-04 · 8 min · ~1094 words · clicks 8 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Here is a confidence interval for an odds ratio, and something about it looks broken. The odds ratio is zero point five zero, yet inside its own ninety-five percent interval, which runs from zero point three four nine to zero point seven zero seven, the estimate does not sit in the middle. It leans to the left. [CLICK 1] Look at the two arms. The shorter arm reaches down from zero point five zero to zero point three four nine, while the longer arm reaches up to zero point seven zero seven. The arms are genuinely unequal, and a natural reaction is to suspect a mistake. By the end of this lecture you will see that this asymmetry is not a bug. It is the correct shape for this back-transformed Wald interval, and it comes directly from one rule: build the interval on the log scale first, then exponentiate. [NEXT]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
section: Step 1 – build it where the model is symmetric
text:
Step 1 – build it where the model is symmetric























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~62s] The fix begins by choosing the right scale. On the log-odds scale the logistic model is symmetric, so this is where we build the interval. The rule is the familiar one: take the coefficient b and go out one point nine six standard errors on each side. This is a Wald interval, which uses the z value rather than a t value, because maximum-likelihood relies on large-sample normality. For our demo example, b is negative zero point seven zero zero and the standard error is zero point one eight zero, so one point nine six times the standard error is zero point three five three. [CLICK 1] Adding and subtracting that margin gives a log-odds interval from negative one point zero five three to negative zero point three four seven, with b sitting exactly in the middle. Here is the first trap to avoid. Do not exponentiate the estimate and then attach plus or minus to it. The plus or minus belongs on the log scale, and we exponentiate only afterward. [NEXT]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
text:
Step 2 – exponentiate the endpoints























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Now make the two scales visible at once. The top rail is the log-odds scale, where we just worked. The interval runs symmetrically about b, and the null value, the point of no association, sits at zero. [CLICK 1] Step two is to exponentiate the two endpoints, and the arrows carry each one down to the bottom rail, the odds-ratio scale. The exponential function stretches large values and compresses small ones, so the symmetric interval on top arrives warped on the bottom. The endpoints become zero point three four nine and zero point seven zero seven, and the odds ratio, zero point five zero, is no longer in the middle. Watch the null value too: zero on the log scale maps to one on the odds-ratio scale, because exponentiating zero gives one. That single mapping, symmetric interval into warped interval, is the heart of the procedure. [NEXT]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
section: The warp is correct, not a bug
text:
The warp is correct, not a bug























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] Let us name the second trap directly. A student looks at that warped interval and concludes that an asymmetric interval means something went wrong. That conclusion is mistaken. For this back-transformed Wald interval, equal distances on the log scale become unequal distances on the odds-ratio scale. That exponential warp also explains the off-center look, because the odds ratio of zero point five zero is the geometric center of its interval rather than the arithmetic center. [CLICK 1] The deeper point is that the odds ratio lives on a ratio scale, bounded below by zero with no upper bound, so this back-transformed interval cannot run negative. On that scale, an odds ratio of two, a doubling, and an odds ratio of zero point five, a halving, are equal-magnitude effects in opposite directions. The asymmetry you see is therefore not a defect to be corrected. It is this ratio-scale interval working as intended, so we build the interval on the log scale and then transform it. [NEXT]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
text:
Step 3 – does the interval cover 1?


































[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~70s] Once you have the odds-ratio interval, significance is a single visual check: does the interval cover the null value. Recall that on the odds-ratio scale the null value is one. Our worked interval, from zero point three four nine to zero point seven zero seven, sits entirely below one, so it clears the null and we call the effect significant. [CLICK 1] For contrast, here is an interval that does cover one. Using the adjusted male odds ratio from the Berkeley example, the estimate is one point one three with a ninety-five percent interval from zero point seven nine to one point six two. That interval straddles one, so we cannot reject the null, and the effect is not significant. [CLICK 2] This is the third trap, and it is the costliest. Once you have exponentiated, you compare the interval to one, never to zero. The null value travelled with the endpoints, from zero on the log scale to one on the odds-ratio scale, so comparing to zero here would answer the wrong question. [NEXT]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
section: Reporting 1 over OR reverses the endpoints
text:
Reporting 1 over OR reverses the endpoints


































[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 2 · ~62s] One last move comes up whenever an odds ratio is below one and we prefer to report its reciprocal, one over the odds ratio. Start from our interval, which runs from a lower endpoint of zero point three four nine to an upper endpoint of zero point seven zero seven. [CLICK 1] Now reciprocate each endpoint. Because one over x is a decreasing function, the larger input produces the smaller output, so the endpoints reverse their order. One over zero point seven zero seven is one point four, and one over zero point three four nine is two point nine, giving a reciprocal interval from one point four to two point nine. The old upper endpoint, zero point seven zero seven, has become the new lower endpoint. [CLICK 2] Here is the rule worth holding onto. Reciprocating reverses the endpoints, so take one over each endpoint and then swap their order. If you merely flip a sign or keep the same arrangement, the interval will come out backwards. [NEXT]

title: OR Confidence Intervals: Log Scale First
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Log scale first. Build the interval where the model is symmetric — b ± 1.96 × SE (a Wald interval, z not t) — then exponentiate the endpoints. The result is asymmetric around the OR, and that is correct: the OR is a ratio scale bounded below by 0. Read significance against the null of 1, never 0 — if the interval covers 1, the effect is not significant. And 1 / OR reverses the endpoints — the old upper becomes the new lower.




Next: Testing Logistic Coefficients — Wald z and the LR Block Test. Notes, Ch 10 §Confidence Intervals for Odds Ratios · Practice Quiz 7

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Let us gather the recipe to carry forward. Build the confidence interval on the log scale first, where the model is symmetric, by taking b plus or minus one point nine six standard errors, a Wald interval that uses z rather than t. Then exponentiate the two endpoints. The interval that results is asymmetric around the odds ratio, and that asymmetry is correct, because the odds ratio is a ratio scale bounded below by zero. Read significance against the null value of one, never zero: if the interval covers one, the effect is not significant. And if you report one over the odds ratio, remember that reciprocating reverses the endpoints. In the next lecture we will test these coefficients directly, with the Wald z test and the likelihood-ratio block test. The notes and Practice Quiz Seven are linked just below. [END]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
text:
Where did the t-test go?



[DECK ch10-05 · 9 min · ~1120 words · clicks 7 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~38s] In linear regression you tested a single coefficient with a t-test, so it is natural to ask where that t-test goes once the outcome is a yes-or-no event. The question itself does not change: you still ask whether one coefficient could plausibly be zero, the claim of no conditional log-odds association in this fitted model. [PAUSE] What changes is the reference curve. Ordinary logistic regression is fitted by maximum likelihood, and the usual coefficient test uses large-sample normal theory rather than a separately estimated residual variance. That is why the statistic is read against a large-sample normal reference, and the output reports a z. Today we will read that z, square it into the Wald test statistic most software prints, and then meet the block test a multi-level factor truly needs. [NEXT]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
section: The Wald test: read the z, then square
text:
The Wald test: read the z, then square


































[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 2 · ~68s] The Wald test takes the simplest possible form. You divide the estimated coefficient by its standard error, and that ratio is the z-statistic. Here, for the Berkeley adjusted male coefficient, the estimate is zero point one two four one and the standard error is zero point one eight one four. [CLICK 1] Read straight from the output, the z-statistic is zero point six eight. Now square it. The square of the z is the Wald chi-squared statistic on one degree of freedom, which works out to zero point four seven, and that chi-squared form is what most software prints. Squaring is exact algebra; the chi-squared reference distribution for the p-value is the large-sample approximation. Read the z, then square it: that is the computation to remember. [CLICK 2] The large-sample two-sided p-value is zero point four nine, far above the usual threshold, so the male coefficient is not significant once department is in the model. Notice the careful wording. We do not conclude that men and women are equal, and we do not conclude that sex has no effect; we conclude only that there is no evidence against equal adjusted odds for men and women within department. [NEXT]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
text:
One conclusion, read two ways























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~68s] This is the fourth time you are seeing this particular coefficient. You met the adjusted male odds ratio of one point one three when we corrected the aggregate comparison, read it as a within-department multiplier, and built its confidence interval. That interval, from zero point seven nine to one point six two, straddles the null value of one. [CLICK 1] Notice the agreement. The Wald test and the matching Wald confidence interval are not two separate findings that happen to line up; they are two readings of the very same standard error and the same large-sample normal approximation. Here, that shared approximation gives the same decision two ways, up to rounding: the p-value of zero point four nine sits above five percent, and this ninety-five percent Wald odds-ratio interval covers one. When the matching interval clears one, the test is significant, and when it covers one, the test is not. As a result, you can report whichever your reader finds clearer and check one conclusion against the other every time. [NEXT]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
section: Does the factor matter at all?
text:
Does the factor matter at all?























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] Passenger class on the Titanic has three levels, so the model represents it with two dummy variables, each with its own z. The second-class dummy has a z of negative five point six eight, and the third-class dummy has a z of negative ten point one four. Both are large, and it is tempting to read either one as the test for class. [CLICK 1] That reading is the trap. Each z compares only one level against the reference, first class, so neither one answers the question you usually care about, which is whether class matters at all. A multi-level factor enters and leaves the model as a single block of dummies, so it deserves a single test. Because dropping class removes two dummies at once, that test lives on two degrees of freedom. This is the same omnibus logic you use for a multi-level factor in linear regression. [NEXT]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
section: Two nested fits, the incremental F reborn
text:
Two nested fits, the incremental F reborn


































[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~70s] The block test is the likelihood-ratio test. You fit a full model with class and a reduced model without it, nested inside the full one. The statistic, written G-squared, is two times the difference in their log-likelihoods, and under the null that class adds nothing it is compared to a large-sample chi-squared reference distribution on as many degrees of freedom as parameters you dropped. [CLICK 1] The important modeling rule is that both models must be fitted on exactly the same rows. That is why we first reduce the Titanic data to the one thousand forty-six passengers who are complete on every variable, out of one thousand three hundred nine. On different rows, the two log-likelihoods would not be comparable. [CLICK 2] Carrying that out, the class block gives a G-squared of one hundred eighteen point eight nine on two degrees of freedom, with an asymptotic p-value below one in a thousand. So class adds substantial model information conditional on sex and age, the clean answer no single dummy z could give you. This is the logistic analogue of the Type three F-test, now on the likelihood scale rather than the residual-sum-of-squares scale. [NEXT]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
section: z-squared is exact; the LR statistic is its own
text:
z-squared is exact; the LR statistic is its own























[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] For a single coefficient, the square of the z is the Wald chi-squared statistic exactly; that equality is pure algebra. The likelihood-ratio G-squared is a genuinely different statistic that tends to agree with the Wald value in large regular samples, but it is not the same calculation. For Titanic age, the Wald chi-squared is twenty-nine point five and the likelihood-ratio G-squared is thirty-one point three, which are close. For the much larger Berkeley engineering coefficient, the Wald chi-squared is eighty-three point six while the likelihood-ratio statistic is ninety-two point one, and the gap is wider, because for a coefficient large in magnitude the Wald test can be conservative. [CLICK 1] The guidance follows directly. Use the Wald test for routine screening, since it is already sitting in the standard output. Prefer the likelihood-ratio test when the sample is small enough that the Wald approximation feels fragile, when a coefficient is large in magnitude, or when you are testing a multi-level factor as a block. And remember the limit of squaring: the square of the z gives you the Wald chi-squared only, never the likelihood-ratio statistic. [NEXT]

title: Testing Logistic Coefficients: Wald z and the LR Block Test
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


One coefficient — Wald. z = b / SE; square it for the Wald χ² statistic = z², then use the large-sample χ²(1) reference for the p-value. The same Wald approximation gives the same decision as the matching Wald odds-ratio interval read against 1, up to rounding.
A multi-level factor — LR block test. G² = 2(ℓ_full − ℓ_reduced), compared to an asymptotic χ² reference on as many degrees of freedom as dummies dropped — the logistic twin of the incremental F, and both fits must use the same rows.
Use Wald to screen; prefer LR for factors, strong effects, and fragile small-sample Wald results.




Next: Predicted Probabilities — turning the model into a probability. Notes, Ch 10 §Inference: Wald and Likelihood Ratio Tests · Practice Quiz 8

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Let us gather what to carry forward. To test one coefficient, divide it by its standard error to get the z, square that for the Wald chi-squared statistic, and use the large-sample chi-squared reference for the p-value. The same Wald approximation gives the same decision as the matching odds-ratio interval, up to rounding. To ask whether a multi-level factor matters at all, do not read the individual dummy z’s; run one likelihood-ratio block test, where G-squared is twice the gain in log-likelihood and the p-value uses an asymptotic chi-squared reference on as many degrees of freedom as dummies you dropped. That block test is the logistic rebirth of the incremental F-test for nested linear models, and it needs both fits on the same rows. In daily work, reach for the Wald test to screen, and the likelihood-ratio test for factors, strong effects, and fragile small-sample Wald results. In the next lecture we will turn the fitted model into predicted probabilities. The notes and Practice Quiz Eight are linked just below. [END]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
section: Nobody boards a lifeboat with an odds ratio
text:
Nobody boards a lifeboat with an odds ratio


[DECK ch10-06 · 9 min · ~1165 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~52s] We have spent this chapter building a logistic model and reading its odds ratios. Odds ratios are useful, but they are not how most readers think. Nobody boards a lifeboat with an odds ratio in hand. A reader wants a probability, the model-predicted chance of survival for a concrete passenger profile. That is the move for this lecture. We will take the very same fitted model, with all of its coefficients and odds ratios already in place, and translate it onto the probability scale. Recall that the model includes sex, class, and age together, with the adjusted odds ratio for sex being about twelve. Instead of multiplying odds, we will report, for a passenger you can picture, the predicted probability of surviving. This is the deliverable that finally makes a logistic model legible. [NEXT]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
text:
Pick profiles that matter
Substantive, not mechanical























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~66s] The first decision is which passengers to predict for, and we make that decision with datagrid. We choose meaningful values for each predictor and let the grid cross every combination. Here we vary sex across female and male, class across first, second, and third, and age across ten, thirty, and fifty years. For each profile, the function predictions returns the model’s predicted probability of surviving. The marginaleffects package is the course default for this, though ggeffects can do the same job. [CLICK 1] Notice that this choice is substantive, not mechanical. You are picking people the reader actually cares about, rather than reaching for round numbers by reflex. A first-class woman of thirty and a third-class man of thirty are two passengers a reader can picture, and that is exactly why they make a good profile. [NEXT]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
section: Read two passengers off the table
text:
Read two passengers off the table
Every row names the event: P of surviving













































[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 3 · ~90s] Here is the table a results section needs, cropped to two profiles at age thirty, and notice that every row names the event, the probability of surviving. Read the first row as a full sentence. For a first-class woman of thirty, the predicted probability of surviving is about ninety-two percent, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight nine five to zero point nine five two. These intervals come straight from predictions on the response scale. [CLICK 1] Now read the second row the same way. For a third-class man of thirty, the predicted probability of surviving is about nine percent, with a confidence interval from zero point zero six six to zero point one one seven. Stated as probabilities, the contrast is immediate in a way that no odds ratio ever is. [CLICK 2] There is one caution to state out loud. These two passengers differ in sex and in class at once, read at the same age. So the gap between ninety-two percent and nine percent describes two different profiles, not a one-predictor contrast. [CLICK 3] To isolate the sex comparison, you hold class fixed and compare a woman with a man inside the same class at age thirty. Within first class the female sits at zero point nine two three and the male at zero point four nine eight; within third class the female sits at zero point five five zero and the male at zero point zero nine one. Those are conditional sex contrasts at age thirty, holding class fixed, and they are far smaller than the gap between the two headline rows. The curves make this comparison across ages. [NEXT]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
section: The whole model, as six curves
text:
The whole model, as six curves
A constant odds ratio, a varying gap























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] The function plot predictions turns the same model into a picture. Each panel is one passenger class, the curves trace the predicted probability of surviving across age, and the shaded bands are ninety-five percent confidence intervals that widen where prediction uncertainty is larger, often near sparse or edge regions of the data. In every panel the female curve sits well above the male curve, and every curve slopes gently downward with age, so older passengers are predicted to survive a little less often. The curves also flatten near the top and bottom, because a probability can never leave the zero-to-one range. [CLICK 1] Now look at the female-minus-male gap at age thirty. In first class it is about forty-two point five percentage points, in second class about fifty-five point four, and in third class about forty-five point nine. We state that gap in percentage points, never in percent, because it is a difference of two probabilities. Notice that this gap is not constant; it changes with the baseline, even though the model’s odds ratio for sex is a single constant multiplier on the odds. That is precisely why we report probabilities. A single summary number, the average marginal effect, is the next lecture. [NEXT]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
section: Why two profiles differ: build the logit
text:
Why two profiles differ: build the logit







[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 0 · ~72s] This is the calculator from the chapter, running on real Titanic data with coefficients rounded for display, so the bars you see are rounded rather than simulated. It shows how this fitted model builds a predicted probability. For a chosen passenger the model first builds a linear predictor, called the logit, by adding up the intercept and the contributions of class, sex, and age, each drawn as one bar. That single number is then squashed through the inverse-logit into a probability between zero and one. Take our two passengers at age thirty. The first-class woman’s contributions sum to a logit of about two point four nine, which the inverse-logit turns into about ninety-two percent. The third-class man’s contributions sum to a logit of about negative two point three zero, which becomes about nine percent. This two-panel view shows the whole mechanism: the coefficients you read as odds ratios are the same numbers stacking up inside the logit, and the inverse-logit bends that straight sum into a probability. Drag the age slider in the live version and watch the bars and the probability move together. [NEXT]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
section: The reporting rule: a table AND a figure
text:
The reporting rule: a table AND a figure
The odds-ratio table is not the results section























[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Let us be concrete about the reporting deliverable. A useful logistic-results section needs both a predicted-probability table at meaningful profiles and a figure of the curves with confidence bands. Omitting the figure is a named common mistake, so plan for both from the start. [CLICK 1] Here is the idea to hold on to. The odds-ratio table is not the full interpretation. A reader who only sees odds ratios still does not know the model-predicted probability for a profile they care about. The probability-scale translation, the table and the figure together, is what answers that question. Report your focal results as probabilities, name the event each time, and state any comparison in percentage points. [NEXT]

title: From Odds Ratios to People: Predicted Probabilities
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Fit on the log-odds scale, but report on the probability scale. Use datagrid() and predictions() for a table at meaningful profiles, and plot_predictions() for the figure with 95% CIs — both are the deliverable. A constant odds ratio becomes a probability gap that varies with the baseline, which is exactly why probabilities communicate.




Next: Average marginal effects — one summary number for a predictor. Notes, Ch 10 §Predicted Probabilities

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Let us gather what to carry forward. You fit a logistic model on the log-odds scale, but you report it on the probability scale, because that is where your reader can understand the result. Use datagrid and predictions for a table at profiles that matter, and use plot predictions for the figure with confidence bands. Keep one idea above all: a single constant odds ratio becomes a probability gap that varies with the baseline, which is the whole reason probabilities communicate. In the next lecture we will collapse that varying gap into one careful summary number, the average marginal effect. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
section: An odds ratio, or points more likely?
text:
An odds ratio, or points more likely?


[DECK ch10-07 · 9 min · ~1165 words · clicks 5 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Throughout this chapter we have read logistic models as odds ratios. An odds ratio is honest and precise, but consider how it actually sounds to a reader. From the simple model in Chapter nine, the odds of survival for women were about eleven point three times as high as for men. That is a true statement, yet most listeners cannot picture eleven point three times the odds. Now consider the same effect said a second way. Being female is associated with about fifty-four percentage points more chance of surviving. That sentence lands immediately, because it speaks in the currency people actually live in, the probability of the event. This lecture is about that second number, the average marginal effect, which is the most communicable way to report a predictor. Notice that both numbers come from the very same simple model, and we will always name the model a number belongs to. [NEXT]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
text:
One effect, three currencies
Defuse: “the logistic effect is one number on every scale”























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~80s] Let us slow down on one familiar effect and watch it change costume. Everything on this slide comes from the simple model in Chapter nine, the one with sex as the only predictor, so the three summaries belong to one fitted model. The model is fitted on the log-odds scale, where the displayed coefficient for sex is two point four two. Exponentiating the full fitted coefficient gives the odds ratio, eleven point three, which says women’s odds of survival are about eleven times as high as men’s odds. The third currency is the one we are after. The average marginal effect is computed from fitted predictions and then averaged; here it is about plus fifty-four percentage points, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval from forty-eight point eight to fifty-eight point five percentage points. [CLICK 1] Here is the trap to retire. A common misreading is to treat the logistic effect as one number that means the same thing on every scale. These are not three different findings; they are three summaries from the same simple model. The average marginal effect is the one your reader can feel because it lives on the probability scale. [NEXT]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
section: What the average marginal effect is
text:
What the average marginal effect is
Defuse: “the AME is the change at the average passenger”























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] Here is what the average marginal effect actually measures. On the probability scale the effect of a predictor is not one fixed number, because the logistic curve is steep in the middle and nearly flat at its ends. The observation-level quantity depends on the predictor type. For age, each passenger has a local probability-scale slope. For sex or class, each passenger has a model-based contrast between two settings. [CLICK 1] Then avg slopes averages those observation-level changes into one number. Notice what that average is, because it is easy to get backwards. It is not the change evaluated at one made-up average passenger. On a curved scale, averaging individual changes and changing the average profile are genuinely different quantities. One discipline follows from this. Because AMEs live on the probability scale, you report them in percentage points, never in percent; for continuous predictors, name the unit, such as percentage points per year. [NEXT]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
text:
The output, read in points
Adjusted model: name it every time























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~76s] Now read the output from the multiple model, the one that adjusts for class and age, and always name the model, because the numbers belong to it. For each passenger, compare the fitted probability if sex is set to female with the fitted probability if sex is set to male, while keeping that passenger’s observed class and age; average those contrasts and the result is about plus forty-nine percentage points. For class, change the passenger’s class from first to second or third while keeping observed sex and age, then average those contrasts: second class is about twenty-two point seven percentage points lower, and third class about thirty-eight point three percentage points lower. Each additional year of age is associated with a small local slope, about negative zero point five two of a percentage point per year. Each row is directly a probability-scale change, which is what makes these numbers useful for a non-statistical audience. [CLICK 1] Notice one thing about sex. In the simple model the average marginal effect was about plus fifty-four percentage points; in this adjusted model it is about plus forty-nine. Those are close, but they come from different models, so they are not the same number. Always say which model a marginal effect belongs to, and never blend the two. [NEXT]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
section: The whole model, as one picture
text:
The whole model, as one picture
Ordered by signed estimate, against zero























[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] The same average marginal effects make a clean picture. Each point is one predictor’s average marginal effect on the probability of surviving, the horizontal bar is its ninety-five percent confidence interval, and the rows are ordered by signed point estimate. Being female has the largest positive effect and sits at the top. The age effect is small and just below zero. The two class contrasts are negative, and the third-versus-first contrast is the most negative of all, sitting at the bottom. The dashed vertical line marks zero, the value of no effect. [CLICK 1] Reading a plot like this is simple. When a confidence interval does not cross the dashed zero line, the effect is statistically distinguishable from no effect at the five percent level, and here every bar clears zero. Because it speaks entirely in probability, this single figure is often the most persuasive thing you can show an audience that finds odds ratios unintuitive. [NEXT]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
text:
One honest caveat
Defuse: “the AME is one constant probability effect”























[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~76s] The word average carries one honest caveat you should always keep in mind. Because the logistic curve bends, the probability-scale change for a predictor is not constant. For a continuous predictor such as age, the local slope is largest where the predicted probability is near zero point five, where the curve is steepest, and it shrinks toward the flat tails near zero and one. For a binary or categorical predictor, the discrete contrast is largest when the two compared predictions span that steep middle region. The average marginal effect summarizes those varying changes into a single typical number. [CLICK 1] There is a second consequence worth stating. Because the average marginal effect averages over the other predictors as they actually occur in your data, it depends on your sample’s composition. If the mix of people changes, the average marginal effect changes too, even when the model’s coefficients stay exactly the same. So report it as what it is, a clear summary for this particular sample. [NEXT]

title: AMEs: The Honest Percentage-Point Answer
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Report up the ladder: fit on log-odds, publish odds ratios, explain in probabilities.

What it averages: observation-level probability changes: slopes for continuous predictors, contrasts for binary/factor predictors.
How to report it: percentage points, never percent; for continuous predictors, state the unit.
What to name: the model and sample, because curve location and sample composition shape the average.





Next: The staged odds-ratio table — watching coefficients move as you adjust. Notes, Ch 10 §Average marginal effects

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~52s] Let us gather what to carry forward. The cleanest habit in logistic regression is to report up the ladder: fit on log-odds, publish odds ratios, and explain the finding in probabilities. The average marginal effect is that final rung. It averages observation-level probability changes: slopes for continuous predictors, contrasts for categorical predictors. State it in percentage points rather than percent, and name the model every time, since the same predictor gave plus fifty-four points alone and plus forty-nine points adjusted. In the next lecture we will lay several models side by side in a staged odds-ratio table and watch the coefficients move as we adjust. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
text:
A reporting table, anatomized


[DECK ch10-08 · 9 min · ~1128 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~46s] When you finish a logistic regression, this is one clean way to report it. A results section often needs a table like this, where the whole model arrives as a grid of odds ratios, one row for each predictor of Titanic survival. The header tells you immediately that every odds ratio is adjusted for the others, and that first class is the reference for passenger class. Notice that there is no scatterplot and no equation on display, only the numbers a reader actually needs. For this lecture the table is the argument, so our task is to read every row correctly and to name the traps that turn a good table into a wrong one. [NEXT]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
section: Odds ratios in the table, log-odds in the appendix
text:
Odds ratios in the table, log-odds in the appendix























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~68s] The very first thing to get right is which column leads. The headline column is the odds ratio, never the raw coefficient. Recall that the model is fit on the log-odds scale, but those raw numbers are hard to read, so you exponentiate them, and each odds ratio is simply the exponential of its log-odds coefficient. Pasting the raw log-odds straight in as the headline is a named reporting mistake; they belong in an appendix, never in the column your reader sees first. [CLICK 1] Read this leading column directly. The odds ratio for a female passenger is twelve point two, which means the odds of survival for a woman are about twelve times the odds for a man of the same class and age. Notice that the odds ratio for age is zero point nine seven, sitting just below one, so each additional year of age goes with slightly lower odds of survival. An odds ratio above one points to higher odds, an odds ratio below one points to lower odds, and one itself would mean no conditional association for this predictor contrast in this model. [NEXT]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
section: Every odds ratio carries a CI and a p
text:
Every odds ratio carries a CI and a p























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~68s] A single odds ratio on its own is only a point estimate, so the table gives each one a measure of its uncertainty. Two more columns do that work: the ninety-five percent confidence interval and the p-value. [CLICK 1] Read across the female row. The odds ratio is twelve point two, and its confidence interval runs from eight point eight three to sixteen point nine. Here is the reading habit worth keeping for this two-sided model-based interval: if the interval excludes one, it agrees with evidence against the no-association value for that predictor contrast. Notice that the dividing line is one, not zero, because we are reading a ratio rather than a difference. The female interval sits far above one, so this model gives clear evidence of a positive conditional association. Age tells the same story from below, with an interval from zero point nine five to zero point nine eight that stays under one. In both rows the p-value is less than zero point zero zero one, which agrees here with what the intervals already show. [NEXT]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
section: One global p for the whole factor
text:
One global p for the whole factor























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] Passenger class needs special care, because it is a three-level factor that the model splits into two comparison rows, second class versus first and third class versus first. Each row has its own odds ratio, and you can read them: second class is at zero point two eight and third class at zero point one zero, both well below first class. [CLICK 1] The question of whether the class term contributes to this model is one question, not two, so it gets one answer. That answer is the global p-value, reported here as less than zero point zero zero one on two degrees of freedom, which tests the two class dummies together. It comes from the likelihood-ratio test you met earlier, with a chi-squared statistic of about one hundred eighteen point nine, the logistic cousin of the Type Three F-test. Report this single global p for the factor as a whole. Quoting only the two separate dummy p-values, and skipping the global test, is a named reporting mistake you can now avoid. [NEXT]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
text:
The fit row, read honestly


































[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~82s] Underneath the coefficients runs the fit row, the line that tells which data and fit summaries support these numbers. [CLICK 1] Read it from left to right. The sample size is one thousand forty-six passengers, those with a recorded age. The AIC is nine hundred ninety-two, a summary for comparing candidate models fit to the same outcome and rows. The log-likelihood is negative four hundred ninety-one; on its own it is mostly a building block for nested likelihood-ratio tests or penalized criteria. The last entry is Tjur’s R-squared, here zero point three eight. [CLICK 2] That last statistic carries a caution worth stating in exactly these words. Tjur’s R-squared is not variance explained. It is a coefficient of discrimination, the gap between the average predicted probability for the survivors and the average for the non-survivors, so a value of zero point three eight means the model separates the two groups reasonably well. As a result, you report it, but you never read it aloud as though it were an ordinary R-squared from linear regression, because it answers a different question. [NEXT]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
section: Staged columns tell the adjustment story
text:
Staged columns tell the adjustment story























[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~64s] A published table is most persuasive when it shows the model in stages. Here the same female odds ratio is fit three times: with sex alone, then adding class, then adding age. It reads eleven point eight, then twelve point five, then twelve point two, while underneath the in-sample AIC falls from eleven hundred six to one thousand twenty-two to nine hundred ninety-two and Tjur’s R-squared climbs from zero point two nine to zero point three eight. One detail keeps the comparison honest: every column uses the same one thousand forty-six passengers, so the crude odds ratio here is eleven point eight, not the eleven point three you saw in Chapter Nine on all one thousand three hundred nine. [CLICK 1] That stability is itself the finding: the female odds ratio stays stable after adding class and age, so it is robust in this staged analysis, while the in-sample fit summaries improve. In the Berkeley admissions example from Session Ten, the same staging told the opposite story: adjusting for department pulled the odds ratio toward one, consistent with department confounding in that example. More generally, staging shows how the conditional odds ratio changes under adjustment, which can reflect confounding and, for odds ratios, noncollapsibility. [NEXT]

title: Reporting Logistic Results: The Staged OR Table
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A publication logistic table reports odds ratios (raw log-odds to the appendix), each with a 95% CI and a p. For a model-based OR interval, 1 is the no-association value. A multi-level factor gets one global p, not a p per dummy. The fit row carries N, AIC, log-likelihood, and Tjur’s R² — a coefficient of discrimination, not variance explained. Stage nested models on the same rows so the reader sees whether an odds ratio is stable or changes under adjustment.




Next: Did the model reproduce the data? The grouped goodness-of-fit test. Notes, Ch 10 §Publication-Quality Table

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~52s] Let us close with the shape of a logistic table you can trust. It leads with odds ratios and sends the raw log-odds to an appendix; each odds ratio carries a confidence interval and a p-value, with one as the no-association value for model-based OR intervals. A multi-level factor earns one global p rather than a scatter of dummy p-values, and the fit row reports the sample size, AIC, log-likelihood, and Tjur’s R-squared, which you report as a coefficient of discrimination and never as variance explained. Above all, stage nested models on the same rows, so the reader can see whether an odds ratio is stable or changes under adjustment. You have now reported the model. In the next lecture we ask a different question, namely whether the model actually reproduced the data, through the grouped goodness-of-fit test. [END]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
section: A rate per pattern — did reality agree?
text:
A rate per pattern — did reality agree?























[DECK ch11-01 · 9 min · ~1190 words · clicks 8 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 1 · ~64s] A fitted logistic model is, in the end, a set of predicted rates, one for every distinct combination of the predictors. The model we will use today predicts whether a death sentence was imposed from two features of each case, the defendant’s race and the victim’s race. Because each predictor takes two values, the model produces exactly four predicted rates, shown here as the four rust dots. Treat this throughout as an observational association and never as a cause. [CLICK 1] Now let us lay the real outcomes on top, the navy triangles, which show what actually happened in each group. The whole question of this lecture is simply whether these two sets of points agree. When the grouped discrepancies are small, prediction and reality sit close together in every group, which is roughly what you see here. The goodness-of-fit test measures that gap and asks whether it is larger than chance alone would give. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
section: A covariate pattern is one combination
text:
A covariate pattern is one combination
The grouping idea

A covariate pattern is one unique combination of predictor values.



Two binary predictors → 2 × 2 = 4 patterns. The 674 cases collapse into 4 cells.



[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Before we test anything, let us define the unit the test works on. A covariate pattern is one unique combination of predictor values, and it is the building block of every grouped goodness-of-fit statistic. Recall that the linear-regression F-test asked whether the model explained variance; here the question is whether the model reproduces the observed frequencies, so the natural unit is the group, not the individual. [CLICK 1] With two binary predictors, there are two times two, which is four covariate patterns in total. This means the six hundred seventy-four individual cases collapse into just four cells, one for each combination of the defendant’s race and the victim’s race. From here on, the test analyzes grouped counts rather than individual rows, which is exactly why we call this a grouped test. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
text:
Observed against expected, per pattern


































[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 2 · ~76s] Here is the artifact at the heart of the test, one row per pattern. The Observed column gives how many death sentences actually occurred, and the Expected column gives the model’s prediction, the group size times the fitted probability. [CLICK 1] Compare the Observed and Expected columns side by side, because their gap is exactly what the test measures, and notice how close they already are, with fifty-three observed against fifty-two point eight expected in the largest group. The row drawn in rust is the cell to watch, because among the sixteen cases with a White defendant and a Black victim, the expected success count is only zero point one eight, well below five. [CLICK 2] Each pattern then contributes one number, shown in the final column, scoring how far observed differed from expected. The four contributions are zero point zero zero one, zero point one eight four, zero point zero zero four, and zero point zero zero nine, so the sparse cell contributes the most while the three crowded cells contribute almost nothing. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
section: Sum the contributions into one statistic
text:
Sum the contributions into one statistic























[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~66s] The Pearson chi-squared statistic is now just the sum of those four contributions, one from each pattern. Adding them gives zero point zero zero one plus zero point one eight four plus zero point zero zero four plus zero point zero zero nine, which totals zero point one nine eight. This is the single most important habit of the day, because the statistic is summed over the grouped patterns, never over the individual binary residuals. Summing the six hundred seventy-four individual zero-and-one residuals one case at a time is a named mistake, so always say grouped out loud. [CLICK 1] Here, a second statistic gives the same non-significant read. The deviance, written G-squared, is the likelihood-ratio version of the same comparison and here equals zero point three eight zero, with p-value zero point five three eight on the same degree of freedom. The two statistics are computed differently, so do not assume they must always agree; in this example they give the same practical conclusion. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
text:
Degrees of freedom count patterns























[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~66s] Now we need degrees of freedom to read the statistic, and this is where many people stumble. For this covariate-pattern grouped Pearson or deviance goodness-of-fit test, the degrees of freedom count covariate patterns, not people. For this model there are four patterns and three estimated parameters, the intercept and the two race coefficients, so the degrees of freedom equal four minus three, which is one. The symbolic card on the right is the transferable rule: J patterns minus p parameters. [CLICK 1] Now notice what the rule is not. It is not the sample size minus the parameters, so it is never six hundred seventy-four minus three; the people do not set the degrees of freedom, the patterns do. Hold on to the scoped form for this grouped Pearson or deviance setup, patterns minus estimated parameters, because the arithmetic changes from problem to problem but the setup matters. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
section: No evidence of lack of fit is not proof
text:
No evidence of lack of fit is not proof























[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~74s] On one degree of freedom, the Pearson statistic of zero point one nine eight gives a p-value of zero point six five seven. A large p-value tells us the grouped discrepancies are not unusually large under the chi-square reference, so the model is not contradicted by these four grouped counts. [CLICK 1] Here is the formulation to state with care. A non-significant result means only that we find no evidence of lack of fit within this fragile test; it is not proof that the model is correct, because failing to find a problem is not the same as showing there is none. This example also earns extra caution, because grouped binary data need expected successes and expected failures in each pattern to be large enough. Here the expected successes fall below five in two patterns, with zero point one eight the most extreme, while expected failures are not the problem. With counts that small and only one degree of freedom, the test has very little power, so read this example as illustrative of the method rather than as an ideal test. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
text:
Misfit suggests missing structure























[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~62s] It helps to know what a failure of this test would actually be telling you. A large p-value, as we found here, says there is no detected lack of fit in these four grouped counts, while a small p-value would suggest missing structure, approximation trouble, or both. [CLICK 1] In this two-binary-predictor example, the natural next structural check would be the defendant-by-victim interaction. Our model carries only the two main effects, the defendant’s race and the victim’s race entering separately. The interaction is a product term rather than two separate main effects, which is the distinction from Session Ten between a main-effects model and one that includes the product. [NEXT]

title: Did the Model Reproduce the Data? The Grouped GOF Test
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


A grouped goodness-of-fit test compares observed vs expected successes within each covariate pattern, then sums one contribution per pattern into the Pearson X² (here 0.198) — grouped, never summed over individual residuals. For this grouped Pearson/deviance setup, refer it to chi-square on df = patterns − parameters (here 4 − 3 = 1, never n − k). A non-significant result (p = 0.657) means no evidence of lack of fit — not proof of fit, and here the test is fragile (expected successes include 0.18 and 3.8 below 5, low power). Significant misfit suggests missing structure; in this example, check the interaction.




Next: Sparse Cells and Separation — when the output lies. Notes, Ch 11 §Goodness of Fit Tests

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~58s] Let us gather the shape of the grouped goodness-of-fit test. You group the cases into covariate patterns, sum one Pearson contribution per pattern into the chi-squared, here zero point one nine eight, summing over the grouped patterns and never over the individual residuals, and for this grouped Pearson or deviance setup you refer it to degrees of freedom equal to patterns minus parameters, here one.
