A p-value of zero point six five seven means no evidence of lack of fit within this fragile test, which is weaker than proof of fit, and here the test is fragile because expected successes are below five in two patterns, with zero point one eight the most extreme. A significant result would suggest missing structure; in this example, the interaction is the natural next check. That fragile cell is also a doorway, because when a cell is not just sparse but empty the model can break entirely. In the next lecture we follow that thread into sparse cells and separation, where the output itself can lie. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
text:
When the output lies












[DECK ch11-02 · 9 min · ~1250 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~44s] Here is a single line of logistic regression output, the kind that stops you in your tracks. We fitted an ordinary model, and one predictor came back with an estimated coefficient of negative fourteen point five and a standard error of about six hundred, which sends the Wald z to about negative zero point zero two four and the p-value to about zero point nine eight. Notice that both of the first two numbers are enormous at the same time, which is the strange part. A coefficient that large would normally signal a powerful effect, yet the standard error is so wide that the estimate could be almost anything. This combination is the warning sign we will learn to read today, and by the end you will recognize it instantly. Throughout, treat this as an observational association and never as a cause. [NEXT]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
text:
Grouped tests need well-populated cells























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~70s] Before we reach the blowup itself, let us start with the gentler problem that points the way, which is sparseness. Recall from last lecture that this covariate-pattern grouped goodness-of-fit test needs the expected outcome counts inside each pattern to be large enough. For binary outcomes, that means expected deaths and expected non-deaths, not just one generic expected count. The most extreme pattern was the group with a White defendant and a Black victim, whose expected deaths were only zero point one eight, far below five; another pattern had expected deaths around three point eight, while expected non-deaths were not the problem. [CLICK 1] Here is the trap that catches many people. The data set holds six hundred seventy-four cases in total, which sounds like plenty, so it is tempting to conclude that a large overall sample fixes everything. That conclusion is false. A large overall sample does not by itself rescue sparse patterns, because what matters is the expected outcome count inside each pattern, not the grand total. Sparseness is local, and you must check it at the pattern level. [NEXT]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
section: A continuous predictor breaks this grouped test
text:
A continuous predictor breaks this grouped test























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~64s] Sparseness becomes unavoidable the moment a predictor is continuous, and a simple contrast makes the reason plain. With only categorical predictors you get a few wide cells, each holding many cases, so the expected outcome counts can comfortably clear five. Now add a continuous predictor such as age or fare. Almost no two cases then share the same combination of values, so the number of distinct patterns, which we call J, climbs until it approaches the sample size itself, and most cells hold just one person. [CLICK 1] With nearly every cell holding a single person, this covariate-pattern grouped chi-square approximation has nothing left to pool, and its p-value would be meaningless. The practical rule is to skip this particular grouped goodness-of-fit test when continuous predictors make patterns nearly unique. You are not left without diagnostics, because binned residuals and other diagnostics designed for continuous predictors can still ask whether the model has the right shape. [NEXT]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
section: One cell predicts the outcome perfectly
text:
One cell predicts the outcome perfectly























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~50s] Now we can see where that impossible coefficient came from, and the cause is a sharper cousin of sparseness called separation. Look at the very same cell as a plain fact about the data. Among the sixteen cases with a White defendant and a Black victim, zero of them resulted in a death sentence. That cell does not merely have a small count; it predicts the outcome perfectly, because every case in it falls on the same side, namely no death sentence. Because the split is perfect for this one pattern, the textbook name for it is quasi-complete separation. [CLICK 1] Perfect prediction then breaks the fitting procedure. To match that cell as closely as possible, the optimizer keeps pushing the coefficient lower, and the likelihood keeps rising the whole way down, with no value where it peaks. As a result, the coefficient runs off toward minus infinity, and the negative fourteen point five you saw is simply where the algorithm stopped, not a real estimate. [NEXT]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
section: The signature, and what to do about it
text:
The signature, and what to do about it


































[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 2 · ~82s] This slide summarizes the diagnostic signature and remedies side by side. On the left is the ordinary fit, with a coefficient of negative fourteen point five and a standard error of about six hundred. That absurd standard error sends the Wald z to about negative zero point zero two four and the p-value to about zero point nine eight, so the Wald test reports no evidence for a coefficient whose maximum-likelihood estimate is running toward minus infinity. The result is meaningless, and the odds ratio it implies is essentially zero. On the right is the same model fitted with a weakly-informative prior, which pulls the estimate back to a finite, usable negative one point eight four with a standard error of one point four five. [CLICK 1] This contrast defuses a tempting misreading. A coefficient of negative fourteen point five does not mean an enormous effect; it means that no finite maximum-likelihood estimate exists. Whenever a coefficient and its standard error both explode together, that is your cue to inspect the data. Separation is a leading culprit, and you should also check sparse levels, relevant patterns, collinearity, scaling, and influential observations. [CLICK 2] The remedies follow directly. You can penalize the likelihood, using Firth’s method or the prior shown here, so the estimate stays finite. You can use exact methods when they are appropriate. You can combine categories only when the combination is substantively defensible. Or you can collect more data. [NEXT]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
section: Catch it before the optimizer does
text:
Catch it before the optimizer does























[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~74s] There is a simple habit that prevents this whole drama, and it takes about a minute. Before you fit the model, start with one-way cross-tabs for key categorical predictors against the outcome, but do not stop there. Also check substantively relevant covariate patterns, especially combinations you plan to include or interpret. You are scanning for any pattern that is empty or nearly empty, like the zero flagged in rust here. If a sparse category can be combined with another category without changing the substantive meaning, combine it; if not, use a penalized or exact approach, or collect more data. [CLICK 1] There is also a red flag you can read straight from the output afterward. When the Wald test and the likelihood-ratio test disagree sharply for the same coefficient, that disagreement is an instability warning, not an automatic command to trust one p-value. The Wald standard error can be unreliable, and the likelihood-ratio test also relies on regularity conditions that separation can break. So inspect the cells, treat the Wald result with skepticism, and repair the sparse pattern before trusting ordinary maximum-likelihood inference. [NEXT]

title: Sparse Cells and Separation: When the Output Lies
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Sparse cells break this covariate-pattern grouped GOF test: expected deaths and non-deaths must be large enough, and a continuous predictor can push patterns J → n so the chi-square reference fails. A large overall n does not by itself rescue sparse patterns. Separation is sharper: one pattern predicts perfectly, so the MLE runs toward ±∞ and the output shows a huge estimate with an absurd SE (here −14.5 ± 600, meaningless) — not a huge effect. Fix it by penalizing/exact methods, substantively defensible category combination, or more data. A sharp Wald-vs-LR split is an instability warning. The habit: check key predictors and relevant patterns before fitting.




Next: Linearity in the logit — binned residuals and the squared-term test. Notes, Ch 11 §Separation

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~66s] Let us gather what the impossible output was telling us. Sparse cells break this covariate-pattern grouped goodness-of-fit test, and a large overall sample does not by itself rescue sparse patterns, because what matters is the expected outcome count inside the pattern. Separation is the sharper version, where one pattern predicts the outcome perfectly, so the maximum-likelihood estimate runs toward minus infinity and you see a huge estimate paired with an absurd standard error. That is a sign of no finite estimate, not a huge effect. You fix it by penalizing the fit, using exact methods when appropriate, combining categories only when the combination is substantively defensible, or collecting more data. As a final red flag, a sharp disagreement between the Wald and likelihood-ratio tests is an instability warning, so inspect the cells before trusting ordinary inference. Having handled sparse and empty cells, the next lecture asks whether the model even has the right shape, testing linearity in the logit with binned residuals. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
Two railroad tracks












[DECK ch11-03 · 9 min · ~1100 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Here is a residual plot from a logistic regression, and it looks broken. We have fitted Titanic survival on age, and we are plotting the raw residual, the survival outcome minus the predicted probability, against age. Instead of the formless cloud you would hope for, the points fall into two clean stripes, like two railroad tracks running across the picture. The rust band on top holds the passengers who survived, and the navy band below holds those who did not, with nothing at all in the gap between them. Treat everything here as an observational association in the data, never as a cause. This shape is not a bug, and by the end you will know what it is, why it happens, and how to turn it into a real diagnostic. [NEXT]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
Why only two bands























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] The two bands are not mysterious once you write down what a residual can be. The outcome is binary, either a zero or a one, so for any fitted probability pi-hat there are only two arithmetic possibilities. If the passenger survived, the residual is one minus pi-hat, the rust dot sitting above the line. If the passenger did not survive, the residual is the negative of pi-hat, the navy dot sitting below. [CLICK 1] That is the whole story. Two outcomes give exactly two residual values at every level of the prediction, so when you plot them all you get two bands and nothing in between. The trouble is that this picture is the same for a good model and a bad one, which means the raw plot cannot tell you whether the model fits. We need a way to see past the bands. [NEXT]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
Bin and average























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~50s] The fix is to stop looking at individual points and start averaging. We sort the passengers by age, cut them into thirty equally sized bins, and inside each bin we average the raw residuals. Because each bin mixes survivors and non-survivors, averaging cancels the two-band noise and leaves the systematic part behind. Notice that the thirty navy dots no longer lie flat along zero. The dots for the youngest passengers sit clearly above the line, so a real pattern has emerged that the bands were hiding. [CLICK 1] Now we need a yardstick for how far from zero deserves attention, and that is the grey band, the plus or minus two standard error envelope. Its half-width is the binomial standard error for a bin, so the band marks the ordinary sampling wobble we should tolerate. A bin inside the band shows no obvious local lack of fit; a bin poking outside flags a place to investigate, especially when several bins form a pattern. [NEXT]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
Reading the envelope























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~53s] Reading the plot is now simple, but the rule is a guide rather than a verdict. A binned point inside the envelope shows no obvious local lack of fit, because its distance from zero is within ordinary sampling noise, and across the middle ages most bins sit comfortably inside. [CLICK 1] The young end is where the interesting pattern shows. The youngest bin is just inside the upper edge, with a mean residual of about plus zero point one six five, while the next young bin pokes outside at about plus zero point two three zero. A positive residual means the actual survival rate ran higher than the straight-line age model predicted, so the model is under-predicting how many children survived. You may recognize this as women and children first, an observational pattern in the data where the youngest passengers survived at a higher rate than a single straight line through age can capture. The curve in these points is a message, and the next slide reads it. [NEXT]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
A curve flags structure























[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~55s] A curved pattern in the binned residuals has a focused meaning. It suggests possible nonlinearity or missing structure for age on the scale where the logistic model is linear. Here is the subtle part, and it defuses a common belief. Many people check linearity during exploratory analysis by eyeballing a scatter of the outcome against the predictor, and they conclude they are done. But that is linearity on the probability scale, and it is a different claim from the one logistic regression actually makes. [CLICK 1] The logistic assumption is linearity in the logit, which means a straight-line relationship between the predictor and the log-odds, not the probability and not the raw scatter. The binned residual plot diagnoses possible logit-linearity problems; the squared-term likelihood-ratio test gives formal evidence for one planned quadratic alternative. State it in those words: linearity in the logit, checked thoughtfully for continuous predictors in your model. [NEXT]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
The diagnostic workflow


































[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 2 · ~66s] Here is one project-ready workflow, in three steps. First, bin the residuals by the continuous predictor and read them against the envelope. Second, choose a substantively defensible nonlinear form, such as age-squared here, and compare the nested models with a likelihood-ratio test. Third, report the evidence and justify the form you retain. The key is to diagnose and justify, not to hunt automatically for extra terms. [CLICK 1] Now run the planned nested test for age in this age-only Titanic model. It returns a chi-squared of five point three three on one degree of freedom, with a p-value of zero point zero two one. That is formal evidence for this quadratic alternative in this demonstration. The squared-term Wald test agrees at a p-value of zero point zero two one, so both routes point the same way. [CLICK 2] The payoff sits side by side. On the left, the linear panel curves up at the young end. On the right, once you add age-squared, the young-end bins pull back toward zero and the pattern relaxes, while a few later-age bins still escape, so we never claim a perfectly clean plot. Keep the scope clear: this check belongs with continuous predictors like age. [NEXT]

title: Linearity in the Logit: Binned Residuals and the Squared-Term Test
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Raw logistic residuals form two useless bands — for any fitted pi-hat the residual is only 1 − pi-hat (y = 1) or −pi-hat (y = 0). Bin and average them within bins of a continuous predictor, and read each bin against the ±2 SE guide: a bin inside shows no obvious local issue, while a bin outside flags a place to investigate. A curved pattern suggests possible nonlinearity on the log-odds scale — this is linearity in the logit, not the EDA scatter you eyeballed. A defensible workflow is bin → choose a planned nonlinear term → nested chi-square (here χ² = 5.33, df = 1, p = 0.021 for age²) → report and justify.




Next: Influence in logistic models — screen, refit, compare. Notes, Ch 11 §Binned Residual Plot

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~40s] Let us gather the shape of this diagnostic. A raw logistic residual plot is two useless bands, because for any prediction the residual is only one minus pi-hat or minus pi-hat. You recover a real diagnostic by binning and averaging within bins of a continuous predictor, then reading each bin against the plus or minus two standard error guide. A curved pattern suggests possible nonlinearity on the log-odds scale, which is linearity in the logit, a different claim from the probability scatter you may have checked. A defensible workflow is to bin, choose a planned nonlinear term, run the nested chi-squared, here five point three three on one degree of freedom with a p-value of zero point zero two one for age-squared, and then report and justify the form you retain. Having checked the model’s shape, the next lecture asks whether a few influential cases are driving the fit. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
text:
Same flashlight, new terrain
Chapter 11 · Logistic diagnostics

The influence workflow you built for linear regression carries over to logistic regression.


Same flashlight — screen, inspect, choose a sensitivity subset, refit, report — pointed at new terrain.


[DECK ch11-04 · 8 min · ~1085 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 1 · ~46s] In Chapter Eight you learned to ask whether a single observation was quietly steering your regression. You screened with Cook’s distance, inspected the flagged points, refit a clearly defined sensitivity subset to see whether your conclusions moved, and reported what you found. That whole workflow was the flashlight, and the good news today is that the same flashlight points at logistic regression. [CLICK 1] The terrain is new, because we are now fitting odds rather than a straight line, and the ingredients are the GLM versions of residuals and leverage. We will screen, inspect, choose a sensitivity subset, refit, and report, in that order. There are only two new wrinkles. The first is that Cook’s distance uses the logistic model’s hat matrix, and the second is that the report becomes a small before-and-after table of odds ratios. We will use the Titanic survival model throughout, treating every estimate as an observational association rather than a cause. [NEXT]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
section: Cook’s distance, on the GLM hat matrix
text:
Cook’s distance, on the GLM hat matrix
\[ D_i = \underbrace{\left(r_i^{P}\right)^2}_{\text{large Pearson residual}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{h_{ii}}{p\,(1 - h_{ii})^2}}_{\text{high leverage}} \]








The only new wrinkle


Same workflow as Chapter 8 — influence = a large residual × high leverage — now the residual and leverage are the GLM versions. cooks.distance() and hatvalues() have glm methods, so the code pattern is familiar.






[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Here is the first wrinkle, and it is a small one. Read the labels under the formula rather than deriving it. Cook’s distance for a logistic model has the same shape you already know: a large Pearson residual squared, which measures how far a case lands from its prediction, multiplied by a leverage term built from the hat value h sub i i, which measures how unusual the case is in its predictors. Because the two pieces multiply, Cook’s distance is large only when both are present at once. [CLICK 1] Read it the way you read it in Chapter Eight, as a product of two ingredients: a large residual times high leverage. The one genuine wrinkle is that both ingredients are now GLM ingredients: the residual is a Pearson residual and the leverage comes from the logistic model’s own hat matrix rather than the linear one. The functions cooks dot distance and hatvalues have methods for fitted glm objects, so the code pattern stays familiar even though the quantities are model-specific. [NEXT]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
text:
Screen with the 4/n line























[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] With the diagnostic computed, you plot one Cook’s distance per passenger against the observation index. This is the index plot, and most of the points sit near zero, which tells you that most passengers carry no special weight in the fit. [CLICK 1] Now drop in the screening line. The common rule of thumb is four divided by n, which here equals zero point zero zero three eight, and it flags the cases that rise above it for a closer look. Fifty-five cases exceed it, the rust points near the top. Notice the vertical scale, because the largest Cook’s distance is only about zero point zero one nine, which is small. That is typical of binary data, where Cook’s distance values tend to be smaller than in the continuous case, so no single passenger dominates. Hear this clearly: the four over n line is a screening rule of thumb, not a significance test, and crossing it only makes a case a candidate for inspection. [NEXT]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
text:
Choose the sensitivity subset
A sensitivity check, not a deletion


After the 55-case screen, inspect the flags. For this demo, set aside the 5 largest Cook’s-D cases and refit. You are asking one question: do the conclusions move? You are not cleaning the data.



[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~52s] The screen is a question, not a verdict, so the next step is to answer it carefully. Fifty-five cases crossed the four-over-n line. You inspect that screen, and for this demonstration you choose the five largest Cook’s-distance cases as the sensitivity subset. Set those five aside and refit the very same model on the remaining passengers. [CLICK 1] Be precise about what this move is and what it is not. This is a sensitivity check, not a cleanup step. You are asking whether your conclusions would change if that clearly named subset were not in the data. The screened passengers are often real and informative observations, such as an older third-class passenger who survived against the odds, exactly the kind of case the notes point to. You refit in order to see what this subset was doing to your estimates, and then you put the cases back. [NEXT]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
text:
Did the conclusions move?























[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~62s] Here is the payoff, the before-and-after table that does all the work. The before column holds the focal odds ratios from the full model, and the after column holds the same odds ratios from the top-five sensitivity refit. [CLICK 1] Read across the rows. The odds ratio for a female passenger moves from twelve point two to twelve point nine, the odds ratio for third class against first moves from zero point one zero to zero point zero nine, and the odds ratio for each year of age moves from zero point nine seven to zero point nine six. Every one of these is a small change for this top-five check. Women had much higher survival odds, while lower class and older age went with lower odds, and that substantive story is stable under this refit. The verdict, then, is not that all fifty-five screen-positive cases are disposable; it is that the top-five sensitivity refit did not move the focal conclusions. [NEXT]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
section: Write the sentence, keep the case
text:
Write the sentence, keep the case







Example sensitivity statement



“In this demonstration, the top-five sensitivity refit changed the focal odds ratios only slightly, so the substantive conclusions were stable.”






Investigate and report — never auto-delete. A flagged case is often a real, informative observation. (dfbetas() does the same per coefficient — it names which one a case moves.)


[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Now turn that finding into a reporting sentence. The example sentence reads as follows. In this demonstration, the top-five sensitivity refit changed the focal odds ratios only slightly, so the substantive conclusions were stable. In your own report, name the subset you checked and summarize the before-and-after evidence. If instead an odds ratio had swung, you would say so just as plainly and then investigate why, but you would still report it. [CLICK 1] Hold on to the doctrine that governs all of this, because it is the same doctrine you learned in Chapter Eight. You investigate and you report, and you never delete a flagged case automatically, since the case you are tempted to discard is often the most informative one you have. One related tool deserves a single mention. Where Cook’s distance summarizes a case’s influence on all the coefficients at once, dee-eff-betas tells you which single coefficient a case is moving. [NEXT]

title: Influence in Logistic Models: Screen, Refit, Compare
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


The influence workflow transfers to logistic regression. Cook’s distance uses the GLM hat matrix — a large Pearson residual × high leverage — and cooks.distance(glm) follows a familiar code pattern. Screen at 4/n (a rule of thumb, not a test), inspect the flags, choose and name a sensitivity subset, and report a small before/after OR table. Here the top-five refit barely moved the focal ORs, so the substantive story was stable under that check. Investigate and report — never auto-delete.




Next: Pseudo-R² — six measures, report two. Notes, Ch 11 §Influence Diagnostics

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~38s] Let us gather the whole idea in one breath. The influence workflow transfers to logistic regression, but Cook’s distance now uses the GLM hat matrix, with Pearson residuals and GLM leverage. You screen with the four over n rule, remembering that it is a rule of thumb and not a test. Then you inspect the flags, choose and name a sensitivity subset, refit as a sensitivity check, and report a small before-and-after table of the focal odds ratios. Here the top-five refit barely moved those odds ratios, so the substantive story was stable under that check. Above all, you investigate and report rather than deleting anything automatically. We have now checked fit, shape, sparseness, and influence. In the next lecture we ask the last diagnostic question, how to report the model’s overall fit, with pseudo-R-squared. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
text:
Your R-squared came back low


[DECK ch11-05 · 9 min · ~1138 words · clicks 6 · advances 7] [SLIDE 1/7 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Suppose you fit a logistic regression, you ask for a pseudo-R-squared, and the number comes back at zero point zero five. If your instinct is shaped by ordinary least squares, where many examples use a much higher yardstick, then zero point zero five looks alarming, and the natural reaction is to conclude that the model is useless. The shaded band higher up the scale marks where you expect an R-squared to live out of that habit, and the worked value sits far below it. By the end of these few minutes you will see that this reaction is usually wrong, because you are reading the value against the wrong yardstick. We will use one real model throughout, fit to data on whether a death-penalty sentence is associated with the defendant’s race and the victim’s race, treating every quantity as an observational association rather than a cause. [NEXT]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
section: Linear R-squared has no single heir
text:
Linear R-squared has no single heir























[SLIDE 2/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] To understand why the number runs low, recall what the linear R-squared actually is. In ordinary least squares, a single number does four jobs at the same time. It is the proportion of variance explained, it is the squared correlation between the observed y and the fitted y-hat, it is the proportional reduction in the residual sum of squares, and it is a simple function of the overall F-statistic. All four descriptions agree, so you can report one value and never have to choose. [CLICK 1] In logistic regression, that happy coincidence breaks down, because no single measure satisfies all four of those properties at once. As a result, there is no single heir to the linear R-squared. Instead there are six common pseudo-R-squared measures, each one emphasizing a different aspect of model performance. The word pseudo is your warning that you are looking at a family of related measures, not one agreed-upon quantity, which is exactly why you must say which one you mean. [NEXT]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
text:
McFadden: reduction in deviance
































[SLIDE 3/7 · clicks 1 · ~66s] The first measure to know is McFadden’s R-squared, and it has a clean meaning. The null deviance, written D-zero, measures how poorly an intercept-only model fits, and for these data it is about four hundred forty-one. The residual deviance, written D-one, measures how poorly your fitted model fits, and here it is about four hundred nineteen. Your model removes the difference between them, about twenty-two units of deviance, and McFadden’s R-squared expresses that reduction as a fraction of the null deviance, which gives zero point zero five. So McFadden is the proportional reduction in deviance, the analogue for logistic regression of the proportional reduction in residual sum of squares you know from least squares. [CLICK 1] Now recalibrate, because this is the heart of it. In many discrete-choice settings, values between zero point two and zero point four are treated as a strong McFadden range; the benchmark is context-dependent and should not be mapped directly onto least-squares R-squared. Against that yardstick, zero point zero five is genuinely low, but it is not automatically useless; it signals limited model improvement or separation. [NEXT]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
section: Tjur: separation of the two groups
text:
Tjur: separation of the two groups























[SLIDE 4/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] The second measure to know is Tjur’s R-squared, and it is the most intuitive of the family, because you can see it directly. For every case the model gives a predicted probability of the event, and you split the cases by what actually happened. Take the average predicted probability among the cases where the event did occur, the y-equals-one group, which here is about zero point one three. Then take the average predicted probability among the cases where it did not, the y-equals-zero group, which here is about zero point one zero. [CLICK 1] Tjur’s R-squared is simply the gap between those two averages, which is about zero point zero three. You can read it as a measure of separation, namely how far apart the model pulls the average predicted probability of the two groups, where a larger gap means cleaner separation. Notice that Tjur needs no likelihood and no deviance at all, only the fitted probabilities, which is exactly why it is so easy to picture. [NEXT]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
section: The table of six: name what you report
text:
The table of six: name what you report























[SLIDE 5/7 · clicks 1 · ~58s] Here is the whole family on one slide. McFadden and Tjur you have just met. Cox-Snell is another likelihood-based measure, about zero point zero three here, and Nagelkerke is a rescaled Cox-Snell whose maximum is one, about zero point zero seven here. The last two, Count and Adjusted Count, come from classification, where Count is the proportion of cases the model classifies correctly at a stated cutoff and Adjusted Count is the gain over simply guessing the most common outcome under that same decision rule. Notice how differently the measures can read on the very same model, with Nagelkerke at zero point zero seven sitting above McFadden at zero point zero five, which is exactly why a bare value with no name is ambiguous for logistic regression. [CLICK 1] The point of the table is not to compute all six. The point is the highlighted pair, because a sensible course default is to report McFadden together with Tjur, and to report each one by its name. [NEXT]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
text:
The sentence for your write-up
How to report it


“McFadden’s R-squared = 0.05 and Tjur’s R-squared = 0.03.” Always name the measure — a bare “R-squared = 0.05” is ambiguous for a logistic model.




Never call either one “the proportion of variance explained.” McFadden is a reduction in deviance; Tjur is group separation — that phrase belongs to OLS alone. For an inference study, a low value can be acceptable, but report the limited fit or separation.



[SLIDE 6/7 · clicks 2 · ~70s] Let us turn all of this into a sentence you can adapt in your write-up. [CLICK 1] The first habit is to name the measure every time. Write McFadden’s R-squared equals zero point zero five, and Tjur’s R-squared equals zero point zero three, naming each one explicitly. A bare R-squared of zero point zero five is genuinely ambiguous for a logistic model, because the reader cannot tell which of the six you computed, and as we saw they disagree. [CLICK 2] The second habit is the phrase you must never use. Never describe either one as the proportion of variance explained, because that phrase belongs to ordinary least squares alone. McFadden is a proportional reduction in deviance, and Tjur is a coefficient of discrimination, which is to say group separation. Then frame the size by the goal of the study. The purpose here is inference, namely testing whether sentencing is associated with the defendant’s race and the victim’s race, and not prediction, so a low pseudo-R-squared can be acceptable; it still tells the reader that overall fit and separation are limited. [NEXT]

title: Pseudo-R-squared: Six Measures, Report by Name
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


No single R-squared exists for a logistic model — report two, by name. McFadden is the proportional reduction in deviance (here 0.05); on its own scale 0.2–0.4 is often treated as strong in discrete-choice settings. Tjur is the gap between the mean predicted probabilities of events and non-events (here 0.03) — a measure of separation. Read neither as “variance explained,” and judge the size by the goal: for an inference study, a low pseudo-R-squared can be acceptable, but it still signals limited fit or separation.




Next: The hidden continuum — latent variables and thresholds. Notes, Ch 11 §Pseudo R-squared

[SLIDE 7/7 · clicks 0 · ~40s] Let us gather the whole idea in one breath. A logistic model has no single R-squared, so you report two and you report them by name. McFadden is the proportional reduction in deviance, which was zero point zero five here, and in many discrete-choice settings zero point two to zero point four is treated as a strong McFadden range. Tjur is the gap between the mean predicted probabilities of the events and the non-events, which was zero point zero three here, and it measures how well the model separates the two groups. Read neither one as variance explained, and judge the size by the goal of the study: for an inference study a low value can be acceptable, but it still reports limited fit or separation. We have now diagnosed the logistic model from every angle. In the next lecture we step back to see logistic regression as one member of a larger family, beginning with the latent-variable view of a yes-or-no outcome. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: A useful view: a hidden score behind yes/no
text:
A useful view: a hidden score behind yes/no


[DECK ch12-01 · 9 min · ~1170 words · clicks 5 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 0 · ~44s] We spent Chapter Nine fitting an S-shaped curve to a yes-or-no outcome, and it worked, but the logit link can feel like a recipe handed down without a reason. Let us pull back the curtain. Recall the Titanic survival example. Each passenger either survived or did not, so the outcome you record is a single bit, the ones and the zeros stacked here in two flat bands. In the latent-variable view, we represent that binary record as coming from a hidden survival score for every passenger, a propensity that varies smoothly from person to person, shaped by characteristics you can observe, such as sex, age, and ticket class, and by factors you cannot, such as plain luck. The faint curve behind the points is that modeled continuum, and the binary outcome you actually see is only its visible tip in this representation. Once you take this idea seriously, Chapter Nine’s S-curve stops being an arbitrary trick and becomes something you can see. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: The hidden score has a name: y*
text:
The hidden score has a name: y*
In this view, the latent propensity is the linear predictor plus a random error:


\[ y_i^{*} \;=\; \underbrace{\alpha + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} + \cdots}_{\text{linear predictor } \eta_i} \;+\; \varepsilon_i \]


The familiar α + xβ — now driving an unobserved score, not the outcome itself

[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~50s] Let us give that hidden score a name and a formula. We call it y-star, the latent propensity, and we never observe it directly. [CLICK 1] The latent variable model writes y-star as a linear predictor plus a random error. The linear predictor, which we call eta, is the familiar alpha plus beta-one x-one plus beta-two x-two and so on, exactly the combination you have built since the first chapter. Added to it is epsilon, a random error that stands for everything the predictors leave out, the luck and the unobserved circumstances. Notice the one change from ordinary regression. The familiar predictor no longer drives the outcome you observe directly; instead it drives this unobserved survival score, where a higher value means a passenger is more inclined toward the positive outcome. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: You observe a yes only when y* clears zero
text:
You observe a yes only when y* clears zero
The visible outcome follows a threshold rule:


\[ y_i \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } y_i^{*} > 0 \\[2pt] 0 & \text{if } y_i^{*} \le 0 \end{cases} \]


With an intercept and fixed error scale, moving the cut is absorbed by α

[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] The bridge from the hidden score to the visible outcome is a single threshold rule. [CLICK 1] You observe y equals one exactly when the latent score y-star rises above zero, and y equals zero whenever it sits at zero or below. In words, a passenger with a positive survival score survives, and one with a negative score does not, while the score itself stays out of view. One detail is worth pausing on. The threshold sitting at zero is a convention, not a law of nature. With an intercept and a fixed error scale, sliding the cut to another value is absorbed by the intercept alpha. As a result, fixing the threshold at zero costs you nothing for this binary setup and keeps the picture clean. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: The shaded area is the probability
text:
The shaded area is the probability























[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~54s] Here is the latent density of y-star. The dashed line marks the threshold at zero, and the curve is centered above it, at a linear predictor of one, so more of the mass falls on the right. [CLICK 1] Now read the probability straight off the picture. The probability that y equals one is the probability that y-star clears zero, which is simply the blue area of the curve to the right of the cut, while the red area to the left is the probability that y equals zero. For a logistic latent error with scale one, that blue area works out to seventy-three point one percent. There is a locked reason it lands on such a clean value: with the error scale set to one, the area above the cut is exactly the logistic function of eta. That is precisely the S-curve from Chapter Nine, arriving here as a consequence rather than a rule. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: Slide the curve, and the probability follows
text:
Slide the curve, and the probability follows























[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] The linear predictor eta does just one thing to this picture: it slides the whole curve along the axis without changing its shape. Start with eta at one, where the curve is centered above the threshold, so most of the mass clears the cut and the probability of a positive outcome is seventy-three point one percent. [CLICK 1] Now lower eta to negative one. The identical curve simply slides to the left, until most of its mass sits below the threshold, the shaded area above zero shrinks, and the probability drops to twenty-six point nine percent. This is the whole mechanism in one motion. Changing a predictor changes eta, eta shifts the curve, and the area above the threshold rises or falls with it, which is precisely how the predictors move the probability. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: The error distribution names the model
text:
The error distribution names the model























[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] This is where the choice of model is actually made. So far the latent error has been logistic, and a logistic error gives the logit model, with the probability of seventy-three point one percent at this center. [CLICK 1] Now hold the numeric eta fixed at one under the standard logit and probit scale conventions, but swap the error for a normal distribution, the teal dashed curve. That single change gives the probit model. The normal curve is taller and narrower than the logistic one, so more of its mass clears the threshold, and the probability rises to eighty-four point one percent. Same numeric center in this teaching picture, two different error distributions, two different areas. After fitting real logit and probit models, compare predicted probabilities or rescaled coefficients, not raw coefficients. This answers a question many people quietly carry, the suspicion that choosing logit over probit is a black-box decision with nothing behind it. In this latent-variable derivation, a logistic error leads naturally to the logit link because that assumption makes the probability the logistic function. Chapter Nine’s recipe was hiding this picture all along. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
section: Go try it: move the curve yourself
text:
Go try it: move the curve yourself



Try the live latent-variable explorer in the course notes.

[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 0 · ~46s] The chapter includes an interactive explorer that lets you move this hidden curve with your own hand. There is no separate slider for the linear predictor, because it is computed for you as eta equals one half plus beta times X. As you drag the beta and X sliders, the whole latent curve slides across the threshold at zero, and the shaded area above zero reports the predicted probability directly. At the default settings the linear predictor sits at one, and the logit reading is seventy-three point one percent, exactly the area you have been watching. Switch the Logit and Probit radio button to swap the latent error distribution, and the same numeric center gives a different area, eighty-four point one percent under the normal. The live version is linked in the notes, and a few minutes there makes the shifting mass far easier to feel than a single slide can show. [NEXT]

title: The Hidden Continuum: Latent Variables and Thresholds
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


In the latent-variable view, a binary y is represented by a hidden score y* = η + ε: you see y = 1 exactly when y* clears the threshold at 0, so P(Y=1) is the shaded area above the cut. The error’s shape names the model — logistic → logit, normal → probit — so Chapter 9’s S-curve is a consequence, never a trick.




Next: Three Parts of Every GLM — and Chapter 13 adds ordered thresholds plus a common-slope assumption to model ordinal outcomes. Notes, Ch 12 §The Latent Variable Formulation

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Let us gather the whole idea into a single breath. In the latent-variable view, a binary outcome is represented as the visible tip of a hidden continuous score, y-star, which equals the linear predictor eta plus a random error, and you observe a one exactly when that score clears the threshold at zero. The probability of a one is therefore the shaded area of the latent curve above the cut, and the shape you assume for the error names the model, giving the logit from a logistic error and the probit from a normal one. Chapter Nine’s S-curve was this picture all along, and this latent view is the conceptual key for the whole generalized-linear-model session ahead. In the next lecture, on the three parts of every generalized linear model, we step back to see the whole machine. And in the chapter after that, the ordinal version adds ordered thresholds plus a common-slope assumption along the latent score. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
text:
Thirteen chapters, one machine


[DECK ch12-02 · 9 min · ~1130 words · clicks 8 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 0 · ~44s] We have covered a great deal of ground. We began with linear regression for continuous outcomes, and then we turned to logistic and probit regression for binary outcomes. It can feel as though we have studied two separate families of models, each with its own rules. The truth is more economical than that. Every model in this course is one machine with three swappable parts: a random component, which is the outcome’s distribution; a systematic component, which is the linear predictor you have built since the first chapter; and a link function, which connects the two. In this lecture we will name the three parts, see that only two of them ever change, and then read a receipt that shows you have been driving this single machine since Session Two. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
text:
Part one: the outcome’s distribution
The random component — the family the outcome is drawn from























[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] The first part is the random component, which names the probability distribution the outcome is drawn from. For ordinary linear regression in this course, with a continuous outcome such as a test score, we use a Gaussian family with an identity link, the rust bell here. [CLICK 1] Now suppose the outcome is binary, recording a yes or a no, such as whether a passenger survived. A single zero-or-one observation is Bernoulli, equivalently binomial with one trial; in R, we use the binomial family. The point to hold onto is that you match the random component to the outcome. The same framework reaches further still, to counts and to other outcome types, but these two families are the ones we work with directly. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
section: Part two: the same old linear predictor
text:
Part two: the same old linear predictor
The systematic component — unchanged since Chapter 1

\[ \eta_i \;=\; \alpha + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} + \cdots \]

The linear predictor — linear in the parameters, and the same in every GLM.


Design logic lives here: coefficients, adjustment, interactions. Interpretation and diagnostics adapt to the family/link.



[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~50s] The second part is the systematic component, and here is the reassuring news: it is the linear predictor you have used since the very first chapter, and it does not change at all. We write it as eta, which equals alpha plus beta-one x-one plus beta-two x-two, and so on for every predictor. It remains linear in the parameters in every generalized linear model. [CLICK 1] Notice what this means for everything you have learned. Building coefficients, adjusting for a confounder, and testing an interaction still happen in this linear predictor, so the design logic carries over on the link scale. But interpretation, marginal effects, uncertainty, and diagnostics must adapt to the family and link you choose. The systematic part is the shared skill; the scale and model assumptions tell you how to read it. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
section: Part three: the link as the bridge
text:
Part three: the link as the bridge
The link function g(μ) = η — this is the part that changes



A probability lives in (0, 1); the predictor roams the whole real line. The link maps the mean to that predictor scale.



[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] The third part is the link function, and this is the one part that genuinely changes between models. A common misreading is that the link transforms the outcome itself. It does not; the link connects the mean of the outcome to the linear predictor, written as g of mu equals eta, where mu is the average of y at a given x. [CLICK 1] Here is the problem the link solves. The linear predictor eta ranges freely over the whole real line, but the mean of a binary outcome is a probability, trapped inside the interval from zero to one. The link is the bridge across that mismatch, mapping the constrained mean on the left onto the unconstrained predictor on the right. For ordinary linear regression in this course, the identity link leaves the mean on the same scale; for a binary outcome, the logit and the probit map the probability mean to eta in their own ways. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
section: The whole family is one table
text:
The whole family is one table























[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] Now we can place every model we know into a single table. Ordinary linear regression pairs a Gaussian family with the identity link. Logistic regression and probit regression use single-trial Bernoulli outcomes, represented in R with the binomial family; the difference is the link, logit versus probit. Reading down the rows, the family and the link are what change from one model to the next. [CLICK 1] Now apply each link and look at the final column. The left-hand side differs by row, because each model applies its own link, but the right-hand side reads alpha plus x-prime beta on every single row, tinted here in gold. That shared right-hand side is the heart of the framework. You pick the family to match your outcome and the link to map its mean onto the real line, but the systematic part you have always used never moves. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
section: Three inverse-link curves, one predictor underneath
text:
Three inverse-link curves, one predictor underneath


































[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 2 · ~56s] These three curves are easiest to understand as pictures, but they are the inverse-link direction. The link maps the mean to eta; here we look back from eta to the mean, because that is how predictions become visible. Begin with the identity case used in ordinary linear regression. The mean simply equals eta, so the picture is a straight line. [CLICK 1] After logistic regression uses the logit transform to define the link scale, the inverse logit bends eta into an S-shaped probability curve. As the linear predictor rises, the mean climbs smoothly, but it is squeezed to live between zero and one, exactly as a probability must. [CLICK 2] The inverse probit gives a very similar S-shaped curve, just a touch steeper through the middle. Look along the bottom of all three panels: the linear predictor eta is the same in every one. Only the inverse link maps it back to the mean differently. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
section: The receipt: lm() was glm() all along
text:
The receipt: lm() was glm() all along


































[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 2 · ~52s] Here is the receipt, proven on the same gcse-on-lrt data you first met back in Chapter One. On the left, we fit it the usual way, with the el-em function. On the right, we fit the same formula on the same data through the generalized model function, declaring a gaussian family with an identity link. [CLICK 1] Look at the intercept first. It reads negative zero point zero one two on the el-em side, and negative zero point zero one two on the generalized-model side, the very same number. [CLICK 2] The slope tells the same story: zero point five nine five on both sides. These are not two close answers; they are identical to the last decimal, because they are the same fit computed two ways. The linear regression you ran back in Session Two was a generalized linear model with a Gaussian family and an identity link, the whole time. You were already driving this machine. [NEXT]

title: Three Parts of Every GLM
section: Swap the parts, keep the skill
text:
Swap the parts, keep the skill







Key takeaway


Every model in this course is one machine with three swappable parts: a random component (the outcome family), the same linear predictor η (your systematic part, unchanged since Chapter 1), and a link g(μ) = η that maps the mean onto the real line. Swap the family and the link, keep the linear predictor — and let interpretation, uncertainty, and diagnostics adapt to the chosen family/link.




The equivalence chain grew exactly as promised: lm() = glm(family = gaussian) is the course’s second great unifying insight.
Next: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model — add ordered thresholds plus a common-slope assumption to model ordinal outcomes. Notes, Ch 12 §The Generalized Linear Model Framework

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~44s] Let us gather the whole framework into a single idea. Every model in this course is one machine with three swappable parts. You choose a random component to match the outcome’s distribution and a link to map that outcome’s mean onto the real line, and underneath both sits the same linear predictor you have used since the first chapter. Because that systematic part is shared, your design logic transfers on the link scale; because the family and link change, interpretation, marginal effects, uncertainty, and diagnostics adapt. The receipt made the unifying idea concrete: the very first regression you ran was already a generalized linear model. This is the second great unifying insight of the course, after the discovery that the t-test, the analysis of variance, and regression are one thing. In the next lecture, we add ordered thresholds plus a common-slope assumption, which is how we will model ordinal outcomes with the proportional-odds model. The notes are linked just below. [END]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
section: Grades are ordered, but they are not numbers
text:
Grades are ordered, but they are not numbers


[DECK ch13-01 · 9 min · ~1136 words · clicks 5 · advances 8] [SLIDE 1/8 · clicks 0 · ~46s] Here are nearly two hundred student essays, each handed one of four grades: Poor, Fair, Good, or Excellent. Notice the one feature that makes this outcome special. The grades have a clear order, running from Poor up to Excellent, so one grade genuinely outranks another. Yet the order is all you have, because the spacing carries no meaning. The distance from Fair to Good is not a number, and there is no sense in which Excellent minus Good equals Fair minus Poor. An outcome like this, ordered but not measured, is called ordinal. Today we will build the model that respects that order without ever pretending the gaps are quantities. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
section: Two wrong roads, then the middle path
text:
Two wrong roads, then the middle path























[SLIDE 2/8 · clicks 1 · ~52s] Two shortcuts tempt us here, and each one fails. The first is to code the grades as one, two, three, four and run the el-em function on those numbers. Lining the grades up on the integer ticks forces the gaps to be equal, so the model quietly assumes the step from Good to Excellent is exactly the step from Poor to Fair. That is the ordinal twin of the numeric-coding trap. [CLICK 1] The opposite shortcut is a multinomial model, which fits the grades as four unrelated boxes. That fixes the spacing problem but commits the reverse error, because it discards the ordering and never learns that Poor sits below Excellent. We want the middle path: a model that respects the order while borrowing strength across categories instead of forcing equal gaps. That middle path is the cumulative-logit, or proportional-odds, model. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
text:
One hidden line, three cuts























[SLIDE 3/8 · clicks 1 · ~45s] The model rests on one picture, the same latent line we built for binary outcomes, simply given more cuts. Imagine a single hidden score of essay quality, y-star, running from weak essays on the left to strong ones on the right. For a yes-or-no outcome we placed one threshold; with four ordered grades we place three, the three dashed cuts, carving the line into four regions so each essay earns the grade of its region. The rust curve shows essays with a low linear predictor, whose quality piles up in Poor and Fair. [CLICK 1] Now raise the linear predictor and watch the slate curve. The whole distribution slides to the right, carrying more of its area into Good and Excellent. In a fitted model, that could mean raising a predictor with a positive beta; a predictor with a negative beta shifts the curve the other way. The crucial detail is that the cuts never move: eta shifts along a line of fixed thresholds, and that single moving picture is the entire model. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
section: The same slope at every cut
text:
The same slope at every cut
For each cut, split the scale and model the cumulative odds:


\[
\begin{aligned}
s = 1:\quad \operatorname{logit} P(y>1) &= \mathbf{x}'\boldsymbol{\beta} - \kappa_1 &&\text{(Fair or better)}\\
s = 2:\quad \operatorname{logit} P(y>2) &= \mathbf{x}'\boldsymbol{\beta} - \kappa_2 &&\text{(Good or better)}\\
s = 3:\quad \operatorname{logit} P(y>3) &= \mathbf{x}'\boldsymbol{\beta} - \kappa_3 &&\text{(Excellent)}
\end{aligned}
\]




Same β in all three lines · the cut comparison changes, but the κ₍s₎ thresholds stay fixed → proportional odds



[SLIDE 4/8 · clicks 2 · ~50s] To turn that picture into a model, we split the scale at each fixed cut and ask one ordinary logistic question: what are the cumulative odds of landing above that cut rather than at or below it. [CLICK 1] Here is one equation for each of the three cuts. The first asks about Fair or better rather than Poor, the second about Good or better, and the third about Excellent against everything below it. In every line, the logit of the probability that y exceeds the cut equals the linear predictor, x-prime-beta, minus the threshold for that cut, kappa-sub-s. [CLICK 2] Now look at what stays the same down the three lines. The very same beta appears in every one. What changes is the cumulative comparison: above cut one, above cut two, or above cut three. Each line uses the corresponding fixed threshold, kappa-sub-s. The thresholds stay fixed, and the slope does not change across cuts. That single shared slope is the proportional-odds assumption: a predictor’s effect on the cumulative odds is the same no matter where you split the scale. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
section: Same shape, shifted: the nested questions
text:
Same shape, shifted: the nested questions












[SLIDE 5/8 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Here is what the shared slope looks like as the linear predictor changes. Each curve is the cumulative probability of clearing one fixed cut: the top curve is the chance of Fair or better, the middle curve Good or better, and the lowest curve Excellent. Notice that the three curves have exactly the same shape and are simply shifted sideways. That shared shape is proportional odds drawn out in full, because the model assumes one common slope for all three cuts, so no curve is steeper than its neighbors. A helpful way to feel this is to picture three nested yes-or-no questions, one per cut. Proportional odds says that when eta changes, every nested question is tilted by the very same amount. In other words, the model assumes the whole ordinal scale moves with a single, uniform strength, rather than hard at one cut and weakly at another. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
text:
One number per predictor: exp(beta)












[SLIDE 6/8 · clicks 0 · ~50s] Under the proportional-odds assumption, each predictor collapses into a single, portable number. Exponentiate its coefficient and you get a cumulative odds ratio, the factor by which the odds of a higher grade are multiplied for a one-unit rise in that predictor, holding the other predictor constant. For word length the odds ratio is three point three two, well above one, so holding spelling errors constant, a one-letter increase in average word length multiplies the cumulative odds of a higher grade by about three and a third at every cut. For spelling errors it is zero point eight zero, below one, so holding word length constant, a one-percentage-point increase in spelling-error percentage multiplies those odds by about four-fifths, a reduction of roughly twenty percent, again at every cut. Proportional odds is what makes this clean, because that one ratio holds across the whole scale. Keep your logistic-regression discipline, though: an odds ratio multiplies odds, not probabilities, so do not read three point three two as tripling any probability. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
section: Go try it, then read the output
text:
Go try it, then read the output




























[SLIDE 7/8 · clicks 1 · ~48s] On the left is an explorer from the chapter that lets you set illustrative cuts, then hold those cuts fixed while the linear predictor slides the latent density. One caution: its round cuts of negative one point five, zero, and one point five are illustrative, not the grades thresholds. On the right is the fitted output, whose three intercepts, four point zero eight, five point one five, and six point one seven, are precisely the fitted thresholds, and whose two coefficients are word length at one point two zero and errors at negative zero point two three. [CLICK 1] One last feature trips many people. The polr function writes the model in its lower-tail form, logit of P of y at or below s equals zeta-sub-s minus x-prime-beta, so the minus sign is part of the formula. The printed coefficients keep their natural sign for the higher-category reading, and the rule to state plainly is that a positive beta increases the odds of a higher category. Word length is positive, so longer words push toward higher grades, while the negative errors coefficient pushes the other way. [NEXT]

title: More Cuts, One Slope: The Proportional-Odds Model
text:
Take this with you







Key takeaway


Give the latent line S − 1 fixed cuts and you get the cumulative-logit / proportional-odds model: \(\operatorname{logit} P(y>s) = \mathbf{x}'\boldsymbol{\beta} - \kappa_s\). The model assumes one slope is shared across every cut comparison, so exp(β) is a single cumulative odds ratio — here 3.32 for average word length holding errors constant, and 0.80 for spelling-error percentage holding word length constant. It respects the order without pretending the gaps are numbers.




The shared slope is a promise — is one β really enough for every cut? That proportional-odds assumption is exactly what a later lecture puts to the test.
Notes, Ch 13 §The Cumulative Logit Model

[SLIDE 8/8 · clicks 0 · ~42s] Let us gather the whole idea into a single breath. An ordinal outcome is one hidden line of quality given more than one fixed cut, and placing S minus one thresholds on it gives the cumulative-logit, or proportional-odds, model. The model assumes one slope is shared across every cut comparison, so a single odds ratio summarizes each predictor at every split at once, three point three two for average word length holding errors constant and zero point eight zero for spelling-error percentage holding word length constant. That shared slope respects the order of the grades without pretending the gaps between them are numbers. But the shared slope is a promise, not a fact, because we have simply assumed one beta is enough for every cut comparison. Whether it really is enough is exactly the question a later lecture takes up. The notes are linked just below. [END]
